topics.title: Selection of Materials for Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Selection of Materials for Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!NOTE]
> **Material Selection for Design** is a systematic, multi-criteria decision-making procedure in engineering design that translates *functional requirements*, *constraints*, *objectives*, and *free variables* into an optimal material choice, using property charts, material indices, performance metrics, and weighted property methods.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned curriculum, material selection is positioned as a **strategic decision** that simultaneously satisfies:

- **Function** → What the part must *do* (transmit force, store energy, insulate, conduct, etc.).
- **Objective** → What we want to *minimize* or *maximize* (mass, cost, volume, energy, environmental impact).
- **Constraints** → What the part must *not violate* (stress limits, deflection limits, temperature range, corrosion, dimensional stability).
- **Free Variables** → Geometric/structural degrees of freedom (cross-section, length, shape).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ashby's Paradigm**: Material selection is NOT picking a familiar material first. It is a *derived* outcome of an optimization problem where material enters as a *property combination* (called the **material index** $M$).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are choosing a **material for a cricket bat handle, a bridge cable, and a frying pan handle** — all are "handles" but require completely different materials (cane, steel, bakelite). Why?

- **Cricket bat handle** → must be *light, stiff, and absorb shock* → Cork/rubber/wood composite.
- **Bridge cable** → must be *strong and light* under tension → High-strength steel.
- **Frying pan handle** → must *resist heat* and not burn the hand → Thermoset plastic (low thermal conductivity).

> [!NOTE]
> The **function** (handle), **objective** (lightweight, strong, heat resistant), and **constraint** (no hand burn) drive three different *material indices* — a single number that ranks materials for that specific design problem.

> **[Standard Metric Highlight]** The most widely used reference for material indices is the **Young's Modulus–Density ($E$–$\rho$) chart** and **Strength–Density ($\sigma_f$–$\rho$) chart** developed by **Michael F. Ashby**, often called the **Ashby Material Selection Charts**.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Material Properties in Design Selection

| Property Class | Examples | Design Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Young's Modulus ($E$), Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$), Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_u$), Hardness ($H$), Fracture Toughness ($K_{IC}$) | Strength, stiffness, wear |
| **Physical** | Density ($\rho$), Thermal Conductivity ($k$), Specific Heat ($c_p$), CTE ($\alpha$) | Mass, thermal management |
| **Chemical** | Corrosion Resistance, Oxidation Resistance | Service environment |
| **Manufacturing** | Castability, Weldability, Machinability, Formability | DFMA integration |
| **Economic** | Cost per kg, Cost per volume | Commercial viability |
| **Environmental** | Recyclability, CO₂ footprint, Bio-degradability | Sustainability (KTU 2024 emphasis) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **KTU 2024 Scheme**, environmental and life-cycle properties carry **increasing weightage** under NEP 2020 outcome-based assessment.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Material Selection Strategy (Ashby Method)

The systematic four-step procedure used globally and tested in KTU examinations:

**Step 1 — Translate the Design Requirements**
- Identify the **function** of the component.
- Express the **objective** mathematically (minimize mass $m$, volume $V$, cost $C$).
- Identify the **constraints** (e.g., $\sigma \leq \sigma_{max}$, $\delta \leq \delta_{max}$).
- Identify the **free variables** (cross-section, length, shape factor).

**Step 2 — Screen Materials Using Property Limits**
- Eliminate materials that violate *non-negotiable* property bounds.
- Example: For a marine application, eliminate all metals with poor corrosion resistance; for high-temperature, eliminate low-melting polymers.

**Step 3 — Rank Materials Using Material Indices**
- Derive a single **material index** $M$ that embodies the optimization.
- Plot $M$ on Ashby charts; the material that maximizes (or minimizes) $M$ is the best.

**Step 4 — Explore Shape and Manufacturing Process**
- Once a material family is chosen, optimize **geometry** and **process** (DFMA integration).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works**: The material index reduces an $n$-dimensional property space to a **scalar metric** that allows rigorous ranking without subjective bias.

---

### 2.2 Derivation Framework — General Procedure

A typical design problem has the form:

$$
\text{Objective} = f(\text{Material Properties}, \text{Free Variables})
$$

The standard procedure to extract the **material index** is:

1. Write the **objective function** in terms of *free variables* and *material properties*.
2. Write the **constraint equation** (e.g., strength or stiffness condition).
3. **Eliminate free variables** by solving the constraint for one free variable.
4. **Substitute** back into the objective — the result contains only material properties.
5. Group material properties into the **material index** $M$.

> [!TIP]
> The mass of a part is generally expressed as:
> $$m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot A \cdot L$$
> where $A$ is the cross-section, $L$ is length, $\rho$ is density. Always begin a derivation from this fundamental relation.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Material Indices)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following indices. KTU frequently tests derivation of any one of these in **14-mark problems**.

| S.No. | Loading Case | Objective | Constraint | Material Index $M$ (to MAXIMIZE) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Light, stiff tension tie** | Minimize mass | Stiffness $\delta = \frac{FL}{AE} = C$ | $M = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ |
| 2 | **Light, strong tension tie** | Minimize mass | Strength $\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{\sigma_f}{S}$ | $M = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{\rho}$ |
| 3 | **Light, stiff beam (deflection)** | Minimize mass | Deflection $\delta = \frac{C F L^3}{B E I} = C$ | $M = \dfrac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}$ |
| 4 | **Light, strong beam (yielding)** | Minimize mass | Bending stress $\sigma = \frac{C F L}{B \sigma_f} = C$ | $M = \dfrac{\sigma_f^{1/2}}{\rho}$ |
| 5 | **Light, stiff column (Euler buckling)** | Minimize mass | Buckling $P_{cr} = \frac{C \pi^2 E I}{L^2}$ | $M = \dfrac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}$ |
| 6 | **Light, strong column (yielding)** | Minimize mass | Yield strength | $M = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{\rho}$ |
| 7 | **Light panel (avoid buckling)** | Minimize mass | Buckling load | $M = \dfrac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}$ |
| 8 | **Light flywheel (max KE/energy)** | Maximize KE per unit mass | $E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | $M = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{\rho}$ |
| 9 | **Light spring (max stored energy)** | Minimize mass for given $U$ | $U = \dfrac{\sigma^2 V}{2E}$ | $M = \dfrac{\sigma_f^2}{E}$ |
| 10 | **Thermal shock resistance** | Minimize thermal stress | $\sigma = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$ | $M = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{E \, \alpha}$ |
| 11 | **Heat sink (thermal management)** | Minimize temp rise | $\Delta T = \dfrac{Q L}{k A}$ | $M = \dfrac{k}{\rho}$ or $\dfrac{k}{a \rho}$ |
| 12 | **Cost-minimized tie** | Minimize cost | Strength | $M = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{C_m \rho}$ where $C_m$ = cost/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> The notation $B$, $C$ above are **geometric constants** (cross-section shape factor, second moment factors). They cancel out during material selection.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Driving Index |
|---|---|---|
| Aerospace wing spar | Light, stiff beam | $E^{1/2} / \rho$ |
| Bicycle frame | Light, strong tube | $\sigma_f^{1/2} / \rho$ |
| Suspension spring | Energy storage | $\sigma_f^2 / E$ |
| Heat exchanger fin | Heat dissipation | $k / \rho$ |
| Pressure vessel | Yield, fatigue | $\sigma_f / \rho$ |
| PCB substrate | Insulating, low CTE | $1/\alpha$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coupling constant / Coupling factor**: When the objective involves BOTH stiffness AND strength simultaneously, the combined index uses a *coupling parameter* $\alpha$ such that the effective index becomes $M = (E^{\alpha} \sigma_f^{1-\alpha}) / \rho$ with $\alpha \in [0,1]$ — this is a **multiple-constraint** selection.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Material Index for a Light, Stiff Tension Tie (14-Mark Favorite)

**Problem Setup:**
A tie of length $L$ must carry a tensile load $F$ without stretching more than a given amount $\delta$. The mass $m$ is to be minimized. Material properties $E$ (Young's Modulus) and $\rho$ (density) are the variables. Cross-section $A$ is the free variable.

**Step 1 — Write the Objective Function:**

The mass of a uniform tie of length $L$ and cross-section $A$ is:

$$
m = \rho \cdot A \cdot L
$$

This is the quantity we wish to minimize.

**Step 2 — Write the Stiffness Constraint:**

The axial extension of the tie under load $F$ is given by Hooke's law:

$$
\delta = \frac{F \cdot L}{A \cdot E}
$$

The design constraint is that $\delta$ must be less than or equal to a specified deflection $\delta_{max}$, so we set $\delta = \delta_{max}$ (we choose the limiting case for an optimal design):

$$
\delta_{max} = \frac{F \cdot L}{A \cdot E}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the Constraint for the Free Variable $A$:**

Rearranging for $A$:

$$
A = \frac{F \cdot L}{E \cdot \delta_{max}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $A$ into the Objective Function:**

$$
m = \rho \cdot L \cdot \left(\frac{F \cdot L}{E \cdot \delta_{max}}\right)
$$

$$
m = \frac{F \cdot L^2}{\delta_{max}} \cdot \left(\frac{\rho}{E}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Material Index:**

The factor $\dfrac{F \cdot L^2}{\delta_{max}}$ is purely a *function of the design load and geometry* and does not depend on material. Therefore, to **minimize $m$**, we must **minimize** the ratio $\rho / E$, which is equivalent to **maximizing**:

$$
\boxed{M = \frac{E}{\rho}}
$$

This is the **specific stiffness** — the material index for a light, stiff tension tie.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: To rank materials, plot $E$ on the y-axis and $\rho$ on the x-axis. The straight line $E = M \cdot \rho$ (with slope $M$) is the *selection line*. Materials lying **above** this line have $E / \rho$ greater than $M$ and are therefore *better*. The **steepest selection line** that touches at least one material gives the **optimum** choice.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Material Index for a Light, Stiff Beam

**Problem Setup:**
A beam of length $L$ and square cross-section of side $B$ (so $I = B^4 / 12$) carries a transverse load $F$ causing a maximum deflection $\delta$ (which is a *function* of $F$, $L$, $B$, and $E$). The mass is to be minimized.

**Step 1 — Objective Function:**

For a beam, the volume is $V = B^2 \cdot L$. Thus:

$$
m = \rho \cdot B^2 \cdot L
$$

**Step 2 — Stiffness Constraint (Cantilever End Deflection):**

For a cantilever beam with end load $F$:

$$
\delta = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I} = \frac{4 F L^3}{E B^4}
$$

Set $\delta = \delta_{max}$:

$$
\delta_{max} = \frac{4 F L^3}{E B^4}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Free Variable $B$:**

$$
B^4 = \frac{4 F L^3}{E \, \delta_{max}}
$$

$$
B = \left(\frac{4 F L^3}{E \, \delta_{max}}\right)^{1/4}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Mass:**

$$
m = \rho \cdot L \cdot \left(\frac{4 F L^3}{E \, \delta_{max}}\right)^{1/2}
$$

$$
m = \rho \cdot L \cdot \left(\frac{4 F L^3}{\delta_{max}}\right)^{1/2} \cdot \frac{1}{E^{1/2}}
$$

Grouping:

$$
m = L \cdot \left(\frac{4 F L^3}{\delta_{max}}\right)^{1/2} \cdot \frac{\rho}{E^{1/2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Material Index:**

To minimize $m$, we must **minimize** $\rho / E^{1/2}$, i.e., **maximize**:

$$
\boxed{M = \frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Insight**: For a beam, the exponent of $E$ drops from $1$ (tie) to $1/2$. This is because in a beam, **stiffness is governed by the second moment of area $I$**, which is a *4th-power* function of dimension $B$. This is why **CFRP and ceramics** (very high $E$) dominate beam applications, not tension ties.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Material Index for a Light Spring (Energy Storage per Unit Mass)

**Problem Setup:**
A helical compression spring of volume $V$ must store a given strain energy $U$. The mass is to be minimized.

**Step 1 — Strain Energy in Spring:**

The elastic strain energy stored in a body of volume $V$ subjected to a uniform uniaxial stress $\sigma$ is:

$$
U = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E} \cdot V
$$

**Step 2 — Objective Function:**

We want to minimize the mass:

$$
m = \rho \cdot V
$$

**Step 3 — Solve Constraint for $V$:**

From the energy equation, given $U$ and assuming the material is loaded to its yield limit $\sigma = \sigma_f$ (i.e., using full strength capacity):

$$
V = \frac{2 E U}{\sigma_f^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Mass:**

$$
m = \rho \cdot \frac{2 E U}{\sigma_f^2} = 2 U \cdot \frac{\rho \, E}{\sigma_f^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Material Index:**

To minimize $m$ for fixed $U$, **minimize** $\rho E / \sigma_f^2$, which is equivalent to **maximizing**:

$$
\boxed{M = \frac{\sigma_f^2}{E \, \rho}}
$$

Or, equivalently (when minimizing mass per unit energy stored):

$$
\boxed{M' = \frac{\sigma_f^2}{E}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Application**: This is why **maraging steels, titanium alloys, and elastomers** (rubber) are excellent spring materials. Rubber has $\sigma_f$ low but $E$ very low — so $\sigma_f^2 / E$ is large. Steel has $\sigma_f$ high and moderate $E$ — also large $\sigma_f^2 / E$. Glass has high $\sigma_f$ but very high $E$ — poor spring material.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Material Index for a Light Column (Euler Buckling)

**Problem Setup:**
A column of length $L$ and circular cross-section with radius $r$ must support an axial compressive load $F$ without Euler buckling. Mass is to be minimized.

**Step 1 — Objective Function:**

For a circular cross-section, $A = \pi r^2$:

$$
m = \rho \cdot \pi r^2 \cdot L
$$

**Step 2 — Euler Buckling Constraint:**

$$
F = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L^2}
$$

For a circular cross-section, $I = \dfrac{\pi r^4}{4}$. So:

$$
F = \frac{\pi^2 E \pi r^4}{4 L^2} = \frac{\pi^3 E r^4}{4 L^2}
$$

Set $F = F_{max}$ (critical buckling load):

$$
r^4 = \frac{4 F_{max} L^2}{\pi^3 E}
$$

$$
r = \left(\frac{4 F_{max} L^2}{\pi^3 E}\right)^{1/4}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Mass:**

$$
m = \rho \pi L \cdot \left(\frac{4 F_{max} L^2}{\pi^3 E}\right)^{1/2}
$$

$$
m = \rho \pi L \cdot \frac{(4 F_{max} L^2)^{1/2}}{(\pi^3 E)^{1/2}}
$$

$$
m = \rho \cdot L \cdot (4 F_{max} L^2)^{1/2} \cdot \pi^{-1/2} \cdot E^{-1/2}
$$

Grouping:

$$
m = \text{(geom/latent factors)} \cdot \frac{\rho}{E^{1/2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Material Index:**

To minimize $m$, **maximize**:

$$
\boxed{M = \frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Same as beam!** This is because both beam stiffness and column buckling are governed by the *second moment of area*, leading to the $E^{1/2}/\rho$ form. This is a *very important* observation in KTU exams.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Material Index for Minimum Cost (Cost-Sensitive Selection)

**Problem Setup:**
Minimize cost $C$ for a tension tie carrying load $F$ with strength constraint.

**Step 1 — Cost Function:**

$$
C = C_m \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot L
$$

where $C_m$ is the **cost per unit mass** (INR/kg or USD/kg).

**Step 2 — Strength Constraint:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{\sigma_f}{n}
$$

(where $n$ is the safety factor) gives:

$$
A = \frac{n F}{\sigma_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Cost:**

$$
C = C_m \cdot \rho \cdot L \cdot \frac{n F}{\sigma_f} = n F L \cdot \frac{C_m \rho}{\sigma_f}
$$

**Step 4 — Material Index:**

To **minimize $C$**, **minimize** $\dfrac{C_m \rho}{\sigma_f}$, which is equivalent to **maximizing**:

$$
\boxed{M = \frac{\sigma_f}{C_m \, \rho}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Trend**: Cost indices are increasingly tested, especially under the **NEP-2020 sustainability and life-cycle cost** emphasis.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Index

| Design Function | Free Variable | Dominant Property | Index $M$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tie** | Area $A$ | $\sigma_f$ (strength) | $\sigma_f / \rho$ |
| **Tie** (stiff) | Area $A$ | $E$ (modulus) | $E / \rho$ |
| **Beam** | Section modulus $B^3$ | $\sigma_f$ | $\sigma_f^{1/2} / \rho$ |
| **Beam** (stiff) | $B^4$ | $E$ | $E^{1/2} / \rho$ |
| **Column** (buckling) | $r^4$ | $E$ | $E^{1/2} / \rho$ |
| **Spring** | $V$ | $\sigma_f^2 / E$ | $\sigma_f^2 / (E \rho)$ |
| **Flywheel** | $V$ | $\sigma_f$ (rim stress) | $\sigma_f / \rho$ |
| **Heat sink** | $A$ | $k$ | $k / \rho$ |
| **Thermal shock** | $V$ | $\sigma_f / (E \alpha)$ | $\sigma_f / (E \alpha \rho)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **systematic 5-step procedure** (objective → constraint → solve free var → substitute → group) is the key to KTU 14-mark answers. Examiners award marks for: (i) correct objective, (ii) correct constraint, (iii) algebra, (iv) final boxed answer with units, (v) physical interpretation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Ashby Material Selection Flowchart (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: TRANSLATE Design Requirements"] --> A1["Identify Function e.g. Tie, Beam, Spring"]
    A1 --> A2["Identify Objective e.g. Minimize mass m or cost C"]
    A2 --> A3["Identify Constraints e.g. sigma less than sigma_f"]
    A3 --> A4["Identify Free Variables e.g. Cross section A, Length L"]
    A4 --> B["Step 2: SCREEN Materials"]
    B --> B1["Apply Property Limits e.g. T greater than 300C"]
    B1 --> B2["Apply Service Filters e.g. No corrosion in seawater"]
    B2 --> B3["Eliminate Unsuitable Materials on Bar Chart"]
    B3 --> C["Step 3: RANK with Material Index M"]
    C --> C1["Derive M from Objective and Constraint"]
    C1 --> C2["Plot M on Ashby Chart E rho OR sigma_f rho"]
    C2 --> C3["Selection Line slope equals M max"]
    C3 --> C4["Material Above Line is BETTER"]
    C4 --> D["Step 4: SELECT Process and Shape"]
    D --> D1["DFMA Compatibility Check"]
    D1 --> D2["Manufacturing Process Selection"]
    D2 --> D3["Final Material and Process Locked"]
    D3 --> E["DESIGN OUTPUT READY"]
```

---

### 4.2 Conceptual Diagram — Material Index Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS["DESIGN VARIABLES"]
        M1["Material Property 1: E"]
        M2["Material Property 2: rho"]
        M3["Material Property 3: sigma_f"]
    end
    subgraph CONSTRAINTS["DESIGN CONSTRAINTS"]
        C1["Stiffness: F over AE greater than limit"]
        C2["Strength: F over A less than sigma_f"]
        C3["Geometry: A and L fixed"]
    end
    subgraph DERIVATION["MATHEMATICAL STEP"]
        D1["Eliminate Free Variable A"]
        D2["Substitute into Objective Function m = rho A L"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["MATERIAL INDEX"]
        O1["M = E over rho  for stiff tie"]
        O2["M = sigma_f over rho  for strong tie"]
        O3["M = E power half over rho  for stiff beam"]
    end
    M1 --> D1
    M2 --> D1
    M3 --> D1
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> O1
    D2 --> O2
    D2 --> O3
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic — Ashby Chart Selection Logic (E vs ρ)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Ashby Chart: E (Y axis) vs rho (X axis)"] --> B["Draw Selection Line: E = M × rho"]
    B --> C{"Does Line Touch Candidate Material?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["Material is VIABLE for Index M"]
    C -- No --> E["Material SUBOPTIMAL for Index M"]
    D --> F["Steepen Line slope M increases"]
    F --> G{"Any Material Above New Line?"}
    G -- Yes --> C
    G -- No --> H["OPTIMAL Material: Tangent Point"]
    H --> I["Lock Material and Compute Geometry"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight**: The slope of the selection line on a log-log Ashby chart is the **material index** $M$ that is being maximized. The steepest line that still touches a material gives the **optimum**.

---

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Multiple Constraints and Conflicting Objectives

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Component Design Brief"] --> B{"Single Objective and Constraint?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Derive SINGLE Material Index M"]
    B -- No --> D["MULTIPLE CONSTRAINTS detected"]
    D --> E["Apply Limiting Property Method: Eliminate by Hard Limits"]
    E --> F["Multiple Material Indices M1 M2 M3..."]
    F --> G{"Tradeoff Plot: M1 on X, M2 on Y"}
    G --> H["Bubble Chart with Cost and Mass as Drivers"]
    H --> I["Pareto Front: Non Dominated Materials"]
    I --> J["Select using Weighted Property Method or CES Database"]
    J --> K["FINAL MATERIAL DECISION"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **multiple constraints** (e.g., *both* stiffness *and* strength), use the **material index with coupling constant**:
> $$M = \frac{E^{\alpha} \, \sigma_f^{1-\alpha}}{\rho}$$
> where $\alpha \in [0,1]$ is the **coupling exponent** determined by the relative weightage of stiffness vs strength.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
> Define the term **"Material Index"** in the context of design for manufacturing. Give **one example** with the index formula.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> A **material index** is a *property combination* that characterizes how well a material performs in a given design application, derived from the objective and constraints of the design problem. It is a scalar quantity that allows ranking of materials for a specific design function.
>
> **Example**: For a *light, stiff tension tie* (minimize mass with stiffness constraint), the material index is:
> $$M = \frac{E}{\rho}$$
> The material with the **highest $E/\rho$** (e.g., carbon fiber, CFRP) is the best choice for the application.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> Distinguish between **performance index** and **material index**. Why is **specific stiffness $E/\rho$** called a material index for a tension tie?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **performance index** is a *property combination* that includes the **shape factor** of the part, while a **material index** is *independent of geometry* and depends **only on material properties**. The performance index is the *engineered quantity* (objective divided by shape factor) and the material index is the *pure material quantity* extracted from it.
>
> For a tension tie of length $L$ and area $A$, the performance index is $P = \dfrac{F / (A \, \delta_{max})}{m / L}$ which, after eliminating the geometry using the constraint $A = F L / (E \delta_{max})$, reduces to the material index $M = E / \rho$. Hence $E/\rho$ is called the material index.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern)**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically with two sub-parts of **7 marks each** or one design problem of 14 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Derive Material Index for a Light, Strong Beam* `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Q.A (a) Derive the material index for a light beam of square cross-section that must not fail in bending under a load $F$, minimizing mass.** **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Objective Function:**

For a square beam of side $B$ and length $L$:

$$
m = \rho \cdot B^2 \cdot L
$$

**Step 2 — Bending Stress Constraint:**

The maximum bending stress in a beam with section modulus $Z = \dfrac{B^3}{6}$ is:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{bending}}{Z} = \frac{C_1 F L}{B^3}
$$

where $C_1$ is a constant depending on loading type (e.g., $C_1 = 1/6$ for simply supported center load). The constraint is:

$$
\sigma_{max} \leq \sigma_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{C_1 F L}{B^3} = \frac{\sigma_f}{n}
$$

Set $C_2 = C_1 n F L$ as a constant for the design:

$$
B^3 = \frac{C_1 n F L}{\sigma_f} = \frac{C_2}{\sigma_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Free Variable $B$:**

$$
B = \left(\frac{C_2}{\sigma_f}\right)^{1/3}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Mass:**

$$
m = \rho \cdot L \cdot \left(\frac{C_2}{\sigma_f}\right)^{2/3} = \rho \cdot L \cdot C_2^{2/3} \cdot \sigma_f^{-2/3}
$$

$$
m = L \cdot C_2^{2/3} \cdot \frac{\rho}{\sigma_f^{2/3}}
$$

**Step 5 — Material Index:**

The factor $L \cdot C_2^{2/3}$ is geometry/load-dependent. To minimize $m$, **minimize** $\rho / \sigma_f^{2/3}$, i.e., **maximize**:

$$
\boxed{M = \frac{\sigma_f^{2/3}}{\rho}}
$$

Or equivalently $M^3 = \sigma_f^2 / \rho^3$, often written as $M = \sigma_f^{1/2} / \rho$ when the constraint is set differently. (Verify with respect to KTU textbook convention.)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Answer Tip**: The exact form $\sigma_f^{2/3}/\rho$ or $\sigma_f^{1/2}/\rho$ depends on whether the constraint is on **maximum stress at the surface** or **average stress**. Always clearly state your assumption in the exam.

---

**Q.A (b) List any **four material indices** with their corresponding design applications.** **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

| S.No. | Design Application | Material Index $M$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Light, stiff tension tie (stiffness constraint) | $M = E / \rho$ |
| 2 | Light, strong tension tie (strength constraint) | $M = \sigma_f / \rho$ |
| 3 | Light, stiff beam (deflection constraint) | $M = E^{1/2} / \rho$ |
| 4 | Light spring (energy storage per unit mass) | $M = \sigma_f^2 / E$ |
| 5 | Heat sink (thermal management) | $M = k / \rho$ |
| 6 | Thermal shock resistant component | $M = \sigma_f / (E \, \alpha)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Note**: Marks are awarded for **(i) correct formula**, **(ii) correct exponent**, and **(iii) correct application context**. A common error is writing $E/\rho$ for a beam — KTU examiners explicitly mark this **wrong** for beam applications.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *Material Selection Procedure and Ashby Charts* `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q.B (a) Explain the systematic procedure for material selection proposed by Ashby. List the steps with a brief description of each.** **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

The Ashby material selection procedure is a four-step systematic method:

**Step 1 — Translation of Design Requirements:**

The design requirements are translated into four terms: **Function** (what the component does), **Objective** (what is to be minimized or maximized, e.g., mass, cost, volume), **Constraints** (conditions that must be satisfied, e.g., $\sigma < \sigma_{max}$, $\delta < \delta_{max}$), and **Free Variables** (parameters the designer can choose, e.g., cross-section, length).

**Step 2 — Screening Using Property Limits:**

Materials are filtered by *non-negotiable* property bounds. A bar chart of each property (e.g., maximum service temperature) is drawn, and materials failing the bound are eliminated. This step typically narrows hundreds of materials to ~30 candidates.

**Step 3 — Ranking Using Material Indices:**

A **material index $M$** is derived from the objective and constraints. Materials are plotted on Ashby property charts (e.g., $E$ vs $\rho$, $\sigma_f$ vs $\rho$, $E$ vs $\sigma_f$). A selection line of slope $M$ is drawn; the material that maximizes $M$ (lies on the steepest viable line) is the **optimum**.

**Step 4 — Supporting Documentation and Final Selection:**

The candidate material is checked against **manufacturing compatibility** (castability, weldability, machinability), **cost**, **availability**, **recyclability**, and **life-cycle factors**. The final material is then locked and the geometry optimized accordingly.

---

**Q.B (b) A cantilever beam of length $L = 1$ m and square cross-section of side $B$ must support an end load $F = 500$ N with maximum deflection $\delta_{max} = 5$ mm. Using the Ashby method, derive the material index and indicate which property chart should be used for ranking.** **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Objective Function:**

$$
m = \rho \cdot B^2 \cdot L = \rho \cdot B^2 \cdot (1 \text{ m})
$$

**Step 2 — Deflection Constraint:**

For a cantilever beam with end load $F$:

$$
\delta_{max} = \frac{F L^3}{3 E I} = \frac{4 F L^3}{E B^4} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $B$:**

$$
B^4 = \frac{4 F L^3}{E \cdot \delta_{max}} = \frac{4 \times 500 \times 1^3}{E \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2000}{5 \times 10^{-3} E} = \frac{4 \times 10^5}{E}
$$

$$
B = \left(\frac{4 \times 10^5}{E}\right)^{1/4}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Mass:**

$$
m = \rho \cdot \left(\frac{4 \times 10^5}{E}\right)^{1/2} = \rho \cdot \frac{(4 \times 10^5)^{1/2}}{E^{1/2}}
$$

$$
m = 632.5 \cdot \frac{\rho}{E^{1/2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Material Index:**

To minimize $m$, **maximize**:

$$
\boxed{M = \frac{E^{1/2}}{\rho}}
$$

**Chart Required**: **Young's Modulus ($E$) vs Density ($\rho$)** plot (Ashby chart). The selection line on a log-log plot has slope $1/2$, and the material on the steepest viable line is the optimal choice (e.g., CFRP, ceramics, carbon fiber).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Confusing tie and beam indices** — A beam gives $E^{1/2}/\rho$, NOT $E/\rho$. Students who use the tie index for a beam lose 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to eliminate the free variable $B$** — KTU specifically awards 2 marks for *algebraic elimination*.
> 3. **Not stating the chart** — In Part (b), explicitly mention "$E$–$\rho$ chart" or "Ashby modulus-density chart" for full marks.
> 4. **Missing units in final answer** — Always state units (e.g., $\text{GPa} \cdot \text{cm}^3/\text{g}$ for $E^{1/2}/\rho$).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Recall List for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Material Index $M$** = scalar property combination that ranks materials for a given design.
- **Specific Stiffness $E/\rho$** → tension tie (stiffness).
- **Specific Strength $\sigma_f/\rho$** → tension tie (strength), light flywheel rim.
- **Stiff Beam Index $E^{1/2}/\rho$** → cantilever, simply supported beams, Euler column.
- **Strong Beam Index $\sigma_f^{1/2}/\rho$** → beam with bending yield constraint.
- **Spring Index $\sigma_f^2 / E$** → helical, leaf springs (energy storage).
- **Thermal Shock Index $\sigma_f / (E \, \alpha)$** → furnace components, rocket nozzles.
- **Heat Sink Index $k / \rho$** → electronic cooling fins, heat exchangers.
- **Cost Index $\sigma_f / (C_m \, \rho)$** → cost-sensitive applications.
- **Ashby Charts**: $E$–$\rho$, $\sigma_f$–$\rho$, $E$–$\sigma_f$, $k$–$\rho$, $\alpha$–$E$ — **memorize the axis labels**.
- **Selection Line slope = material index** on log-log plot.
- **Optimization Procedure**: Objective → Constraint → Solve for free variable → Substitute → Group into $M$.
- **NEP 2020 Emphasis**: Sustainability, life-cycle cost, and recyclability weigh heavily.
- **DFMA Link**: Material selection feeds into process selection (e.g., polymers → injection molding; metals → casting/machining).
- **Multiple Constraints**: Use coupling exponent $\alpha$ or weighted property method.
- **Always state units** in the final boxed answer.
- **Always identify the Ashby chart** to be used for ranking.

> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Aspirants**: The most frequently asked 14-mark question is the **derivation of a material index from a given design problem**. Practice the 5-step algebraic elimination at least 10 times before the exam. The numerical part is usually simple — the **algebraic manipulation** is what examiners test.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Developments in Material Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Developments in Material Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Developments in Material Technology** refer to the systematic evolution, innovation, and engineering application of advanced metallic, polymeric, ceramic, composite, and hybrid materials that directly influence the *Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)* philosophy. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic encompasses the study of novel materials, smart/functional materials, and emerging material processing routes whose properties — strength, machinability, formability, joinability, and recyclability — dictate the manufacturability and assemblability of a product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):**
> "Developments in material technology deal with the advances in engineering materials — including smart materials, composites, biomaterials, and nanomaterials — and their influence on design decisions related to manufacturing processes, assembly methods, cost reduction, and product life-cycle performance."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine building a house. Earlier, you only had clay bricks, cement, and wood. As construction technology advanced, you got **fly-ash bricks, tempered glass, reinforced concrete, polymer-modified mortars, and self-healing concrete**. None of these *replace* the old materials completely — instead, each new material **expands the designer's vocabulary**, allowing a more efficient, lighter, cheaper, or smarter structure.

In the same way, **developments in material technology expand the designer's vocabulary** in mechanical/product engineering. A designer is no longer limited to mild steel, brass, and cast iron — they can now choose:
- **Carbon-fiber reinforced polymers (CFRP)** for lightweight aerospace parts
- **Shape memory alloys (Nitinol)** for actuators and medical stents
- **Additively manufactured (3D-printed) titanium alloys** for custom implants
- **Self-healing polymers** for longer service life

Each of these materials changes the *manufacturing process*, the *joining method*, the *tolerances achievable*, and the *assembly strategy*.

## 1.3 Key Metrics and Standard Indicators

The following standard engineering metrics govern material selection in modern DFMA:

- **Specific Strength** $\left(\dfrac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho}\right)$ — units: $\dfrac{\text{MPa}}{\text{kg/m}^{3}}$ or $\dfrac{\text{N}\cdot \text{m}}{\text{kg}}$
- **Specific Modulus** $\left(\dfrac{E}{\rho}\right)$ — units: $\dfrac{\text{GPa}}{\text{kg/m}^{3}}$
- **Strength-to-Weight Ratio** $\left(\dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\rho \cdot g}\right)$ — dimensionless when $g$ is normalized
- **Recyclability Index (R)** — dimensionless, $0 \le R \le 1$
- **Manufacturing Energy per kg** $\left(\dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{kg}}\right)$ — embodied energy
- **Ashby's Material Index** $M = f\left(\dfrac{\sigma_{y}^{a} \cdot E^{b}}{\rho^{c}}\right)$ — application-specific

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU Board Exams:**
> Every question on "developments in material technology" tests whether the student can *link* a new material property to a *manufacturing consequence* and an *assembly consequence*. The examiner is checking design-level integration, not just material definitions.

## 1.4 Categorization of Modern Materials (DFMA Viewpoint)

| # | Category | Examples | DFMA Implication |
|---|----------|----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Smart / Functional Materials** | Shape Memory Alloys (SMA), Piezoelectric ceramics, Magnetostrictive materials | Actuation without motors → fewer parts → simpler assembly |
| 2 | **Advanced Composites** | CFRP, GFRP, Kevlar-epoxy, Metal Matrix Composites (MMC) | High specific strength → reduced part count via integral design |
| 3 | **High-Performance Alloys** | Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel 718, Maraging steel 300 | Difficult to machine → demands near-net-shape processes |
| 4 | **Biomaterials** | Hydroxyapatite, PEEK, bioresorbable Mg alloys | Drives clean-room assembly and medical-grade tolerances |
| 5 | **Nanomaterials** | Graphene, CNTs, nano-Alumina | New joining techniques (nano-adhesives, diffusion bonding) |
| 6 | **Additive Manufacturing (AM) Materials** | PA12, Ti powder, Inconel 718 powder, photopolymers | Eliminates fixturing; enables lattice structures |
| 7 | **Sustainable / Green Materials** | Bioplastics (PLA, PHA), recycled aluminium, bamboo composites | Drives design-for-disassembly and circular economy |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Material Development — Driver Analysis

Modern material technology does **not** evolve randomly. It is pushed by four engineering drivers that intersect directly with DFMA:

1. **Performance Demand** — aerospace, defense, motorsport require $\sigma/\rho$ ratios that traditional alloys cannot deliver → leads to **composites and Ti-alloys**.
2. **Manufacturing Cost Pressure** — high machining cost of Inconel/Ti → leads to **near-net-shape AM and powder metallurgy**.
3. **Assembly Simplification** — desire to reduce fasteners → leads to **smart materials** (SMA fasteners, snap-fit polymers) and **integral composites**.
4. **Environmental & Regulatory Pressure** — REACH, RoHS, end-of-life vehicle (ELV) directives → leads to **biodegradable polymers and recyclable composites**.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hack:** Whenever the question says *"Explain how developments in material technology affect DFMA"* — answer using these **four drivers**. Examiners love structured answers.

## 2.2 The "How" — Material Property → Manufacturing Consequence Mapping

The chain of design logic is:
$$\text{New Material Property} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{New Process Window} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{New Assembly Strategy}$$

### 2.2.1 Smart Materials and DFMA Consequences

**Shape Memory Alloys (SMA), e.g., Nitinol**
- Property: recovers up to **8%** strain upon heating above transformation temperature $A_f$.
- Manufacturing consequence: cold-workable, but final shape is *set* by heat treatment.
- Assembly consequence: **shrink-fit, rivets, and couplers** can be replaced by SMA rings that contract on heating — eliminating threaded fasteners.

**Piezoelectric Materials (PZT, PVDF)**
- Property: $d_{33}$ charge coefficient (typically $300{-}600 \; \text{pC/N}$ for PZT-5H).
- Manufacturing consequence: requires controlled poling; brittle → needs embedding in composites.
- Assembly consequence: enables **active vibration damping**, removing mechanical dampers from assemblies.

### 2.2.2 Advanced Composites and DFMA Consequences

| Property | Manufacturing Effect | Assembly Effect |
|----------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Anisotropic strength | Lay-up sequence matters; cutting needs diamond tooling | Bolted joints cause delamination → bonded or co-cured joints preferred |
| Low thermal expansion | Near-dimensionally stable in service | Allows snap-fits with metallic inserts |
| High $\sigma/\rho$ | Enables integral one-piece design | Reduces fastener count by 30–60% |

### 2.2.3 Additive Manufacturing (AM) Materials

- **Powder bed fusion (PBF)** for Ti-6Al-4V, AlSi10Mg, Inconel 718.
- **FDM/FFF** for PLA, ABS, Nylon, PEEK.
- **SLA/DLP** for photopolymers, dental resins.

Manufacturing consequence: **layer-by-layer** → no fixturing, supports overhangs.
Assembly consequence: **lattice infill** and **conformal cooling channels** are *embodied in the part* → fewer parts in the BOM.

### 2.2.4 Biomaterials and DFMA Consequences

- **PEEK (Polyetheretherketone)** — high $T_g = 143^\circ\text{C}$, biocompatible.
- **Ti-6Al-4V ELI** — Extra Low Interstitial grade for implants.
- Manufacturing consequence: must be machined or AM'd in **cleanroom-class environments (ISO 14644 Class 7 or better)**.
- Assembly consequence: implant assemblies use **laser welding, ultrasonic welding, or snap-fits**, never threaded fasteners (to avoid stress concentrations in bone).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|--------------------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\text{Specific Strength} = \dfrac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho}$ | $\sigma_{uts}$ = ultimate tensile strength, $\rho$ = density | Lightweight design selection |
| 2 | $\text{Specific Stiffness} = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | $E$ = Young's modulus, $\rho$ = density | Aerospace & robotics beam selection |
| 3 | $\text{Ashby Material Index (lightweight tie)} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}^{2/3}}{\rho}$ | $\sigma_{y}$ = yield strength | Optimizing a tension tie for minimum mass |
| 4 | $\text{Ashby Index (lightweight beam)} = \dfrac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}$ | $E, \rho$ | Optimizing a beam for minimum mass |
| 5 | $\text{Ashby Index (lightweight panel)} = \dfrac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}$ | $E, \rho$ | Panel stiffness for given mass |
| 6 | $\text{SMA Recovery Stress} = \sigma_{r} = E \cdot \varepsilon_{r}$ | $E, \varepsilon_{r}$ | Force exerted by SMA actuator |
| 7 | $\text{Piezo Strain} = d_{33} \cdot E_{field}$ | $d_{33}$, $E_{field}$ | Piezo actuator displacement |
| 8 | $\text{Composite Rule of Mixtures (longitudinal)} = E_{c} = E_{f} V_{f} + E_{m} (1 - V_{f})$ | $V_{f}$ = fiber volume fraction | Estimating CFRP stiffness |
| 9 | $\text{Composite Density} = \rho_{c} = \rho_{f} V_{f} + \rho_{m} (1 - V_{f})$ | $\rho_{f}, \rho_{m}$ | Estimating composite mass |
| 10 | $\text{Recyclability Score} = R = \dfrac{m_{recycled}}{m_{total}}$ | mass of recycled vs total | Eco-design metric |
| 11 | $\text{Biot Number} = Bi = \dfrac{h L_{c}}{k}$ | $h, L_{c}, k$ | Self-healing material activation |
| 12 | $\text{Hall-Petch Relation} = \sigma_{y} = \sigma_{0} + \dfrac{k_{y}}{\sqrt{d}}$ | $d$ = grain size | Nanomaterial strengthening |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Table Cell Rule:** All absolute values or vertical-bar notation in formulas are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to keep this markdown table intact for the PDF compiler.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350):** Designers use the **specific strength index** $\sigma_{uts}/\rho$ to switch from Al-2024 to CFRP — reducing airframe mass by ~20%.
- **Automotive (EV battery enclosures):** The **specific stiffness index** $E/\rho$ drives the use of **aluminium extrusions + glass-fibre SMC hybrids** to offset battery mass.
- **Medical implants (hip stems):** The **biocompatibility + density match to bone** leads to **Ti-6Al-4V ELI** (or even CFRP hip stems) instead of Co-Cr.
- **Smart civil structures:** **SMA rebar** in concrete bridges provides self-centering after earthquakes — governed by the **recovery stress equation**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Material Index for a Lightweight Tension Tie

A tension tie is a structural member carrying axial load $F$ without buckling. We wish to find the material index that minimizes its mass.

### Step 1: Functional Requirement
The tie must not yield under load $F$.

### Step 2: Geometric Constraint
The cross-sectional area $A$ is required to satisfy the yield criterion.

### Step 3: Free Variable
The cross-section shape is free; only the load $F$ is fixed.

### Step 4: Derivation
The strength requirement gives:
$$
A \;\ge\; \dfrac{F}{\sigma_{y}}
$$

The mass of the tie is:
$$
m \;=\; \rho \cdot A \cdot L
$$

Substitute the minimum cross-section $A = F/\sigma_{y}$:
$$
m \;=\; \rho \cdot L \cdot \dfrac{F}{\sigma_{y}}
$$

### Step 5: Minimize Mass
Since $L$ and $F$ are fixed by the design, to **minimize $m$** we must **maximize the index**:
$$
M_{tie} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\rho}
$$

The material with the **highest $\sigma_{y}/\rho$** wins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This is why CFRP (with $\sigma_y/\rho \approx 1500 \; \text{kN\cdot m/kg}$) replaces aluminium ($\sigma_y/\rho \approx 150 \; \text{kN\cdot m/kg}$) in aircraft tie-rods.

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Stiffness of a Composite (Rule of Mixtures)

For a unidirectional fibre-reinforced composite loaded along the fibre direction:

### Step 1: Iso-strain Assumption
Both fibre and matrix experience the same strain $\varepsilon$ (perfect bonding):
$$
\varepsilon_{c} \;=\; \varepsilon_{f} \;=\; \varepsilon_{m} \;=\; \varepsilon
$$

### Step 2: Load Sharing
The total load is the sum of fibre and matrix loads:
$$
\sigma_{c} A_{c} \;=\; \sigma_{f} A_{f} \;=\; \sigma_{m} A_{m}
$$

### Step 3: Divide by $A_{c}$
$$
\sigma_{c} \;=\; \sigma_{f} V_{f} + \sigma_{m} (1 - V_{f})
$$

where $V_{f} = A_{f}/A_{c}$.

### Step 4: Apply Hooke's Law
$$
\sigma_{f} = E_{f} \varepsilon, \quad \sigma_{m} = E_{m} \varepsilon, \quad \sigma_{c} = E_{c} \varepsilon
$$

### Step 5: Divide by $\varepsilon$
$$
E_{c} \;=\; E_{f} V_{f} + E_{m} (1 - V_{f})
$$

### Step 6: Composite Density (similarly)
$$
\rho_{c} \;=\; \rho_{f} V_{f} + \rho_{m} (1 - V_{f})
$$

### Step 7: Numerical Example
For Carbon Fibre / Epoxy with $V_{f} = 0.6$:
- $E_{f} = 230 \; \text{GPa}$, $E_{m} = 3.5 \; \text{GPa}$
- $\rho_{f} = 1800 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$, $\rho_{m} = 1200 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$

Using the aligned block:
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{c} &= 230 \cdot 0.6 + 3.5 \cdot 0.4 \\
      &= 138.0 + 1.4 \\
      &= 139.4 \;\; \text{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the aligned block:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{c} &= 1800 \cdot 0.6 + 1200 \cdot 0.4 \\
        &= 1080 + 480 \\
        &= 1560 \;\; \text{kg/m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8: Specific Stiffness
$$
\dfrac{E_{c}}{\rho_{c}} \;=\; \dfrac{139.4 \times 10^{9}}{1560} \;\approx\; 89.4 \;\; \dfrac{\text{MN\cdot m}}{\text{kg}}
$$

Compare to mild steel: $E/\rho \approx 25 \; \text{MN\cdot m/kg}$ → **CFRP is 3.6× stiffer per kg**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Material Development → Manufacturing Process → Assembly Strategy

| Material Development | Specific Property | Best-fit Manufacturing Process | Resulting Assembly Strategy |
|----------------------|-------------------|--------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Shape Memory Alloy (Nitinol)** | 8% recoverable strain | Cold rolling + shape-setting heat treatment | Shrink-fit couplings; eliminates bolts |
| **CFRP (Carbon/Epoxy)** | $\sigma/\rho$ ≈ 1500 kN·m/kg | Autoclave curing, RTM, AFP lay-up | Co-cured or bonded joints; metallic inserts |
| **Ti-6Al-4V (AM powder)** | Complex lattice possible | Selective Laser Melting (SLM) | Snap-fits; threaded inserts pressed in |
| **Bulk Metallic Glass (BMG)** | High hardness, low $E$ | Injection moulding (viscous flow) | Precision gears without finish-machining |
| **Self-Healing Polymer** | Microcapsule activation | Casting, 3D printing | Field-repairable; reduces disassembly |
| **Magnesium Alloy (AZ91D)** | $\rho = 1.74$ g/cm³ | Die casting, thixomoulding | Lightweight housings; ultrasonic welding |
| **PEEK (biopolymer)** | $T_g = 143^\circ$C, biocompatible | Injection moulding, AM (PEEK filaments) | Sterilizable snap-fit medical devices |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Recyclability Score for a Hybrid Product

A product uses 5 kg of aluminium, 2 kg of CFRP, and 1 kg of adhesive. Aluminium is 100% recyclable, CFRP is 30% recyclable (through pyrolysis), and adhesive is 0% recyclable.

Using the formula:
$$
R \;=\; \dfrac{\sum m_{i} \cdot r_{i}}{\sum m_{i}}
$$

where $r_i$ is the recyclability fraction of each material.

### Step 1: Compute weighted sum
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum m_{i} \cdot r_{i} &= 5 \cdot 1.0 + 2 \cdot 0.3 + 1 \cdot 0.0 \\
                       &= 5.0 + 0.6 + 0.0 \\
                       &= 5.6 \;\; \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute total mass
$$
\sum m_{i} \;=\; 5 + 2 + 1 \;=\; 8 \;\; \text{kg}
$$

### Step 3: Compute $R$
$$
R \;=\; \dfrac{5.6}{8.0} \;=\; 0.70
$$

### Step 4: Interpret
70% of the product mass is recoverable — exceeds the EU ELV directive target of 85% only after energy-recovery credit, indicating the **CFRP + adhesive combination is the bottleneck** for circular design.

> [!TIP]
> **Design Action Triggered:** Replace thermoset CFRP with thermoplastic CFRP (e.g., PEEK-matrix) → recyclable to $r \approx 0.95$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Modern Materials (DFMA Lens)


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

> *Define "Shape Memory Alloy" and give **two** examples. Explain how SMA contributes to Design for Assembly (DFA).*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** A Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) is a metallic material that can recover its original shape after being deformed, when subjected to an appropriate thermal or stress cycle. The shape recovery is due to a reversible solid-state phase transformation between **martensite** (low-temperature phase) and **austenite** (high-temperature phase).
2. **Examples (0.5 + 0.5 = 1 mark):** **Nitinol** (Nickel-Titanium alloy) and **Cu-Zn-Al** (Copper-Zinc-Aluminium alloy).
3. **DFA Contribution (1 mark):** SMA rings are used as **shrink-fit couplings and pipe-joiners**. On heating above the austenite finish temperature $A_f$, the ring contracts radially, gripping the mating parts **without bolts, threads, or adhesives** — drastically reducing the number of fasteners and assembly steps.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

> *What is a "Material Index" in the Ashby methodology? Give one example and state its application.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** A **material index** is a combination of material properties (e.g., $\sigma_y$, $E$, $\rho$, $k$, $c_p$) that characterizes a material's fitness for a given design function, derived by expressing the objective (e.g., minimize mass) and constraints (e.g., must not yield) in terms of free variables.
2. **Example (1 mark):** For a **lightweight tension tie**, the index is $M = \sigma_y / \rho$. For a **lightweight beam**, the index is $M = E^{1/3} / \rho$.
3. **Application (1 mark):** Used in **Ashby material selection charts** plotted on log-log axes of property $P_1$ vs $P_2$, where the line of constant material index guides the designer to the optimum material family (e.g., CFRP, Al-alloys, Ti-alloys).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Regular]**

> *(a)* Discuss the **classification of advanced composite materials** with at least **two examples per category**. Explain how the rule of mixtures is applied to estimate the longitudinal Young's modulus of a unidirectional fibre-reinforced composite. **(7 Marks)**
>
> *(b)* A unidirectional CFRP composite has $V_f = 0.55$, $E_f = 240 \; \text{GPa}$, $E_m = 3.5 \; \text{GPa}$, $\rho_f = 1800 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_m = 1250 \; \text{kg/m}^3$. Compute the **specific stiffness** of the composite and compare it with **mild steel** ($E = 210 \; \text{GPa}, \rho = 7850 \; \text{kg/m}^3$). Comment on the suitability of CFRP for **aircraft wing-skin applications**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Classification of Advanced Composites (4 marks):**

| Category | Matrix | Reinforcement | Example |
|----------|--------|---------------|---------|
| **Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC)** | Epoxy, polyester, PEEK | Glass, carbon, Kevlar fibres | CFRP (Carbon/Epoxy), GFRP (Glass/Polyester) |
| **Metal Matrix Composites (MMC)** | Al, Mg, Ti | SiC particles, Al₂O₃ fibres | Al-SiC (pistons), Ti-SiC (aerospace rings) |
| **Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC)** | Al₂O₃, SiC, Si₃N₄ | Carbon fibres, SiC whiskers | SiC/SiC (jet engine combustors) |

**Rule of Mixtures Derivation (3 marks):**

- Assume **iso-strain** condition: $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$
- Apply load-sharing: $P_c = P_f + P_m \Rightarrow \sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$
- Divide by $A_c$: $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m (1 - V_f)$
- Apply Hooke's Law: $\sigma_c = E_c \varepsilon$, $\sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon$, $\sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon$
- Final result:
$$
E_c \;=\; E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)
$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Compute $E_c$ (2 marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_c &= 240 \cdot 0.55 + 3.5 \cdot (1 - 0.55) \\
    &= 240 \cdot 0.55 + 3.5 \cdot 0.45 \\
    &= 132.0 + 1.575 \\
    &= 133.575 \;\; \text{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $\rho_c$ (1 mark)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_c &= 1800 \cdot 0.55 + 1250 \cdot 0.45 \\
       &= 990 + 562.5 \\
       &= 1552.5 \;\; \text{kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Specific Stiffness of CFRP (1 mark)**
$$
\left(\dfrac{E_c}{\rho_c}\right)_{CFRP} \;=\; \dfrac{133.575 \times 10^{9}}{1552.5} \;\approx\; 86.0 \;\; \dfrac{\text{MN\cdot m}}{\text{kg}}
$$

**Step 4: Specific Stiffness of Mild Steel (1 mark)**
$$
\left(\dfrac{E}{\rho}\right)_{steel} \;=\; \dfrac{210 \times 10^{9}}{7850} \;\approx\; 26.75 \;\; \dfrac{\text{MN\cdot m}}{\text{kg}}
$$

**Step 5: Comparison and Comment (2 marks)**
$$
\text{Ratio} \;=\; \dfrac{86.0}{26.75} \;\approx\; 3.21
$$

CFRP is **3.21× stiffer per kg** than mild steel. For aircraft wing-skins, this directly translates to:
- **~20–25% mass reduction** of the wing structure.
- **Larger payload or longer range** for the same fuel.
- **Lower inertia** → improved maneuverability.

Hence CFRP is **highly suitable** for aircraft wing-skin applications (already proven in Boeing 787 and Airbus A350).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Lose-Mark Pitfall):**
> 1. Students often forget the **iso-strain assumption** when stating the rule of mixtures — costing 1 mark.
> 2. In numerical problems, students must **show ALL intermediate steps** — board examiners allocate marks per step, not for the final answer only.
> 3. Do not confuse $E_c$ (composite modulus) with $E_{c,\perp}$ (transverse modulus) — the rule of mixtures **only** applies in the fibre direction.
> 4. Always **state units** in the final answer — losing units = losing 0.5 mark.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

> *(a)* Explain the **role of smart materials in DFMA**. Describe the working principle of **piezoelectric materials** with a relevant equation, and discuss **two** DFMA advantages of using piezo-integrated assemblies. **(7 Marks)**
>
> *(b)* With the help of a **flowchart**, explain the **Ashby material selection methodology** for choosing a material for a **lightweight beam** of fixed length and stiffness. List any **three** modern materials that would appear on the optimum selection line. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Role of Smart Materials in DFMA (2 marks):**

Smart materials (SMA, piezoelectric, magnetostrictive, self-healing polymers) **sense and respond** to external stimuli (temperature, stress, electric field, magnetic field). In DFMA, they allow the designer to **embed functionality directly into the part**, reducing the number of discrete components such as actuators, sensors, and dampers. This leads to lower part count, simpler assembly, reduced tolerances, and lower life-cycle cost.

**Piezoelectric Working Principle and Equation (3 marks):**

- Certain crystals (e.g., **Quartz, PZT-5H, BaTiO₃, PVDF**) develop an electric charge $D$ (Coulomb/m²) when subjected to mechanical stress $\sigma$ (direct effect), or conversely, deform when subjected to an electric field $E_{field}$ (converse effect).
- The constitutive equations are:
$$
D = d \cdot \sigma + \varepsilon^{T} \cdot E_{field}
$$
$$
\varepsilon = s^{E} \cdot \sigma + d \cdot E_{field}
$$
- For pure actuation (converse effect), the induced strain is:
$$
\varepsilon \;=\; d_{33} \cdot E_{field}
$$
where $d_{33}$ is the **piezoelectric strain coefficient** (e.g., $d_{33} \approx 593 \; \text{pC/N}$ for PZT-5H).

**Two DFMA Advantages (2 marks):**

1. **Active vibration damping in precision assemblies** — piezo patches bonded to a beam sense vibration and apply counter-strain, eliminating the need for discrete dampers. **Reduced part count, reduced assembly time.**
2. **Precision positioning in micro-assemblies** — piezo stacks provide nanometer-level actuation, replacing geared motors. **Simplifies BOM, eliminates backlash, improves reliability.**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Ashby Material Selection Flowchart (4 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Translate Design Requirement: Minimize Mass of Beam]:::qbox
    node2[Identify Free Variable: Cross Section Shape]:::step1
    node3[Apply Stiffness Constraint: E I Fixed Value]:::step2
    node4[Express Mass in terms of Material Properties]:::step3
    node5[Derive Material Index: E to power 1 third over rho]:::step4
    node6[Plot Ashby Chart of E versus rho on Log Log Scale]:::step5
    node7[Draw Slope 3 Line of Constant Material Index]:::step6
    node8[Identify Top Candidate Materials above the Line]:::step7
    node9[Validate with Secondary Constraints Cost Manufacturability]:::step8

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9

    classDef qbox fill:#1B2631,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px
    classDef step1 fill:#5DADE2,color:#000000,stroke:#1B4F72,stroke-width:1px
    classDef step2 fill:#48C9B0,color:#000000,stroke:#117A65,stroke-width:1px
    classDef step3 fill:#F4D03F,color:#000000,stroke:#7D6608,stroke-width:1px
    classDef step4 fill:#E59866,color:#000000,stroke:#935116,stroke-width:1px
    classDef step5 fill:#A569BD,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#4A235A,stroke-width:1px
    classDef step6 fill:#F1948A,color:#000000,stroke:#922B21,stroke-width:1px
    classDef step7 fill:#7DCEA0,color:#000000,stroke:#196F3D,stroke-width:1px
    classDef step8 fill:#85C1E9,color:#000000,stroke:#21618C,stroke-width:1px
```

**Analytical Derivation (2 marks):**

- Stiffness constraint: $S = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{L^{3}} = \text{fixed}$
- For a given shape (e.g., square section of side $b$), $I = b^{4}/12$, so $b \propto (S L^{3}/E)^{1/4}$
- Mass: $m = \rho \cdot b^{2} \cdot L \propto \rho \cdot L \cdot (S L^{3}/E)^{1/2}$
- Eliminating $S$ and $L$ (both fixed): $m \propto \rho / E^{1/2}$
- Wait — correct form is $m \propto \rho / E^{1/2}$ but for minimizing $m$ we maximize $E^{1/2}/\rho$, i.e., **index $M = E^{1/2}/\rho$** for a fixed-shape beam, or $M = E^{1/3}/\rho$ for a free-shape beam (Ashby textbook standard form).
- For board exam: cite the textbook form $\dfrac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}$.

**Three Modern Materials on the Optimum Line (1 mark):**

1. **CFRP (Carbon Fibre Reinforced Polymer)**
2. **Ti-6Al-4V (Titanium Alloy)**
3. **Al-Li (Aluminium-Lithium) Alloys**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Lose-Mark Pitfall):**
> 1. Do **NOT** confuse $E^{1/3}/\rho$ (free-shape beam) with $E^{1/2}/\rho$ (fixed-shape beam). Most board questions use the free-shape form unless explicitly stated.
> 2. The flowchart must show **all 5–6 sequential steps** — leaving out "secondary constraints" loses 1 mark.
> 3. Always state that the Ashby chart uses **log-log axes** — many students omit this and lose 0.5 mark.
> 4. For numerical problems, **double-check the units** of $E$ and $\rho$ before computing the index ratio.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Developments in Material Technology" (DFMA Module 1):**

- **Definition:** New materials → new process windows → new assembly strategies → reduced BOM.
- **Four Drivers:** Performance, Cost, Assembly Simplification, Environmental Compliance.
- **Smart Materials:** SMA (Nitinol, Cu-Zn-Al) — **8% recoverable strain**; Piezo (PZT, PVDF) — equation $\varepsilon = d_{33} E_{field}$.
- **Composites:** Rule of mixtures $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$; rule applies in **fibre direction only**; density $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$.
- **High-Performance Alloys:** Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel 718, Maraging 300 — drive **near-net-shape** processes (AM, powder metallurgy).
- **Biomaterials:** PEEK, Ti-ELI, Hydroxyapatite — drive **clean-room assembly**, **laser/ultrasonic welding**.
- **Nanomaterials:** Graphene, CNT, Nano-Al₂O₃ — drive **nano-adhesives** and **diffusion bonding**.
- **AM Materials:** Ti powder, PA12, PEEK filaments — enable **lattice infill** and **conformal cooling**.
- **Sustainable Materials:** PLA, PHA, recycled Al — drive **design-for-disassembly** and **circular economy**.
- **Material Index — Tension Tie:** $M = \sigma_y / \rho$.
- **Material Index — Lightweight Beam:** $M = E^{1/3} / \rho$.
- **Material Index — Lightweight Panel:** $M = E^{1/3} / \rho$.
- **Hall-Petch:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y / \sqrt{d}$ — basis for nano-strengthening.
- **Recyclability Score:** $R = \dfrac{\sum m_i r_i}{\sum m_i}$; values $0 \le R \le 1$.
- **Piezo Equation:** $\varepsilon = d_{33} E_{field}$; $d_{33}$ for PZT-5H ≈ 593 pC/N.
- **SMA Recovery Stress:** $\sigma_r = E \cdot \varepsilon_r$, where $\varepsilon_r \approx 0.08$ for Nitinol.
- **Specific Strength Champion:** CFRP $\approx 1500 \; \text{kN\cdot m/kg}$ vs Al $\approx 150$ vs Steel $\approx 60$.
- **DFMA Golden Rule:** *Every new material must justify its manufacturing cost by enabling part-count reduction, weight reduction, or life-cycle improvement.*
- **Common Mistake:** Students confuse *property* (e.g., high $E$) with *specific property* (e.g., high $E/\rho$); board examiners check the latter.
- **Common Mistake 2:** In Ashby selection, the slope of the constant-index line on a log-log $E$-$\rho$ plot is **3** for a beam, **2** for a plate, **1** for a tie — remember this!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Criteria for Material Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Criteria for Material Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)**, *Material Selection* is defined as the systematic, engineering-driven process of identifying the most suitable engineering material from a vast universe of available options (metals, polymers, ceramics, and composites) that simultaneously satisfies a defined set of **functional requirements**, **manufacturing constraints**, **economic limits**, **service environment demands**, and **sustainability goals** for a given component or product.

The **Criteria for Material Selection** refers to the structured set of performance attributes, design constraints, and decision parameters that a design engineer evaluates and prioritizes when comparing candidate materials. These criteria act as a *filtering sieve*, narrowing down thousands of available materials to the single most optimal choice for a specific application.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Material selection is taught as the foundational precursor to *Process Selection* (Module 2). The KTU framework explicitly demands that students understand the *interdependence* between material choice, manufacturing process, and final product cost.

## 1.2 The Material Selection Problem — Formal Framing

The classical statement of the material selection problem, as articulated by **Michael F. Ashby** (a foundational reference framework used globally in engineering education), is:

> *"Given a design function, a set of geometric constraints, a set of functional constraints, and a set of objective constraints, find the material that best meets the design requirements."*

Mathematically, this is expressed as:

$$
\text{Optimize} = f(\text{Function}, \text{Geometry}, \text{Force}, \text{Energy}, \text{Environment}, \text{Cost})
$$

Where each variable is a vector of constraints that the selected material must satisfy.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Real Estate" Intuition

Imagine you are buying a **house**. You don't just pick the first one you see. Instead, you have a *checklist* (the criteria):

| Real Estate Analogy | Material Selection Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Budget (cost ceiling) | Unit cost of material per kg/volume |
| Location (proximity to work) | Manufacturing process availability |
| Size of family (loading) | Mechanical strength/load-bearing capacity |
| Climate (humid/coastal) | Corrosion resistance |
| Future resale value | Recyclability / lifecycle cost |
| Number of bedrooms (function) | Functional requirement (e.g., insulation) |

Just like you would *not* buy a beach house in a snowy, landlocked area, you would *not* select a corrosion-prone low-carbon steel for a marine propeller. **Material selection is the engineering equivalent of "house hunting" with a quantified, weighted criteria matrix.**

## 1.4 Why Does This Topic Exist in DFMA?

The inclusion of material selection as Module 1 is deliberate. The **KTU 2024 curriculum** enforces a logical design hierarchy:

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Sequence Mandated by KTU:**
> 1. **Define Function** → What must the part do?
> 2. **Select Material** → What is it made of? *(Module 1 Topic)*
> 3. **Select Process** → How is it made? *(Module 2 Topic)*
> 4. **Design for Assembly** → How is it joined? *(Module 3 Topic)*
> 5. **Optimize** → Cost, weight, lifecycle. *(Module 5 Topic)*

Selecting the *wrong* material at Step 2 cascades failures into every downstream step — leading to parts that cannot be cast, machined, or assembled economically.

## 1.5 The Five Pillars of Material Selection Criteria (KTU Classification)

The KTU 2024 Scheme groups material selection criteria into **five primary pillars**:

1. **Performance Criteria** — Mechanical, thermal, electrical, and chemical properties
2. **Manufacturing Criteria** — Castability, machinability, formability, weldability
3. **Economic Criteria** — Raw material cost, processing cost, lifecycle cost
4. **Service/Environmental Criteria** — Temperature, corrosion, wear, fatigue
5. **Sustainability & Regulatory Criteria** — Recyclability, toxicity, compliance with standards (ROHS, REACH, IS)

Each of these will be unpacked rigorously in the following sections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Material Selection Methodology

Material selection is *not* a single-step decision. It is a **multi-stage funnel** that progressively filters candidate materials. The standard KTU-prescribed methodology follows four sequential stages:

### Stage 1 — Translation of Design Requirements
The design *function* is expressed in terms of **material properties** (Function → Property → Material).

**Example:** A leaf spring must "store elastic energy per unit volume."

$$
\text{Function: store elastic energy} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Property: } \sigma_y^2 / E
$$

### Stage 2 — Screening Using Property Limits (Constraints)
Apply *constraints* (boundary conditions) on material properties. Materials that violate even one constraint are eliminated.

**Example:** The material must have $E \geq 100$ GPa, $\rho \leq 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

### Stage 3 — Ranking Using Material Indices (Objective)
Maximize or minimize a *Material Index* (M) — a combined property expression that captures the design goal.

**Common Form:**

$$
M = f(\text{Properties}) = \text{Property}_1^a \cdot \text{Property}_2^b \cdot \text{Property}_3^c
$$

### Stage 4 — Documentation of Final Selection
Document the final choice, including the **process route**, **cost estimate**, **risk assessment**, and **substitution alternatives**.

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Selection Criterion

### A. Performance Criteria (Mechanical Properties)

| Property | Symbol | Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | GPa | Stiffness — resistance to elastic deformation |
| Yield Strength | $\sigma_y$ | MPa | Onset of plastic deformation |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | $\sigma_u$ | MPa | Maximum stress before fracture |
| Fatigue Strength | $\sigma_f$ | MPa | Endurance limit under cyclic loading |
| Fracture Toughness | $K_{IC}$ | MPa·m$^{1/2}$ | Resistance to crack propagation |
| Hardness | $HV$ / $HRC$ | — | Resistance to surface indentation/wear |
| Density | $\rho$ | kg/m$^3$ | Mass per unit volume (weight-sensitive apps) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Materials are rarely selected for a *single* property. They are selected to optimize a *combination* — e.g., a beam in a helicopter needs **high stiffness AND low weight**, leading to the material index $E^{1/3}/\rho$.

### B. Manufacturing Criteria

The selected material must be compatible with at least one viable manufacturing process. Critical manufacturing indices include:

- **Castability** — fluidity of molten metal; lower melting point improves it
- **Machinability** — rated as a percentage relative to free-cutting steel (B1112 = 100%)
- **Formability** — ability to undergo plastic deformation (sheet metal)
- **Weldability** — susceptibility to fusion welding without defects
- **Heat-treatability** — ability to be hardened by quenching/tempering
- **Moldability** — for polymers, the melt flow index (MFI)

### C. Economic Criteria

Cost is multi-dimensional:

$$
C_{total} = C_{material} + C_{processing} + C_{assembly} + C_{finishing} + C_{recycling}
$$

The **specific cost per unit property** is often more meaningful than raw cost:

$$
C_{specific} = \frac{C_{material}}{M} = \frac{\text{Cost per kg}}{\text{Material Index}}
$$

### D. Service / Environmental Criteria

| Service Condition | Required Material Attribute |
|---|---|
| High temperature (>500°C) | Creep resistance, oxidation resistance |
| Cryogenic temperature | Toughness at low T, BCC → FCC transition avoidance |
| Marine / humid | Corrosion resistance (CRAs, stainless, polymers) |
| Abrasive contact | Hardness, wear resistance |
| Cyclic loading | Fatigue strength, endurance limit |
| Radiation exposure | Radiation-hardened ceramics, special alloys |
| UV exposure | UV-stabilized polymers |

### E. Sustainability Criteria (Emerging in KTU 2024)

$$
\text{Sustainability Score} = w_1 \cdot \text{Recyclability} + w_2 \cdot \text{CO}_2\text{ footprint} + w_3 \cdot \text{Toxicity} + w_4 \cdot \text{Energy intensity}
$$

Where $w_i$ are weighted importance factors.

## 2.3 The Ashby Method — Material Indices for Common Components

The following table is **high-yield for KTU exams** — memorize the material indices for at least these standard components:

| Component | Function | Constraint | Objective | **Material Index (M)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tie rod** (tensile member) | Carry axial load $F$ | Length $L$ fixed, $\sigma \leq \sigma_y$ | Minimize mass | $M = \sigma_y / \rho$ |
| **Lightweight beam** (bending) | Carry bending moment $M$ | Stiffness $S$ fixed | Minimize mass | $M = E^{1/2} / \rho$ |
| **Lightweight stiff panel** | Resist buckling | Buckling load $P$ fixed | Minimize mass | $M = E^{1/3} / \rho$ |
| **Spring** (store energy) | Store elastic energy $U$ | Volume fixed | Maximize stored energy | $M = \sigma_y^2 / E$ |
| **Heat sink** | Transfer heat $Q$ | Temperature rise $\Delta T$ fixed | Minimize cost | $M = \lambda / (C_p \cdot \rho)$ |
| **Flywheel** | Store kinetic energy $KE$ | Angular velocity $\omega$ fixed | Maximize $KE$ | $M = \sigma_y / \rho$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "derive a material index," the examiner expects you to: (1) state the function, (2) identify the free variable, (3) write the constraint equation, (4) solve for the free variable, and (5) substitute back into the objective. The derivation for the **tie rod** is shown in Section 3.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = F/A$ | Axial stress | Pa |
| 2 | $\delta = FL / (AE)$ | Axial elongation | m |
| 3 | $I = \sigma^2 V / (2E)$ | Elastic strain energy stored | J |
| 4 | $E / \rho$ | Specific stiffness | $\text{GPa} \cdot \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ |
| 5 | $\sigma_y / \rho$ | Specific strength | $\text{MPa} \cdot \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ |
| 6 | $\sigma_y^2 / E$ | Elastic energy storage per unit volume | $\text{kJ/m}^3$ |
| 7 | $E^{1/2} / \rho$ | Light beam material index | $(\text{GPa})^{1/2} \cdot \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ |
| 8 | $E^{1/3} / \rho$ | Light panel material index | $(\text{GPa})^{1/3} \cdot \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ |
| 9 | $\lambda / (C_p \cdot \rho)$ | Thermal diffusivity-related index | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 10 | $C_{total} = \sum C_i$ | Total cost summation | ₹ / unit |
| 11 | $M = \prod p_i^{a_i}$ | General material index form | varies |
| 12 | $K_{IC} = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Fracture mechanics (Griffith-Irwin) | $\text{MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula notation rule:** In the KTU exam script, always define your symbols *before* using them in an equation. A mark is often deducted for ambiguous variable names.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The material selection criteria framework is not academic — it is the **backbone of every major engineering product**:

- **Aerospace (Boeing 787 Dreamliner):** 50% composite by weight → selected for $E/\rho$ index
- **Automotive (EV battery enclosures):** Aluminum alloys → selected for $\sigma_y/\rho$ and recyclability
- **Marine propellers:** Nickel-aluminum bronze → selected for corrosion + cavitation resistance
- **Biomedical implants:** Ti-6Al-4V → selected for biocompatibility + fatigue strength
- **Heat exchangers:** Copper / aluminum → selected for $\lambda$ (thermal conductivity)
- **Aircraft tires:** Nylon-reinforced rubber → selected for toughness + fatigue
