  - Cast/forged: $R_a = 6.3 - 25$ μm (N10–N12)

### DFMA Decision Logic

- **First principle:** Specify the **loosest tolerance** and **roughest surface** that meet functional requirements.
- **Cost driver #1:** Tightening tolerance is the **single largest cost amplifier** in manufacturing.
- **Process selection linkage:** Each manufacturing process has a **capability envelope** (Cpk ≥ 1.33) — choose a process whose natural tolerance is **3–5x looser** than the design tolerance (to ensure 6σ capability).
- **Always justify stack-up choice** — worst-case for safety-critical; RSS for normal assemblies.
- **Always state the rule of thumb** $R_a \approx (0.05 \text{ to } 0.20) \cdot T$ in design questions.
- **Taguchi's loss function** is the philosophical backbone of DFMA tolerance allocation.
- **Hole-shaft fits** must follow ISO 286 nomenclature — never invent codes.
- **Mating parts** → use **unilateral tolerance**; **non-mating parts** → bilateral is acceptable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Machining Ease
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design for Machining Ease

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design for Machining Ease (DFMe)** is a sub-domain of **Design for Manufacturing (DFM)** that establishes a set of geometric, dimensional, and tolerance guidelines aimed at simplifying the **machining operations** required to produce a component from raw stock, while simultaneously reducing **machining time**, **tool wear**, **scrap rate**, and **overall production cost**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, DFMe is classified under **Module 1: Introduction to DFM/DFA**, and forms the conceptual foundation for all subsequent modules on **process selection, tolerancing, and assembly design**. The principle asserts that a component's manufacturability must be evaluated *during the design phase* — not after the drawing is released to the shop floor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Phrase Used in KTU Valuation:** *"A part designed for ease of machining eliminates the need for special tooling, minimizes fixturing setups, and uses standardized features that align with the capabilities of standard machine tools."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **carpenter building a chair**. Two scenarios are possible:

- **Scenario A (Poor Design):** The customer gives you a sketch with **12 different unique leg lengths**, **curved mortise joints inside hidden corners**, and requests a tolerance of ±0.1 mm on every dimension. You will spend hours setting up jigs, switching tools, and measuring.
- **Scenario B (DFMe-Compliant Design):** The customer gives a sketch with **4 identical legs**, **standard 90° joints accessible from the top**, and realistic tolerances (±2 mm). You cut, drill, and assemble in a fraction of the time with off-the-shelf tools.

**Scenario B is "Design for Machining Ease."** The product performs identically, but the second design is **manufacturable at a fraction of the cost, time, and rejection rate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** The designer does not *change the function* of the part; the designer changes its *manufacturable geometry* so that standard machines and standard tools can produce it without struggle.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Machining-Ease Design

| Pillar | Focus | Key Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometric Simplification** | Reduce complex contours, deep cavities, internal corners | Number of distinct features |
| **Standardization** | Use standard hole sizes, radii, chamfers, tolerances | % of standard features used |
| **Tool & Fixture Accessibility** | Ensure tools can reach surfaces; reduce setups | Number of setups required |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Benchmarks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard drill bit sizes** follow the **ISO 286 (preferred numbers)** system, with metric increments typically at **0.5 mm, 1.0 mm, 2.0 mm** steps.
> - **Minimum internal corner radius** is dictated by the **standard tool nose radius** of milling cutters (commonly **R3 mm, R6 mm, R8 mm**).
> - **Standard surface roughness (Ra)** values used in engineering drawings: **0.8 µm, 1.6 µm, 3.2 µm, 6.3 µm, 12.5 µm, 25 µm**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation: Boothroyd's DFM Cost Model

The most cited academic model for understanding machining cost is the **Boothroyd-Redford Dewhurst Cost Model**. It states that the total cost of a machined feature is the sum of:

1. **Material Cost** (function of volume and raw stock)
2. **Machining Cost** (function of time on the machine)
3. **Tooling Cost** (function of tool complexity and wear)
4. **Setup Cost** (function of the number of fixturing operations)

> [!NOTE]
> **DFMe Objective:** Minimize the sum of (1)+(2)+(3)+(4) by **engineering the geometry to be inherently easier to cut**.

The governing relationship for **machining time** is:

$$
t_m = \frac{L}{f \cdot N}
$$

Where:
- $t_m$ = machining time per pass (seconds)
- $L$ = length of cut (mm)
- $f$ = feed rate (mm/rev)
- $N$ = spindle speed (rev/sec)

The corresponding material removal rate is:

$$
MRR = v \cdot f \cdot d
$$

Where:
- $MRR$ = material removal rate (mm³/s)
- $v$ = cutting speed (mm/s)
- $f$ = feed (mm/rev)
- $d$ = depth of cut (mm)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates every formula a KTU student must memorize for Design for Machining Ease problems.**

| # | Formula | Symbols | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$ | $t_m$ = time, $L$ = length, $f$ = feed, $N$ = spindle speed | s, mm, mm/rev, rev/s | Single-pass turning/milling time |
| 2 | $v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ | $v$ = cutting speed, $D$ = diameter | m/min, mm | Relating spindle speed to cutting speed |
| 3 | $MRR = v \cdot f \cdot d$ | $v$ = speed, $f$ = feed, $d$ = depth | mm³/s | Volume of material removed per second |
| 4 | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D}$ | $v$ in m/min, $D$ in mm | rev/min | Required spindle speed |
| 5 | $T = \dfrac{C}{v^{n}}$ | $T$ = tool life, $C$ = constant, $n$ = exponent | min | Taylor's tool life equation |
| 6 | $C_{total} = C_{mat} + C_{mach} + C_{tool} + C_{setup}$ | Each term = cost component | ₹ (or currency) | Total DFM cost per part |
| 7 | $L_{eff} = L + L_{approach} + L_{over}$ | $L_{eff}$ = effective cut length | mm | Tool path length for entry/exit |
| 8 | $h_{min} = 0.5 \cdot D_{tool} + \delta$ | $h_{min}$ = minimum pocket depth, $\delta$ = clearance | mm | Tool accessibility for pocket milling |
| 9 | $\phi_{min} = 2 \cdot r_{nose} + \Delta$ | $\phi_{min}$ = minimum internal corner radius | mm | Corner radius for milling cutter |
| 10 | $A_{chip} = f \cdot d$ | $A_{chip}$ = uncut chip cross-section | mm² | Chip load analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Rule:** When writing inline math within KTU answer sheets, **never** use the absolute-value symbol `|x|`. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to preserve LaTeX formatting.

## 2.3 The Ten Golden Rules of Design for Machining Ease

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to enumerate and *justify* the following ten design rules. Each rule is paired with its **cost-driving mechanism**.

### Rule 1: Avoid Separate Machined Surfaces (Combine Features)
- **Bad:** A part with two separate flat surfaces requiring two separate milling setups.
- **Good:** A single large flat surface machined in one setup.
- **Cost Impact:** Reduces **setup count** and **fixturing cost**.

### Rule 2: Standardize Dimensions
- **Use ISO preferred numbers** for diameters, lengths, and thicknesses.
- A designer specifying Ø7.3 mm holes forces a **special drill** to be procured; Ø8 mm uses a **standard off-the-shelf drill**.

### Rule 3: Use Through Holes Instead of Blind Holes
- **Blind holes** require:
  - Peck-drilling cycles (interrupted cut → time penalty)
  - Specialized drill geometry (more expensive)
  - Cannot be easily inspected for depth
- **Through holes** are drilled in one pass and are inspectable end-to-end.

### Rule 4: Provide Adequate Internal Corner Radii
- **Sharp internal corners cannot be machined** by standard end-mills.
- Minimum radius = **nose radius of the largest standard end-mill** that can access the corner.
- A part designed with sharp internal corners forces the use of **EDM** (expensive) or **hand filing** (slow).

### Rule 5: Avoid Deep Pockets and Cavities
- A pocket deeper than **4× its width** causes:
  - Tool deflection → poor tolerance
  - Chip evacuation problems → tool breakage
  - Long cycle time
- **Design solution:** Open the pocket on one side, or split it into two shallower pockets.

### Rule 6: Use Flat Surfaces Over Complex 3D Contours
- **Flat surfaces** can be machined by face milling (high MRR, low cost).
- **Complex 3D surfaces** require 5-axis CNC, multi-axis programming, and qualified operators.

### Rule 7: Design for Tool Accessibility
- A machining tool needs **approach angle** and **clearance**.
- Standard tool holders require at least **3–5 mm** clearance from adjacent features.
- Recessed features hidden behind walls are *inaccessible* without special tooling.

### Rule 8: Specify Realistic Tolerances
- **Tighter tolerance = higher cost.** The relationship is exponential, not linear.
- As a rule of thumb: the cost of **±0.01 mm** tolerance can be **10×** the cost of **±0.1 mm** tolerance for the same feature.

### Rule 9: Minimize the Number of Setups
- Each setup costs **non-productive time** (clamping, indicating, tool setting).
- A part designed to be machined in **one or two setups** is far cheaper than one requiring **five setups**.
- **Design solution:** Use symmetry so that all four sides can be accessed by rotating the part 90° in a single fixture (a "3-2-1" fixture concept).

### Rule 10: Specify Achievable Surface Finishes
- A roughness of **Ra 0.1 µm** requires **grinding + lapping + polishing** (3 separate operations).
- Most engineering functions are satisfied by **Ra 1.6 µm or Ra 3.2 µm**, achievable in one turning or milling pass.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles above are used in:
- **CNC programming:** Tool path planning uses minimum corner radii to select end-mills.
- **Cost estimation:** Quotation engineers apply DFMe rules to flag "design red flags" that drive up the quote.
- **Design review checklists:** Industry standards like **ASME Y14.5** and **ISO 1101** are cross-referenced with DFMe guidelines.
- **Concurrent engineering:** The DFMe rules are inputs to the **DFMEA (Design Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** process.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relating Machining Time to Cost (Boothroyd Model)

**Objective:** Derive a closed-form expression for the *machining cost per feature* as a function of design parameters.

### Step 1 — Define the Time Per Cut

For a single turning or milling pass, the time required is the **effective length of cut** divided by the **feed per revolution times spindle speed**.

$$
t_{cut} = \frac{L_{eff}}{f \cdot N}
$$

**Explanation:** $L_{eff}$ accounts for the tool's approach length, the actual feature length, and the over-travel. The denominator $f \cdot N$ is the linear feed rate of the tool relative to the workpiece.

### Step 2 — Convert Spindle Speed to Cutting Speed

Using the kinematic relationship between rotational motion and tangential speed:

$$
v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N
$$

**Explanation:** The circumference of a workpiece of diameter $D$ rotating at $N$ rev/sec passes a fixed point at a linear speed of $\pi D N$.

### Step 3 — Solve for Spindle Speed

Re-arranging Step 2:

$$
N = \frac{v}{\pi \cdot D}
$$

**Explanation:** If a designer changes the *diameter* of a machined feature, the spindle speed must change inversely to maintain the recommended cutting speed $v$.

### Step 4 — Substitute N into the Cutting Time

$$
t_{cut} = \frac{L_{eff}}{f \cdot \dfrac{v}{\pi \cdot D}} = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot L_{eff}}{f \cdot v}
$$

**Explanation:** This shows that for a *given material and cutting speed*, the machining time is **directly proportional to the diameter and the effective length of cut**. A designer who reduces the *diameter* of a bored hole or shortens the *length* of a milled slot directly reduces cutting time.

### Step 5 — Compute the Number of Passes

For roughing, the depth of cut $d$ and the total stock to be removed $D_{total}$ determine the number of passes:

$$
n_{passes} = \left\lceil \frac{D_{total}}{d} \right\rceil
$$

**Explanation:** A larger per-pass depth reduces passes, but is limited by the machine's rigidity, the tool's strength, and the material. A designer who specifies a **smaller rough-feature size** reduces $D_{total}$ and hence $n_{passes}$.

### Step 6 — Total Machining Time

$$
T_{total} = n_{passes} \cdot t_{cut} = \left\lceil \frac{D_{total}}{d} \right\rceil \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot L_{eff}}{f \cdot v}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the closed-form design equation. It links *design decisions* ($D$, $L_{eff}$, $D_{total}$) to *machining time* (and thus cost).

### Step 7 — Total Machining Cost

$$
C_{mach} = T_{total} \cdot C_{machine} + T_{toolchange} \cdot C_{tool}
$$

**Explanation:** The machine cost rate is added to the cost of tool changes. A design requiring harder-to-machine features increases $T_{total}$ and tool wear, both inflating $C_{mach}$.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Cost Comparison of Two Designs

**Problem Statement:** Two bracket designs require the same function. Calculate the relative machining cost.

- **Design A:** Pocket of depth 50 mm, width 20 mm, length 80 mm, sharp internal corners.
- **Design B:** Same pocket, but **open on one side** (so it becomes a U-slot), with internal corner radius R6 mm.

### Given Parameters
- Cutter diameter $D_{tool}$ = 12 mm
- Feed $f$ = 0.1 mm/rev
- Cutting speed $v$ = 60 m/min = 1000 mm/s
- Machine rate $C_m$ = ₹5/min
- Cost premium for EDM (sharp corner) $C_{EDM}$ = ₹150 extra

### Step 1: Compute Spindle Speed

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot 60}{\pi \cdot 12} = \frac{60000}{37.699} \approx 1591.5 \text{ rev/min}
$$

**Explanation:** Convert cutting speed to mm/s by multiplying by 1000/60. Divide by $\pi D$ to obtain rev/min.

### Step 2: Effective Length per Pass for Design A

$$
L_{eff,A} = 80 + 5_{approach} + 5_{over} = 90 \text{ mm}
$$

**Explanation:** A deep narrow pocket requires extra approach and over-travel for tool entry/exit.

### Step 3: Machining Time for Design A

$$
t_{A} = \frac{90}{0.1 \cdot (1591.5 / 60)} = \frac{90}{2.6525} \approx 33.93 \text{ s/pass}
$$

**Explanation:** The denominator is the linear feed in mm/s. Divide $L_{eff}$ by feed rate.

### Step 4: Number of Passes for Design A

$$
n_{A} = \left\lceil \frac{50}{3} \right\rceil = 17 \text{ passes}
$$

**Explanation:** Assume depth of cut $d = 3$ mm. A 50 mm deep pocket requires 17 passes.

### Step 5: Total Time and Cost for Design A

$$
T_A = 17 \cdot 33.93 = 576.8 \text{ s} \approx 9.61 \text{ min}
$$

$$
C_A = 9.61 \cdot 5 + 150 = 198.07 \text{ ₹}
$$

### Step 6: Repeat for Design B (open slot, shallow passes)

For Design B, the open slot means:
- Effective length $L_{eff,B} = 60$ mm (no approach needed for an open slot)
- Depth now only 30 mm (open side has no wall)

$$
n_B = \left\lceil \frac{30}{3} \right\rceil = 10 \text{ passes}
$$

$$
T_B = 10 \cdot \frac{60}{2.6525} = 10 \cdot 22.62 = 226.2 \text{ s} \approx 3.77 \text{ min}
$$

$$
C_B = 3.77 \cdot 5 + 0 = 18.85 \text{ ₹}
$$

### Step 7: Cost Saving

$$
\Delta C = 198.07 - 18.85 = 179.22 \text{ ₹ per part}
$$

**Conclusion:** The DFMe-compliant design (open slot, R6 corners) is **~10.5× cheaper** than the closed pocket with sharp corners.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Design Rule → Cost Impact

| Design Rule | Geometric Change | Machining Parameter Affected | % Cost Reduction (typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Through hole instead of blind | Hole depth reduction | $L_{eff}$ ↓, $n_{passes}$ ↓ | 20–35 % |
| Open pocket instead of closed | Wall removal | $n_{passes}$ ↓, tool change ↓ | 40–60 % |
| Standard drill sizes | Diameter to preferred number | Tool inventory cost ↓ | 5–15 % |
| Generous corner radius | $R$ increased | End-mill size ↑, MRR ↑ | 15–30 % |
| Realistic tolerance | Tolerance relaxed | Inspection time ↓, machine choice ↓ | 10–50 % |
| Fewer setups | Symmetry added | Setup time ↓ | 20–40 % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of Design for Machining Ease

The diagram below maps the **decision flow** a designer follows when applying DFMe rules, starting from the initial concept and ending in a cost-optimized geometry.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Conceptual Design] --> B[Identify Machined Features]
    B --> C{Is feature MACHINABLE with STANDARD tools?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Apply Standard Sizes and Radii]
    C -->|No| E[Redesign Geometry]
    E --> F{Does feature need DEEP cavity or BLIND hole?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Convert to OPEN slot or THROUGH hole]
    F -->|No| H{Is internal corner sharp?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Add minimum RADIUS equal to cutter nose]
    H -->|No| J{Tolerance tighter than IT8?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Relax tolerance to IT10 or IT11]
    J -->|No| L{Can feature be done in 1 or 2 setups?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Finalize Drawing]
    L -->|No| N[Add symmetry for 3-axis fixture]
    N --> M
    D --> M
    G --> M
    I --> M
    K --> M
    M --> O[Cost-Optimized Design]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: DFMe Cost Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Design Inputs]
        A1[Material Choice]
        A2[Feature Geometry]
        A3[Tolerance Specification]
        A4[Surface Finish]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[Machining Process]
        B1[Number of Setups]
        B2[Tool Selection]
        B3[Cutting Parameters]
        B4[Cycle Time]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Cost Outputs]
        C1[Material Cost]
        C2[Machining Cost]
        C3[Tooling Cost]
        C4[Inspection Cost]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
    A4 --> B4
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    B4 --> C4
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
```

## 4.3 Before/After Schematic Representation (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BEFORE[Poor Design - High Machining Cost]
        BA[Closed Deep Pocket] --> BB[Sharp Internal Corners]
        BB --> BC[Blind Hole at Bottom]
        BC --> BD[Tolerance 0.01 mm]
        BD --> BE[Result: 5 Setups, EDM Required]
    end
    subgraph AFTER[DFMe-Compliant Design]
        FA[Open U-Slot] --> FB[R6 Internal Corners]
        FB --> FC[Through Hole]
        FC --> FD[Tolerance 0.1 mm]
        FD --> FE[Result: 2 Setups, Standard Tools]
    end
    BEFORE -.->|Redesign| AFTER
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The first flowchart is a **decision tree** the designer walks through.
> - The second diagram is a **block-level input–process–output model** showing how each design input propagates to a cost component.
> - The third diagram is a **side-by-side comparison** of poor vs. DFMe-compliant design, with the redesign arrow indicating that the *same functional part* can be re-engineered for ease of machining.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students commonly **lose 2–3 marks** by stating DFMe rules *without justification*. KTU examiners expect a **reasoning line** (e.g., "Standardize hole sizes **because** non-standard sizes require special drill procurement, increasing tooling cost").
> - When a question asks to "**list and explain**" the design rules, the word *explain* carries **at least 50% of the marks**. A bare list is a guaranteed 5–6 mark loss.
> - In numerical problems, students often **forget to convert units** (e.g., m/min to mm/s) and lose 2 marks. Always state the unit at every step.
> - Do not confuse *Design for Machining Ease* with *Design for Assembly* — they are distinct domains. A part easy to machine may still be hard to assemble.

---

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
**Define Design for Machining Ease. List any four guidelines for designing components for ease of machining.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):**
> Design for Machining Ease (DFMe) is a DFM principle that establishes design guidelines to ensure a component can be manufactured using standard machining processes, standard tools, and minimum setups, while achieving the required tolerances and surface finish at the lowest cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Four Guidelines (2 marks – ½ mark each):**
> 1. **Use standard hole sizes** to avoid procurement of special drills.
> 2. **Prefer through holes** over blind holes to reduce peck-drilling cycles.
> 3. **Provide adequate internal corner radii** equal to the nose radius of standard end-mills.
> 4. **Design for minimum setups** by using symmetry that allows multi-sided machining in one fixture.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
**Why is it cost-effective to use generous internal corner radii in machined components? Illustrate with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Reason (2 marks):** A larger corner radius permits the use of a **larger-diameter end-mill**, which has a higher material removal rate, higher stiffness (less tool deflection), and a longer tool life. It also eliminates the need for secondary operations such as EDM or hand filing for sharp corners.

> **Example (1 mark):** A pocket with R3 mm internal corners requires a Ø6 mm end-mill, which deflects easily and produces a poor surface finish. Increasing the corner to R8 mm allows a Ø16 mm end-mill, which machines the same feature in **less than half the time** with **better surface finish and tolerance control**.

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each and offer an **internal choice** (either Question A or Question B). Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Solutions must include **diagrams and labeled steps**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Regular Exam, Question A]**

**(a)** Explain the **Boothroyd cost model** for machined components. Derive the relationship between cutting speed, spindle speed, and tool diameter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mild-steel shaft of diameter 60 mm and length 300 mm is to be turned down to 50 mm over a length of 200 mm. Calculate the **(i)** spindle speed, **(ii)** machining time, and **(iii)** material removal rate, given cutting speed $v = 180$ m/min, feed $f = 0.25$ mm/rev, and depth of cut $d = 2$ mm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Boothroyd Cost Model (3 marks):**
The Boothroyd model expresses the total cost of a machined component as:
$$
C_{total} = C_{material} + C_{machining} + C_{tooling} + C_{setup}
$$
Where:
- $C_{material}$ = blank cost (₹) — depends on raw stock size
- $C_{machining} = C_{machine} \cdot T_{machining}$ — proportional to cycle time
- $C_{tooling}$ — depends on tool complexity and tool-changing frequency
- $C_{setup}$ — depends on the number of fixturing operations

**Design for Machining Ease aims to minimize all four terms by simplifying geometry, standardizing features, and reducing setups.**

**Derivation (4 marks):**
The tangential cutting speed is the linear velocity at the tool-workpiece interface:
$$
v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N
$$
Solving for spindle speed $N$:
$$
N = \frac{v}{\pi \cdot D}
$$
For $v$ in m/min, the constant becomes:
$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D} \text{ rev/min}
$$
This shows that for a given material (fixed $v$), **reducing the diameter $D$** of a machined feature **increases the required spindle speed**, which is limited by the machine's maximum RPM. Hence, DFMe prefers **moderate diameters** that fall within the machine's optimal speed range.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:** $v = 180$ m/min, $D = 60$ mm (initial), $L = 200$ mm, $f = 0.25$ mm/rev, $d = 2$ mm.

**(i) Spindle Speed (2 marks):**
$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot 180}{\pi \cdot 60} = \frac{180000}{188.496} \approx 954.93 \text{ rev/min}
$$

**(ii) Machining Time (3 marks):**
$$
t_m = \frac{L}{f \cdot N / 60} = \frac{200}{0.25 \cdot (954.93 / 60)} = \frac{200}{0.25 \cdot 15.9155} = \frac{200}{3.979} \approx 50.27 \text{ s}
$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate (2 marks):**
$$
MRR = v \cdot f \cdot d
$$
Convert $v$ to mm/min: $v = 180 \cdot 1000 = 180000$ mm/min. Then:
$$
MRR = 180000 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 2 = 90000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 90 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary Exam, Question B]**

**(a)** Discuss the **"Ten Golden Rules of Design for Machining Ease"** with at least **six rules explained in detail**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two alternative designs for a locating block are being evaluated. **Design X** has a closed rectangular pocket 80 mm × 50 mm × 30 mm deep with sharp internal corners. **Design Y** has the same pocket but opened on one face (depth reduced to 15 mm) with R8 internal corners. If the cutter diameter is 16 mm, feed 0.15 mm/rev, cutting speed 80 m/min, and machine cost is ₹4/min, compute the **machining time and cost** for both designs. Which design is DFMe-compliant? Justify. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Six rules in detail (7 marks – approx. 1.2 marks per rule):**

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Avoid Separate Machined Surfaces:** Combining multiple small machined faces into one large face reduces the number of setups and the fixturing cost. *Example:* A bracket with two small pads machined separately needs two setups; one large pad needs one setup.

> 2. **Standardize Dimensions:** Use ISO preferred numbers for hole diameters, slot widths, and chamfers. A Ø7.3 mm hole requires a custom drill; Ø8 mm uses a standard off-the-shelf drill, saving 15–20 % on tooling cost per part.

> 3. **Use Through Holes:** Through holes are drilled in one continuous pass, are easy to inspect, and require no peck-drilling. Blind holes need peck cycles (interrupted cut → tool wear), and their depth is hard to verify.

> 4. **Provide Generous Internal Corner Radii:** Internal corners must equal the nose radius of the largest end-mill that can physically enter the corner. A larger radius allows a larger (stiffer, faster) cutter.

> 5. **Avoid Deep Pockets:** Pockets deeper than 4× their width cause tool deflection, chip evacuation issues, and long cycle times. Design solutions: open the pocket on one side, or split it into two shallower pockets.

> 6. **Design for Tool Accessibility:** Ensure clearance for tool approach and exit. Recessed features hidden behind walls require special angled tooling, which is more expensive and harder to set up.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:** $D_{tool} = 16$ mm, $f = 0.15$ mm/rev, $v = 80$ m/min, $C_m = ₹4$/min.

**Spindle Speed (common to both):**
$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot 80}{\pi \cdot 16} = \frac{80000}{50.265} \approx 1591.55 \text{ rev/min}
$$

**Feed rate in mm/s:**
$$
f_{linear} = 0.15 \cdot (1591.55 / 60) = 0.15 \cdot 26.526 = 3.979 \text{ mm/s}
$$

---

**Design X (Closed Pocket, 30 mm deep):**

Effective cut length per pass (perimeter milling):
$$
L_{eff,X} = 2 \cdot (80 + 50) + 5_{approach} + 5_{over} = 270 \text{ mm}
$$

Time per pass:
$$
t_{pass,X} = \frac{270}{3.979} \approx 67.86 \text{ s}
$$

Number of passes:
$$
n_X = \left\lceil \frac{30}{2} \right\rceil = 15 \text{ passes}
$$

Total time:
$$
T_X = 15 \cdot 67.86 \approx 1017.9 \text{ s} \approx 16.97 \text{ min}
$$

Cost (assuming additional EDM cost for sharp corner = ₹80):
$$
C_X = 16.97 \cdot 4 + 80 = 67.88 + 80 = ₹147.88
$$

---

**Design Y (Open Pocket, 15 mm deep):**

Effective cut length per pass (no approach needed for open face):
$$
L_{eff,Y} = 2 \cdot 80 + 50 = 210 \text{ mm}
$$

Time per pass:
$$
t_{pass,Y} = \frac{210}{3.979} \approx 52.78 \text{ s}
$$

Number of passes:
$$
n_Y = \left\lceil \frac{15}{2} \right\rceil = 8 \text{ passes}
$$

Total time:
$$
T_Y = 8 \cdot 52.78 \approx 422.2 \text{ s} \approx 7.04 \text{ min}
$$

Cost:
$$
C_Y = 7.04 \cdot 4 = ₹28.16
$$

---

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
- Cost saving: $\Delta C = 147.88 - 28.16 = ₹119.72$ per part.
- Time saving: $16.97 - 7.04 = 9.93$ min per part.
- **Design Y is DFMe-compliant** because it uses **open geometry** (no closed pocket), **shallower depth** (halved), and **R8 internal corners** (matching a standard Ø16 mm end-mill). It requires **no EDM**, fewer passes, and shorter cycle time.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Definition:** DFMe = designing parts so they can be machined with **standard tools, standard parameters, and minimum setups**.
- **Boothroyd cost equation** has **four components** — material, machining, tooling, setup.
- **Key formula** for spindle speed: $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D}$ (with $v$ in m/min, $D$ in mm).
- **Key formula** for cutting time: $t_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$.
- **Key formula** for MRR: $MRR = v \cdot f \cdot d$.
- **Through holes > Blind holes** in nearly every scenario.
- **Open pockets > Closed pockets** — open the cavity wherever functionally possible.
- **Sharp corners are FORBIDDEN** in machined parts — always specify at least R3 mm.
- **Standard hole sizes** are based on **ISO 286 preferred numbers**.
- **Tighter tolerance = exponential cost increase** — relax tolerances to IT10/IT11 unless functional need demands otherwise.
- **Tool clearance rule:** Allow at least **3–5 mm** clearance around features for standard tool holders.
- **Pocket depth rule:** Avoid pocket depths greater than **4× pocket width**.
- **Setup reduction rule:** Use **symmetry** so that the part can be flipped 90° in a single 3-2-1 fixture.
- **Surface finish rule:** Default to **Ra 1.6 µm or Ra 3.2 µm** unless the design demands otherwise.
- **Examiners expect justification** — never list a rule without explaining its *cost impact*.
- **Unit conversions are the #1 source of numerical errors** — always state units at every step.
- **Distinguish DFMe from DFA:** DFMe focuses on *manufacturing the part*; DFA focuses on *assembling the part*. Confusing the two is a common KTU pitfall.
- **Common Viva Question:** *"What is the minimum internal corner radius for a Ø10 mm end-mill?"* — Answer: R5 mm (half the cutter diameter).
- **Common Viva Question:** *"Why are through holes preferred?"* — Answer: (1) Single-pass drilling, (2) easier inspection, (3) no chip evacuation issue, (4) cheaper drill bit.
- **Common Viva Question:** *"Name the four Boothroyd cost components."* — Answer: Material + Machining + Tooling + Setup.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Appraisal of Various Casting Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Appraisal of Various Casting Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# APPRAISAL OF VARIOUS CASTING PROCESSES

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Casting (KTU 2024 Definition):** Casting is a manufacturing process in which a liquid material (typically metal, but also plastic, ceramic, or composite) is poured into a mold cavity that contains the desired hollow shape, allowed to solidify within the mould, and then extracted as a finished or near-finished part.

**Appraisal of Casting Processes** refers to the systematic evaluation, comparison, and selection of the most appropriate casting method based on:
- Production volume (batch size)
- Part geometry and complexity
- Dimensional accuracy and surface finish required
- Material being cast
- Cost economics
- Lead time
- Equipment availability

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA) framework, the *appraisal* stage is critical because approximately **45% of all manufactured components** start as castings (per KTU Module 2 reference data). The choice of casting process directly affects the downstream machining, joining, and assembly operations.

### Conceptual Analogy

Think of casting processes like **different methods of cooking rice**:
- **Sand Casting** = Cooking rice in a basic pot on a wood fire (cheap, flexible, low precision, slow)
- **Investment Casting** = Cooking rice in a precise measured-cup rice cooker (excellent precision, slower for large batches, higher cost per piece)
- **Die Casting** = Industrial-scale rice steamers in a canteen (extremely fast for mass production, high initial setup cost)
- **Centrifugal Casting** = Specialized cookware for cylindrical items like idlis (perfect for one specific shape class)

Just as a chef chooses the cooking method based on *what* they are cooking, *how much*, and *how precisely*, a design engineer must **appraise** (evaluate) the casting methods to choose the optimal one during the product design phase.

### Standard Casting Performance Metrics

The following metrics are universally used to *appraise* (evaluate) any casting process:

- **Dimensional Tolerance:** Typically **±0.5 mm to ±5 mm** depending on process
- **Surface Finish (Ra):** Ranges from **0.4 μm to 25 μm**
- **Minimum Section Thickness:** From **0.5 mm (investment)** to **6 mm (sand)**
- **Maximum Weight:** From **grams (investment)** to **hundreds of tons (sand)**
- **Production Rate:** From **< 5 pieces/hour (sand)** to **> 500 pieces/hour (die casting)**
- **Casting Yield:** Ratio of good castings to metal poured (typically **40%–95%**)
- **Tooling Cost (Amortized):** From **₹50 (sand)** to **₹50,00,000+ (die casting)**

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Casting Processes

Casting processes are classified by the **mold type**, **mold material**, and **pouring mechanism**:

### A. Expendable (Non-Permanent) Mold Processes
- Sand Casting
- Shell Molding (Croning Process)
- Investment Casting (Lost Wax)
- Plaster Mold Casting
- Ceramic Mold Casting
- Vacuum Casting (V-Process)
- Expanded Polystyrene Casting (Lost Foam)

### B. Permanent (Reusable) Mold Processes
- Die Casting (Hot Chamber / Cold Chamber)
- Permanent Mold Casting (Gravity Die Casting)
- Low-Pressure Die Casting
- High-Pressure Die Casting
- Centrifugal Casting
- Continuous Casting
- Squeeze Casting

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module 2 Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes the **appraisal (comparative evaluation)** of **Sand, Investment, Die, Centrifugal, Shell, Permanent Mold, and Continuous casting** processes against DFMA criteria.

## 2.2 Process-by-Process Appraisal

### 1. Sand Casting (Green Sand / Dry Sand)

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Pattern is placed in a **flask** (two-part box).
2. Green sand (sand + bentonite clay + water + additives) is packed around the pattern.
3. Pattern is withdrawn, leaving a cavity.
4. Molten metal is poured through a **sprue** into the cavity.
5. After solidification, the sand mold is broken (hence "expendable").
6. Casting is cleaned, fettled, and inspected.

**Why & How:**
- *Why* is sand used? Sand is cheap, refractory, and can be reformed.
- *How* does it work? The pattern creates a negative impression; sand grains are bonded temporarily by clay moisture.

**DFMA Appraisal:**
| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Tolerance | **±1.5 mm to ±5 mm** |
| Surface Finish (Ra) | **5 μm to 25 μm** |
| Min. Section | **3 mm to 6 mm** |
| Max. Weight | **Up to 200 tonnes** |
| Tooling Cost | **Very Low (₹₹)** |
| Production Rate | **5–20 parts/hour** |
| Best Suited For | Large parts, low–medium volume, ferrous metals |

### 2. Investment Casting (Lost Wax Process)

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. A **wax pattern** is injection-molded (or hand-crafted for prototypes).
2. Multiple wax patterns are **assembled onto a wax sprue** (tree).
3. The tree is dipped into a **ceramic slurry**, then stuccoed with refractory grains (multiple dips build the shell).
4. The ceramic shell is **dried** and then the wax is **melted out** in an autoclave (hence "lost wax").
5. Shell is **fired** at high temperature (~1000 °C) for strength.
6. Molten metal is poured into the hot shell (often under vacuum).
7. Shell is mechanically broken to retrieve the casting.

**Why & How:**
- *Why* wax? Wax melts cleanly without residue, allowing complex internal features.
- *How* does it achieve precision? The fired ceramic shell is dimensionally stable and gives an excellent surface.

**DFMA Appraisal:**
| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Tolerance | **±0.1 mm to ±0.5 mm** |
| Surface Finish (Ra) | **0.4 μm to 6.3 μm** |
| Min. Section | **0.5 mm to 1.5 mm** |
| Max. Weight | **Up to 50 kg typically** |
| Tooling Cost | **Medium-High (₹₹₹)** |
| Production Rate | **5–50 parts/hour** |
| Best Suited For | Complex aerospace, medical, jewelry, turbine blades |

### 3. Die Casting

**Two Variants:**

#### (a) Hot-Chamber Die Casting
- Injection mechanism is **immersed** in the molten metal bath.
- Used for **low-melting-point alloys** (Zn, Sn, Pb, Mg).
- Cycle time: **< 1 second injection**; **1000+ shots/hour**.

#### (b) Cold-Chamber Die Casting
- Molten metal is **ladled** into a separate cold chamber.
- Used for **high-melting-point alloys** (Al, Cu, Mg).
- Cycle time: **50–500 shots/hour**.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Two die halves (made of tool steel) close, forming cavity.
2. Molten metal is injected at **high pressure (35–1400 bar)**.
3. Metal solidifies rapidly under pressure.
4. Dies open; casting is ejected by pins.
5. Die halves are sprayed with lubricant; cycle repeats.

**DFMA Appraisal:**
| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Tolerance | **±0.05 mm to ±0.25 mm** |
| Surface Finish (Ra) | **0.4 μm to 1.6 μm** |
| Min. Section | **0.6 mm to 3 mm** |
| Max. Weight | **Up to 25 kg (Al), 5 kg (Zn)** |
| Tooling Cost | **Very High (₹₹₹₹₹)** |
| Production Rate | **100–1000+ parts/hour** |
| Best Suited For | High-volume non-ferrous parts, automotive housings |

### 4. Centrifugal Casting

**Two Variants:**

- **True Centrifugal:** Mold rotates about its own axis; used for hollow cylindrical parts (pipes, tubes, bushings).
- **Semi-Centrifugal:** Mold rotates; parts are formed about the axis but with central core.
- **Centrifuge (Multi-axis):** Multiple molds rotate to fill via centrifugal force.

**DFMA Appraisal:**
| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Tolerance | **±0.5 mm to ±3 mm** |
| Surface Finish (Ra) | **3.2 μm to 12.5 μm** |
| Min. Section | **2.5 mm to 5 mm** |
| Max. Weight | **Up to 5000 kg** |
| Tooling Cost | **Medium (₹₹₹)** |
| Production Rate | **20–200 parts/hour** |
| Best Suited For | Pipes, cylinder liners, bearings, brake drums |

### 5. Shell Molding (Croning Process)

**DFMA Appraisal:**
| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Tolerance | **±0.25 mm to ±1 mm** |
| Surface Finish (Ra) | **1.6 μm to 6.3 μm** |
| Min. Section | **1.5 mm to 3 mm** |
| Max. Weight | **Up to 100 kg** |
| Tooling Cost | **Medium (₹₹₹)** |
| Production Rate | **30–100 parts/hour** |
| Best Suited For | Small-to-medium precision ferrous castings |

### 6. Permanent Mold (Gravity Die) Casting

**DFMA Appraisal:**
| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Tolerance | **±0.25 mm to ±1 mm** |
| Surface Finish (Ra) | **1.6 μm to 6.3 μm** |
| Min. Section | **2 mm to 4 mm** |
| Max. Weight | **Up to 50 kg** |
| Tooling Cost | **Medium-High (₹₹₹₹)** |
| Production Rate | **30–200 parts/hour** |
| Best Suited For | Non-ferrous, medium volume, pistons, gear blanks |

### 7. Continuous Casting

**Operational Logic:** Molten metal is poured continuously through a water-cooled mold; the solidified strand is withdrawn at a constant rate and cut to length.

**DFMA Appraisal:**
| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Output Form | Billets, blooms, slabs, strips |
| Tolerance | **±1 mm to ±5 mm** |
| Production Rate | **Very High (continuous)** |
| Tooling Cost | **Very High (₹₹₹₹₹)** |
| Best Suited For | Steel mills, aluminum producers — primary metal stock |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{casting} = \dfrac{m_{usable}}{m_{poured}} \times 100$ | Casting Yield (%); usable casting mass over poured mass | % |
| 2 | $C_{piece} = \dfrac{C_{tooling}}{N} + C_{material} + C_{labor} + C_{overhead}$ | Per-piece cost (amortized tooling over batch size N) | ₹/piece |
| 3 | $C_{break-even} = \dfrac{C_{tooling,A} - C_{tooling,B}}{(C_{piece,B} - C_{piece,A})}$ | Break-even production volume between two processes A and B | pieces |
| 4 | $\Delta L = L_0 \cdot S$ | Shrinkage allowance; L₀ is original length, S is shrinkage fraction (e.g., 0.01 for 1%) | mm |
| 5 | $R_p = \dfrac{3600 \cdot n}{T_{cycle}}$ | Production Rate; n = parts/cycle, T_cycle = cycle time | parts/hr |
| 6 | $T_{cycle} = T_{pour} + T_{solidify} + T_{cool} + T_{eject} + T_{setup}$ | Total cycle time decomposition | seconds |
| 7 | $F_{centrifugal} = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$ | Centrifugal force in centrifugal casting | Newtons |
| 8 | $v_{sprue} = \sqrt{2gh}$ | Velocity at sprue base (Torricelli's law for gating) | m/s |
| 9 | $A_{sprue} = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{v_{sprue}}$ | Sprue cross-section for volumetric flow rate $\dot{V}$ | m² |
| 10 | $t_{solidify} = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^2$ | Chvorinov's Rule; B = mold constant, V = volume, A = surface area | seconds |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM RULE:** In all numerical answer scripts, **always show units** at every step. KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units. Also, in formula tables, never use the vertical bar `|` inside math; use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

## 2.4 Selection Criteria — The "APPREISE" Method

A design engineer should use the following criteria to **appraise** (evaluate) casting processes:

1. **A** — Annual Volume Required
2. **P** — Part Complexity (undercuts, internal cavities, thin walls)
3. **P** — Precision Required (tolerance & surface finish)
4. **R** — Required Material (ferrous vs. non-ferrous; melting point)
5. **E** — Economic Tooling Budget
6. **I** — Internal Features (cores needed?)
7. **S** — Size & Weight of Part
8. **E** — Existing Equipment & Lead Time

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Chvorinov's Rule for Solidification Time

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Significance:** Chvorinov's Rule is a **frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in Part B questions.

**Premise:** Heat loss from a casting occurs primarily through the mold-metal interface, and solidification time is governed by the rate of heat extraction.

### Step 1: Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction
Heat flux from a casting surface is governed by Fourier's law of conduction through the mold:

$$
q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{x}
$$

where $k$ = thermal conductivity of mold, $A$ = surface area, $\Delta T$ = temperature difference, $x$ = mold thickness.

### Step 2: Heat to be Removed
The total heat that must be removed equals the latent heat released plus sensible cooling:

$$
Q = \rho \cdot V \cdot (L + C_p \cdot \Delta T_s)
$$

where $\rho$ = density, $V$ = volume, $L$ = latent heat, $C_p$ = specific heat, $\Delta T_s$ = superheat.

### Step 3: Equate Heat Flow to Heat Lost
Assuming the mold acts as a semi-infinite medium and integrating over time:

$$
\int_0^{t_s} q \cdot A \, dt = Q
$$

### Step 4: Solve the Heat Diffusion Equation
Using the error function solution for transient heat conduction in a semi-infinite mold, the instantaneous surface temperature gradient is:

$$
\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right)_{x=0} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\sqrt{\pi \alpha t}}
$$

where $\alpha = k / (\rho C_p)$ = thermal diffusivity of the mold.

### Step 5: Substitute and Integrate
Substituting into the heat balance:

$$
Q = \int_0^{t_s} \dfrac{k A \Delta T}{\sqrt{\pi \alpha t}} \, dt
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{2 k A \Delta T \sqrt{t_s}}{\sqrt{\pi \alpha}}
$$

### Step 6: Solve for Solidification Time
Rearranging for $t_s$:

$$
t_s = \dfrac{\pi \alpha Q^2}{4 k^2 A^2 \Delta T^2}
$$

Substituting $Q = \rho V (L + C_p \Delta T_s)$ and grouping all material/process constants into a single constant $B$:

$$
t_s = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^2
$$

**This is Chvorinov's Rule.**

### Step 7: Physical Interpretation
- A **sphere** has the maximum V/A ratio for a given volume, so it solidifies fastest.
- A **long bar** has a low V/A, so it solidifies slowly.
- A **chill** (metal insert) lowers $B$ locally, accelerating solidification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The rule $t_s = B (V/A)^2$ implies that adding a chill or insulation selectively changes solidification direction — this is the **principle behind directional solidification** used in investment-cast turbine blades.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Break-Even Volume Analysis

**Problem (KTU Standard 14-Mark Style):**
A manufacturer is choosing between **Sand Casting** and **Investment Casting** for a turbine blade component.

- Sand Casting: Tooling = ₹ 50,000, per-piece cost = ₹ 800
- Investment Casting: Tooling = ₹ 5,00,000, per-piece cost = ₹ 400

**Find:** (a) The break-even production volume.
**Find:** (b) The cost for 500 pieces and 2000 pieces. Which is cheaper in each case?

### Part (a): Break-Even Volume

For break-even, total cost of both processes must be equal:

$$
C_{tooling,S} + N \cdot C_{piece,S} = C_{tooling,I} + N \cdot C_{piece,I}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
N = \dfrac{C_{tooling,I} - C_{tooling,S}}{C_{piece,S} - C_{piece,I}}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000}{800 - 400}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{4{,}50{,}000}{400} = 1125 \text{ pieces}
$$

### Part (b): Cost Comparison

**For N = 500 pieces (below break-even):**

$$
C_{Sand} = 50{,}000 + 500 \times 800 = 50{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
C_{Invest} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 500 \times 400 = 5{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Sand casting is cheaper** (₹ 4,50,000 < ₹ 7,00,000) at N = 500.

**For N = 2000 pieces (above break-even):**

$$
C_{Sand} = 50{,}000 + 2000 \times 800 = 50{,}000 + 16{,}00{,}000 = 16{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
C_{Invest} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 2000 \times 400 = 5{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 = 13{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Investment casting is cheaper** (₹ 13,00,000 < ₹ 16,50,000) at N = 2000.

### Analytical Insight
The break-even point separates the economic domains of the two processes. Below N = 1125, sand casting wins due to low tooling amortization. Above N = 1125, the higher per-piece cost of investment is offset by its lower recurring cost.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: List any six advantages of investment casting over sand casting. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Higher dimensional accuracy** — tolerances of ±0.1 mm to ±0.5 mm compared to ±1.5 mm in sand casting.
2. **Superior surface finish** — Ra 0.4 to 6.3 μm versus 5 to 25 μm in sand casting.
3. **Capability to cast complex geometries** with intricate internal passages, undercuts, and thin walls (down to 0.5 mm).
4. **Wider material compatibility** — ferrous, non-ferrous, superalloys, and titanium can be cast.
5. **Reduced or no machining** — near-net-shape output reduces post-processing.
6. **Lower material wastage** as no parting line, draft, or cores are required.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "investment casting" with "die casting." Remember: **investment uses expendable ceramic shell + wax; die casting uses permanent steel dies + high pressure.** If the answer mixes these concepts, the examiner deducts 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: What is casting yield? Define the term and state its significance. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Casting yield is the ratio of the mass of usable (good) castings produced to the total mass of molten metal poured into the mold, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\eta_{casting} = \dfrac{m_{usable}}{m_{poured}} \times 100 \, \%
$$

**Significance:**
1. **Economic Indicator:** Higher yield = lower metal cost per piece (less scrap).
2. **Process Efficiency:** Indicates gating and riser design effectiveness.
3. **Sustainability Metric:** Lower yield means more remelt energy, more emissions.
4. **Benchmarking:** Investment casting (60–90%) > Die casting (85–95%) > Sand casting (40–70%).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse **casting yield** with **first-pass yield (FPY)**. Casting yield is purely a mass ratio; FPY is a quality ratio. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Pattern with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q: With neat sketches, explain the working principle of centrifugal casting. Compare it with sand casting on the basis of (a) dimensional accuracy, (b) surface finish, (c) production rate, and (d) suitable applications. (14 Marks)**

### Part (a) — Working Principle (7 Marks)

**Centrifugal casting** is a permanent-mold casting process in which the mold is rotated at high speed (typically 300–3000 RPM) about its own axis during pouring and solidification. Molten metal is poured into the rotating mold, and centrifugal force (rather than gravity) drives the metal to conform to the mold cavity.

**Steps of Operation:**

1. A **cylindrical mold** (of steel, iron, or graphite) is mounted on a rotating spindle.
2. The mold is accelerated to the operating speed.
3. Molten metal is poured through a **lance or pour basin** into the rotating mold.
4. **Centrifugal force** $F_c = m \omega^2 r$ pushes the metal outward, forming a hollow tubular shape whose inner diameter is controlled by the **volume of metal poured**.
5. Solidification proceeds from the outer surface inward; impurities and gases migrate to the inner (bore) surface, which is later machined off.
6. After solidification, the mold is slowed, and the casting is removed.

**Three Variants:**

- **True Centrifugal:** Axisymmetric hollow parts (pipes, tubes, liners).
- **Semi-Centrifugal:** Parts with a central core (gear blanks, pulleys).
- **Centrifuge Casting:** Multiple small molds rotated on arms.

### Part (b) — Comparative Appraisal (7 Marks)

| Criterion | Centrifugal Casting | Sand Casting |
|---|---|---|
| **Dimensional Accuracy** | ±0.5 to ±3 mm (better; no parting line) | ±1.5 to ±5 mm (inferior) |
| **Surface Finish** | Ra 3.2 to 12.5 μm (outer surface excellent) | Ra 5 to 25 μm (rougher, sand grains imprint) |
| **Production Rate** | 20 to 200 pcs/hr (semiautomatic) | 5 to 20 pcs/hr (manual, slow) |
| **Applications** | Cast iron pipes, cylinder liners, brake drums, bushings, rolling mill rolls | Engine blocks, machine tool beds, large housings, impellers |
| **Casting Yield** | 80–95% (no riser needed) | 40–70% (risers + gating loss) |
| **Metallurgical Quality** | Directional solidification → dense, defect-free | Prone to gas porosity, inclusions |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to mention that **centrifugal casting produces parts without a separate core** for the inner diameter — the bore is naturally formed by centrifugal force. Missing this loses 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: A manufacturer has to choose between die casting and sand casting for an aluminum alloy automotive housing. The relevant data is given below:**

| Parameter | Die Casting | Sand Casting |
|---|---|---|
| Tooling Cost (₹) | 10,00,000 | 1,00,000 |
| Per-piece Cost (₹) | 250 | 700 |
| Production Rate (pcs/hr) | 100 | 10 |
| Annual Demand | 50,000 pieces | 50,000 pieces |
| Tolerance (mm) | ±0.15 | ±1.5 |

**Calculate: (a) The break-even production volume between the two processes. (b) The total cost of each process for the annual demand. (c) Justify which process is more economical. (14 Marks)**

### Part (a) — Break-Even Volume (5 Marks)

Setting total cost equal:

$$
C_{tooling,D} + N \cdot C_{piece,D} = C_{tooling,S} + N \cdot C_{piece,S}
$$

$$
10{,}00{,}000 + 250 N = 1{,}00{,}000 + 700 N
$$

$$
9{,}00{,}000 = 450 N
$$

$$
N = \boxed{2000 \text{ pieces}}
$$

### Part (b) — Total Cost at N = 50,000 (5 Marks)

**Die Casting:**

$$
C_D = 10{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 \times 250 = 10{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}25{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}35{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Sand Casting:**

$$
C_S = 1{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 \times 700 = 1{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}50{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}51{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

### Part (c) — Justification (4 Marks)

1. **Economic Justification:** Since annual demand (50,000) is far above break-even (2,000), die casting is **vastly more economical**, saving ₹ 2,16,00,000 annually.
2. **Tolerance Justification:** The required tolerance of ±0.15 mm cannot be achieved by sand casting (±1.5 mm). Die casting's ±0.15 mm is acceptable.
3. **Production Rate:** Die casting produces 100 pcs/hr vs sand's 10 pcs/hr — die casting is 10× faster.
4. **Time to recover tooling:** For die casting, $\dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{700-250} = 2222$ pieces recover the extra investment; well within the 50,000 annual demand.

**Conclusion:** Die casting is the **unambiguously correct choice** for this application.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Common Mistake):** Students often divide the *total cost by annual demand* to compute per-piece cost AFTER the calculation, then forget to ADD the amortized tooling. Always show:
> **Total Cost = Tooling + (Per-piece × Volume)**
> Writing only the per-piece cost loses 2 marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this section for last-minute revision before KTU ESE.**

### Key Definitions
- **Casting:** Pouring molten material into a mold, solidifying, and extracting the part.
- **Casting Yield:** % of poured metal that becomes usable casting.
- **Chvorinov's Rule:** $t_s = B (V/A)^2$ — solidification time ∝ square of volume/area ratio.
- **Break-even Volume:** Production quantity at which two competing processes have equal total cost.

### Critical Numerical Values to Remember
- Sand Casting tolerance: **±1.5 mm**
- Investment Casting tolerance: **±0.1 mm**
- Die Casting tolerance: **±0.05 mm**
- Die casting cycle time: **< 1 sec (hot chamber)**
- Investment casting min section: **0.5 mm**
- Sand casting min section: **3 mm**

### Process Selection Shortcuts
- **Low volume + complex shape** → Investment casting.
- **High volume + simple shape + non-ferrous** → Die casting.
- **Low volume + simple shape** → Sand casting.
- **Cylindrical hollow parts** → Centrifugal casting.
- **Continuous cross-section (billets/slabs)** → Continuous casting.
- **Medium volume + medium complexity** → Shell molding or Permanent mold.

### Design for Casting (DFMA) Golden Rules
1. Avoid sharp corners — use fillets of minimum r = 1.5× wall thickness.
2. Uniform wall thickness prevents hot spots and shrinkage porosity.
3. Add **fillets** at all internal corners (reduces stress concentration).
4. Add **draft angles** (1°–3° for sand, 0.5°–1° for die) for easy pattern withdrawal.
5. **Directional solidification:** Place risers at the heaviest section, farthest from the sprue.
6. **Avoid undercuts** if possible — they increase tooling cost and complexity.
7. **Parting line** should be a flat surface, located at the widest cross-section.

### Common Exam Pitfalls (Top 5)
1. Confusing **investment** and **die** casting.
2. Forgetting units in numerical answers.
3. Missing the **amortized tooling** term in cost calculations.
4. Writing **$|x|$** with vertical bars inside a table — breaks the table. Use `\vert` or `\mid`.
5. Confusing **casting yield** with **first-pass yield**.

### Final Formula Flashcard
$$
\boxed{\eta = \dfrac{m_{usable}}{m_{poured}} \times 100} \quad \boxed{t_s = B \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^2} \quad \boxed{N_{BE} = \dfrac{\Delta C_{tooling}}{\Delta C_{piece}}}
$$

**END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — APPRAISAL OF VARIOUS CASTING PROCESSES**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Casting Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Selection of Casting Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Casting** is a primary metal forming process in which a molten or liquid material is poured into a specially prepared cavity (mold) of the desired shape and allowed to solidify, after which the solidified part is removed from the mold. The resulting product is called a **casting**, and the process is fundamental to the manufacture of complex-shaped engineering components across virtually all industrial sectors.

**Selection of a Casting Process** is a systematic, engineering decision-making methodology used by manufacturing engineers to identify the *optimum* casting method from a wide range of available processes (sand, investment, die, permanent mold, centrifugal, shell, plaster, continuous, etc.) for a given component, considering technical requirements, economic constraints, and production logistics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The selection of a casting process is a **multi-criteria decision-making problem** that balances the *technical feasibility* of producing the part (geometry, material, tolerance) against the *economic viability* (tooling cost, piece price, lead time) for the *intended production volume* (prototype, low-volume, high-volume).

## 1.2 The Universe of Casting Processes (Classification Overview)

Casting processes are classified by:
1. **Mold Type** — Expendable (sand, shell, investment) vs. Permanent (die, permanent mold)
2. **Mold Material** — Sand, metal, ceramic, plaster, graphite
3. **Pouring Method** — Gravity, pressure, vacuum, centrifugal, low-pressure
4. **Production Quantity** — Job shop, batch, mass production

| S. No. | Process | Mold Type | Typical Materials |
|:------:|---------|:---------:|-------------------|
| 1 | **Sand Casting** (Green / Dry / No-bake) | Expendable | Ferrous & non-ferrous |
| 2 | **Shell Mold Casting** | Expendable | Ferrous & non-ferrous |
| 3 | **Investment Casting** (Lost-Wax) | Expendable | All metals, superalloys |
| 4 | **Plaster Mold Casting** | Expendable | Aluminum, copper alloys |
| 5 | **Ceramic Mold Casting** (Shaw process) | Expendable | Steels, tool steels |
| 6 | **Permanent Mold Casting** | Permanent | Aluminum, magnesium, copper |
| 7 | **Die Casting** (Hot / Cold chamber) | Permanent | Zn, Al, Mg, Cu alloys |
| 8 | **Centrifugal Casting** | Permanent | Cylinders, pipes |
| 9 | **Continuous Casting** | Permanent | Blooms, billets, slabs |
| 10 | **Lost Foam Casting** | Expendable | Ferrous & non-ferrous |
| 11 | **Squeeze Casting** | Permanent | Light alloys, composites |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cooking Method Selection" Intuition

Imagine you are a chef asked to cook a dish for **10 guests vs. 10,000 guests**:
- For 10 guests, you can hand-roll each dumpling on your kitchen counter (low tooling, high labor per piece) — this is like **Sand Casting** (or investment casting for a single turbine blade prototype).
- For 10,000 guests, you invest in a factory with an automated dumpling machine (high tooling, low labor per piece) — this is like **Die Casting** for mass-producing an aluminum housing.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Casting Selection:**
> *"The right casting process is the one that meets the technical specifications of the part at the lowest total cost for the required production quantity."*

Similarly, if your "dish" requires intricate garnish and mirror-smooth finish (like a wedding cake), you choose a slow, detailed method (investment casting). If your "dish" is a simple bread loaf for daily use, you use a high-volume bakery process (continuous casting or high-pressure die casting).

This volume-vs-detail trade-off is the **central theme** of casting process selection.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Primary Selection Criteria (KTU High-Yield Points)

The selection of a casting process depends on **seven principal factors**. Examiners frequently ask 2-mark or short-note questions directly from this list. Master these:

### Criterion 1 — Production Quantity / Batch Size
- **Sand casting**: Prototypes to ~1000 pieces/year
- **Shell mold**: 1000 to 10,000 pieces/year
- **Investment casting**: 1 to 1000 pieces/year (especially complex parts)
- **Permanent mold**: 1000 to 20,000 pieces/year
- **Die casting**: 10,000 to >1,000,000 pieces/year

### Criterion 2 — Material
- **Ferrous (steel, cast iron)**: Sand, investment, ceramic mold
- **Aluminum alloys**: Sand, die, permanent mold, investment
- **Magnesium alloys**: Sand, die (with care), investment
- **Copper alloys**: Sand, permanent mold, die
- **Superalloys / Titanium**: Investment casting (almost mandatory)
- **Zinc alloys**: Die casting (predominantly)

### Criterion 3 — Size & Weight
- **Sand casting**: Few grams → 200+ tonnes (largest range)
- **Investment casting**: Few grams → ~50 kg typical
- **Die casting**: Few grams → ~50 kg
- **Permanent mold**: Few hundred grams → few hundred kg
- **Centrifugal**: Pipes, tubes, cylindrical parts

### Criterion 4 — Complexity of Shape
- **Investment casting**: Highest complexity (no draft, undercuts, internal passages, thin walls ~0.5 mm)
- **Sand casting**: High complexity with cores
- **Permanent mold / Die casting**: Moderate complexity, parting line constraints
- **Continuous / Centrifugal**: Limited to simple cross-sections

### Criterion 5 — Dimensional Accuracy & Tolerance
| Process | Typical Tolerance (mm/mm) | Achievable (mm/mm) |
|---------|:------------------------:|:------------------:|
| Sand (green) | ± 0.8 mm | ± 0.4 mm |
| Shell mold | ± 0.25 mm | ± 0.10 mm |
| Plaster mold | ± 0.10 mm | ± 0.05 mm |
| Investment | ± 0.10 mm | ± 0.05 mm |
| Permanent mold | ± 0.40 mm | ± 0.15 mm |
| Die casting | ± 0.10 mm | ± 0.05 mm |

### Criterion 6 — Surface Finish (Ra value)
| Process | Typical Ra (μm) |
|---------|:---------------:|
| Sand casting | 12.5 – 25 |
| Permanent mold | 3.2 – 12.5 |
| Die casting | 0.8 – 3.2 |
| Investment casting | 0.8 – 6.3 |
| Shell mold | 1.6 – 6.3 |

### Criterion 7 — Cost (Tooling vs. Piece Price)
- **High tooling + Low piece price**: Die casting, continuous casting
- **Low tooling + High piece price**: Sand casting (green sand)
- **Very high tooling + Very low piece price**: Die casting (massive volumes)

## 2.2 The Casting Process Selection Flowchart (Logic Tree)

The classic **DFMA / KTU decision tree** proceeds as follows:

```
START
  │
  ├── Is the production quantity > 10,000 pieces/year?
  │       │
  │       ├── YES → Is the material Zn, Al, or Mg alloy?
  │       │           │
  │       │           ├── YES → DIE CASTING (Hot chamber for Zn, Cold for Al/Mg)
  │       │           └── NO  → Check if permanent mold is feasible
  │       │
  │       └── NO  → Is complexity very high & tolerance tight?
  │                   │
  │                   ├── YES → INVESTMENT CASTING
  │                   └── NO  → Continue
  │
  ├── Is the part large (>50 kg) or heavy section?
  │       │
  │       ├── YES → SAND CASTING
  │       └── NO  → Continue
  │
  ├── Is surface finish & accuracy critical?
  │       │
  │       ├── YES → SHELL MOLD or INVESTMENT
  │       └── NO  → SAND CASTING (most economical)
  │
  └── Is the part cylindrical (pipes, tubes)?
          │
          ├── YES → CENTRIFUGAL or CONTINUOUS CASTING
          └── NO  → Re-evaluate criteria
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula and key metric relevant to the **Selection of Casting Process** module. Memorize these for Part A and Part B of the KTU ESE.

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables | Application |
|:-:|:------------------:|:---------:|:-----------:|
| 1 | $C_{total} = C_{tool} + (N \times C_{piece})$ | $C_{tool}$ = tooling cost, $C_{piece}$ = unit piece cost, $N$ = quantity | Total cost calculation, break-even analysis |
| 2 | $N_{break-even} = \dfrac{C_{tool,A} - C_{tool,B}}{C_{piece,B} - C_{piece,A}}$ | A, B = two competing processes | Identify quantity at which one process becomes cheaper than the other |
| 3 | $V_{pour} = \dfrac{V_{part}}{E}$ | $V_{pour}$ = volume of molten metal poured, $V_{part}$ = final part volume, $E$ = yield efficiency | Material requirement calculation |
| 4 | $E = \dfrac{V_{part}}{V_{part} + V_{gates} + V_{risers} + V_{runners}}$ | E = casting yield (typically 0.4 – 0.95) | Process yield comparison |
| 5 | $t_{solid} = C \left( \dfrac{V}{A} \right)^n$ | $V$ = volume, $A$ = surface area, $C, n$ = Chvorinov's rule constants | Solidification time comparison across processes |
| 6 | $R_{machining} = 1 - \dfrac{V_{final}}{V_{blank}}$ | $R$ = material removal ratio | Net-shape capability ranking |
| 7 | $C_{piece}(N) = C_{piece,0} + \dfrac{C_{tool}}{N}$ | Fixed + variable cost decomposition | Learning curve, economies of scale |
| 8 | $C_{piece}(N) = A \cdot N^{-b}$ | $A$ = first-piece cost, $b$ = learning index | Learning curve estimation |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **Selection of Casting Process** methodology is the cornerstone of:
1. **Automotive industry** — engine blocks (sand or die), pistons (permanent mold), turbocharger wheels (investment).
2. **Aerospace industry** — turbine blades (investment casting of superalloys), structural brackets (sand or investment).
3. **Pump and valve industry** — casings, impellers (sand, investment).
4. **Defense** — gun bodies, armor components (sand, investment).
5. **Pipe industry** — centrifugal casting for ductile iron pipes.
6. **Electronics housing** — die casting for high-volume aluminum/magnesium enclosures.

## 2.5 Casting Process Selection Matrix (Qualitative Comparison)

| Selection Criterion | Sand | Shell | Investment | Permanent Mold | Die Casting | Centrifugal | Continuous |
|:-------------------:|:----:|:-----:|:----------:|:--------------:|:-----------:|:-----------:|:----------:|
| **Max size** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Min size** | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★ |
| **Complexity** | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★ | ★ |
| **Accuracy** | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ |
| **Surface finish** | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ |
| **Low volume cost** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★★★ | ★ |
| **High volume cost** | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Tooling cost** | ★★★★★ (low) | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★ | ★★★ | ★ |
| **Material range** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |

> Legend: ★ = poor, ★★★★★ = excellent
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Break-Even Quantity Between Two Casting Processes

When two casting processes are technically feasible, the engineer must determine the **break-even quantity** $N_{BE}$ at which one process becomes economically more attractive than the other.

### Given Data
- Process A (e.g., Sand Casting): Tooling cost $C_{tool,A}$, Piece price $C_{piece,A}$
- Process B (e.g., Die Casting): Tooling cost $C_{tool,B}$, Piece price $C_{piece,B}$
- $C_{tool,B} > C_{tool,A}$ (die tooling is expensive)
- $C_{piece,A} > C_{piece,B}$ (sand casting has higher per-piece cost)

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the total cost for Process A:
$$C_{A}(N) = C_{tool,A} + N \cdot C_{piece,A}$$

**Step 2:** Write the total cost for Process B:
$$C_{B}(N) = C_{tool,B} + N \cdot C_{piece,B}$$

**Step 3:** At break-even, total costs are equal:
$$C_{tool,A} + N_{BE} \cdot C_{piece,A} = C_{tool,B} + N_{BE} \cdot C_{piece,B}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to isolate $N_{BE}$:
$$N_{BE} \cdot (C_{piece,A} - C_{piece,B}) = C_{tool,B} - C_{tool,A}$$

**Step 5:** Final formula:
$$N_{BE} = \dfrac{C_{tool,B} - C_{tool,A}}{C_{piece,A} - C_{piece,B}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation Rule:**
> - If required quantity $N < N_{BE}$ → **choose Process A (low tooling, high piece price)**
> - If required quantity $N > N_{BE}$ → **choose Process B (high tooling, low piece price)**

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:**
> A manufacturer wants to produce **5,000 pieces/year** of an aluminum alloy housing. Two processes are feasible:
> - **Sand Casting:** Tooling = ₹ 25,000; Piece price = ₹ 150
> - **Die Casting:** Tooling = ₹ 2,50,000; Piece price = ₹ 60
>
> **Determine:** (a) The break-even quantity. (b) The economical process for 5,000 pieces/year. (c) The cost savings per year at the chosen volume.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Break-even quantity:**

Apply the formula derived above:
$$N_{BE} = \dfrac{C_{tool,B} - C_{tool,A}}{C_{piece,A} - C_{piece,B}}$$

Substitute values (B = Die Casting, A = Sand Casting):
$$N_{BE} = \dfrac{2{,}50{,}000 - 25{,}000}{150 - 60}$$

Compute numerator:
$$2{,}50{,}000 - 25{,}000 = 2{,}25{,}000$$

Compute denominator:
$$150 - 60 = 90$$

Therefore:
$$N_{BE} = \dfrac{2{,}25{,}000}{90} = 2500 \text{ pieces}$$

**Part (b) — Economical process for N = 5,000:**

Since required quantity (5,000) **>** break-even (2,500), choose **Die Casting**.

**Part (c) — Cost savings at N = 5,000:**

Total cost by Sand Casting:
$$C_{A} = 25{,}000 + (5{,}000 \times 150) = 25{,}000 + 7{,}50{,}000 = 7{,}75{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

Total cost by Die Casting:
$$C_{B} = 2{,}50{,}000 + (5{,}000 \times 60) = 2{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

Savings:
$$\Delta C = 7{,}75{,}000 - 5{,}50{,}000 = 2{,}25{,}000 \text{ ₹ per year}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this example matters:** It demonstrates the textbook KTU pattern — examiners love this kind of 7-mark problem combining *formula* + *substitution* + *engineering interpretation*.

## 3.3 Analytical Selection Matrix — Worked Procedure

When the KTU question gives you a *description* of a component (e.g., "complex turbine blade, Inconel 718, 200 pieces, surface finish 1.6 μm Ra, tight tolerance"), the answer must follow a **structured analytical procedure**:

| Step | Analytical Action | Output |
|:----:|:-----------------|:------|
| 1 | Identify the **material** | Eliminates die casting if ferrous / superalloy |
| 2 | Identify the **production volume** | Eliminates die casting for low volume |
| 3 | Identify **tolerance & finish** | Eliminates green sand casting |
| 4 | Identify **complexity & undercuts** | Eliminates permanent mold, continuous casting |
| 5 | Cross-check against **size** constraints | Eliminates investment for very large parts |
| 6 | Shortlist **2–3 candidate processes** | Rank them |
| 7 | **Compute break-even / cost** | Select optimum |
| 8 | Provide **justification** with reference to criteria | Conclude |

## 3.4 Application of Chvorinov's Rule for Process Selection

The **Chvorinov's Rule** is used to estimate solidification time and therefore influences the choice between processes for thick vs. thin sections:
$$t_{s} = C \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{n}$$

- For **die casting** and **permanent mold**: high $C$ (large $C$ value for metal molds), giving *faster solidification* and *finer grain* → good for thin sections.
- For **sand casting**: low $C$ (sand is insulating), giving *slower solidification* and *rougher grain* → suitable for thick sections.

The selection between a permanent mold and sand casting for a part can often be justified using the **modulus** ($V/A$) of the part.

## 3.5 KTU-Style Numerical Model Answer Skeleton

For a 14-mark selection question, structure your answer as:

1. **Given** — restate data clearly.
2. **Identify Criteria** — list the 4–5 key criteria you will evaluate.
3. **Eliminate Infeasible Processes** — apply binary elimination (material, size, volume).
4. **Shortlist** — 2–3 candidate processes.
5. **Compute Cost Comparison** — use break-even formula.
6. **Justify Selection** — state chosen process with technical + economic reasoning.
7. **Conclusion** — single-line statement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** just for the structured presentation. Always use a table or step-numbered list.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Selection Flowchart (Casting Process)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Component to be Cast] --> B{Production Quantity?}
    B -->|Less than 1000/yr| C{Is Complexity Very High?}
    B -->|1000 to 10000/yr| D{Is Tolerance Tight and Finish Critical?}
    B -->|Greater than 10000/yr| E{Material: Zn, Al, Mg?}
    
    C -->|Yes| F[Investment Casting]
    C -->|No| G{Is the Part Large or Heavy Section?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Sand Casting Green or Dry]
    G -->|No| I[Shell Mold or Investment Casting]
    
    D -->|Yes| J[Shell Mold Casting]
    D -->|No| K[Sand Casting or Permanent Mold]
    
    E -->|Yes| L[Die Casting: Hot Chamber for Zn, Cold Chamber for Al or Mg]
    E -->|No| M{Is Material Ferrous?}
    M -->|Yes| N[High Pressure Die Casting Limited / Sand Preferred]
    M -->|No| O[Permanent Mold Casting]
    
    H --> P[End: Selected Process]
    I --> P
    F --> P
    J --> P
    K --> P
    L --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

## 4.2 Decision Matrix Architecture (Block-Level Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[Input Parameters]
        I1[Material Type]
        I2[Production Volume]
        I3[Size and Weight]
        I4[Tolerance Required]
        I5[Surface Finish Required]
        I6[Geometric Complexity]
        I7[Cost Budget and Tooling]
    end
    
    subgraph ENGINE[Selection Engine: Multi Criteria Decision]
        E1[Filter by Material]
        E2[Filter by Volume]
        E3[Filter by Size]
        E4[Filter by Tolerance and Finish]
        E5[Filter by Cost]
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUTS[Output: Feasible Process List]
        O1[Sand Casting]
        O2[Shell Mold Casting]
        O3[Investment Casting]
        O4[Permanent Mold Casting]
        O5[Die Casting Hot or Cold]
        O6[Centrifugal Casting]
        O7[Continuous Casting]
    end
    
    I1 --> E1 --> OUTPUTS
    I2 --> E2 --> OUTPUTS
    I3 --> E3 --> OUTPUTS
    I4 --> E4 --> OUTPUTS
    I5 --> E4 --> OUTPUTS
    I6 --> E1 --> OUTPUTS
    I7 --> E5 --> OUTPUTS
```

## 4.3 Break-Even Cost Analysis Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Two Technically Feasible Processes A and B] --> B[Obtain Tooling Cost A and Tooling Cost B]
    B --> C[Obtain Piece Price A and Piece Price B]
    C --> D[Apply Break Even Formula]
    D --> E[Compute N sub BE]
    E --> F{Required N greater than N sub BE?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Choose Process B: Higher Tooling, Lower Piece Price]
    F -->|No| H[Choose Process A: Lower Tooling, Higher Piece Price]
    G --> I[Final Selection]
    H --> I
```

## 4.4 Casting Process Selection — Volume vs. Cost Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOW_VOL[Low Volume Region: 1 to 1000 pieces]
        LV1[Sand Casting: Lowest Tooling]
        LV2[Investment Casting: For Complex Geometry]
        LV3[Plaster Mold Casting: For Intricate Non Ferrous]
    end
    
    subgraph MID_VOL[Medium Volume Region: 1000 to 10000 pieces]
        MV1[Shell Mold Casting: Better Finish and Accuracy]
        MV2[Permanent Mold Casting: Reusable Metal Mold]
        MV3[Ceramic Mold Casting: For Tool Steels]
    end
    
    subgraph HIGH_VOL[High Volume Region: Greater than 10000 pieces]
        HV1[Die Casting: Fastest Cycle Time]
        HV2[Continuous Casting: For Blooms, Billets, Slabs]
        HV3[Centrifugal Casting: For Pipes and Tubes]
    end
    
    LOW_VOL --> MID_VOL
    MID_VOL --> HIGH_VOL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Architecture:**
> The three subgraphs represent *volume zones*. Within each zone, the dominant processes change. A student must locate their required volume and material in this map, then verify the remaining criteria (tolerance, finish, complexity) before final selection.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any four factors that influence the selection of a casting process.**

**Model Answer (4 key points, each ~0.75 marks + coherence):**

The selection of a casting process is governed by the following factors:

1. **Material of the casting** — Different processes suit different materials. For example, superalloys require investment casting; zinc alloys are best suited for die casting; steel castings are typically produced by sand or investment casting.

2. **Production quantity / batch size** — Low volumes favor sand or investment casting (low tooling cost), while high volumes justify die casting or continuous casting (high tooling amortized over many parts).

3. **Dimensional accuracy and surface finish requirements** — Investment casting, die casting, and shell molding provide tight tolerances and superior surface finish compared to green sand casting.

4. **Size and weight of the casting** — Sand casting is unmatched for very large castings; investment and die casting are best for smaller, intricate parts.

*(Additional valid points: complexity of geometry, cost of tooling, lead time, post-machining requirements, mechanical property requirements.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Compare sand casting and investment casting with respect to (a) tolerance, (b) surface finish, and (c) production volume.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Sand Casting | Investment Casting |
|:---------:|:------------:|:------------------:|
| **(a) Tolerance** | ± 0.4 to ± 0.8 mm typical; relatively poor | ± 0.05 to ± 0.10 mm; excellent |
| **(b) Surface Finish (Ra)** | 12.5 – 25 μm; rough | 0.8 – 6.3 μm; very smooth |
| **(c) Production Volume** | Low to medium (1 to 1000/year economical) | Low to medium (1 to 1000/year for high complexity) |

**Conclusion:** Investment casting offers superior accuracy and finish but at higher cost per piece; sand casting is more economical for larger parts and higher quantities where tight tolerance is not critical.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**A) i)** Explain in detail the various factors that influence the selection of a casting process. **(7 marks)**

**ii)** For a production run of **8,000 pieces/year** of a small brass fitting, compare **shell mold casting** (Tooling = ₹ 60,000; Piece price = ₹ 40) and **die casting** (Tooling = ₹ 5,00,000; Piece price = ₹ 15). Determine the economical process and compute the annual savings. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (i) — Model Answer (7 marks)

The selection of a casting process is a multi-criteria decision based on the following factors:

1. **Material of the casting (1.5 marks)**
   - Ferrous metals (steel, cast iron) → sand, investment, ceramic mold
   - Light alloys (Al, Mg) → die, permanent mold, sand, investment
   - Zinc alloys → die casting (predominantly)
