   - Superalloys / Titanium → investment casting (almost mandatory due to reactivity and high temperature)

2. **Production quantity / batch size (1 mark)**
   - 1 to 1000/year: Sand, investment, plaster, ceramic
   - 1000 to 10,000/year: Shell mold, permanent mold
   - > 10,000/year: Die casting, continuous casting

3. **Size and weight of the casting (0.5 marks)**
   - Very large (tonnes) → sand casting
   - Small to medium → investment, die, permanent mold
   - Long cylindrical → centrifugal casting

4. **Dimensional accuracy and tolerance required (0.5 marks)**
   - Tighter than ± 0.1 mm → investment, die, plaster mold
   - ± 0.25 to ± 0.5 mm → shell mold, permanent mold
   - Looser than ± 0.5 mm → sand casting acceptable

5. **Surface finish required (0.5 marks)**
   - Mirror smooth (Ra < 1.6 μm) → investment, die
   - Moderate (Ra 1.6 to 6.3 μm) → shell, permanent mold
   - Rough (Ra > 12.5 μm) → sand

6. **Complexity of geometry (1 mark)**
   - Internal passages, undercuts, thin walls → investment casting
   - Moderate complexity → sand with cores, die casting
   - Simple cross-section → continuous, centrifugal

7. **Cost considerations (1 mark)**
   - Tooling cost vs. piece price trade-off, break-even analysis
   - Machining cost reduction (net-shape vs. near-net-shape)
   - Scrap and rework rate

8. **Other factors (0.5 marks)**
   - Lead time, available foundry capacity, environmental regulations, post-casting operations (heat treatment, welding)

**Concluding statement:** The *optimum process* is the one that meets technical specifications at the lowest total cost for the required production volume.

#### Part (ii) — Model Answer (7 marks)

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Shell Mold (A) | Die Casting (B) |
|:---------:|:--------------:|:---------------:|
| Tooling cost $C_{tool}$ | ₹ 60,000 | ₹ 5,00,000 |
| Piece price $C_{piece}$ | ₹ 40 | ₹ 15 |
| Required quantity $N$ | 8,000 | 8,000 |

**Step 1: Apply the break-even formula.**

$$N_{BE} = \dfrac{C_{tool,B} - C_{tool,A}}{C_{piece,A} - C_{piece,B}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$N_{BE} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}000}{40 - 15}$$

$$N_{BE} = \dfrac{4{,}40{,}000}{25}$$

$$N_{BE} = 17{,}600 \text{ pieces/year}$$

**Step 3: Compare with required quantity.**

Required $N$ = 8,000 pieces/year < $N_{BE}$ = 17,600 pieces/year.

**Step 4: Select the economical process.**

Since $N < N_{BE}$, choose the process with **lower tooling cost** → **Shell Mold Casting (Process A)**.

**Step 5: Compute total costs at N = 8,000.**

Total cost by Shell Mold:
$$C_{A} = 60{,}000 + (8{,}000 \times 40) = 60{,}000 + 3{,}20{,}000 = 3{,}80{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

Total cost by Die Casting:
$$C_{B} = 5{,}00{,}000 + (8{,}000 \times 15) = 5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 = 6{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 6: Annual savings.**

$$\Delta C = C_{B} - C_{A} = 6{,}20{,}000 - 3{,}80{,}000 = 2{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹ per year}$$

**Conclusion:** Shell mold casting is the economical process for 8,000 pieces/year, yielding an annual saving of **₹ 2,40,000** over die casting.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

**B) i)** Compare the salient features of **sand casting, shell mold casting, investment casting, permanent mold casting, and die casting** in a tabular form, indicating the typical tolerance, surface finish, materials, and production volume suited to each. **(7 marks)**

**ii)** A company needs to manufacture **5,000 turbine blades/year** made of **Inconel 718 (nickel-based superalloy)** with very tight tolerance (± 0.05 mm), excellent surface finish (Ra < 1.6 μm), and complex internal cooling passages. The annual demand is stable. Identify the most suitable casting process and justify with at least four technical reasons. Also state two economic considerations that support the selection. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (i) — Model Answer (7 marks)

| Process | Mold Type | Typical Tolerance | Surface Finish Ra (μm) | Typical Materials | Production Volume |
|:-------:|:---------:|:-----------------:|:----------------------:|:-----------------:|:-----------------:|
| **Sand Casting** (Green / Dry) | Expendable (sand bonded with clay / resin) | ± 0.4 to ± 0.8 mm | 12.5 – 25 | All metals; large castings (up to 200 t) | 1 to 1000/yr |
| **Shell Mold Casting** | Expendable (thermoset resin + sand) | ± 0.10 to ± 0.25 mm | 1.6 – 6.3 | Ferrous and non-ferrous small-to-medium | 1000 to 10,000/yr |
| **Investment Casting** | Expendable (ceramic shell over wax pattern) | ± 0.05 to ± 0.10 mm | 0.8 – 6.3 | All metals including superalloys, Ti | 1 to 1000/yr (high value) |
| **Permanent Mold Casting** | Permanent (metal, typically cast iron / steel) | ± 0.15 to ± 0.40 mm | 3.2 – 12.5 | Non-ferrous (Al, Mg, Cu, Zn) | 1000 to 20,000/yr |
| **Die Casting** (Hot / Cold chamber) | Permanent (hardened tool steel) | ± 0.05 to ± 0.10 mm | 0.8 – 3.2 | Low melting point (Zn, Al, Mg) | 10,000 to >10⁶/yr |

#### Part (ii) — Model Answer (7 marks)

**Selection: Investment Casting (Lost-Wax Process)**

**Justification — Four Technical Reasons (4 marks):**

1. **Material compatibility (Inconel 718)**: Inconel 718 is a nickel-based superalloy with a high melting point (~1330 °C) and is highly reactive with oxygen at pouring temperature. Investment casting uses a ceramic shell mold that can withstand these temperatures and provides an inert environment. Sand casting molds may react; die casting molds cannot survive these temperatures.

2. **Tight tolerance capability (± 0.05 mm)**: Investment casting routinely achieves ± 0.05 mm tolerance, matching the requirement. Sand casting (± 0.4 mm) and shell mold (± 0.10 mm) are inadequate; die casting is restricted to non-ferrous low-melting alloys.

3. **Surface finish (Ra < 1.6 μm)**: Investment casting produces a smooth as-cast surface (0.8 – 6.3 μm Ra). This eliminates or drastically reduces expensive post-casting machining, which is critical for turbine aerofoil surfaces.

4. **Complex internal cooling passages**: Investment casting is unique in its ability to form intricate internal geometries using wax pattern assemblies that are melted out. This is essential for the modern turbine blade's serpentine cooling channels.

**Two Economic Considerations (2 marks):**

1. **Acceptable piece price at moderate volume**: At 5,000 pieces/year, the higher per-piece cost of investment casting (₹ 200–500 typical) is justified because machining and rejection costs of other processes would far exceed the casting cost saving.

2. **Minimal material waste (high yield)**: Investment casting has a high yield (60–90%) compared to sand casting (40–60%), and Inconel 718 is expensive (~$50/kg), so the material savings alone make investment casting the more economical choice for superalloy parts.

**Conclusion:** For 5,000 Inconel 718 turbine blades/year with tight tolerance and complex internal passages, **investment casting is the most suitable process** on both technical and economic grounds.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Skipping the production-volume criterion** — Examiners specifically look for *volume-based filtering* before any cost analysis. If your answer does not mention quantity, you lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing die casting and investment casting** — Die casting is *NOT* feasible for steels or superalloys. Many students incorrectly suggest die casting for ferrous parts. Maximum 0 marks if the process is infeasible.
> 3. **Omitting the formula derivation in break-even problems** — A direct substitution without stating the formula earns *only 2–3 marks* out of 7. Always state the formula first.
> 4. **Forgetting the units** — The break-even quantity must be expressed in "pieces" or "pieces/year", not just a number. Missing unit = −0.5 mark.
> 5. **Wrong sign in break-even** — Students frequently invert the formula and get negative values. Verify: $C_{tool,B} - C_{tool,A}$ must be positive (B has higher tooling). If negative, swap the labels A and B.
> 6. **Not justifying the final selection** — A 7-mark question requires *interpretation* of the answer. Simply computing a number without stating "Hence Process X is selected because…" loses 1 mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Selection of Casting Process**

- **The 7 primary selection criteria** are: (1) Material, (2) Production volume, (3) Size & weight, (4) Complexity, (5) Tolerance, (6) Surface finish, (7) Cost.
- **Volume zones** to memorize: Sand (1–1000), Shell (1000–10000), Die (>10000), Investment (1–1000 but high value).
- **Die casting is for non-ferrous low-melting alloys** only (Zn, Al, Mg, some Cu). Never specify die casting for steel.
- **Investment casting is the go-to for superalloys and titanium** due to ceramic mold inertness and high complexity capability.
- **Sand casting is the universal process** — largest material range, largest size range, lowest tooling cost.
- **Centrifugal casting is for cylindrical parts** (pipes, tubes, rings).
- **Continuous casting is for long uniform cross-sections** (slabs, blooms, billets for rolling).
- **Break-even formula:** $N_{BE} = \dfrac{C_{tool,B} - C_{tool,A}}{C_{piece,A} - C_{piece,B}}$ — memorize in this exact form.
- **Chvorinov's rule:** $t_{s} = C \left( \dfrac{V}{A} \right)^{n}$ — the modulus $V/A$ governs solidification time and influences mold material choice.
- **Tolerance ranking (best to worst):** Investment ≈ Die ≈ Plaster < Shell < Permanent < Sand.
- **Surface finish ranking (best to worst):** Investment ≈ Die < Shell < Permanent < Sand.
- **Tooling cost ranking (lowest to highest):** Sand < Plaster < Investment < Shell < Permanent < Die.
- **Piece price ranking (lowest to highest):** Die < Continuous < Centrifugal < Permanent < Shell < Sand < Investment.
- **Always structure the answer** with: Given → Criteria → Elimination → Shortlist → Calculation → Justification → Conclusion.
- **Always end with an engineering interpretation**, not just a number.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Design Considerations for Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Design Considerations for Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Design Considerations for Casting** refer to the systematic set of geometric, dimensional, and metallurgical guidelines that a product designer must follow to ensure that a part can be produced economically, defect-free, and with consistent quality by any of the available casting processes (sand, die, investment, shell, permanent mould, etc.).

These considerations are essentially a **bridge between the design intent of the part and the practical feasibility of its manufacture via molten-metal solidification**. They govern everything from the **orientation of the part in the mould** (parting line) to the **machining stock, draft, fillets, and shrinkage allowance** that must be incorporated into the casting drawing.

### The Three Pillars of Casting Design

| Pillar | Focus | Designer Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometrical** | Shape, parting, draft, fillets, ribs | Can the part be withdrawn from the mould? |
| **Metallurgical** | Shrinkage, alloy flow, solidification | Will the part solidify without defects? |
| **Economical** | Material yield, machining stock, gating | Is the part cost-competitive? |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Ice-Cube Tray Metaphor**
>
> Imagine you are freezing water in a homemade ice-cube tray. If the tray has:
> - **No draft (taper)** → you will crack the tray trying to pop the ice out.
> - **Sharp internal corners** → the ice cube forms a crack (stress concentration) exactly at those corners.
> - **Very thick sections** → the centre freezes last and leaves a hollow void (shrinkage cavity).
> - **Unnecessary holes/undercuts** → you will have to cut the tray, which is impossible.
>
> **Casting is the molten-metal equivalent of the ice-cube tray problem**, but with much higher stakes — the "water" is at 1500 °C and any mistake in the design becomes a **scrap casting** that costs the foundry money. Hence, the designer must think like a *foundryman* before submitting a drawing.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU Examinations

> [!TIP]
> - KTU frequently tests **draft angle values, fillet radii ranges, shrinkage allowance values**, and **selection of parting line** as 3-mark direct questions.
> - 14-mark Part B questions typically ask for a **complete redesign of a defective casting** with all allowances justified — this is the most common ESE pattern.
> - Understanding the "**why**" behind each guideline is essential because KTU 2024 Scheme questions are increasingly **application-based** under NEP 2020 outcome-based education (OBE) norms.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ten Fundamental Design Considerations

### 2.1.1 Parting Line (Mould Separation Plane)
The **parting line** is the imaginary plane that separates the **cope (upper half)** from the **drag (lower half)** of the mould.

**Why it matters:** The position of the parting line dictates:
- The complexity of the pattern (and hence pattern cost)
- The need for cores (and hence core cost)
- The location of flash (excess material at the parting plane)

**Selection rule of thumb:** Always choose the parting line at the **largest cross-section** of the part so that the pattern can be withdrawn without undercuts.

### 2.1.2 Draft (Taper Angle)
Draft is a **slight taper** provided on all vertical surfaces of the pattern so that the pattern can be withdrawn from the sand mould without damaging the cavity walls.

**Why it matters:** Sand grains have a finite angle of repose; without draft, the pattern tears the mould cavity during withdrawal, producing a **scratch or mold erosion defect**.

**Standard Draft Values:**

| Casting Process | External Draft | Internal Draft |
|---|---|---|
| **Sand casting (manual)** | 1° – 2° | 2° – 3° |
| **Sand casting (machine)** | 0.5° – 1° | 1° – 1.5° |
| **Die casting (non-ferrous)** | 0.5° – 1° | 1° – 1.5° |
| **Investment casting** | 0° – 0.5° | 0.5° – 1° |
| **Shell moulding** | 0.5° – 1° | 1° – 1.5° |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb:** Draft is always **more on internal surfaces** than external surfaces because internal surfaces abrade the mould more during withdrawal.

### 2.1.3 Fillet and Chamfer
- **Fillet**: A small rounded internal corner that replaces a sharp 90° angle.
- **Chamfer**: A small bevelled edge that replaces a sharp external corner.

**Why it matters:**
- Eliminates **stress concentrations** in the finished casting.
- Promotes **directional solidification** from the fillet toward the riser (the fillet acts as a thermal "hot spot" feeding point).
- Prevents **hot tears** and **cracks** at corners.

**Standard sizes:**

$$
R_f = 0.15 \, t \;\; \text{to} \;\; 0.25 \, t
$$

where $R_f$ is the fillet radius and $t$ is the **thickness of the thinner adjoining wall**.

### 2.1.4 Wall Thickness Uniformity
A uniform wall thickness ensures **uniform cooling rate**, which:
- Reduces **shrinkage cavities** (voids in thick sections).
- Avoids **residual stresses** caused by differential contraction.
- Eliminates **hot tears** at the junction of thick and thin sections.

> [!WARNING]
> If varying thickness is unavoidable, the **transition should be gradual** (1:3 to 1:5 taper) to allow smooth heat flow and avoid stress concentration.

### 2.1.5 Ribs and Webs
When stiffness is required without increasing overall wall thickness, designers add **ribs** (projections) or **webs** (thin connecting plates).

**Design rules:**
- Rib thickness $\le 0.7 \times$ adjoining wall thickness.
- Rib height should not exceed **3 times** the rib thickness.
- Avoid rib intersections at a single point — stagger them to prevent **hot spot** formation.

### 2.1.6 Cored Holes
Holes in castings are formed by **sand cores** placed in the mould cavity.

**Why it matters:** Small diameter holes are *more expensive* to core than to **cast solid and machine through**. As a general rule:

$$
d_{\min} \ge 0.25 \, t \quad \text{or} \quad d_{\min} \ge 6 \text{ mm (whichever is greater)}
$$

where $d_{\min}$ is the minimum core diameter and $t$ is the wall thickness.

### 2.1.7 Bosses
**Bosses** are projections used to provide **machined bearing surfaces** or **mounting points**.

**Design rules:**
- Boss diameter $\ge 1.5 \times$ hole diameter.
- Boss height $\le 2 \times$ boss diameter.
- Blend smoothly into the adjoining wall with a fillet.

### 2.1.8 Undercuts
**Undercuts** are features that prevent straight-line withdrawal of the pattern. They require **loose pieces** (split patterns) or **cores**, both of which add cost.

> [!NOTE]
> As a design-for-manufacture rule, **avoid undercuts whenever possible**. If unavoidable, locate them in the same direction so they can be formed with a single loose piece.

### 2.1.9 Shrinkage Allowance
Almost all metals **contract** when they solidify and cool from the pouring temperature to room temperature. The pattern must therefore be **slightly larger** than the desired casting.

**Why it matters:** Without compensation, the casting will be **undersized** after cooling.

### 2.1.10 Machining Allowance
A small extra thickness (typically **1.5 mm to 6 mm**) is left on surfaces that will be subsequently machined. This is because cast surfaces are **rough and slightly inaccurate**, and machining provides the final dimensional and surface finish.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE** — it is the single most-tested concept in the casting-design module of KTU ESE.

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Standard Value | Unit | Applicable Process |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pattern dimension | $D_p$ | $D_p = D_c \left(1 + S\right)$ | mm | All casting |
| 2 | Shrinkage allowance (grey cast iron) | $S$ | **0.008 – 0.012** (≈ **1 %**) | m/m | Sand |
| 3 | Shrinkage allowance (steel) | $S$ | **0.015 – 0.020** (≈ **2 %**) | m/m | Sand |
| 4 | Shrinkage allowance (aluminium) | $S$ | **0.012 – 0.015** (≈ **1.5 %**) | m/m | Sand / Die |
| 5 | Shrinkage allowance (brass / bronze) | $S$ | **0.012 – 0.016** (≈ **1.5 %**) | m/m | Sand |
| 6 | Machining allowance | $M$ | **1.5 – 6.0** | mm | All |
| 7 | Draft angle (external, sand) | $\alpha_e$ | **1° – 2°** | degree | Sand |
| 8 | Draft angle (internal, sand) | $\alpha_i$ | **2° – 3°** | degree | Sand |
| 9 | Fillet radius | $R_f$ | $R_f = 0.15\,t$ to $0.25\,t$ | mm | All |
| 10 | Rib thickness | $t_r$ | $t_r \le 0.7\,t$ | mm | All |
| 11 | Rib height / thickness | $h_r/t_r$ | $\le 3$ | ratio | All |
| 12 | Minimum core diameter | $d_{min}$ | $\ge 0.25\,t$ and $\ge 6$ mm | mm | Sand |
| 13 | Boss diameter / hole diameter | $D_b/d$ | $\ge 1.5$ | ratio | All |
| 14 | Boss height / diameter | $H_b/D_b$ | $\le 2$ | ratio | All |
| 15 | Chvorinov's solidification time | $t_s$ | $t_s = B \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{n}, \; n \approx 2$ | s | All |
| 16 | Section modulus of riser neck | $Z$ | $Z = (1.05 \text{ to } 1.15) \times Z_{casting}$ | mm³ | Sand |
| 17 | Modulus of cylinder | $M_{cyl}$ | $M = \dfrac{D}{4}$ | cm | All |
| 18 | Modulus of rectangular bar | $M_{rect}$ | $M = \dfrac{a\,b}{2(a+b)}$ | cm | All |
| 19 | Modulus of sphere | $M_{sph}$ | $M = \dfrac{D}{6}$ | cm | All |
| 20 | Effective riser modulus | $M_r$ | $M_r \ge 1.2 \, M_c$ | cm | Sand |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chvorinov's Rule** states that the solidification time of a casting section is proportional to the square of its **modulus** $M = V/A$, where $V$ is the volume and $A$ is the surface area through which heat is dissipated. A section with a **higher modulus** solidifies **slower** and therefore acts as a **riser feeder**.
> The relationship is:
>
> $$t_s = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{2}$$
>
> where $B$ is the mould constant (depends on metal and mould material).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry** (engine blocks, cylinder heads): These design rules govern the casting of aluminium alloys with rib patterns, bosses, and complex cored water jackets.
- **Pump and valve industry**: Wall thickness uniformity and directional solidification are used to produce defect-free pressure housings.
- **Aerospace industry** (investment cast turbine blades): Near-zero draft, very thin walls, and intricate cored cooling passages are possible only because the designer has rigorously applied these considerations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Shrinkage Allowance Formula

### 3.1.1 Statement
A grey cast iron gear blank is required with a final outer diameter of $D_c = 200$ mm at room temperature. Determine the **pattern diameter** $D_p$ to be used, given that the **shrinkage allowance** for grey cast iron is $S = 0.01$ (i.e., 1 %).

### 3.1.2 Given Data
- Final casting diameter: $D_c = 200$ mm
- Shrinkage allowance (grey cast iron): $S = 0.01$ m/m

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Understanding the relationship between pattern size and casting size.**

> When a metal cools from its pouring temperature to room temperature, it contracts. The pattern must therefore be **larger** than the finished casting by an amount equal to this contraction. Mathematically:

$$
D_p = D_c + \text{(amount of contraction)}
$$

**Step 2: Expressing the amount of contraction as a fraction of the casting dimension.**

> The amount of contraction is given by the **shrinkage allowance** $S$ multiplied by the casting dimension:

$$
D_p = D_c + S \cdot D_c
$$

**Step 3: Factoring out $D_c$.**

$$
D_p = D_c \left(1 + S\right)
$$

**Step 4: Substituting numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_p &= 200 \left(1 + 0.01\right) \\
D_p &= 200 \times 1.01 \\
D_p &= 202 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Physical interpretation.**

> The pattern will be made **2 mm larger** in diameter (1 mm on each side) than the final casting. After pouring, solidification, and cooling to room temperature, the casting will contract by exactly this 2 mm and emerge with the required 200 mm diameter.

### 3.1.4 Generalised Formula

$$
D_p = D_c \left(1 + S\right) \quad \text{(for linear dimensions)}
$$

> For dimensions **greater than 600 mm**, an additional **distortion allowance** may need to be added separately, because the casting can warp on cooling.

## 3.2 Derivation of Chvorinov's Rule and Modulus Calculations

### 3.2.1 Statement
A cylindrical riser of diameter $D_r = 100$ mm and height $H_r = 100$ mm is to feed a steel casting of modulus $M_c = 1.5$ cm. Verify whether this riser is adequate using Chvorinov's rule, given that the mould constant $B = 12.5$ s/cm² for sand-cast steel.

### 3.2.2 Given Data
- Riser diameter: $D_r = 100$ mm $= 10$ cm
- Riser height: $H_r = 100$ mm $= 10$ cm
- Casting modulus: $M_c = 1.5$ cm
- Mould constant: $B = 12.5$ s/cm²

### 3.2.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Calculate the volume of the riser.**

> A cylinder of diameter $D_r$ and height $H_r$ has volume:

$$
V_r = \pi \left(\frac{D_r}{2}\right)^{2} H_r
$$

> Note: The top of the riser is usually covered with an insulating sleeve; if not, the top also loses heat. For a conservative (worst-case) design, we include the top surface.

**Step 2: Calculate the cooling surface area of the riser.**

> The lateral surface plus the bottom surface (we exclude the top if it is open to atmosphere; here we include it for a fully enclosed riser):

$$
A_r = \pi D_r H_r + 2 \cdot \pi \left(\frac{D_r}{2}\right)^{2}
$$

> Wait — for a riser sitting on a sprue, the **bottom is insulated by the molten feed metal** and the **top loses heat to the atmosphere**. The conservative design assumes the top **does** lose heat.

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values (in cm).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_r &= \pi (5)^{2} (10) = 250 \pi \text{ cm}^{3} \\
A_r &= \pi (10)(10) + \pi (5)^{2} \\
A_r &= 100\pi + 25\pi = 125\pi \text{ cm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the riser modulus $M_r = V_r / A_r$.**

$$
M_r = \frac{250 \pi}{125 \pi} = 2.0 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the design criterion.**

> For a riser to feed the casting effectively, its solidification time must be **at least 1.2 times** that of the casting. Since $t_s \propto M^2$ (Chvorinov's rule), this means:

$$
M_r \ge 1.2 \, M_c
$$

**Step 6: Verify the criterion.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.2 \times M_c &= 1.2 \times 1.5 = 1.8 \text{ cm} \\
M_r &= 2.0 \text{ cm} \\
M_r &\ge 1.2 \, M_c \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The riser of modulus 2.0 cm is **adequate** to feed a casting of modulus 1.5 cm, because it will solidify later (1.56 times slower, since $(2.0/1.5)^2 = 1.78 \ge 1.44$).

### 3.2.4 Solidification Time Comparison (Chvorinov's)

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{s,\,riser} &= B \cdot M_r^{2} = 12.5 \times (2.0)^{2} = 50.0 \text{ s} \\
t_{s,\,casting} &= B \cdot M_c^{2} = 12.5 \times (1.5)^{2} = 28.125 \text{ s} \\
\dfrac{t_{s,\,riser}}{t_{s,\,casting}} &= \dfrac{50.0}{28.125} \approx 1.78 \;\; \ge 1.2 \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Decision Table

> [!TIP]
> The following decision matrix is the **single most useful analytical tool** for KTU 14-mark Part B questions. Memorise the column "Designer's Choice".

| Design Situation | Defect Produced if Ignored | Designer's Choice | Numerical Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vertical face on pattern | Mould cavity erosion on withdrawal | Add **draft** | 1° – 2° external, 2° – 3° internal |
| Sharp 90° internal corner | Hot tear, stress concentration | Add **fillet** | $R_f = 0.15\,t$ to $0.25\,t$ |
| Sharp 90° external corner | Handling injury, stress | Add **chamfer** | $C = 0.5\,t$ or 1 – 3 mm |
| Thick section (> 2× adjacent wall) | Shrinkage cavity (pipe) | Use **riser** on top | $M_r \ge 1.2\,M_c$ |
| Thin section next to thick | Residual stress, warping | **Gradual transition** | 1:3 to 1:5 taper |
| Large flat area needing stiffness | Warping, distortion | Add **ribs/webs** | $t_r \le 0.7\,t$, $h_r/t_r \le 3$ |
| Hole required in casting | — | Use **core** | $d_{min} \ge 0.25\,t$ and $\ge 6$ mm |
| Hole too small or deep | Core breakage, cost | **Cast solid, drill later** | If $d < 0.25\,t$ or $L/D > 4$ |
| Boss for mounting | Hot spot at boss root | Blend with **fillet** | $D_b \ge 1.5\,d$, $H_b \le 2\,D_b$ |
| Feature requiring undercut | Pattern cannot be withdrawn | **Re-design** to eliminate | If unavoidable, use loose piece |
| Molten metal flows long distance | Cold shut, misrun | Place **gate** at thick section | Gating ratio design |
| Sand mould contact area | Sand inclusion | Provide **machining stock** | 1.5 – 6 mm |
| Casting contracts on cooling | Undersized casting | **Enlarge pattern** | $D_p = D_c (1+S)$ |
| Casting may warp in service | Distortion | Add **distortion allowance** | 0.5 – 2 % extra for $L > 600$ mm |

## 3.4 Worked-Out Example — Combined Application

**Problem (KTU ESE-style):**
> A steel flange (shrinkage allowance 2 %) is to be sand cast with the following specifications: outer diameter 200 mm, bore 80 mm, thickness 25 mm. Determine:
> (a) Pattern outer diameter and bore diameter.
> (b) Recommended fillet radius at the boss root.
> (c) Whether a boss of diameter 30 mm and height 70 mm is acceptable.

**Solution:**

(a) **Pattern dimensions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_p^{out} &= 200 (1 + 0.02) = 204 \text{ mm} \\
D_p^{bore} &= 80 (1 + 0.02) = 81.6 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

(b) **Fillet radius** (boss blends with a 25 mm wall):

$$
R_f = 0.20 \times 25 = 5.0 \text{ mm}
$$

(c) **Boss acceptability check:**

- Diameter rule: $D_b = 30$ mm; required $\ge 1.5 \times d_{bolt} = ?$. Assuming an M16 bolt hole ($d = 16$ mm):
  $1.5 \times 16 = 24$ mm. Actual $D_b = 30$ mm. **✓ OK.**
- Height rule: $H_b \le 2 \, D_b = 60$ mm. Actual $H_b = 70$ mm. **✗ NOT OK.**

> **Conclusion:** The boss height is **excessive** (70 mm > 60 mm) and must be **reduced to 60 mm** to comply with the casting design rule, or a relief fillet must be added at the root.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Casting Design Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Part Design Received] --> B{Is the wall thickness uniform?}
    B -- NO --> C[Redesign with gradual transition 1:3 to 1:5]
    B -- YES --> D{Are there any undercuts?}
    C --> D
    D -- YES --> E[Re-design to eliminate or use loose piece]
    D -- NO --> F{Are vertical faces present?}
    E --> F
    F -- YES --> G[Add draft 1 to 2 deg external 2 to 3 deg internal]
    F -- NO --> H{Are there sharp internal corners?}
    G --> H
    H -- YES --> I[Add fillet Rf equal to 0.15 t to 0.25 t]
    H -- NO --> J{Does the part need holes?}
    I --> J
    J -- YES --> K{Core diameter greater than 0.25 t and greater than 6 mm?}
    J -- NO --> L[Add shrinkage plus machining allowance]
    K -- YES --> M[Provide core prints and chaplets]
    K -- NO --> N[Cast solid and drill later]
    M --> L
    N --> L
    L --> O[Final Pattern Drawing Approved]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Sand Casting Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PatternShop[Pattern Shop]
        P1[Wooden Pattern] --> P2[Shrinkage Allowance Added]
        P2 --> P3[Machining Allowance Added]
        P3 --> P4[Draft and Fillets Added]
    end
    subgraph Foundry[Foundry Floor]
        P4 --> F1[Mould Assembly Cope and Drag]
        F1 --> F2[Core Placement]
        F2 --> F3[Pouring Molten Metal]
        F3 --> F4[Solidification Chvorinov]
        F4 --> F5[Shakeout and Fettling]
    end
    subgraph MachineShop[Machine Shop]
        F5 --> M1[Final Machining]
        M1 --> M2[Inspection]
        M2 --> M3[Finished Component]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Cast Feature Geometry Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Casting Component] --> B1[Boss]
    A --> B2[Web]
    A --> B3[Rib]
    A --> B4[Core Formed Hole]
    A --> B5[Riser Feeder]
    B1 --> C1[Use fillet at root]
    B2 --> C2[Keep thickness less than 0.7 t]
    B3 --> C3[Stagger intersections]
    B4 --> C4[Minimum diameter 0.25 t]
    B5 --> C5[Modulus greater than 1.2 times casting modulus]
```

## 4.4 Description of Physical Geometry (for reference)

Since Mermaid cannot draw freeform mechanical sketches, the textual description of a properly designed sand casting is as follows:

**Cross-section of a properly designed flanged hub (cored hole with boss and rib):**

```
                ┌───── Risers (2, top) ─────┐
                │                            │
         ┌──────┴────────────────────────────┴──────┐
         │                                            │
         │   ╔═════════════╗         ╔═════════════╗  │   ← Cope (upper mould)
         │   ║   Riser     ║         ║   Riser     ║  │
         │   ║             ║         ║             ║  │
         │   ║┌─fillets─┐  ║         ║ ┌─fillets─┐ ║  │   ← Fillet radius Rf = 5 mm
         │   ║│         │  ║         ║ │         │ ║  │
         │   ╠═Parting═══╣         ╠═Parting═══╣     │   ← Parting line
         │   ║ │ CORE  │ ║          ║ │ CORE  │ ║    │
         │   ║ │ (sand)│ ║   Ribs   ║ │ (sand)│ ║    │   ← Boss + core + rib
         │   ║ │       │ ║  ╱   ╲   ║ │       │ ║    │
         │   ║ └───────┘ ║ ╱     ╲  ║ └───────┘ ║    │
         │   ╚═══════════╝╱       ╲╚═══════════╝     │   ← Drag (lower mould)
         └────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                └────── Draft (≈ 1.5° outward) ──────┘
```

> The four critical features visible in the diagram are: **(1) Draft** on the outer vertical wall, **(2) Fillets** at the boss-casting junction, **(3) Sand Cores** forming the central bore, and **(4) Top Risers** feeding the casting as it shrinks during solidification.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper 2)]**
Define the term **"draft"** in casting. Why is a greater draft angle recommended on internal surfaces than on external surfaces?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Draft** is the slight taper provided on the vertical surfaces of a pattern to facilitate its easy withdrawal from the sand mould without damaging the mould cavity. Typical values are **1° – 2° on external surfaces** and **2° – 3° on internal surfaces** for sand casting.
>
> A **greater draft is recommended on internal surfaces** because when the pattern is withdrawn, the internal sand surface is **scraped and dragged outward** by the pattern's sidewall. This sliding contact creates more wear on the internal mould face than on the external face, so additional taper is required to compensate. Additionally, internal cavities are **harder to repair** than external ones, so a small erosion there is more critical.
>
> *(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the typical values, 1 mark for the "why" with the scraping-drag explanation.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is **shrinkage allowance** in pattern making? List the shrinkage allowance values for **grey cast iron, steel, and aluminium**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Shrinkage allowance** is the extra dimensional compensation provided in the pattern to account for the **contraction of the metal** as it cools from its solidification temperature to room temperature. The pattern dimension $D_p$ is made larger than the casting dimension $D_c$ by the relation:
>
> $$D_p = D_c (1 + S)$$
>
> where $S$ is the fractional shrinkage allowance.
>
> Standard values:
>
> | Material | Shrinkage Allowance $S$ |
> |---|---|
> | **Grey cast iron** | 0.008 – 0.012 (≈ 1 %) |
> | **Steel** | 0.015 – 0.020 (≈ 2 %) |
> | **Aluminium alloys** | 0.012 – 0.015 (≈ 1.5 %) |
>
> *(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for definition with formula, 1 mark for grey CI and steel values, 1 mark for aluminium value. The formula is essential.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper based on KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern]**
**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **general design considerations** that a designer must follow to obtain a **defect-free sand casting**. Discuss at least **six** key considerations. (7 marks)

**(b)** A cylindrical steel casting of **150 mm diameter and 300 mm length** is to be produced in sand mould. A **top riser** of diameter 120 mm and height 150 mm is proposed. Given that the mould constant $B = 12.5$ s/cm² for sand-cast steel, **verify using Chvorinov's rule** whether this riser will be effective. Use a design safety factor of 1.2. (7 marks)

### Model Answer for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The designer must follow these key considerations:

1. **Parting Line Selection:** Place the parting line at the **largest cross-section** of the part to avoid undercuts and minimise pattern cost.

2. **Draft Angle:** Provide **1° – 2°** external and **2° – 3°** internal draft to enable pattern withdrawal without mould damage.

3. **Fillet and Chamfer:** Replace sharp internal corners with fillets of radius $R_f = 0.15\,t$ to $0.25\,t$, and sharp external corners with chamfers of 1 – 3 mm, to eliminate stress concentrations and promote directional solidification.

4. **Wall Thickness Uniformity:** Maintain **uniform wall thickness** wherever possible. If varying thickness is unavoidable, provide a **gradual transition** (1:3 to 1:5 taper) to avoid shrinkage cavities and residual stresses.

5. **Ribs and Webs:** Use ribs to provide stiffness without increasing wall thickness. Ensure **rib thickness $t_r \le 0.7\,t$** and **height-to-thickness ratio $\le 3$** to avoid hot spots.

6. **Cored Holes:** Holes of diameter **$d \ge 0.25\,t$ and $\ge 6$ mm** should be formed by sand cores. Smaller holes should be cast solid and machined through.

7. **Shrinkage Allowance:** Enlarge pattern dimensions by the appropriate shrinkage factor (e.g., 1 % for grey CI, 2 % for steel).

8. **Machining Allowance:** Add **1.5 – 6 mm** extra on surfaces to be subsequently machined.

9. **Boss Design:** Boss diameter $\ge 1.5 \times$ bolt diameter, boss height $\le 2 \times$ boss diameter, with a fillet at the root.

10. **Avoid Undercuts:** Re-design to eliminate undercuts; use loose pieces or cores only as a last resort.

*(Allocate approximately 0.7 marks per consideration for a total of 7 marks. Sketches are essential for full marks.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks: Verification using Chvorinov's Rule**

**Step 1: Convert dimensions to centimetres.**
- Casting: $D_c = 15$ cm, $L_c = 30$ cm
- Riser: $D_r = 12$ cm, $H_r = 15$ cm
- Mould constant: $B = 12.5$ s/cm²
- Safety factor: $k = 1.2$

**Step 2: Calculate casting modulus $M_c$.**

> The casting is a cylinder, so $M_{cyl} = D/4 = 15/4 = 3.75$ cm.
>
> (Note: For a long cylinder where $L \gg D$, the end surfaces are negligible, so $M \approx D/4$.)

$$
M_c = 3.75 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate riser modulus $M_r$.**

> For the riser, we treat it as a cylinder with **all** surfaces losing heat (top to atmosphere, bottom to mould, sides to mould):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_r &= \pi \left(\frac{12}{2}\right)^{2} \times 15 = \pi \times 36 \times 15 = 540 \pi \text{ cm}^{3} \\
A_r &= \pi \times 12 \times 15 + 2 \times \pi \times 6^{2} = 180\pi + 72\pi = 252\pi \text{ cm}^{2} \\
M_r &= \frac{V_r}{A_r} = \frac{540 \pi}{252 \pi} = 2.143 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compare with the design criterion.**

> For effective feeding, the riser modulus must be at least 1.2 times the casting modulus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.2 \times M_c &= 1.2 \times 3.75 = 4.50 \text{ cm} \\
M_r &= 2.143 \text{ cm} \\
M_r &< 1.2 \times M_c \quad \text{✗ NOT ADEQUATE}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The proposed riser is **NOT adequate** to feed the casting. A larger riser is required.

**Step 5: Recommend a suitable riser diameter.**

> For $M_r \ge 4.5$ cm with $H_r/D_r$ ratio kept equal to 1.25 (same shape), and noting that for a cylinder with $H/D = k$, the modulus is:
>
> $$M = \frac{D}{4} \cdot \frac{k}{k+0.5}$$
>
> For $k = 1.25$:
>
> $$M = \frac{D}{4} \cdot \frac{1.25}{1.75} = 0.1786 \, D$$
>
> Setting $M = 4.5$ cm:
>
> $$D = \frac{4.5}{0.1786} \approx 25.2 \text{ cm}, \quad H = 1.25 \times 25.2 \approx 31.5 \text{ cm}$$
>
> Therefore, a riser of **diameter ≈ 252 mm and height ≈ 315 mm** would be required — i.e., a riser substantially larger than the casting itself, which is uneconomical.
>
> **Conclusion:** The original riser of 120 mm × 150 mm is **inadequate** by a factor of approximately 2.1. Either use a **side riser (with insulating sleeve)** to reduce heat loss from the top, or use **chills** to accelerate solidification of the casting and reduce the required riser size.

*(Allocate 1 mark for setting up the problem, 2 marks for casting modulus, 2 marks for riser modulus, 1 mark for the comparison, 1 mark for the final conclusion and recommendation.)*

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, alternate question for the same 14-mark slot]**
**(a)** What are **patterns**? Explain the different **types of patterns** used in sand casting with suitable sketches. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **CI flange casting** has an outer diameter of **300 mm** and is to be produced by sand casting. The shrinkage allowance for CI is **1 %** and the machining allowance is **3 mm** per surface.
   (i) Determine the **pattern outer diameter**.
   (ii) If the flange has 6 bolt holes of **20 mm diameter**, determine whether these holes should be cored or cast solid and drilled. Take the wall thickness as 25 mm. (7 marks)

### Model Answer for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

> A **pattern** is a replica of the final casting, slightly enlarged to account for **shrinkage and machining allowances**, used to produce the mould cavity in the sand.
>
> **Types of patterns:**
>
> 1. **Single piece (solid) pattern** — Used for small, simple castings. Cheapest but has parting line limitations.
> 2. **Two piece (split) pattern** — Most common; split along the parting line. Used for symmetrical castings.
> 3. **Multi-piece pattern** — Used for complex castings with multiple parting planes.
> 4. **Loose piece pattern** — For castings with undercuts; one part of the pattern is removed separately to release the undercut.
> 5. **Match plate pattern** — Two pattern halves mounted on opposite sides of a metal plate. Used for high-volume production.
> 6. **Follow board pattern** — Used for thin, fragile patterns that would distort under their own weight; the board accompanies the pattern during moulding.
> 7. **Sweep pattern** — A wooden board (sweep) rotated about a fixed axis to generate a mould surface of revolution.
> 8. **Skeleton pattern** — A wooden frame that forms the outline of the mould; the sand is shaped manually between the frame members. Used for large castings.
>
> *(Allocate marks as: 1 mark for the definition, then ~0.7 mark per pattern type, with 1 extra mark for a labelled sketch of the most common type — the split pattern.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Pattern outer diameter:**

> Given: $D_c = 300$ mm, $S = 0.01$ (CI), $M = 3$ mm per surface.
>
> Total enlargement per side = shrinkage + machining = 1 % × 300 + 3 mm (applied to diameter) = 3 + 3 = 6 mm.
>
> Wait — strictly, the shrinkage allowance is a fractional amount, and the machining allowance is an absolute amount per side. For diameter:
>
> $$D_p = D_c (1 + S) + 2 M$$
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> D_p &= 300 (1 + 0.01) + 2 \times 3 \\
>     &= 303 + 6 \\
>     &= 309 \text{ mm}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Pattern outer diameter = 309 mm.**

**(ii) Decision on cored holes:**

> Minimum core diameter rule: $d_{min} \ge 0.25\,t$ and $d_{min} \ge 6$ mm.
>
> Wall thickness: $t = 25$ mm.
>
> $$0.25 \times 25 = 6.25 \text{ mm} \quad \text{and} \quad 6 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_{min} = 6.25 \text{ mm}$$
>
> Proposed hole diameter: 20 mm.
>
> Since $20 \text{ mm} > 6.25 \text{ mm}$ and $> 6$ mm, the holes **can be economically cored** in the sand mould.
>
> **Recommendation: Use sand cores** to form the six 20 mm bolt holes. (1 mark for rule statement, 1 mark for calculation, 1 mark for decision, 1 mark for the formula/reasoning behind casting solid as alternative.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost KTU students marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing draft and taper units:** Draft is expressed in **degrees**, not millimetres. Writing "draft = 3 mm" is incorrect — it should be "draft = 3°".
>
> 2. **Wrong shrinkage formula application:** Students often write $D_c = D_p (1 + S)$ instead of $D_p = D_c (1 + S)$. **The pattern is bigger, not smaller.**
>
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply machining allowance by 2 for diameters:** When applying machining allowance to a diameter, remember to add it on **both sides**, i.e., $2M$ for diameters, $M$ for lengths.
>
> 4. **Neglecting the 6 mm absolute minimum for core diameter:** A common trap in the question bank is to give a wall thickness of 20 mm, which gives $0.25 \times 20 = 5$ mm, but the **absolute minimum is 6 mm**. The cored-hole rule is the **maximum of the two** criteria.
>
> 5. **Mixing up the riser modulus safety factor:** The factor is $M_r \ge 1.2 \, M_c$, **not** $M_r \le 1.2\,M_c$. Students frequently invert the inequality.
>
> 6. **Not sketching in 14-mark answers:** KTU 2024 Scheme ESE explicitly awards 2 – 3 marks for "neat sketches" in Part B. **Always include a labelled diagram** of the casting with the design features annotated.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — General Design Considerations for Casting**
>
> - ☐ **Parting line** = at the **largest cross-section** to avoid undercuts.
> - ☐ **Draft** = 1° – 2° external, 2° – 3° internal (sand casting). Always **more on internal** surfaces.
> - ☐ **Fillet radius** $R_f = 0.15\,t$ to $0.25\,t$ at all internal corners; promotes **directional solidification** and reduces stress concentration.
> - ☐ **Chamfer** = 1 – 3 mm at all external sharp corners (avoids handling injury and stress).
> - ☐ **Wall thickness** = as **uniform** as possible; transition ratio **1:3 to 1:5** when change is unavoidable.
> - ☐ **Rib thickness** $\le 0.7\,t$; **rib height / rib thickness** $\le 3$; **stagger intersections**.
> - ☐ **Cored hole minimum diameter** $d \ge 0.25\,t$ **and** $d \ge 6$ mm. Otherwise, cast solid and drill.
> - ☐ **Boss diameter** $\ge 1.5 \times$ bolt diameter; **boss height** $\le 2 \times$ boss diameter; add **fillet at root**.
> - ☐ **Avoid undercuts**; if unavoidable, use loose piece or core.
> - ☐ **Shrinkage allowance** values to memorise: **CI = 1 %, Steel = 2 %, Aluminium = 1.5 %, Brass = 1.5 %**.
> - ☐ **Pattern dimension** $D_p = D_c (1 + S) + 2M$ (for diameters, including machining).
> - ☐ **Machining allowance** = **1.5 – 6 mm** per surface (depends on size and process).
> - ☐ **Chvorinov's rule** $t_s = B (V/A)^2$; **riser modulus** $M_r \ge 1.2 \, M_c$.
> - ☐ **Cylinder modulus** $M = D/4$; **rectangular bar** $M = ab / [2(a+b)]$; **sphere** $M = D/6$.
> - ☐ **Distortion allowance** = 0.5 – 2 % extra for castings longer than **600 mm** (e.g., long bed plates, lathe beds).
> - ☐ **Riser placement** = on the **thickest section** (or last-to-solidify region) of the casting, with a **riser neck** having section modulus $1.05 - 1.15$ times the casting section modulus at the neck.
> - ☐ **Top riser** = effective modulus reduced by top heat loss; **side riser** is generally more efficient.
> - ☐ **Insulating sleeve** on the riser top can reduce required riser size by 20 – 30 %.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Casting Tolerances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Casting Tolerances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Casting Tolerance** is the total permissible variation in the dimensions of a cast component, expressed as the permissible deviation from the specified nominal dimension. It accounts for the inevitable inaccuracies arising during the sand casting, die casting, investment casting, or permanent mold casting process. Casting tolerance is governed primarily by the international standard **ISO 8062 (formerly ISO 8062-1:1994 / ISO 8062-3:2007)**, which establishes the **DCTG (Dimensional Casting Tolerance Grade)** system.

In DFMA philosophy, casting tolerance is a critical design parameter because:
- Excessively tight tolerances **increase cost exponentially**.
- Excessively loose tolerances **compromise functional fit and assembly**.
- The right tolerance is the **economic optimum** between machining cost and casting accuracy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Wooden Furniture Builder**
> Imagine you order a custom wooden bookshelf. The carpenter cannot cut every plank to **exactly 1000 mm** because wood grain, saw vibration, and humidity cause small variations. The carpenter will tell you: *"It will be 1000 mm, give or take 2 mm."* That **±2 mm** is the **tolerance** — it is the practical concession to the imperfections of the process.
>
> Similarly, in casting, molten metal solidifies, shrinks unevenly, and is shaped by a deformable sand mold. The casting will never be *exactly* the drawing dimension. Casting tolerance quantifies how much deviation is acceptable. The DCTG system is the carpenter's ruler — a standardized way of saying *"how good can the foundry be?"*

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Nominal Dimension** | The theoretical dimension shown on the engineering drawing. |
| **Actual Dimension** | The dimension measured on the produced casting. |
| **Tolerance** | The permissible difference between upper and lower limits. |
| **Upper Limit** | Nominal + (Tolerance/2). |
| **Lower Limit** | Nominal − (Tolerance/2). |
| **DCTG** | Dimensional Casting Tolerance Grade (ISO 8062). |
| **Allowance** | An intentional deviation (e.g., machining, draft, shrinkage allowance). |

### 1.4 Types of Casting Tolerances

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Dimensional Tolerance** — Permissible variation in linear dimensions (length, width, thickness, diameter).
> 2. **Geometric Tolerance** — Permissible variation in form, orientation, location, and run-out (governed by ISO 1101, applied via GD&T symbols).
> 3. **Mass Tolerance** — Permissible variation in the cast component's mass (used for weight-critical parts, e.g., aerospace counterweights).
> 4. **Wall Thickness Tolerance** — Critical in casting because uneven walls cause shrinkage cavities.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The ISO 8062 DCTG System — Conceptual Breakdown

The ISO 8062 standard defines **16 tolerance grades**, named **DCTG 1 (tightest) to DCTG 16 (loosest)**. As the grade number increases, the tolerance value increases (i.e., the casting is less precise).

**How it works — Step by step:**

1. **Identify the casting process** (Sand, Die, Investment, Shell, Permanent Mold).
2. **Select the appropriate DCTG grade** based on the process capability.
3. **Determine the nominal dimension** of the feature in question.
4. **Look up the tolerance** from the ISO 8062 table corresponding to the (grade, nominal length) pair.
5. **Apply ± tolerance** to the drawing dimension.

**General principle:**
- Sand casting → DCTG 8 to DCTG 12 (looser).
- Die casting → DCTG 4 to DCTG 7 (tighter).
- Investment casting → DCTG 3 to DCTG 6 (very tight).
- Shell molding / Permanent mold → DCTG 5 to DCTG 8 (medium).

### 2.2 Casting Process — Typical Achievable DCTG

| Process | Typical DCTG | Relative Cost |
|---|---|---|
| Sand Casting (Green) | 10–12 | Low |
| Sand Casting (Resin-bonded) | 8–10 | Medium |
| Shell Molding | 5–7 | Medium-High |
| Permanent Mold | 5–7 | Medium-High |
| Die Casting (High Pressure) | 4–6 | High |
| Investment Casting | 3–5 | Very High |
| Centrifugal Casting | 7–9 | Medium |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Always use these formulas as your first line of attack in any KTU numerical.**

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = T_{upper} - T_{lower}$ | Total tolerance band | mm |
| 2 | $\Delta T = T_{grade} \times L_{nominal}$ | Linear tolerance (from ISO 8062 chart) | mm |
| 3 | $L_{final} = L_{nominal} \pm \frac{T}{2}$ | Final allowable dimension | mm |
| 4 | $S_a = S_p \times L_{nominal}$ | **Shrinkage allowance** = Shrinkage % × Length | mm |
| 5 | $D_a = L_{nominal} \times \tan(\theta)$ | **Draft allowance** (linear draft) | mm |
| 6 | $M_a = M_{table} \times f$ | **Machining allowance** (from process table) | mm |
| 7 | $T_{stack} = \sqrt{T_1^2 + T_2^2 + \dots + T_n^2}$ | **Statistical tolerance stack-up** (RSS) | mm |
| 8 | $T_{stack(worst)} = T_1 + T_2 + \dots + T_n$ | **Arithmetic (worst-case) stack-up** | mm |
| 9 | $C \propto \frac{1}{T^n}$ | **Cost increases** as tolerance tightens (n ≈ 1.5 to 3) | Relative |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Rule:** Always use $\vert$ (vertical bar) or `\mid` in LaTeX tables — never the pipe character `|` — to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive**: Engine blocks use DCTG 9–10 (sand casting) with localized DCTG 5–6 on bearing bores (machined later).
- **Aerospace**: Turbine blades use investment casting at DCTG 3–4 with near-net-shape features.
- **Pumps & Valves**: Body castings at DCTG 8–10 to balance cost and sealing performance.
- **Cost Implication**: Tightening tolerance from DCTG 10 to DCTG 6 typically **doubles the casting cost**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Casting Tolerance from DCTG Table

> **Problem:** A sand casting has a nominal length of 250 mm. The foundry works to DCTG 10. Find the upper and lower limits.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$
\text{Nominal Length, } L = 250 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{DCTG Grade} = 10
$$

**Step 2 — Reference the ISO 8062 table for DCTG 10, nominal 250 mm.**

For DCTG 10, the tolerance is read from the chart as:

$$
T = 5.6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute upper and lower limits.**

$$
L_{upper} = L + \frac{T}{2} = 250 + \frac{5.6}{2} = 250 + 2.8 = 252.8 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_{lower} = L - \frac{T}{2} = 250 - \frac{5.6}{2} = 250 - 2.8 = 247.2 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Answer.**

$$
\boxed{L_{acceptable} = 252.8 \text{ mm (max)}, \quad 247.2 \text{ mm (min)}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shrinkage Allowance

> **Problem:** A steel casting pattern is to be made for a part whose final dimension is 400 mm. The shrinkage rate for steel is 2%. Compute the pattern dimension.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**

$$
L_{final} = 400 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
S_p = 2\% = 0.02
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the shrinkage allowance $S_a$.**

$$
S_a = S_p \times L_{final} = 0.02 \times 400 = 8 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute pattern dimension $L_p$ (larger than final, since metal shrinks).**

$$
L_p = L_{final} + S_a = 400 + 8 = 408 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Answer.**

$$
\boxed{L_p = 408 \text{ mm}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why add?** Because molten steel contracts upon cooling, the casting shrinks. So the pattern must be **oversized** to compensate.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Draft Allowance

> **Problem:** A vertical face of height 80 mm on a wooden pattern requires a draft angle of 1.5°. Compute the draft allowance per side.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**

$$
H = 80 \text{ mm}, \quad \theta = 1.5°
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the draft formula.**

$$
D_a = H \times \tan(\theta)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$
D_a = 80 \times \tan(1.5°)
$$

$$
\tan(1.5°) = 0.02619
$$

$$
D_a = 80 \times 0.02619 = 2.095 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Answer.**

$$
\boxed{D_a \approx 2.10 \text{ mm per side}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Statistical Tolerance Stack-Up

> **Problem:** Three cast features stack in series with tolerances: $T_1 = 0.4$ mm, $T_2 = 0.6$ mm, $T_3 = 0.2$ mm. Compute the RSS and worst-case stack-up.

**Step 1 — RSS Method (statistical, assumes independent tolerances).**

$$
T_{RSS} = \sqrt{T_1^2 + T_2^2 + T_3^2}
$$

$$
T_{RSS} = \sqrt{0.4^2 + 0.6^2 + 0.2^2}
$$

$$
T_{RSS} = \sqrt{0.16 + 0.36 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.56}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{RSS} \approx 0.748 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 2 — Worst-Case (Arithmetic) Method.**

$$
T_{worst} = T_1 + T_2 + T_3
$$

$$
T_{worst} = 0.4 + 0.6 + 0.2
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{worst} = 1.2 \text{ mm}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick:** When the question says "if dimensions vary independently" → use RSS. When it says "worst possible condition" → use arithmetic sum.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Tolerance Selection Logic

| Process | Min Wall (mm) | Min Draft (°) | Typical DCTG | Cost Index |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Green Sand | 4–6 | 1.0–2.0 | 10–12 | 1.0× |
| Resin Sand | 3–5 | 0.5–1.5 | 8–10 | 1.5× |
| Shell Molding | 2–3 | 0.5–1.0 | 5–7 | 2.0× |
| Investment | 0.5–1.5 | 0–0.5 | 3–5 | 5.0× |
| Die Casting | 0.8–2.0 | 0.5–1.0 | 4–6 | 3.0× |
| Permanent Mold | 3–4 | 1.0–2.0 | 5–7 | 2.5× |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Casting Tolerance Selection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Part Function] --> B{Load Bearing or Sealing?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Tight Tolerance DCTG 4-6]
    B -- No --> D[Standard Tolerance DCTG 8-10]
    C --> E{Process Selected?}
    D --> E
    E -- Investment --> F[DCTG 3-5]
    E -- Die Cast --> G[DCTG 4-6]
    E -- Sand Cast --> H[DCTG 8-12]
    E -- Permanent Mold --> I[DCTG 5-7]
    F --> J[Apply ISO 8062 Lookup]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Calculate Final Limits]
    K --> L[Apply GD&T Symbols]
    L --> M[Document on Drawing]
    M --> N[End]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Allowance Hierarchy in Casting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pattern Dimension] --> B[Add Draft Allowance]
    B --> C[Add Shrinkage Allowance]
    C --> D[Add Machining Allowance]
    D --> E[Final Casting Dimension]
    E --> F[Apply Tolerance Band]
    F --> G[Acceptable Range]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Cost vs Tolerance Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tolerance Required] --> B{Process Capable?}
    B -- No, Need Tighter --> C[Switch to Better Process]
    C --> D[Investment Casting]
    D --> E[Cost Increases 5x]
    B -- Yes --> F[Use Standard Process]
    F --> G[Cost Baseline 1x]
    E --> H[Evaluate Functional Need]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Justified?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Proceed with Tight Tolerance]
    I -- No --> K[Relax to Economic Tolerance]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Tolerance Stack-Up Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feature 1 +/- 0.4mm] --> B[Feature 2 +/- 0.6mm]
    B --> C[Feature 3 +/- 0.2mm]
    C --> D[Total Assembly Gap]
    D --> E{RSS Method?}
    E -- Yes --> F[+/- 0.748mm]
    E -- No --> G[Worst Case +/- 1.2mm]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define casting tolerance. What is DCTG?**
> **Model Answer:** Casting tolerance is the permissible variation in a cast part's dimensions from the specified nominal value, accounting for process limitations. DCTG stands for **Dimensional Casting Tolerance Grade**, defined by ISO 8062. It is a system of 16 grades (DCTG 1 to DCTG 16), where lower numbers indicate tighter (more precise) castings and higher numbers indicate looser (less precise) castings. Each grade prescribes tolerance values as a function of nominal dimension.

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **List the four main types of allowances in casting design.**
> **Model Answer:** The four main allowances are: (i) **Shrinkage allowance** — compensates for metal contraction on cooling, (ii) **Draft allowance** — taper on vertical faces to allow pattern withdrawal, (iii) **Machining allowance** — extra material left for subsequent machining, and (iv) **Distortion/shift allowance** — compensates for core shift or mold deformation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer either Question A or Question B.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the ISO 8062 tolerance system for castings. Discuss how the DCTG grade is selected based on the casting process, with examples for sand casting, die casting, and investment casting.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A steel casting has a nominal length of 600 mm and is produced by green sand molding. Compute: (i) the pattern dimension assuming 2% shrinkage, (ii) the draft allowance for a 150 mm tall vertical face with 1.5° draft, and (iii) the upper and lower limits if the foundry works to DCTG 11.

> **Model Solution (a):**
>
> The ISO 8062 standard provides a unified system for specifying dimensional tolerances in cast components. It defines **16 grades (DCTG 1 to 16)**, where DCTG 1 is the tightest and DCTG 16 is the loosest. The tolerance value depends on both the grade and the nominal dimension of the feature.
>
> **Process-Based Grade Selection:**
>
> - **Sand Casting (Green Sand):** DCTG 10–12. Sand molds are deformable and have low dimensional control. Typical for large ferrous castings (machine beds, engine blocks).
> - **Die Casting:** DCTG 4–6. Steel dies under high pressure yield good accuracy. Typical for automotive and consumer parts.
> - **Investment Casting:** DCTG 3–5. Wax patterns and ceramic shells give excellent surface finish and dimensional control. Used for aerospace and medical implants.
>
> The selection principle is to use the **loosest (highest DCTG number) tolerance that still meets functional requirements** to minimize cost.

> **Model Solution (b):**
>
> **Given:** $L = 600$ mm, $S_p = 2\% = 0.02$, $H = 150$ mm, $\theta = 1.5°$, DCTG 11.
>
> **(i) Pattern dimension (with shrinkage):**
>
> $$
> S_a = 0.02 \times 600 = 12 \text{ mm}
> $$
>
> $$
> L_p = 600 + 12 = 612 \text{ mm}
> $$
>
> **(ii) Draft allowance:**
>
> $$
> D_a = 150 \times \tan(1.5°) = 150 \times 0.02619 = 3.93 \text{ mm}
> $$
>
> **(iii) Limits at DCTG 11 for 600 mm:**
> From ISO 8062 chart, $T \approx 8.0$ mm for DCTG 11 at 600 mm.
>
> $$
> L_{upper} = 600 + 4.0 = 604.0 \text{ mm}
> $$
>
> $$
> L_{lower} = 600 - 4.0 = 596.0 \text{ mm}
> $$
>
> **Final Answers:** Pattern = 612 mm; Draft = 3.93 mm; Limits = 596.0 mm to 604.0 mm.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the concept of tolerance stack-up in cast assemblies. Compare the arithmetic (worst-case) and statistical (RSS) methods with a suitable example.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A cast bracket has three features in series with tolerances $\pm 0.3$ mm, $\pm 0.5$ mm, and $\pm 0.4$ mm respectively. Calculate: (i) the worst-case stack-up and (ii) the RSS stack-up. Comment on which method is more realistic and why.

> **Model Solution (a):**
>
> **Tolerance stack-up** is the accumulation of individual part tolerances when features are assembled in series. In cast assemblies, gaps accumulate across multiple cast features, and the total tolerance determines whether the assembly will fit.
>
> **Arithmetic (Worst-Case) Method:**
>
> $$
> T_{worst} = T_1 + T_2 + \dots + T_n
> $$
> This method assumes all dimensions deviate to their extreme limits simultaneously. It is **pessimistic but guaranteed safe** — the assembly will always fit. Used in safety-critical applications (aerospace, defense).
>
> **Statistical (RSS) Method:**
>
> $$
> T_{RSS} = \sqrt{T_1^2 + T_2^2 + \dots + T_n^2}
> $$
> This assumes dimensions vary **independently and normally** around the mean. It is more realistic for mass production and gives a smaller, more economical tolerance band. Used in consumer goods and automotive.

> **Model Solution (b):**
>
> **Given:** $T_1 = 0.3$ mm, $T_2 = 0.5$ mm, $T_3 = 0.4$ mm.
>
> **(i) Worst-Case Stack-Up:**
>
> $$
> T_{worst} = 0.3 + 0.5 + 0.4 = 1.2 \text{ mm}
> $$
>
> **(ii) RSS Stack-Up:**
>
> $$
> T_{RSS} = \sqrt{0.3^2 + 0.5^2 + 0.4^2}
> $$
>
> $$
> T_{RSS} = \sqrt{0.09 + 0.25 + 0.16} = \sqrt{0.50}
> $$
>
> $$
> T_{RSS} = 0.707 \text{ mm}
> $$
>
> **Comment:** The RSS method (0.707 mm) is more realistic because in practice not all three features deviate to their extreme limits at the same time. The worst-case method (1.2 mm) overestimates the gap and leads to over-constrained, costly designs. RSS is preferred for non-safety-critical, high-volume production.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Do **not** use RSS for safety-critical parts (e.g., brake calipers, pressure vessels). Examiners specifically deduct marks if you use RSS for aerospace/defense assemblies.
> - Always **state the assumption** (independent tolerances, normal distribution) before using RSS.
> - For DCTG look-up questions, **mention the standard** (ISO 8062) explicitly. Marks are reserved for standard citation.
> - In shrinkage problems, remember the pattern is **larger** than the final part — students often subtract instead of adding.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Casting Tolerances**

- **DCTG = Dimensional Casting Tolerance Grade** (ISO 8062), 16 grades from 1 (tightest) to 16 (loosest).
- **Sand casting → DCTG 10–12; Die casting → DCTG 4–6; Investment → DCTG 3–5.**
- **Shrinkage allowance** $S_a = S_p \times L$ — pattern is **always larger** than the final part.
- **Draft allowance** $D_a = H \tan(\theta)$ — required on vertical faces for pattern withdrawal.
- **Machining allowance** — extra material left on surfaces requiring post-casting machining.
- **Tolerance stack-up:**
  - **Arithmetic (worst-case):** $T = T_1 + T_2 + \dots + T_n$ — safe, pessimistic.
  - **RSS (statistical):** $T = \sqrt{T_1^2 + T_2^2 + \dots + T_n^2}$ — realistic for mass production.
- **Cost vs Tolerance:** Cost $\propto \frac{1}{T^n}$ — tightening tolerance increases cost **exponentially**.
- **Always cite ISO 8062** when answering tolerance-based questions.
- **GD&T symbols** (per ISO 1101) supplement DCTG for geometric tolerances (flatness, cylindricity, position).
- **Typical draft angles:** Sand 1°–2°, Die 0.5°–1°, Investment 0°–0.5°.
- **Wall thickness variation** is a major tolerance concern — uneven walls cause shrinkage cavities.
- **Mass tolerance** is critical for aerospace counterweights and rotating machinery.
- **KTU favorite topics:** DCTG grade selection, shrinkage/draft calculations, tolerance stack-up comparison.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Solidification Simulation in Casting Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Use of Solidification Simulation in Casting Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solidification Simulation** is a numerical, computer-aided engineering (CAE) technique used in casting design to mathematically model and predict the physical behavior of molten metal as it transitions from a liquid to a solid state within a mold cavity. It employs **Finite Element Method (FEM)** or **Finite Difference Method (FDM)** to solve coupled governing equations governing heat transfer, fluid flow, and solidification mechanics. The objective is to predict **casting defects**, optimize **gating and riser design**, and ensure **sound casting metallurgy** before physical tooling is manufactured.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, it falls under the umbrella of **Concurrent Engineering (CE)** and **Design for Manufacturing (DFM)**, specifically aiding in **Casting Process Simulation** to achieve first-time-right castings.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Solidification simulation is positioned as a critical **DFM (Design for Manufacturing)** enabler. It directly supports the course outcome of designing cast components that are simultaneously functional, manufacturable, and defect-free.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** *"Simulate first, cast later."* Solidification simulation allows a designer to virtually test thousands of design iterations on a computer, drastically reducing the **lead time**, **scrap rate**, and **tooling cost** associated with traditional trial-and-error casting development.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Real-World Analogy"

Imagine you are a baker trying to bake a giant, multi-layered wedding cake. If you simply pour the batter into the pan and shove it into the oven, you will inevitably get a **raw center, burnt edges, and large air bubbles** trapped inside. To avoid this disaster, you would:

1. **Mentally visualize** how heat will travel through the cake (where will it cool first, where will it stay hot longest?).
2. **Predict** where the cake will shrink and pull away from the pan (and thus need a "reservoir" of extra batter to feed that shrinkage).
3. **Identify** where air pockets will form (and design a "vent" to let them escape).

**Solidification simulation does EXACTLY this for castings.** The molten metal is the cake batter, the mold is the pan, and the computer acts as the master baker's brain. It visualizes **thermal gradients**, predicts **shrinkage cavities**, and locates **porosity defects** so that the casting designer can position **risers (feeders)** and **gates** at the most strategic locations.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are used in standard solidification simulation software packages:

- **Latent Heat of Fusion ($L$):** Typically **$\mathbf{200 \text{ to } 400 \text{ kJ/kg}}$** for most industrial alloys (e.g., Aluminum $\approx$ 397 kJ/kg, Cast Iron $\approx$ 260 kJ/kg).
- **Thermal Conductivity ($k$):** Range **$\mathbf{10 \text{ to } 400 \text{ W/(m}\cdot\text{K)}$** depending on alloy and temperature.
- **Specific Heat Capacity ($C_p$):** Typically **$\mathbf{400 \text{ to } 1000 \text{ J/(kg}\cdot\text{K)}$**.
- **Solidus ($T_s$) and Liquidus ($T_L$) Temperatures:** Critical control points defining the **mushy zone**.
- **Mushy Zone:** The semi-solid region between $T_s$ and $T_L$ where feeding is most critical.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Three Coupled Physics

Solidification is governed by three simultaneously interacting physical phenomena. A robust simulation must solve all three:

### 2.1.1 Heat Transfer (Energy Equation)
Heat flows from the superheated molten metal, through the solidified shell, into the mold, and finally into the surrounding environment. The **transient, 3D heat conduction equation** forms the backbone of the simulation.

### 2.1.2 Fluid Flow (Momentum & Continuity Equations)
Molten metal is not static. It flows due to **thermal buoyancy** (natural convection), **pouring momentum**, and **solidification shrinkage**. The simulation solves the **Navier-Stokes equations** coupled with the continuity equation.

### 2.1.3 Solidification Mechanics (Latent Heat Release)
As metal solidifies, it releases **latent heat**. The simulation models this using techniques such as the **enthalpy method** or **apparent heat capacity method**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic

The simulation process follows a strict sequential logic:

1. **Geometry Discretization:** The CAD model of the casting + mold + risers is imported and divided into millions of small finite elements/control volumes (the **mesh**).
2. **Material Property Assignment:** Thermophysical properties ($k, C_p, L, \rho, \mu$) are assigned to each element as temperature-dependent functions.
3. **Initial & Boundary Conditions:** Pouring temperature, mold temperature, ambient temperature, and heat transfer coefficients (e.g., at the metal-mold interface) are defined.
4. **Solution Phase:** The solver iteratively advances in time ($\Delta t$), solving the energy, momentum, and continuity equations at each node.
5. **Post-Processing:** The results are visualized as color-mapped contours showing temperature fields, solid fraction, shrinkage porosity, and velocity vectors.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are the mathematical backbone of solidification simulation. **Memorize these for KTU exams.**

| # | Formula Name | Governing Equation | Key Terms & Units |
|---|--------------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Fourier's Heat Conduction** | $\displaystyle \rho C_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \dot{Q}$ | $\rho$: density ($\text{kg/m}^3$); $C_p$: specific heat ($\text{J/kg}\cdot\text{K}$); $T$: temperature ($\text{K}$); $k$: thermal conductivity ($\text{W/m}\cdot\text{K}$); $\dot{Q}$: volumetric heat source ($\text{W/m}^3$) |
| 2 | **Heat Conduction with Latent Heat (Enthalpy Form)** | $\displaystyle \frac{\partial H}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T)$ | $H$: volumetric enthalpy ($\text{J/m}^3$) = $\rho \left[ C_p(T - T_{\text{ref}}) + f_s L \right]$; $f_s$: solid fraction ($0 \le f_s \le 1$); $L$: latent heat of fusion ($\text{J/kg}$) |
| 3 | **Apparent Heat Capacity Method** | $\displaystyle \rho C_p^{*} \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T)$ | $C_p^{*}$: apparent heat capacity = $C_p + \dfrac{L}{T_L - T_s}$ ($\text{J/kg}\cdot\text{K}$) |
| 4 | **Scheil Solidification Model (Solute Redistribution)** | $\displaystyle f_s = 1 - \left( \frac{C_L}{C_0} \right)^{\frac{1}{k_p - 1}}$ | $C_L$: liquid composition; $C_0$: initial alloy composition; $k_p$: partition coefficient |
| 5 | **Navier-Stokes Momentum Equation** | $\displaystyle \rho \frac{D \vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{v} + \rho \vec{g} \beta (T - T_{\text{ref}})$ | $\vec{v}$: velocity vector ($\text{m/s}$); $p$: pressure ($\text{Pa}$); $\mu$: dynamic viscosity ($\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$); $\vec{g}$: gravity ($\text{m/s}^2$); $\beta$: thermal expansion coefficient ($\text{1/K}$) |
| 6 | **Continuity Equation (Incompressible Flow)** | $\displaystyle \nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$ | Represents conservation of mass for an incompressible fluid. |
| 7 | **Shrinkage Porosity Criterion (Niyama Criterion)** | $\displaystyle N_y = \frac{\vert \nabla T \vert}{\sqrt{G \cdot R_s}}$ | $\vert \nabla T \vert$: temperature gradient; $G$: local solidification time gradient; $R_s$: cooling rate. **A Niyama value below $\mathbf{0.3 \text{ to } 1.0}$** indicates a likely shrinkage defect. |
| 8 | **Chvorinov's Rule (Solidification Time)** | $\displaystyle t_s = B \left( \frac{V}{A} \right)^n$ | $t_s$: solidification time ($\text{s}$); $V$: volume ($\text{m}^3$); $A$: surface area ($\text{m}^2$); $B$: mold constant; $n \approx 2$. Used for riser design. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The **Niyama Criterion (Row 7)** is a **favourite 7-mark question**. Know what it predicts, the threshold values, and the physical meaning of each term.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Solidification simulation is industrially indispensable for:

- **Critical Safety Components:** Aero-engine turbine blades, nuclear reactor housings, automotive brake calipers, railway couplers.
- **Cost Reduction:** Eliminates physical trial castings, which can cost **₹50,000 to ₹5,00,000 per iteration** depending on size and alloy.
- **Lightweighting:** Enables the design of thin-walled, complex castings (e.g., EV battery housings, aerospace structural brackets) with confidence.
- **Sustainability:** Reduces scrap metal wastage and energy consumption by ensuring near-net-shape castings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Transient 3D Heat Conduction Equation (Enthalpy Form)

This is the **fundamental governing equation** solved at every node of the mesh during solidification simulation.

### Starting Point: Conservation of Energy

For a differential control volume of molten metal, the net rate of heat flowing in plus the rate of heat generated internally must equal the rate of heat accumulation.

**Step 1: Net Heat Inflow by Conduction**

By Fourier's Law, the conductive heat flux vector is $\vec{q} = -k \nabla T$. The net rate of heat entering the control volume is:

$$\text{Net Inflow} = -\nabla \cdot \vec{q} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T)$$

This is a **divergence operation**. The negative sign of $\vec{q}$ becomes positive upon applying the divergence, meaning heat flows from hot to cold regions.

**Step 2: Internal Heat Generation (Latent Heat Release)**

As the metal solidifies, it releases latent heat $L$ at a rate proportional to the rate of change of solid fraction $f_s$:

$$\dot{Q} = \rho L \frac{\partial f_s}{\partial t}$$

**Step 3: Rate of Energy Accumulation**

The rate at which thermal energy accumulates in the control volume is:

$$\text{Accumulation} = \rho C_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4: Equating the Three Terms**

Applying conservation of energy:

$$\rho C_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \rho L \frac{\partial f_s}{\partial t}$$

**Step 5: Reframing in the Enthalpy Form**

Define the **volumetric enthalpy** as $H = \rho [C_p T + f_s L]$. Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\frac{\partial H}{\partial t} = \rho \left[ C_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} + L \frac{\partial f_s}{\partial t} \right]$$

By direct comparison with Step 4, the equation elegantly reduces to:

$$\frac{\partial H}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T)$$

This is the **enthalpy form of the heat equation** — compact, robust, and handles latent heat release without explicitly tracking $f_s$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Apparent Heat Capacity Method

This is an **alternative to the enthalpy method**, commonly used because it is easier to implement in standard FEM solvers.

**Step 1: Approximate the Mushy Zone**

In the temperature range $T_s \le T \le T_L$, the solid fraction is assumed to vary linearly:

$$f_s(T) = \frac{T_L - T}{T_L - T_s}$$

**Step 2: Differentiate the Solid Fraction**

$$\frac{\partial f_s}{\partial t} = -\frac{1}{T_L - T_s} \cdot \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the Energy Equation**

Substituting the above into $\rho C_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \rho L \frac{\partial f_s}{\partial t}$:

$$\rho C_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) - \frac{\rho L}{T_L - T_s} \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4: Group the Temperature Derivative Terms**

$$\rho \left[ C_p + \frac{L}{T_L - T_s} \right] \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T)$$

**Step 5: Define the Apparent Heat Capacity**

Let $C_p^{*} = C_p + \dfrac{L}{T_L - T_s}$. The final equation becomes:

$$\rho C_p^{*} \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T)$$

This has the same mathematical form as a standard heat equation but uses an **artificially inflated heat capacity** to absorb the latent heat effect over the mushy zone.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Niyama Criterion (Shrinkage Porosity Predictor)

This criterion is used to predict where shrinkage porosity will form.

**Step 1: Define Local Solidification Time**

The local solidification time at a point is the time taken for the temperature to drop from $T_L$ to $T_s$:

$$t_{LS} = \frac{T_L - T_s}{\vert \dot{T} \vert}$$

where $\vert \dot{T} \vert$ is the local cooling rate.

**Step 2: Define the Temperature Gradient**

The temperature gradient just before solidification completion is $\vert \nabla T \vert$.

**Step 3: Formulate the Niyama Number**

E. Niyama et al. (1982) empirically correlated shrinkage porosity to the ratio:

$$N_y = \frac{\vert \nabla T \vert}{\sqrt{\dot{T}}} \cdot k_{\text{const}}$$

A more general and widely used form is:

$$N_y = \frac{\vert \nabla T \vert}{\sqrt{G \cdot R_s}}$$

where $G$ is the gradient of local solidification time and $R_s$ is the cooling rate.

**Step 4: Interpret the Threshold**

- If $N_y \ge$ critical value (typically $\mathbf{0.3 \text{ to } 1.0}$ depending on alloy): **Sound casting**, sufficient feeding.
- If $N_y <$ critical value: **Shrinkage porosity** likely to form (the metal freezes before liquid feed metal can reach the location).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Intuition for Niyama:** A **low temperature gradient** means the mushy zone is wide (interdendritic feeding is difficult), and a **low cooling rate** means solidification is slow (no driving pressure gradient to push feed metal). Both lead to porosity.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Simulation Methods

| Feature | Enthalpy Method | Apparent Heat Capacity | Temperature-Recovery Method |
|---------|----------------|------------------------|------------------------------|
