# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
> **Define solidification simulation. List any four casting defects that can be predicted using this technique.**

**Model Answer:**

Solidification simulation is a **numerical CAE technique** that uses the Finite Element Method (FEM) or Finite Difference Method (FDM) to model the coupled heat transfer, fluid flow, and phase change behavior of molten metal as it solidifies inside a mold. It enables the prediction of casting defects and the optimization of design parameters before physical tooling is produced.

**Four casting defects predicted by solidification simulation:**
1. **Shrinkage porosity** (macro and micro).
2. **Hot tears / hot cracks** (due to restrained thermal contraction).
3. **Misruns and cold shuts** (incomplete filling due to premature freezing).
4. **Gas porosity** (entrapment of dissolved gases during solidification).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
> **State the Niyama criterion. What does a low Niyama value indicate about the casting?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Niyama criterion** is an empirical metallurgical rule used in solidification simulation to predict the location of **shrinkage porosity** in a casting. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$N_y = \frac{\vert \nabla T \vert}{\sqrt{G \cdot R_s}}$$

where $\vert \nabla T \vert$ is the temperature gradient, $G$ is the gradient of local solidification time, and $R_s$ is the cooling rate.

**Interpretation:** A **low Niyama value (typically below $\mathbf{0.3 \text{ to } 1.0}$)** indicates a high probability of **shrinkage porosity formation**, because the wide mushy zone and slow cooling prevent interdendritic feed metal from reaching the location before final solidification.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, ESE Module 2]

> **Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **enthalpy method** for handling latent heat release during solidification simulation. Derive the governing equation.

**Model Solution:**

The enthalpy method is a **numerical technique** used in casting simulation to handle the release of latent heat during the liquid-to-solid phase change without explicitly tracking the solid-liquid interface.

**Step 1: Define Volumetric Enthalpy**
The volumetric enthalpy $H$ combines the sensible heat and latent heat contributions:

$$H = \rho \left[ C_p (T - T_{\text{ref}}) + f_s L \right]$$

where $f_s$ is the solid fraction ($0 \le f_s \le 1$) and $L$ is the latent heat of fusion.

**Step 2: Apply Energy Conservation**
The transient 3D heat equation with latent heat as a source term is:

$$\rho C_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \rho L \frac{\partial f_s}{\partial t}$$

**Step 3: Reformulate Using $H$**
Differentiate $H$ with respect to time:

$$\frac{\partial H}{\partial t} = \rho C_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} + \rho L \frac{\partial f_s}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4: Combine**
By direct substitution, the governing equation reduces to:

$$\frac{\partial H}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T)$$

This is the **enthalpy form of the heat equation**. It is solved iteratively by the FEM solver, with $H$ updated at each time step based on the current temperature.

**Advantages of the Enthalpy Method:**
- **No explicit tracking** of the solidification front is required.
- **Numerically stable** across the mushy zone.
- **Compatible** with arbitrary alloy solidification paths (not just pure metals).

---

> **Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **step-by-step procedure** of conducting a solidification simulation for a sand-cast aluminum alloy component. Mention the inputs and outputs at each stage.

**Model Solution:**

**Stage 1: CAD Modeling and Pre-Processing**
- The casting, mold, cores, gating system, and risers are modeled in CAD.
- A **shrinkage allowance** (typically **1 - 1.5%** for aluminum) is added to compensate for solidification contraction.
- Geometry is imported into the simulation software (e.g., MAGMA, ProCAST).

**Stage 2: Mesh Generation**
- The geometry is discretized into **millions of finite elements** (typically tetrahedral).
- Finer mesh is used in critical regions (thin walls, junctions, near risers).

**Stage 3: Material Property Assignment**
- Thermophysical properties of the alloy (Al-Si, e.g., A356) and mold (silica sand) are assigned from the software database.
- Properties are defined as **temperature-dependent functions**.

**Stage 4: Boundary and Initial Conditions**
- **Pouring temperature** (e.g., 720°C for A356), **mold temperature** (e.g., 25°C), **ambient temperature**.
- **Heat transfer coefficients** at the metal-mold interface (typically **500 - 2000 W/m²K** for sand casting).
- **Convective boundary** on the outer mold surface.

**Stage 5: Solver Run**
- The coupled energy, momentum, and continuity equations are solved iteratively over time.
- Run time may range from **30 minutes to several hours** depending on mesh size.

**Stage 6: Post-Processing**
- **Temperature contours** at various time steps.
- **Solid fraction maps** showing the progression of solidification.
- **Niyama contour plots** to identify shrinkage-prone regions.
- **Velocity vectors** showing fluid flow patterns.
- **Hot spot identification** for riser placement.

**Stage 7: Design Iteration**
- If defects are predicted, the gating system, riser size, chill placement, or pouring parameters are modified.
- The simulation is re-run until a sound design is achieved.

**Sketch (described):**
A block diagram showing the **CAD → Mesh → Properties → BCs → Solver → Post-Process → Iterate** loop.

---

> ### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, ESE Module 2 — Alternative Choice]

> **Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **significance of the Niyama criterion** in casting design. Explain how the gating and riser system is optimized using solidification simulation results.

**Model Solution:**

**Significance of the Niyama Criterion:**
The Niyama criterion is the **industry-standard shrinkage porosity predictor** in casting simulation. It is significant because:
1. It provides a **quantitative, color-mapped visualization** of porosity-prone regions.
2. It correlates **metallurgical feeding theory** (interdendritic flow resistance) with **thermal fields**.
3. It enables **objective comparison** of multiple design iterations.

**Mathematical form:**

$$N_y = \frac{\vert \nabla T \vert}{\sqrt{G \cdot R_s}}$$

**Threshold values:**
- For **steel**: $N_y \ge 0.7$ indicates sound metal.
- For **aluminum**: $N_y \ge 0.3$ indicates sound metal.
- Below these thresholds, **shrinkage porosity** is likely.

**Optimization of Gating and Riser System Using Simulation:**
1. **Initial Design:** Place risers at the heaviest sections (hot spots) based on experience.
2. **Simulate:** Run solidification simulation and overlay the Niyama contour.
3. **Identify Problem Areas:** Locate regions where $N_y$ is below the threshold.
4. **Iterative Refinement:**
   - **Increase riser size** at the hot spot to extend feeding time.
   - **Add chills** to steepen the temperature gradient (increase $\vert \nabla T \vert$).
   - **Add padding** on thick sections to ensure directional solidification toward the riser.
   - **Modify gate locations** to alter heat extraction patterns.
5. **Re-simulate** until all critical regions satisfy the Niyama threshold.
6. **Apply Chvorinov's Rule** for final riser sizing: $t_{s,\text{riser}} \ge 1.25 \cdot t_{s,\text{casting}}$ to ensure the riser freezes last.

---

> **Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **apparent heat capacity method** and the **enthalpy method** for handling latent heat in casting simulation. Which is preferred for industrial use and why?

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Apparent Heat Capacity Method | Enthalpy Method |
|--------|------------------------------|-----------------|
| **Latent Heat Modeling** | Artificially inflates $C_p$ by $\dfrac{L}{T_L - T_s}$ over mushy zone. | Adds $f_s L$ directly to enthalpy. |
| **Governing Equation** | $\rho C_p^{*} \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T)$ | $\frac{\partial H}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T)$ |
| **Mesh Sensitivity** | Highly sensitive to mesh size near $T_s, T_L$ | Robust, less mesh-sensitive |
| **Numerical Stability** | May produce **temperature oscillations** at $T_s, T_L$ | Highly stable across phase change |
| **Ease of Implementation** | Easy in standard FEM solvers | Requires enthalpy-based formulation |
| **Mushy Zone Treatment** | Approximate (assumes linear $f_s$) | Accurate (handles non-linear $f_s$ curves) |
| **Commercial Software Use** | Educational codes | **MAGMA, ProCAST, FLOW-3D** (preferred) |

**Preferred Method for Industrial Use: The Enthalpy Method**

**Reasons:**
1. **Numerical stability** in real-world castings with complex geometry.
2. **Accurate representation** of the mushy zone, especially for alloys with wide freezing ranges.
3. **Robust handling** of eutectic and peritectic reactions.
4. **Compatibility with adaptive meshing** schemes used in modern solvers.

The apparent heat capacity method is generally reserved for **teaching purposes** and **preliminary screening** due to its simpler implementation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Solidification Simulation Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Students often write $k$ without units, or use inconsistent units across equations. Always specify $\text{W/(m}\cdot\text{K)}$, $\text{J/kg}$, etc.
> 2. **Confusing $f_s$ and $f_L$:** Solid fraction $f_s$ increases from 0 to 1 during solidification; liquid fraction $f_L = 1 - f_s$. Examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Skipping Boundary Conditions:** When asked to "set up the simulation," many students list only initial conditions. Always include **boundary conditions** (heat transfer coefficient, ambient temperature, mold-metal interface condition).
> 4. **Misapplying Niyama:** Stating that **high** Niyama means porosity (it is the **opposite**). High Niyama = sound; low Niyama = defective.
> 5. **Ignoring the Mushy Zone:** A common error is treating solidification as a sharp event at $T = T_m$ instead of acknowledging the **mushy zone** between $T_s$ and $T_L$.
> 6. **No diagram in 7-mark answers:** A neat labeled block diagram of the simulation workflow fetches **1-2 extra marks** in KTU valuation.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Use of Solidification Simulation in Casting Design**
>
> - **Core Goal:** Predict casting defects **before** physical tooling is cut, reducing cost and lead time.
> - **Three Coupled Physics:** Heat transfer, fluid flow, phase change.
> - **Governing Equation (Enthalpy form):** $\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T)$.
> - **Governing Equation (Apparent Heat Capacity):** $\rho C_p^{*} \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T)$ with $C_p^{*} = C_p + \dfrac{L}{T_L - T_s}$.
> - **Latent Heat:** Released during solidification; handled via $H$ or $C_p^{*}$. Typical values: Al $\approx$ 397 kJ/kg, Fe $\approx$ 247 kJ/kg.
> - **Niyama Criterion:** $N_y = \dfrac{\vert \nabla T \vert}{\sqrt{G \cdot R_s}}$. Low $N_y$ = shrinkage porosity.
> - **Chvorinov's Rule:** $t_s = B \left( \dfrac{V}{A} \right)^n$. Riser must solidify **last**: $t_{s,\text{riser}} \ge 1.25 \cdot t_{s,\text{casting}}$.
> - **Defects Predicted:** Shrinkage porosity, hot tears, misruns, gas porosity, cold shuts, inclusions.
> - **Mushy Zone:** The semi-solid region $T_s \le T \le T_L$ where feeding is most critical.
> - **Process:** CAD → Mesh → Material Properties → BCs → Solver → Post-Process → Iterate.
> - **Industrial Software:** MAGMAsoft, ProCAST, FLOW-3D, ANSYS Fluent (with casting module).
> - **DFM Benefit:** Enables first-time-right castings, supports concurrent engineering, and enables lightweight design of complex castings.
> - **Sustainability:** Reduces scrap metal and energy consumption significantly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Design Rules for Sand Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Product Design Rules for Sand Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Product Design Rules for Sand Casting** constitute a codified set of geometric, dimensional, and process-oriented guidelines that engineers must incorporate into a component's design to ensure that the part can be **manufactured economically, defect-free, and consistently** using the sand casting process. These rules govern features such as wall thickness, draft angles, fillets, parting line location, cores, ribs, and tolerances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Product design rules for sand casting are a collection of empirical and analytical guidelines that ensure castability, minimize defects (porosity, shrinkage, hot tears), reduce machining costs, and simplify moulding and core-making operations while maintaining functional integrity of the part.*

The term **Castability** refers to the ease with which a molten metal flows through a mould cavity to produce a sound casting without defects.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Baking Cake Analogy:**
> Imagine you are baking a cake inside a mould. If your mould has:
> - **Sharp corners** → the cake batter gets stuck and cracks come out uneven.
> - **Thick walls** → the centre remains uncooked while the edges burn.
> - **No taper on the sides** → you cannot pull the cake out without breaking it.
> - **Undercuts** → the cake gets locked inside; you must destroy the mould.
>
> **Sand casting is exactly the same!** Sand is the "cake mould" and molten metal is the "batter." Product design rules exist to ensure the metal flows in smoothly, solidifies uniformly, and the sand mould can be **removed without damage**, leaving a clean casting behind.

## 1.3 Why Are These Rules Critical in KTU Examinations?

In KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE), questions on casting design rules test:
- The ability to **justify design choices** (not just memorize them).
- Recognition of **defect-causing geometries** (hot tears, shrinkage cavities).
- Numerical computation of **draft, fillet, and shrinkage allowances**.

Key physical metrics students must remember:

- **Standard Draft Angle:** $1°$ to $3°$ (external); $3°$ to $5°$ (internal/core).
- **Minimum Wall Thickness (Grey Cast Iron):** $\geq 5$ mm.
- **Pattern Shrinkage Allowance (Steel):** approx. **2.0 %**; (Cast Iron): approx. **1.0 %**.
- **Machining Allowance:** typically **2 mm to 5 mm** per machined face.
- **Fillet Radius ($R$):** typically $R \geq 0.25 \times$ adjacent wall thickness.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ten Pillars of Sand Casting Design

A well-designed sand casting obeys ten universal rules. Each rule addresses a specific failure mode.

### Pillar 1 — Uniform Section Thickness
- **Why:** Non-uniform thickness causes the **hot spot** (the thickest region) to solidify last, leading to **shrinkage porosity / cavities**.
- **How:** Maintain nearly equal wall thicknesses; if a thicker section is mandatory, use **insulating sleeves / chills** or **cores** to equalize the cooling rate.

### Pillar 2 — Generous Fillets and Radii
- **Why:** Sharp internal corners act as **stress raisers** during solidification, causing **hot tears** (intergranular cracks).
- **How:** Add fillet radius $R_f$ at every internal corner.
- **Standard rule:** $R_f = \frac{D - d}{2} \geq 0.25 \, t$, where $t$ is the local wall thickness.

### Pillar 3 — Adequate Draft (Taper) Angle
- **Why:** Sand mould surfaces are friable. Vertical faces generate **friction** as the pattern is withdrawn; without taper, the cavity walls crumble.
- **How:** Taper every surface perpendicular to the parting line.
- **General rule:**

$$D = H \cdot \tan(\theta)$$

where $D$ is the draft (linear taper per side), $H$ is the height of the vertical face, and $\theta$ is the draft angle.

### Pillar 4 — Avoid Undercuts (or Split the Pattern)
- **Why:** Undercuts lock the pattern inside the mould, requiring **loose pieces** or **drawbacks** (sliding inserts), which complicate moulding.
- **How:** Eliminate undercuts by **redesigning the geometry**; if unavoidable, design the pattern as a **split pattern** with appropriate parting plane.

### Pillar 5 — Rib and Web Design
- **Why:** Thick ribs in isolation freeze slowly → porosity. Thin interconnected ribs strengthen without hot spots.
- **How:** Use **criss-cross / staggered ribs** of thickness $t_{rib} \approx 0.6 \, t_{wall}$.

### Pillar 6 — Boss Design
- **Why:** Bosses (cylindrical projections) for holes or fastenings often meet the main wall at right angles — a hot spot.
- **How:** Use **cored bosses** with a generous fillet junction, or merge boss with wall via a tapered transition.

### Pillar 7 — Parting Line Selection
- **Why:** A poor parting line forces complex mould assembly, increasing **mismatch** and **flash** defects.
- **How:** Place the parting line at the **largest cross-section**, ideally flat, to allow a two-part mould with simple drag and cope.

### Pillar 8 — Machining & Shrinkage Allowances
- **Why:** Castings have rough surfaces and contract during solidification.
- **How:**
   - Add **machining allowance** ($2$–$5$ mm) on surfaces to be later machined.
   - Make the **pattern oversized** by a **shrinkage factor** ($S$) corresponding to the alloy.

### Pillar 9 — Core Design Considerations
- **Why:** Unsupported long cores sag; thin core sections burn through.
- **How:** Core thickness $t_{core} \geq 1.5 \, t_{wall}$ for ferrous metals; provide **core prints** for proper location.

### Pillar 10 — Dimensional Tolerances
- **Why:** Sand moulds are not rigid; cumulative variation is large.
- **How:** Reference the **KTU / IS 5519** tolerance grade; typical linear tolerance for hand-moulded sand casting is $\pm 1.5$ mm to $\pm 3.0$ mm for small castings.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:**
> Whenever asked "Why this rule?", your answer **must** contain three elements: (1) the *defect prevented*, (2) the *mechanism of formation*, and (3) the *corrective design feature*. A single-sentence answer typically scores only 1 of 3 marks.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities below are board-exam ready. **Use `\vert` instead of `|` inside this table to avoid markdown breakage.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Boundary / Standard Value | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------------------|-------|
| 1 | Draft (linear) | $D = H \tan(\theta)$ | $\theta = 1°$–$3°$ external; $3°$–$5°$ internal | mm |
| 2 | Draft (height compensation) | $H' = H + D$ | $H' = H \left( 1 + \tan(\theta) \right)$ | mm |
| 3 | Fillet radius (junction) | $R_f = \tfrac{1}{2} (D - d)$ | $R_f \geq 0.25 \, t_{wall}$ | mm |
| 4 | Rib thickness | $t_{rib} = 0.6 \, t_{wall}$ | $0.5 \leq \tfrac{t_{rib}}{t_{wall}} \leq 0.75$ | mm |
| 5 | Core thickness | $t_{core} = 1.5 \, t_{wall}$ | $t_{core} \geq 1.5 \, t_{wall}$ (ferrous) | mm |
| 6 | Pattern shrinkage | $L_p = L_c (1 + S)$ | $S = 0.01$ (CI); $0.02$ (Steel); $0.015$ (Al) | — |
| 7 | Boss wall thickness | $t_{boss} = 0.75 \, t_{wall}$ | — | mm |
| 8 | Min. wall thickness (FC) | $t_{min} = 5$ mm | Grey Cast Iron reference | mm |
| 9 | Mismatch tolerance | $\delta_m = \pm 0.5$ mm | Hand moulded (small parts) | mm |
| 10 | Total linear tolerance | $T = \pm (A + B L)$ | $A = 1.0$–$2.0$, $B = 0.005$–$0.02$ | mm |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These design rules govern industries ranging from **automotive cylinder blocks and heads**, **pump housings**, **valve bodies**, **machine tool beds** to **turbine housings** in aerospace. A well-designed casting reduces:
- **Material waste** (no over-thick walls),
- **Post-casting weld repairs** (no hot tears),
- **Rejection rate** (uniform shrinkage → fewer scrapped parts),
- **Tooling cost** (no complex loose pieces or core boxes needed).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Draft Angle from Pattern Withdrawal Mechanics

Consider a vertical face of height $H$ on a sand pattern. The sand grain cohesion force resisting pattern withdrawal is:

$$F_{resist} = \mu_s \cdot W_{sand} \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

where $\mu_s$ is the sand-metal friction coefficient, $W_{sand}$ is the normal weight of sand pressing against the face, and $\theta$ is the angle between the face and vertical (the draft angle).

The vertical lifting force is:

$$F_{lift} = W_{sand} \cdot \cos(\theta - \alpha)$$

where $\alpha$ is the friction angle. The pattern lifts cleanly only when:

$$F_{lift} > F_{resist}$$

Substituting and simplifying for the critical (minimum) draft:

$$\tan(\theta_{min}) = \mu_s$$

For typical **green sand mould**, $\mu_s \approx 0.02$–$0.05$. Hence:

$$\theta_{min} = \arctan(0.05) \approx 2.86°$$

This validates the empirical rule of $\theta = 1°$–$3°$ for external faces. Internally (on a core), friction is higher due to the confined mould cavity, which is why a larger draft ($3°$–$5°$) is recommended.

> **Conversion logic:** We derived the minimum draft by equating the tangential pull-out force to the static friction of sand grains against the pattern face. The empirical value $\mu_s \approx 0.05$ yields $\theta_{min} \approx 3°$, matching the KTU-suggested range.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fillet Radius and Stress Concentration

At a sharp internal corner joining two walls of thicknesses $t_1$ and $t_2$ (assume $t_1 \leq t_2$), the local section is effectively thickened. The **effective junction thickness** is:

$$t_{eff} = t_1 + t_2 - r$$

where $r$ is the corner radius. The **thermal modulus** $M_t$ (the ratio of volume to surface area, which dictates solidification time) becomes:

$$M_t = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{t_{eff}}{2}$$

For a **hot spot** to be avoided, the local thermal modulus must equal the thermal modulus of the surrounding wall:

$$\frac{t_{eff}}{2} = \frac{t_1}{2}$$

Solving:

$$t_1 + t_2 - r = t_1 \implies r = t_2$$

However, for design simplicity, the **standard rule** uses the smaller wall:

$$r_{min} = 0.25 \, t_1$$

This ensures the local modulus is at most $1.25 \times$ the wall modulus — empirically safe for grey cast iron and steel.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Computing Pattern Dimensions

A steel sand casting of nominal length $L_c = 250$ mm is to be produced. The pattern maker must:
1. Apply shrinkage allowance ($S = 0.02$ for steel).
2. Add machining allowance $M = 3$ mm on each machined face.
3. Apply a draft of $\theta = 2°$ on a vertical face of height $H = 100$ mm.

**Step 1 — Shrinkage compensation (linear):**

$$L_p = L_c \cdot (1 + S) = 250 \cdot 1.02 = 255 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Machining allowance (additive):**

$$L_{p,mach} = 255 + 2M = 255 + 2(3) = 261 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Draft (linear taper, single side):**

$$D = H \tan(\theta) = 100 \cdot \tan(2°) = 100 \cdot 0.03492 = 3.492 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Adjusted pattern height:**

$$H' = H + D = 100 + 3.492 = 103.492 \text{ mm}$$

**Final pattern dimensions:** $L_{p,mach} = 261$ mm, $H' = 103.49$ mm.

> **Conversion logic:** The shrinkage factor enlarges the pattern so that, after metal solidification and contraction, the casting dimension matches the design intent. Machining allowance is then added *after* shrinkage because the surface is removed later, not because the metal contracts there. Draft is applied last since it is a *geometric taper*, not a uniform enlargement.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Defect → Rule → Feature

| Defect Mode | Mechanism | Design Rule Violated | Corrective Feature |
|-------------|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Shrinkage cavity** | Local hot spot solidifies last | Non-uniform thickness | Equalize wall, add chill |
| **Hot tear** | Tensile stress at junction | Sharp internal corner | Fillet radius $R_f \geq 0.25 t$ |
| **Mould wall collapse** | Friction on pattern | Insufficient draft | Apply $\theta = 1°$–$3°$ |
| **Moulding difficulty** | Pattern cannot lift out | Undercut | Eliminate or use split pattern |
| **Flash / mismatch** | Cope-drag misalignment | Poor parting line | Place parting at max area |
| **Core sag / shift** | Long unsupported core | Thin / unanchored core | Thicker core + core prints |
| **Warping** | Differential cooling | Long thin unsupported features | Add ribs of $0.6 t$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Sand Casting Design Rules

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component Functional Design] --> B{Apply Casting Design Rules}
    B --> C1[Rule 1: Uniform Thickness]
    B --> C2[Rule 2: Fillets and Radii]
    B --> C3[Rule 3: Draft Angle]
    B --> C4[Rule 4: Avoid Undercuts]
    B --> C5[Rule 5: Rib and Web Layout]
    B --> C6[Rule 6: Boss Design]
    B --> C7[Rule 7: Parting Line Selection]
    B --> C8[Rule 8: Machining and Shrinkage Allowance]
    B --> C9[Rule 9: Core Design]
    B --> C10[Rule 10: Dimensional Tolerances]
    C1 --> D[Verified Castable Geometry]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    C7 --> D
    C8 --> D
    C9 --> D
    C10 --> D
    D --> E[Pattern Drawing]
    E --> F[Mould and Core Box]
    F --> G[Pouring]
    G --> H[Defect Free Sand Casting]
```

## 4.2 Draft Angle Compensation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Vertical Wall Height H] --> N2[Apply Draft Angle Theta]
    N2 --> N3[Linear Draft D equals H tan Theta]
    N3 --> N4[Modified Wall Height H plus D]
    N4 --> N5[Pattern Face Geometry]
    N5 --> N6[Pattern Withdrawn Without Damage]
    N6 --> N7[Clean Mould Cavity]
```

## 4.3 Defect Prevention Logic Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Identify Geometrical Feature] --> P2{Sharp Corner?}
    P2 -- YES --> P3[Add Fillet Radius]
    P2 -- NO --> P4{Variable Thickness?}
    P4 -- YES --> P5[Add Chill or Redesign Wall]
    P4 -- NO --> P6{Vertical Face?}
    P6 -- YES --> P7[Apply Draft 1 to 3 Degrees]
    P6 -- NO --> P8{Internal Cavity?}
    P8 -- YES --> P9[Design Core with Prints]
    P8 -- NO --> P10[Approved Geometry]
    P3 --> P10
    P5 --> P10
    P7 --> P10
    P9 --> P10
```

> **Note on Mermaid safeguards applied:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters (`A`, `N1`, `P1`, `stepA`-style).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names.
> - All quoted labels contain **plain uppercase alphanumeric text only** (no bold, italic, or HTML markup).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]`
**Define "draft angle" in sand casting. Why is it necessary, and what is its typical range for external and internal surfaces?**

**Model Answer:**
Draft angle is the intentional taper provided on the vertical faces of a pattern, parallel to the direction of withdrawal from the mould, to allow the pattern to be drawn out without damaging the sand mould walls. It is necessary because green sand has low tensile strength and the friction between pattern and sand can cause surface crumbling, resulting in a rough or defective casting surface. The typical range is **$1°$ to $3°$ for external surfaces** and **$3°$ to $5°$ for internal surfaces (cores)** because internal surfaces have higher confining pressure and friction. The linear draft is computed as $D = H \tan(\theta)$, where $H$ is the height of the vertical face. *(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark necessity + 1 mark range with formula.)*

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]`
**State the design rule for fillet radius at the junction of two walls in a sand casting. What defect does it prevent and how?**

**Model Answer:**
The design rule states that the fillet radius at the junction of two walls of thickness $t_1$ (smaller) and $t_2$ (larger) should satisfy:
$$R_f \geq 0.25 \, t_1$$
This ensures the local section modulus does not exceed $1.25$ times the wall modulus, preventing the formation of a **hot spot** at the junction. The defect prevented is **hot tearing** (intergranular shrinkage cracking) which occurs when a still-mushy region is pulled apart by thermal contraction stresses of the surrounding solidified metal. By blending the corners with a generous fillet, the cross-section transition is smooth, freezing is uniform, and stress concentration is reduced. *(3 marks: 1 mark rule + 1 mark defect + 1 mark mechanism.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Each Part B question has internal choice; the two alternatives below represent either of the two questions that may appear on the topic in any given exam cycle.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper, ESE 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the design rules for **uniform section thickness and rib design** in sand castings. With neat sketches, show how non-uniform thickness leads to shrinkage porosity, and how staggered rib design eliminates this.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A grey cast iron sand casting has a wall of thickness $25$ mm, a ribbed section, and a boss of outer diameter $80$ mm. Compute:
1. Recommended rib thickness.
2. Boss wall thickness.
3. Minimum fillet radius at the boss–wall junction.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

1. **Uniform Section Thickness Rule:** All sections of a casting should solidify as nearly simultaneously as possible. Where a thicker section is functionally unavoidable, an **insulating pad / chill** must be inserted to match the local cooling rate with the surrounding wall. The thermal modulus $M = V/A$ must remain approximately constant.

2. **Mechanism of Shrinkage Porosity:** When a thick boss is joined to a thin wall, the boss has a higher modulus and freezes last. The shrinkage of the boss cannot be fed by the already-rigid wall, leading to an internal cavity — **shrinkage porosity**.

3. **Rib Design Rules:**
   - Rib thickness $t_{rib} = (0.5 \text{ to } 0.75) \, t_{wall}$.
   - Ribs should be **staggered / criss-crossed**, not continuous, to break the thermal path.
   - Rib-to-wall junction should use a fillet of radius $R \geq 0.25 \, t_{rib}$.

**Sketch description (textual):**
- *Left figure:* A thick boss (50 mm) on a thin wall (15 mm) showing a shrinkage void at the centre of the boss.
- *Right figure:* The same geometry re-engineered with staggered ribs of $t_{rib} = 9$ mm and an equalizing fillet, showing no central void.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:** $t_{wall} = 25$ mm, $D_{boss} = 80$ mm, alloy = grey cast iron.

**1. Recommended rib thickness:**

$$t_{rib} = 0.6 \, t_{wall} = 0.6 \times 25 = 15 \text{ mm}$$

**2. Boss wall thickness:**

$$t_{boss} = 0.75 \, t_{wall} = 0.75 \times 25 = 18.75 \text{ mm}$$

**3. Minimum fillet radius at the boss–wall junction:**

$$R_f = 0.25 \, t_{wall} = 0.25 \times 25 = 6.25 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer summary:** $t_{rib} = 15$ mm, $t_{boss} = 18.75$ mm, $R_{f,min} = 6.25$ mm. *(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3 with units.)*

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper, ESE 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **draft angle, parting line, and core design rules** in sand casting. State their typical values and explain how poor compliance causes moulding defects.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel casting of nominal dimensions $300 \text{ mm} \times 150 \text{ mm} \times 80 \text{ mm}$ is to be produced. Apply a shrinkage allowance of $2\%$, a machining allowance of $3$ mm per face, and a draft angle of $2°$ on the $80$ mm vertical face. Determine the **pattern dimensions** in all three directions.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

1. **Draft Angle:** $\theta = 1°$–$3°$ external, $3°$–$5°$ internal. Insufficient draft causes sand wall crumbling → surface roughness / mould erosion. Formula: $D = H \tan(\theta)$.

2. **Parting Line Rules:**
   - Place at the **largest cross-section**.
   - Prefer a **flat, straight parting line** for a two-part mould.
   - Minimize the number of cores and loose pieces.
   - Poor parting line causes **mismatch** (cope-drag offset) and **flash** (excess metal at the joint).

3. **Core Design Rules:**
   - Core thickness $t_{core} \geq 1.5 \, t_{wall}$ for ferrous alloys.
   - Provide **core prints** at both ends to lock the core into the mould.
   - Avoid long slender cores; they sag under their own weight — use **core supports / chaplets**.
   - Vents must be provided in cores to release trapped gases.

**Defect linkage:** Non-compliance with core thickness leads to **core burn-through** (molten metal penetrates the core), and lack of prints causes **core shift** (misaligned internal cavity).

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:** $L_c = 300$ mm, $W_c = 150$ mm, $H_c = 80$ mm, $S = 0.02$, $M = 3$ mm/face, $\theta = 2°$ on the 80 mm face.

**Step 1 — Length direction (300 mm), both ends machined:**

$$L_p = L_c (1 + S) + 2M = 300(1.02) + 6 = 306 + 6 = 312 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Width direction (150 mm), both ends machined:**

$$W_p = W_c (1 + S) + 2M = 150(1.02) + 6 = 153 + 6 = 159 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Height direction (80 mm), with draft:**

Draft linear taper:
$$D = H_c \tan(\theta) = 80 \cdot \tan(2°) = 80 \cdot 0.03492 = 2.794 \text{ mm}$$

Machining allowance on top face only:
$$H_p = H_c (1 + S) + M + D = 80(1.02) + 3 + 2.794 = 81.6 + 5.794 = 87.39 \text{ mm}$$

**Final pattern dimensions:** $312 \text{ mm} \times 159 \text{ mm} \times 87.39 \text{ mm}$. *(7 marks: 3 + 2 + 2 for stepwise computation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the shrinkage factor in pattern dimensions.** Pattern length is *not* equal to casting length; it is $L_c(1+S)$.
> 2. **Mixing up external vs internal draft angles.** Internal/core draft is always higher ($3°$–$5°$). Examiners explicitly test this.
> 3. **Quoting rib thickness as a single number without the $0.5$–$0.75$ range or the formula $t_{rib} = 0.6 t_{wall}$.** A bare value loses the methodology marks.
> 4. **Not linking each rule to the defect it prevents.** KTU valuation keys award marks for the *causal mechanism*, not just the rule statement.
> 5. **Unit errors in draft calculation:** always convert angle to radians if using calculators in `tan` mode, and quote final $D$ in mm.
> 6. **Ignoring parting line placement** as a separate rule — it is a frequent Part A trick question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Draft angle:** $1°$–$3°$ external, $3°$–$5°$ internal; $D = H \tan(\theta)$.
- [ ] **Fillet radius:** $R_f \geq 0.25 \, t_{wall}$ (smaller wall governs).
- [ ] **Uniform wall thickness:** prevents **shrinkage porosity** at hot spots.
- [ ] **Rib thickness:** $t_{rib} = 0.6 \, t_{wall}$ (range $0.5$–$0.75$); use **staggered** layout.
- [ ] **Boss wall thickness:** $t_{boss} = 0.75 \, t_{wall}$.
- [ ] **Core thickness:** $t_{core} \geq 1.5 \, t_{wall}$ for ferrous metals.
- [ ] **Pattern shrinkage:** $L_p = L_c (1 + S)$; $S = 0.01$ (CI), $0.015$ (Al), $0.02$ (Steel).
- [ ] **Machining allowance:** typically $2$–$5$ mm per machined face, applied *after* shrinkage compensation.
- [ ] **Parting line:** place at the **largest cross-section**, prefer a flat horizontal plane.
- [ ] **Avoid undercuts** or design as **split pattern** with loose pieces.
- [ ] **Chills and insulating pads** are used to equalize local thermal modulus.
- [ ] **Tolerances:** linear tolerance is $\pm (A + B L)$; consult IS 5519 for grade selection.
- [ ] **Always state the defect → rule → feature triad** in long answers to secure full marks.
- [ ] **Convert all angles from degrees to radians** in numerical calculations for safety.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Metal Joining: Appraisal of Various Welding Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design for Metal Joining: Appraisal of Various Welding Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welding (as per AWS / ISO Standard):**
> *Welding is a localized metal joining process in which coalescence of materials is produced by heating to a suitable temperature, with or without the application of pressure, and with or without the use of filler metal.* A weld pool is formed by fusion of the base metal (and filler) which, upon solidification, produces a continuous metallic bond.

In the context of **Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)**, **Appraisal of Welding Processes** refers to the **systematic evaluation, comparison, and selection of an appropriate welding process** during the product design stage. The objective is to choose a process that satisfies functional, geometrical, metallurgical, economic, and quality requirements while ensuring **design for weldability (DFW)**.

### 1.2 Key Design Terminologies

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Weldability** | The capacity of a metal to be welded under imposed fabrication conditions into a specific, suitable structure that performs satisfactorily in its intended service. |
| **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** | The region of base metal whose microstructure and mechanical properties have been altered by the heat of welding, but without melting. |
| **Heat Input (H)** | The amount of thermal energy supplied per unit length of weld, expressed in **kJ/mm**. Governs HAZ width, distortion, and grain growth. |
| **Joint Efficiency (η)** | Ratio of strength of welded joint to the strength of the unwelded base metal, expressed as a **percentage (≤ 100 %)**. |
| **Filler Metal** | Additional metal added to the weld pool to facilitate coalescence and fill the joint gap. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "Choosing the Right Glue for the Job"**
> Imagine you are a carpenter deciding how to fix two wooden pieces. You could use *Fevicol* (slow but strong for large surfaces), *super-glue* (fast but brittle), *nails* (cheap but leaves holes), or *screws* (reusable but need holes). Each method has trade-offs in **strength, speed, cost, skill required, and surface finish**. *Appraisal of welding processes* is exactly the same logic — a mechanical engineer must pick the right "welding glue" for each application by comparing arc welding, resistance welding, gas welding, laser welding, and so on. The "best" process is **not universal**; it depends on the material, thickness, joint geometry, production volume, and cost ceiling.

### 1.4 Why Welding Appraisal is Critical in DFMA

A designer's choice of welding process **directly affects**:
1. **Manufacturability** – Whether the joint can be made with available equipment.
2. **Cost** – Equipment, filler, shielding gas, labour, and rework.
3. **Quality** – Defects (porosity, cracks, under-cut, distortion).
4. **Assembly strategy** – Number of parts, fasteners, sub-assemblies.
5. **Service life** – Fatigue, corrosion, creep behaviour of the weld.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Favourite:** Examiners frequently test the *selection logic* (which process for which application) rather than rote definitions. Always justify your selection with **at least two appraisal criteria**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Welding Processes (Appraisal Framework)

The appraisal is structured around **four families** of welding processes recognised by the American Welding Society (AWS):

#### Family A — **Fusion Welding (with localized melting)**

**A1. Arc Welding Processes**
- **SMAW** (Shielded Metal Arc Welding) – "Stick welding"
- **GMAW** (Gas Metal Arc Welding) – MIG / MAG
- **GTAW** (Gas Tungsten Arc Welding) – TIG
- **FCAW** (Flux-Cored Arc Welding)
- **SAW** (Submerged Arc Welding)
- **PAW** (Plasma Arc Welding)

**A2. Gas Welding**
- **Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW)**
- **Oxy-Hydrogen Welding**

**A3. High-Energy Beam Welding**
- **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)**
- **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)**

#### Family B — **Resistance Welding (No filler, pressure + heat)**
- **Spot Welding (RSW)**
- **Seam Welding (RSEW)**
- **Projection Welding (RPW)**
- **Flash Butt Welding (FBW)**
- **Stud Welding**

#### Family C — **Solid-State Welding (Below melting point)**
- **Friction Welding (FRW)** – inertia & direct drive
- **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)**
- **Ultrasonic Welding (USW)**
- **Cold Welding (CW)**
- **Diffusion Welding (DFW)**
- **Explosion Welding (EXW)**

#### Family D — **Brazing, Soldering & Adhesive Bonding (Non-fusion)**
- **Brazing** (> 450 °C, capillary action, e.g., torch, induction, furnace)
- **Soldering** (< 450 °C)
- *Included for completeness in joining appraisal.*

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step in Appraisal

1. **Step 1 — Identify the Base Material** — Determines *weldability* (carbon equivalent, thermal conductivity, melting point).
2. **Step 2 — Identify the Joint Geometry** — Butt, lap, T, corner, edge — limits process options.
3. **Step 3 — Quantify Production Volume** — Low/medium/high → manual vs. automated process.
4. **Step 4 — Quantify Quality & Tolerance** — Cosmetic vs. structural; codes (ASME, AWS D1.1, EN 1090).
5. **Step 5 — Compute Heat Input and Distortion Risk** — Critical for thin sections and high-precision assemblies.
6. **Step 6 — Cost Analysis** — Equipment amortisation + consumables + labour + rework.
7. **Step 7 — Make Final Selection** — Best trade-off among the above six criteria.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas required for KTU ESE numerical / design problems on welding appraisal are tabulated below. Master these.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Heat Input (H)** | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}$ | **kJ/mm** | HAZ width, distortion |
| 2 | **Heat Input (alternate form)** | $H = \dfrac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$ | J/mm | Used when S in mm/min |
| 3 | **Cooling Rate t8/5** | $t_{8/5} = \dfrac{H}{2 \pi \lambda \rho c} \left[\dfrac{1}{500-T_0} - \dfrac{1}{800-T_0}\right]$ | s | Determines HAZ microstructure |
| 4 | **Carbon Equivalent (IIW)** | $C_{eq} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | % | Cold-cracking tendency |
| 5 | **Welding Speed** | $S = \dfrac{L}{t}$ | mm/min or mm/s | Time estimation |
| 6 | **Deposition Rate (GMAW/SAW)** | $R_d = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v_f \cdot \rho$ | kg/h | Productivity analysis |
| 7 | **Total Weld Cost** | $C_T = C_{setup} + (C_{labour} + C_{filler} + C_{gas} + C_{power}) \cdot t_w$ | ₹ / m | Cost appraisal |
| 8 | **Weld Metal Area (single-V, 60°)** | $A_w = \dfrac{t^2 \sin 60°}{2} + b \cdot t$ | mm² | Filler metal weight |
| 9 | **Filler Metal Required** | $W_f = A_w \cdot L \cdot \rho_{filler}$ | kg | Cost of consumable |
| 10 | **Joint Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{\sigma_{weld}}{\sigma_{base}} \times 100$ | % | Design check |

Where:
- $V$ = arc voltage (V); $I$ = welding current (A); $S$ = travel speed (mm/min); $\eta$ = process efficiency (0.6 – 0.9)
- $d$ = wire diameter (mm); $v_f$ = wire feed rate (m/min); $\rho$ = density of filler (kg/m³)
- $t_w$ = welding time (h); $L$ = weld length (m); $t$ = plate thickness (mm); $b$ = root gap (mm)
- $T_0$ = preheat / interpass temperature (°C); $\lambda$ = thermal conductivity; $\rho$ = density; $c$ = specific heat

### 2.4 Comparative Appraisal Matrix (Most Tested in KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Examiner's Tip:** This table is the *single most repeated* appraisal tool. Memorise the relative ratings.

| Criterion | SMAW | GMAW (MIG) | GTAW (TIG) | SAW | RSW (Spot) | LBW | FSW |
|-----------|------|-----------|------------|-----|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Material thickness** | 2–50 mm | 1–25 mm | 0.5–6 mm | 6–50 mm | 0.5–3 mm | < 6 mm | 1–50 mm |
| **Weld quality** | Medium | High | Excellent | High | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Deposition rate** | Low | High | Very low | Highest | N/A | Medium | Medium |
| **Distortion** | Moderate | Moderate | Low | High | Minimal | Very low | Low |
| **Skill required** | Medium | Medium | High | Medium | Low | High | Medium |
| **Automation** | Difficult | Easy | Moderate | Easy | Trivial | Easy | Easy |
| **Cost / m (low vol.)** | Lowest | Medium | Highest | Low | Very low | High | High |
| **Position capability** | All | All | All | Flat/H | Spot only | All | All |
| **Best suited for** | Field, repair | Production, carbon steel | Stainless, Al, exotic | Heavy plate, ship | Sheet metal, auto | Precision, auto, aero | Al alloys, aerospace |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive body-in-white** — RSW dominates due to high speed and low distortion.
- **Aerospace fuselage** — FSW is the *new benchmark* for Al-alloy panels (Boeing, Airbus).
- **Ship hulls and boilers** — SAW for thick plates (high deposition rate).
- **Nuclear and chemical** — GTAW for root pass + SAW for fill (highest quality).
- **Micro-electronics and medical devices** — LBW and USW for miniaturisation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Heat Input (H) — from First Principles

The **heat input per unit length of weld** is the energy delivered by the welding arc onto the workpiece as it traverses a unit distance.

**Step 1 — Electrical Power of the Arc**

The arc is essentially an electric load. The instantaneous electrical power consumed at the arc is:

$$P = V \cdot I$$

where $V$ is the arc voltage (V) and $I$ is the welding current (A). The unit of $P$ is **watts (W = J/s)**.

**Step 2 — Energy Transferred per Unit Time**

In time $t_w$ (seconds), the total electrical energy consumed is:

$$E_{total} = V \cdot I \cdot t_w \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

**Step 3 — Effective Heat Delivered to the Workpiece**

Not all the electrical energy reaches the weld pool. A fraction is lost as radiation, spatter, and convection from the arc. This is captured by the **arc / process thermal efficiency** $\eta$ (dimensionless, 0 < $\eta$ < 1).

$$E_{useful} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t_w$$

**Step 4 — Heat per Unit Length**

The torch travels a distance $L = S \cdot t_w$, where $S$ is the travel speed in **mm/s**. Therefore, the heat input per millimetre is:

$$H = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{L} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t_w}{S \cdot t_w} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \quad \text{[J/mm]}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kJ/mm (KTU / Industry Standard Unit)**

Dividing numerator and denominator by 1000:

$$\boxed{H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}}$$

If $S$ is given in **mm/min**, the formula becomes $H = \dfrac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \times 1000}$ kJ/mm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Heat Input Matters:** A high H (e.g., > 2 kJ/mm in steel) leads to **coarse grains**, **wider HAZ**, and **greater distortion**. Codes such as EN 10164 and ASME IX impose upper limits on H for specific steel grades.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Heat Input for a GMAW Process

**Given:** A mild steel plate is welded by GMAW with $V = 28$ V, $I = 250$ A, $S = 6$ mm/s, and process efficiency $\eta = 0.85$.

**Find:** (a) Heat input in kJ/mm, (b) Comment on weldability.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify consistent units.**
$S = 6$ mm/s, so we use the J/mm form first.

**Step 2 — Compute power.**

$$P = V \cdot I = 28 \times 250 = 7000 \text{ W} = 7 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Apply efficiency.**

$$P_{useful} = \eta \cdot P = 0.85 \times 7 = 5.95 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Heat input per mm.**

$$H = \dfrac{P_{useful}}{S} = \dfrac{5950 \text{ J/s}}{6 \text{ mm/s}} = 991.67 \text{ J/mm}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kJ/mm.**

$$\boxed{H \approx 0.992 \text{ kJ/mm}}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering comment.**
For a 6 mm thick mild steel plate, a heat input around 1 kJ/mm is **acceptable** (typical range 0.5 – 1.5 kJ/mm). Distortion is moderate; preheating is **not mandatory** for a low-carbon equivalent ($C_{eq} < 0.40$).

### 3.3 Worked Example: Cost Appraisal for a Fillet Weld

**Given:** A lap joint requires 12 m of 6 mm fillet weld on a mild steel chassis. Filler rod cost = ₹ 350 / kg, shielding gas = ₹ 80 / hr, labour = ₹ 250 / hr, power = ₹ 60 / hr, equipment overhead = ₹ 500 (one-time). The weld metal cross-section area (fillet, leg = 6 mm) is $A_w = 0.707 \times 6 \times 6 = 25.45$ mm². Filler density = 7850 kg/m³, deposition efficiency = 0.9, travel speed = 4 mm/s, arc-on time = 25 % overhead (i.e., effective welding = 75 % of clock time).

**Find:** Total weld cost.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Weld metal volume.**

$$V_w = A_w \times L = 25.45 \text{ mm}^2 \times 12000 \text{ mm} = 305400 \text{ mm}^3 = 3.054 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Mass of filler required.**

$$m_f = \dfrac{V_w \cdot \rho}{\eta_{deposition}} = \dfrac{3.054 \times 10^{-4} \times 7850}{0.9} = 2.664 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Filler cost.**

$$C_f = 2.664 \times 350 = \text{₹ } 932.4$$

**Step 4 — Total welding time.**
Pure travel time: $t = L / S = 12 / 0.004 = 3000$ s. Including 25 % overhead:
$t_w = 3000 / 0.75 = 4000$ s $\approx$ 1.111 h.

**Step 5 — Variable operating cost.**

$$C_{op} = (250 + 80 + 60) \times 1.111 = \text{₹ } 433.3$$

**Step 6 — Total cost.**

$$\boxed{C_T = 500 + 932.4 + 433.3 \approx \text{₹ } 1865.7 \text{ (for 12 m weld)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Per metre:** $C_T / 12 \approx$ **₹ 155 / m** — a typical SMAW / GMAW value for thin structural steel.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Process Selection Logic

| Question Asked in Design | Decision Criterion | Preferred Process |
|--------------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| Sheet metal auto body, 0.8 mm steel, 10⁶ units/yr? | High speed, low distortion | **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)** |
| Aluminium aerospace panel, 4 mm, fatigue-critical? | No HAZ, solid state, high strength | **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** |
| Stainless-steel pipe root pass, sanitary? | Highest purity, no spatter | **GTAW (TIG)** |
| Ship hull plate 25 mm, flat position, thick? | High deposition, single-pass | **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** |
| Field repair in remote location, no power? | Portable, no electricity | **Oxy-Acetylene / SMAW** |
| Precision electronic leads, 0.1 mm Cu? | No HAZ, pinpoint energy | **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** |
| Dissimilar metals (Cu–Al)? | Avoiding brittle intermetallics | **Ultrasonic / Friction welding** |
| Thin brass sheet, low heat input? | Below melting, capillary fill | **Brazing** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Welding Process Classification (KTU Appraisal Framework)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[WELDING PROCESS APPRAISAL] --> B1[FUSION WELDING]
    A0 --> B2[RESISTANCE WELDING]
    A0 --> B3[SOLID STATE WELDING]
    A0 --> B4[BRAZING AND SOLDERING]

    B1 --> C1A[Arc Welding]
    B1 --> C1B[Gas Welding]
    B1 --> C1C[High Energy Beam]

    C1A --> D1A[SMAW]
    C1A --> D1B[GMAW MIG MAG]
    C1A --> D1C[GTAW TIG]
    C1A --> D1D[FCAW]
    C1A --> D1E[SAW]
    C1A --> D1F[Plasma Arc PAW]

    C1B --> D2A[Oxy Acetylene OAW]

    C1C --> D3A[Laser Beam LBW]
    C1C --> D3B[Electron Beam EBW]

    B2 --> C2A[Spot RSW]
    B2 --> C2B[Seam RSEW]
    B2 --> C2C[Projection RPW]
    B2 --> C2D[Flash Butt FBW]

    B3 --> C3A[Friction FRW]
    B3 --> C3B[Friction Stir FSW]
    B3 --> C3C[Ultrasonic USW]
    B3 --> C3D[Diffusion DFW]
    B3 --> C3E[Cold CW]
    B3 --> C3F[Explosion EXW]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Decision Logic for Welding Process Selection

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[START] --> S1{Base Material}
    S1 --> S2{Plate Thickness t mm}
    S2 --> S3{t less than 3}
    S2 --> S4{t between 3 and 12}
    S2 --> S5{t greater than 12}
    S3 --> S6[RSW or LBW or GTAW]
    S4 --> S7[GMAW or GTAW plus SMAW]
    S5 --> S8[SAW or GMAW multi pass]
    S6 --> S9{Production Volume}
    S7 --> S9
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10{High Volume}
    S9 --> S11{Low Volume}
    S10 --> S12[Automated Process]
    S11 --> S13[Manual SMAW or OAW]
    S12 --> S14{Heat Sensitive Alloy}
    S14 --> S15[Use FSW or LBW]
    S14 --> S16[Use GTAW for low H]
    S13 --> S17[END]
    S15 --> S17
    S16 --> S17
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ) in a Fusion Weld

```mermaid
graph LR
    A0[BASE METAL] --> A1[UNCHANGED ZONE]
    A1 --> A2[TEMPERED OR PREHEAT ZONE]
    A2 --> A3[PARTIAL TRANSFORMATION ZONE]
    A3 --> A4[GRAIN REFINED ZONE]
    A4 --> A5[COARSE GRAIN HAZ]
    A5 --> A6[FUSION ZONE WELD METAL]
    A6 --> A7[COARSE GRAIN HAZ]
    A7 --> A8[GRAIN REFINED ZONE]
    A8 --> A9[PARTIAL TRANSFORMATION ZONE]
    A9 --> A10[TEMPERED OR PREHEAT ZONE]
    A10 --> A11[UNCHANGED ZONE]
    A11 --> A12[BASE METAL]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A *low-heat-input* process (GTAW, LBW) keeps the HAZ narrow (1–2 grains), which improves fatigue and impact toughness. A *high-heat-input* process (SAW, Electroslag) yields a wide, coarse HAZ — a frequent KTU viva question on **why LBW is preferred for thin aerospace skins**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Define weldability. List any four factors that affect the weldability of steel.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Weldability is the capacity of a metal to be welded under imposed fabrication conditions into a specific, suitable structure that performs satisfactorily in its intended service* **(1 mark)**.
Factors affecting weldability of steel **(0.5 each, 2 marks):**
1. **Chemical composition** – especially Carbon Equivalent (Ceq).
2. **Thickness and joint geometry** – affects heat dissipation and residual stress.
3. **Welding process and heat input** – controls HAZ size.
4. **Preheat / post-weld heat treatment** – reduces hydrogen cracking tendency.
5. **Service conditions** – temperature, corrosive media, fatigue loading.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*Differentiate between fusion welding and solid-state welding. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Fusion Welding | Solid-State Welding |
|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| Base metal state | Melts to form a pool | No melting; plastic deformation |
| Filler metal | Often required | Rarely required |
| HAZ | Significant | Minimal / absent |
| Distortion | Higher | Lower |
| Example (1 each) | SMAW, GTAW, GMAW, SAW, LBW **(1 mark)** | Friction Welding, Friction Stir Welding, Ultrasonic Welding **(1 mark)** |
| Definition (remaining 1 mark) | Both statements from above | |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Scheme]**

*Q. A. (a)* Explain the **classification of welding processes** as per AWS, with neat sketches and give the **principle of operation** of any one arc-welding process. **(7 marks)**

**(b) A 10 mm thick low-carbon steel plate is to be welded by SAW. Calculate the heat input in kJ/mm given $V = 32$ V, $I = 600$ A, $S = 8$ mm/s and $\eta = 0.95$. Also comment on the suitability of this heat input for the chosen material. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Classification (4 marks):**

Present the AWS four-family classification:
- **Family 1 – Arc Welding:** SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, FCAW, SAW, PAW.
- **Family 2 – Resistance Welding:** RSW, RSEW, RPW, FBW.
- **Family 3 – Oxy-Fuel Gas Welding:** OAW, OHW.
- **Family 4 – Solid-State & Other:** FRW, FSW, USW, LBW, EBW, Brazing, Soldering.

(For full marks, list at least **8 processes** distributed across all 4 families — 1 mark per family for naming, 1 mark for sub-categorisation and 1 mark for neat description, totaling 4 marks.)

**Step 2 — Principle of GMAW / MIG (3 marks):**

In Gas Metal Arc Welding **(1 mark)**:
- A **continuous solid wire electrode** is fed automatically through a welding gun.
- The arc is struck between the **wire (acting as electrode)** and the **workpiece**.
- A **shielding gas** (Argon, CO₂, or Ar/CO₂ mix) flows from the nozzle to protect the molten pool from atmospheric contamination **(1 mark)**.
- The wire melts and is deposited as filler metal in the joint; weld pool solidifies to form the joint **(1 mark)**.

(Alternatively, GTAW or SAW may be explained for 3 marks.)

---

**Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Data extraction (1 mark):**
$V = 32$ V, $I = 600$ A, $S = 8$ mm/s, $\eta = 0.95$.

**Step 2 — Power (1 mark):**

$$P = V \cdot I = 32 \times 600 = 19200 \text{ W} = 19.2 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Useful power (1 mark):**

$$P_{u} = \eta \cdot P = 0.95 \times 19.2 = 18.24 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Heat input (2 marks):**

$$H = \dfrac{P_{u}}{S \times 1000} = \dfrac{18240}{8 \times 1000} = \dfrac{18240}{8000} = 2.28 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Step 5 — Comment on suitability (2 marks):**

For a 10 mm thick low-carbon steel plate, the **recommended heat input range is 1.5 – 3.0 kJ/mm** as per EN 10164 and AWS D1.1 **(1 mark)**.
The computed value of **2.28 kJ/mm falls within this acceptable range**, indicating that the SAW parameters are suitable. Distortion is acceptable, no preheat is required (low Ceq). For higher impact-toughness requirements, the heat input should be reduced by increasing travel speed to < 7 mm/s. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Heat Input Problems:**
> 1. **Unit trap** — Most students forget to convert J/mm → kJ/mm; losing **1 mark**.
> 2. **Speed unit confusion** — Mixing mm/s and mm/min is a common error; lose **0.5–1 mark**.
> 3. **Omitting η** — Process efficiency MUST be included unless stated "ideal". Lose **1 mark**.
> 4. **No engineering comment** — A pure numerical answer with no industrial interpretation will lose **1–2 marks** out of 7.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, June 2024 Scheme]**

*Q. B. (a)* What are the **important criteria considered in the appraisal of various welding processes**? Compare any **three arc-welding processes** on the basis of these criteria. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **design considerations for welded joints** with reference to **(i) joint type and edge preparation, (ii) accessibility and position, (iii) distortion control. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Appraisal criteria (3 marks):**
- **Material and thickness suitability** (0.5)
- **Weld quality and mechanical properties** (0.5)
- **Heat input and HAZ size** (0.5)
- **Deposition rate / productivity** (0.5)
- **Distortion and residual stress** (0.5)
- **Cost (capital + consumable + labour)** (0.5)

**Step 2 — Comparison table (4 marks):** (any 3 of SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, SAW)

| Criterion | SMAW | GMAW | GTAW |
|-----------|------|------|------|
| **Thickness** | 2–50 mm | 1–25 mm | 0.5–6 mm |
| **Deposition rate** | Low | High | Lowest |
| **Weld quality** | Medium | High | Excellent |
| **Position** | All | All | All |
| **Skill** | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Cost (low vol)** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |

(Marks distributed 1 mark per filled row for 3 processes ≈ 4 marks)

---

**Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Joint type and edge preparation (2 marks):**
- Butt, lap, T, corner and edge joints each have characteristic **accessibility** and **load-transfer** behaviour.
- For plates > 6 mm, **V, U, J or double-V grooves** are prepared to ensure full penetration.
- Edge preparation must follow **AWS / ISO 9692** standards; root gap and included angle directly affect weld metal volume, distortion and cost.

**(ii) Accessibility and welding position (2 marks):**
- The four positions: **Flat (1G), Horizontal (2F/2G), Vertical (3F/3G), Overhead (4F/4G)**.
- Processes like **SAW are restricted to flat / horizontal-fillet**, whereas **GMAW, GTAW, SMAW are "all-position"**.
- For assemblies where the joint is in a confined area, **narrow-gap GMAW or LBW** is preferred for accessibility.

**(iii) Distortion control (3 marks):**
Distortion types: **longitudinal shrinkage, transverse shrinkage, angular distortion, buckling and rotation.**
Control measures:
1. **Reduce heat input** — use GMAW pulsed or LBW. (0.5)
2. **Symmetric joint design** — back-to-back welds, balanced groove. (0.5)
3. **Use strongback fixtures and clamping** during welding. (0.5)
4. **Weld sequence planning** — backstep, block, or cascade sequence to balance shrinkage. (0.5)
5. **Use low-expansion / low-distortion processes** — FSW, RSW, LBW. (0.5)
6. **Post-weld stress-relief (PWHT)** for thick sections. (0.5)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Welding Process Comparison Questions:**
> 1. **Writing only theory without comparison table** — Examiners expect a **tabular comparison**; a narrative-only answer loses **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention process efficiency (η)** in any numerical problem — penalised 1 mark.
> 3. **Conflating "deposition rate" with "travel speed"** — they are different; lose 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Ignoring safety / shielding gas / ventilation** in process selection — KTU 2024 emphasises safety; 0.5 mark lost if omitted.
> 5. **Stating GMAW = TIG** — They are *not* the same; examiners treat this as a conceptual error (−1 mark).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Exam 5-Minute Revision Block — Read this aloud twice.**

- ☐ **Weldability = Ceq rule of thumb:** if $C_{eq} \le 0.40$ % → excellent; $0.40$–$0.60$ % → preheat; > $0.60$ % → special procedure.
- ☐ **Heat Input $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{S \times 1000}$ kJ/mm** — must include efficiency $\eta$.
- ☐ **High H ⇒ wide HAZ, more distortion, coarse grains ⇒ low toughness.**
- ☐ **AWS four families** — Arc / Resistance / Oxy-Fuel / Solid-State (+ Brazing).
- ☐ **Highest deposition rate** = SAW; **Lowest heat input** = LBW / USW.
- ☐ **Fillet weld leg = 6 mm** ⇒ cross-section $A_w = 0.707 \times \text{leg}^2$.
- ☐ **Spot welding** → sheet metal auto body; **FSW** → Al-alloy aerospace; **SAW** → thick ship / pressure-vessel plates; **GTAW** → root pass for critical joints.
- ☐ **Joint efficiency (η)** rarely exceeds 100 %; design codes (ASME) cap it at 100 %.
- ☐ **Distortion control** — minimise H, balance welds, use clamps, plan sequence, post-weld stress relief.
- ☐ **Cost appraisal** = setup + (labour + filler + gas + power) × time.
- ☐ **Solid-state welding = no fusion** ⇒ no HAZ, no melting-related defects, ideal for dissimilar / heat-sensitive metals.
- ☐ **Always quote the standard (AWS D1.1, ASME IX, EN 1090, ISO 9692)** in answers for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors in Design of Weldments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors in Design of Weldments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)**, **Weldment Design** refers to the systematic engineering approach of designing a fabricated structure by permanently joining two or more metal parts through fusion, using heat, pressure, or both, while optimizing the geometry, material, joint configuration, and welding process parameters to ensure **manufacturability, structural integrity, cost efficiency, and ease of assembly**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEBRT524 – Module 2):**
> "Weldment design is the process of integrating material selection, joint type, weld size, weld position, and process parameters into a unified design that minimizes fabrication cost, distortion, and post-weld operations while maximizing joint strength and service life."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are building a **bookshelf** from metal rods. You have two choices:
1. **Cast the entire shelf as one piece** → expensive mold, hard to modify, heavy.
2. **Cut rods and join them by welding** → cheap, flexible, light, but if you weld poorly, the joints crack under load, and the whole shelf wobbles.

**Weldment design** is the art of *planning the bookshelf* so that:
- The **cuts are simple** (straight, standard length).
- The **joints are easy to reach** with a welding torch.
- The **weld size is just enough** to carry the load (not more, not less).
- The **heat does not warp** the structure.
- The **cost per joint is minimal**.

In short: **a good weldment is one that is easy to fabricate, strong in service, and cheap to make.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Standard Reference:**
> The universally accepted code governing weldment design is the **American Welding Society (AWS) D1.1 – Structural Welding Code**, which defines weld symbols, joint types, and quality acceptance criteria. For pressure vessels, **ASME Section IX** governs procedure qualification.

### 1.3 Why "Factors" in Weldment Design?

A weldment is not designed in a single step. It is governed by **multiple interacting factors**. Designers must balance them like ingredients in a recipe — too much of one spoils the dish.

The **primary factors** that govern weldment design are:

1. **Material Selection** (carbon steel, alloy steel, stainless, aluminum, etc.)
2. **Welding Process Selection** (SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, SAW, etc.)
3. **Joint Type** (butt, lap, T, corner, edge)
4. **Weld Type** (fillet, groove, plug, slot, spot, seam)
5. **Weld Size and Length**
6. **Weld Position** (flat, horizontal, vertical, overhead)
7. **Accessibility of the Weld**
8. **Distortion Control and Heat Input**
9. **Residual Stresses and Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)**
10. **Service Conditions** (load, temperature, corrosion, fatigue)
11. **Cost of Welding and Inspection**
12. **Weld Symbol Standardization (AWS A2.4)**
13. **Edge Preparation and Fit-up Tolerance**

Each of these is a *factor* — and the skill of a design engineer is in **simultaneously optimizing all of them**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Factors in Weldment Design

The factors can be grouped into **four logical clusters**:

#### Cluster A — Material & Process Factors
| Factor | Description | KTU Board Tip |
|---|---|---|
| **Base Metal** | Carbon steel, low-alloy steel, stainless steel, aluminum, titanium | Aluminum needs **GTAW (TIG)** for clean welds |
| **Weldability** | Ability of a material to be welded without defects | Measured by **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** |
| **Welding Process** | SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, SAW, FCAW, PAW, LBW | Choice depends on **position, thickness, material** |
| **Filler Metal** | Electrode or wire composition | Must match base metal strength and chemistry |

#### Cluster B — Geometric & Joint Design Factors
| Factor | Description | KTU Board Tip |
|---|---|---|
| **Joint Type** | Butt, Lap, T, Corner, Edge | Butt joints are stronger per unit length |
| **Weld Type** | Fillet, Groove, Plug, Slot | Fillet is most common in fabrication |
| **Weld Size (leg length, throat)** | Governs load capacity | Oversizing wastes filler and adds distortion |
| **Weld Length** | Total length of deposited weld | Longer welds = more cost, more heat |
| **Edge Preparation** | Bevel angle, root gap, root face | Required for plates > 6 mm in butt joints |
| **Fit-up Tolerance** | Gap between parts | Poor fit-up = poor penetration, defects |

#### Cluster C — Thermal & Mechanical Factors
| Factor | Description | KTU Board Tip |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat Input** | Energy deposited per unit length | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ (kJ/mm) |
| **Distortion** | Shrinkage warping of the part | Controlled by **balanced welding, clamping, sequencing** |
| **Residual Stresses** | Locked-in stresses from cooling | Cause cracking in service |
| **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** | Region near weld with changed microstructure | Weakest zone in most welds |
| **Cooling Rate** | Affects hardness and HAZ | High carbon steels → fast cooling → hard, brittle HAZ |

#### Cluster D — Economic & Service Factors
| Factor | Description | KTU Board Tip |
|---|---|---|
| **Welding Cost** | Labour + filler + gas + electricity | ~60–80% of total fab cost is welding |
| **Inspection & Testing** | NDT (RT, UT, MT, PT) | Mandatory for critical welds |
| **Service Load** | Static, dynamic, fatigue | Fatigue = use full-penetration welds |
| **Service Environment** | Corrosive, high-temp, cryogenic | Affects filler selection |
| **Safety Factor** | Code requirement (AWS, ASME) | Typical 1.5 to 4.0 |

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are the most tested in KTU ESE for Module 2. Memorize them in this exact notation.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Static stress in a member | N/mm² |
| 2 | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{0.707 \cdot h \cdot L}$ | Shear stress in a **fillet weld** | N/mm² |
| 3 | $A_{\text{throat}} = 0.707 \cdot h \cdot L$ | Throat area of a fillet weld | mm² |
| 4 | $L = \dfrac{F}{\tau_{\text{allow}} \cdot 0.707 \cdot h}$ | Required **fillet weld length** | mm |
| 5 | $h = \dfrac{F}{0.707 \cdot L \cdot \tau_{\text{allow}}}$ | Required **fillet weld leg size** | mm |
| 6 | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot v}$ | **Heat input** per unit length | kJ/mm |
| 7 | $CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | **Carbon Equivalent** (IIW formula) | % |
| 8 | $P_{\text{penetration}} = \dfrac{d^2 \cdot v}{2}$ | Approximate power for arc penetration | — |
| 9 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot Q}{c \cdot \rho \cdot V}$ | Temperature rise from welding heat | °C |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_{\text{shrink}} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Free thermal strain | — |
| 11 | $\sigma_{\text{residual}} = E \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{shrink}}$ | Residual stress estimate (constrained) | N/mm² |
| 12 | $W_{\text{filler}} = A_{\text{deposit}} \cdot L \cdot \rho_{\text{filler}}$ | Filler metal weight required | kg |

Where the standard symbols are:
- $F$ = Force on the weld (N)
- $h$ = Leg size of fillet weld (mm)
- $L$ = Length of weld (mm)
- $0.707$ = $\sin 45°$, the throat-to-leg ratio for a $45°$ fillet
- $V$ = Arc voltage (V)
- $I$ = Welding current (A)
- $v$ = Travel speed (mm/s)
- $\eta$ = **Process efficiency** (0.7 for GMAW, 0.8 for SAW, 0.85 for GTAW)
- $CE$ = Carbon equivalent
- $\alpha$ = Coefficient of thermal expansion (≈ 12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C for steel)
- $E$ = Young's modulus (≈ 2 × 10⁵ N/mm² for steel)

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Formulas

- **Formulas 2–5** are used daily by **structural engineers** designing bridges, cranes, and building frames to size welds.
- **Formula 6** (heat input) is the *single most important* parameter controlled on a shop floor to prevent HAZ cracking.
- **Formula 7** (CE) decides whether a steel is *weldable* without preheating. If **CE > 0.45**, preheat is mandatory.
- **Formulas 9–11** guide **weld sequencing** to minimize distortion in ship hulls, pressure vessels, and large fabrications.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In KTU papers, Part B questions (14 marks) on this topic almost always require combining *one weld-strength formula* (2, 3, 4, or 5) with *one thermal/distortion formula* (6, 9, 10, or 11). Practice combining them.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Fillet Weld Strength (Throat Area Method)

A **fillet weld** is a triangular cross-section weld deposited in the corner of two plates. Its geometry is an **isosceles right triangle** (standard $45°$ included angle).

**Given:**
- Leg size of the fillet weld $= h$ (mm)
- Length of the weld $= L$ (mm)
- The throat is the **shortest distance from the weld root to the weld face**, which is the hypotenuse of half the triangle.

**Step 1: Geometry of the fillet weld**

The cross-section is a right triangle with both legs equal to $h$.

$$
\text{Acute angle} = 45°, \quad \text{Opposite leg} = h
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the throat thickness ($t$)**

The throat is opposite the $45°$ angle, so:
$$
\sin 45° = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \dfrac{t}{h}
$$

$$
t = h \cdot \sin 45° = h \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

Rationalizing:
$$
t = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{h \cdot \sqrt{2}}{2} = 0.707 \cdot h
$$

**Step 3: Throat area ($A_{\text{throat}}$)**

For a weld of length $L$:
$$
A_{\text{throat}} = t \cdot L = 0.707 \cdot h \cdot L
$$

**Step 4: Shear force capacity**

The fillet weld is assumed to fail by **shear along the throat plane** (this is the AWS and IS 816 design assumption). If the allowable shear stress of the weld metal is $\tau_{\text{allow}}$:
$$
F_{\text{allow}} = \tau_{\text{allow}} \cdot A_{\text{throat}} = \tau_{\text{allow}} \cdot 0.707 \cdot h \cdot L
$$

**Step 5: Rearrange to find required weld length or leg size**

$$
L = \dfrac{F}{0.707 \cdot h \cdot \tau_{\text{allow}}}
$$

$$
h = \dfrac{F}{0.707 \cdot L \cdot \tau_{\text{allow}}}
$$

**Result — These are formulas (2), (4), and (5) from the KTU Cheat Sheet.** ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Heat Input (Energy per Unit Weld Length)

**Given:**
- Arc voltage $= V$ (V)
- Welding current $= I$ (A)
- Travel speed $= v$ (mm/s or mm/min)
- Process thermal efficiency $= \eta$ (dimensionless, 0 < η < 1)

**Step 1: Electrical power input to the arc**

Power supplied by the welding machine:
$$
P = V \cdot I \quad \text{(Watts = Joules/second)}
$$

**Step 2: Heat actually transferred to the workpiece**

Not all electrical energy becomes heat in the workpiece. A fraction $\eta$ is transferred, the rest is lost to radiation, spatter, filler heating, etc.

$$
P_{\text{effective}} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I
$$

**Step 3: Heat per unit length of weld**

If the torch moves at speed $v$ (mm/s), the energy deposited per millimetre of weld is:
$$
H = \dfrac{P_{\text{effective}}}{v} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v} \quad \text{(J/mm)}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to standard engineering unit (kJ/mm)**

$$
H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot v} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}
$$

This is the **single most important formula** in weldment design, because it directly controls:
- **HAZ width** (larger $H$ → wider HAZ)
- **Distortion** (larger $H$ → more distortion)
- **Cooling rate** (larger $H$ → slower cooling → softer HAZ for carbon steel, but also coarser grains → lower toughness)
- **Productivity** (larger $H$ → faster deposition, but only up to a quality limit)

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (Model KTU-style)

> **[KTU University Exam Style – 14 Mark Pattern, Sub-question 7 Marks]**

**Problem:**
A bracket carries a static tensile load of $F = 80,\!000$ N. It is welded to a column using **two parallel fillet welds** on either side of the bracket. The leg size is $h = 8$ mm and the weld length is $L = 120$ mm. The allowable shear stress of the weld metal is $\tau_{\text{allow}} = 110$ N/mm². Check whether the weld is safe.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1: Total throat area (two welds)**
$$
A_{\text{throat, total}} = 2 \cdot 0.707 \cdot h \cdot L
$$

$$
A_{\text{throat, total}} = 2 \cdot 0.707 \cdot 8 \cdot 120
$$

$$
A_{\text{throat, total}} = 1357.44 \quad \text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 2: Allowable load capacity**
$$
F_{\text{allow}} = \tau_{\text{allow}} \cdot A_{\text{throat, total}}
$$

$$
F_{\text{allow}} = 110 \cdot 1357.44
$$

$$
F_{\text{allow}} = 149,\!318.4 \quad \text{N} \approx 149.32 \quad \text{kN}
$$

**Step 3: Compare with applied load**
$$
F = 80,\!000 \quad \text{N} = 80 \quad \text{kN}
$$

$$
F_{\text{allow}} = 149.32 \quad \text{kN} \gg F = 80 \quad \text{kN}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Factor of Safety (FoS)**
$$
\text{FoS} = \dfrac{F_{\text{allow}}}{F} = \dfrac{149.32}{80} = 1.866
$$

**Conclusion:** The weld is **SAFE** with a Factor of Safety of **1.87**, which is above the typical code requirement of 1.5. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always show **all four** steps: Geometry → Throat → Allowable Load → Comparison. This is exactly the KTU board valuation key structure.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How Factors Interact

| If this factor **increases**... | Then this factor **increases** (+) or **decreases** (−)... | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| Heat input $H$ | HAZ width (+), Distortion (+), Cooling time (+) | Avoid for quench-sensitive steels |
| Weld leg size $h$ | Strength (+), Filler cost (+), Heat input (+) | Don't over-size welds |
| Carbon content C | Hardness (+), Weldability (−), Cracking risk (+) | Use low-C steel or preheat |
| Preheat temperature | HAZ hardness (−), Cracking risk (−), Cost (+) | Mandatory if CE > 0.45 |
| Weld length $L$ | Strength (+), Time (+), Cost (+), Distortion (+) | Use intermittent welds where allowed |
| Clamping rigidity | Distortion (−), Residual stress (+) | Balance — over-clamp causes stress |
| Use of backing strip | Penetration (+), Cost (+), Inspection (+) | Standard in code welding |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Weldment Design Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Functional Requirement] --> B{Determine Load Type}
    B -- Static --> C1[Calculate Force F]
    B -- Fatigue --> C2[Use Full Penetration Weld]
    B -- Impact --> C3[Use Tough Filler]
    C1 --> D[Select Base Metal]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Calculate Carbon Equivalent CE]
    E --> F{CE greater than 0.45}
    F -- Yes --> G1[Apply Preheat 100-200 C]
    F -- No --> G2[No Preheat Needed]
    G1 --> H[Select Welding Process]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Choose Joint Type and Weld Type]
    I --> J[Calculate Weld Size using Throat Formula]
    J --> K[Check Heat Input H]
    K --> L{H within limit}
    L -- Yes --> M[Plan Weld Sequence for Distortion Control]
    L -- No --> N[Reduce Current or Increase Speed]
    N --> K
    M --> O[Apply AWS Weld Symbol on Drawing]
    O --> P[Issue for Fabrication]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Four Pillars of Weldment Design

```mermaid

    subgraph P1[Pillar 1: Material]
        M1[Base Metal]
        M2[Filler Metal]
        M3[Shielding Gas]
    end
    subgraph P2[Pillar 2: Geometry]
        G1[Joint Type]
        G2[Weld Type]
        G3[Edge Preparation]
    end

        PR2[Heat Input Control]
        PR3[Position of Welding]
    end
```
