```mermaid
        E1[Cost per Weld]
        E2[NDT Requirement]
        E3[Service Load and Environment]
    end
    P1 --> OUT[Optimum Weldment Design]
    P2 --> OUT
    P3 --> OUT
    P4 --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Fillet Weld Geometry (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plate 1 Horizontal] --> B[Fillet Weld with Leg h]
    C[Plate 2 Vertical] --> B
    B --> D[Throat t equals 0.707 times h]
    B --> E[Length L along weld axis]
    D --> F[Fails by shear along throat plane]
    E --> F
```

### 4.4 Heat Input vs. HAZ vs. Distortion — Causal Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Increase Current I] --> B[Increase Heat Input H]
    C[Decrease Travel Speed v] --> B
    B --> D[Increase HAZ Width]
    B --> E[Increase Distortion]
    B --> F[Decrease Cooling Rate]
    D --> G[Reduce Joint Toughness]
    E --> H[Dimensional Inaccuracy]
    F --> I[Coarse Grain in HAZ]
    G --> J[Possible Service Failure]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*List any **six factors** that must be considered in the design of weldments.*

**Model Answer (6 points):**

1. **Material selection** – base metal type, weldability, thickness.
2. **Joint type and weld type** – butt/lap, fillet/groove.
3. **Weld size and length** – throat area must be sufficient.
4. **Welding process** – SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, SAW.
5. **Heat input and distortion control.**
6. **Residual stresses and heat-affected zone (HAZ).**
7. **Accessibility and weld position.**
8. **Cost of welding, filler metal, and inspection.**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** and state its significance in weldment design.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** is a numerical value that expresses the combined effect of carbon and other alloying elements on the **weldability of steel**. The **IIW (International Institute of Welding) formula** is:

$$
CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}
$$

**Significance:**

- If $CE \le 0.45$ → steel is **weldable without preheating** under normal conditions.
- If $CE > 0.45$ → **preheating is mandatory** to slow cooling, reduce HAZ hardness, and prevent **hydrogen-induced cracking**.
- Higher CE → greater **hardenability**, but also greater **cracking risk** in the HAZ.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A tie-bar of a structural frame carries a **tensile force of 120 kN** and is joined to a gusset plate by **two parallel fillet welds**, one on each side. The length of each weld is **150 mm**, and the allowable shear stress in the weld is **108 N/mm²**. Calculate the required **leg size $h$** of the fillet weld. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain **any four** important factors that influence the design of weldments, with one sentence on each. (7 marks)

---

##### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

**Step 1: Apply the throat-area strength formula**

$$
F = 2 \cdot \tau_{\text{allow}} \cdot 0.707 \cdot h \cdot L
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the known values**

$$
120,\!000 = 2 \cdot 108 \cdot 0.707 \cdot h \cdot 150
$$

**Step 3: Simplify the right-hand side**

$$
120,\!000 = 108 \cdot 0.707 \cdot 300 \cdot h
$$

$$
120,\!000 = 22,\!906.8 \cdot h
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $h$**

$$
h = \dfrac{120,\!000}{22,\!906.8} = 5.238 \quad \text{mm}
$$

**Step 5: Apply code rounding**

The AWS code requires rounding **up** to the next standard size. Standard leg sizes are 5, 6, 8, 10, 12 mm.

$$
\boxed{h_{\text{provided}} = 6 \quad \text{mm}}
$$

**Verification of new FoS:**
$$
F_{\text{allow}} = 2 \cdot 108 \cdot 0.707 \cdot 6 \cdot 150 = 137,\!441 \quad \text{N}
$$

$$
\text{FoS} = \dfrac{137,\!441}{120,\!000} = 1.145
$$

The FoS is just above 1.0, which is low. A practical designer would select **$h = 8$ mm** for a safer design.

---

##### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Four important factors influencing weldment design:**

1. **Material Selection:** The base metal determines weldability, filler choice, and preheat requirement. Higher carbon and alloy content lowers weldability.

2. **Joint Type and Geometry:** Butt joints are stronger but require edge preparation; lap joints are easier but introduce eccentric loading. Joint design must minimize stress concentration.

3. **Heat Input:** Excessive heat input causes distortion, wide HAZ, and grain coarsening. It must be controlled by adjusting current, voltage, and travel speed.

4. **Weld Position and Accessibility:** Welds should be designed in the **flat (1G/1F)** position wherever possible, as it is the most economical and produces the highest quality.

5. *(Optional additional factor)* **Cost of Welding:** Total weld cost includes labour, filler, gas, electricity, and inspection — the weldment should use the *minimum weld metal* that satisfies strength.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A lap joint between two 6 mm thick mild-steel plates is made by **fillet welding on both sides**. The joint is subjected to a **shear force of 50 kN**. The weld length is **100 mm** on each side, and the allowable shear stress of the weld is **110 N/mm²**. Determine whether the joint is adequate. If not, find the required leg size. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **fillet weld geometry** and derive the expression for the **throat thickness** in terms of leg size. (7 marks)

---

##### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**Step 1: Given data**
- $F = 50,\!000$ N
- $L = 100$ mm per side, **2 sides** → total effective length $= 200$ mm
- $\tau_{\text{allow}} = 110$ N/mm²
- Plate thickness $= 6$ mm → maximum possible leg size $h = 6$ mm (cannot exceed plate thickness)

**Step 2: Allowable force with $h = 6$ mm**

$$
F_{\text{allow}} = 2 \cdot \tau_{\text{allow}} \cdot 0.707 \cdot h \cdot L
$$

$$
F_{\text{allow}} = 2 \cdot 110 \cdot 0.707 \cdot 6 \cdot 100
$$

$$
F_{\text{allow}} = 93,\!324 \quad \text{N} \approx 93.32 \quad \text{kN}
$$

**Step 3: Check adequacy**
$$
F_{\text{allow}} = 93.32 \quad \text{kN} > F = 50 \quad \text{kN} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The joint is **ADEQUATE** with the maximum possible leg size of 6 mm.

**Step 4 (optional): Find minimum required $h$**

$$
h = \dfrac{F}{2 \cdot \tau_{\text{allow}} \cdot 0.707 \cdot L}
$$

$$
h = \dfrac{50,\!000}{2 \cdot 110 \cdot 0.707 \cdot 100} = 3.21 \quad \text{mm}
$$

So technically only 3.21 mm is needed, but 6 mm is used as the practical maximum.

---

##### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Fillet Weld Geometry — Sketch (in Mermaid block form):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plate A horizontal at top] --> B[Corner where two plates meet]
    C[Plate B horizontal at bottom] --> B
    B --> D[Fillet weld triangle with leg h and leg h]
    D --> E[Throat t equals 0.707 times h perpendicular to weld face]
    D --> F[Length L along weld direction]
    D --> G[Failure plane along throat]
```

**Derivation of Throat Thickness:**

The cross-section of a standard fillet weld is an **isosceles right triangle** with two equal legs of length $h$ and an included angle of $90°$ between the two parent plates (and hence $45°$ at the weld root).

The **throat** is the shortest distance from the root of the weld to its face, lying along a line at $45°$ to both legs.

By trigonometry:
$$
\sin 45° = \dfrac{\text{throat}}{\text{leg}} = \dfrac{t}{h}
$$

$$
t = h \cdot \sin 45° = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707 \cdot h
$$

This is the **throat thickness**. The throat is the **failure plane** because it is the smallest cross-section, and welds are assumed to fail in **shear** along this plane per AWS D1.1.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on Weldment Design Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for double-sided welds** — Most common error. If the problem says *two parallel fillet welds* or *welded on both sides*, multiply the throat area by 2.
> 2. **Using $h$ instead of $0.707h$ for the throat** — The throat is NEVER equal to the leg. The throat is **always** $0.707 h$. Using $h$ overestimates strength by 41% and loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units** — Heat input must be in kJ/mm, not J/mm. Stress must be in N/mm², not MPa (they are numerically equal, but write the unit).
> 4. **Not rounding weld size UP to the next standard size** — KTU board examiners mark this strictly.
> 5. **Confusing CE formula (IIW vs. Pcm)** — For KTU, **always use the IIW formula**: $CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$.
> 6. **Skipping the sketch** — A question worth 7 marks without a diagram loses at least 1–2 marks. **Always draw** the joint geometry, label $h$, $L$, and the throat.
> 7. **Writing "welding is easy" in cost questions** — Cost = labour + filler + gas + electricity + overhead + NDT. Show all six.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary)

- **Weldment design balances 13+ factors** — material, joint type, weld type, size, length, process, position, accessibility, heat input, distortion, residual stress, HAZ, and cost.
- **The single most important formula for fillet weld strength:**
  $$\tau = \dfrac{F}{0.707 \cdot h \cdot L}$$
  Memorize it, derive it, and apply it for any fillet weld problem.
- **Throat thickness** is always $0.707 h$, never $h$. The throat is the assumed failure plane.
- **For double-sided or double-welded joints**, multiply the area by **2** — never forget this.
- **Heat input** controls distortion and HAZ:
  $$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot v} \quad \text{kJ/mm}$$
- **Carbon Equivalent (IIW formula):**
  $$CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$$
  If $CE > 0.45$, **preheat** is mandatory.
- **Always design welds in the flat (1G/1F) position** — it is the cheapest and gives the best quality.
- **Minimum weld size** is governed by the **thicker of the two plates** (AWS D1.1 Table 5.4 or IS 816 Table).
- **Maximum weld leg size** = thickness of the thinner plate.
- **Intermittent welds** are cheaper but only permitted in **low-stress, non-corrosive, non-fatigue** applications.
- **Backing strips** ensure full penetration in butt welds and reduce cost.
- **Weld symbols follow AWS A2.4** — every weldment drawing must specify the symbol, size, length, pitch, and contour.
- **Total weld cost ≈ 60–80%** of total fabrication cost in welded structures.
- **Always round up** the calculated leg size to the next standard value (5, 6, 8, 10, 12 mm).
- **Final formula to remember** — for design of **fillet weld length** when force, leg size, and allowable stress are given:
  $$L = \dfrac{F}{0.707 \cdot h \cdot \tau_{\text{allow}}}$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Design Guidelines for Weldments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Design Guidelines for Weldments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Weldment** refers to a metal assembly formed by permanently joining two or more components through fusion, with or without filler material, using heat and/or pressure. **General Design Guidelines for Weldments** constitute a structured set of engineering rules and best practices governing weld type selection, sizing, placement, sequencing, and inspection to ensure structural integrity, manufacturability, cost-efficiency, and compliance with codes such as **AWS D1.1 (American Welding Society Structural Welding Code)**, **IS 816 (Code of Practice for Use of Metal Arc Welding for General Construction in Mild Steel)**, and **Eurocode 3 (EN 1993)**.

A weldment design guideline fundamentally addresses:
- **Geometric parameters** (leg length $h$, throat thickness $t$, weld length $L$)
- **Mechanical parameters** (allowable shear stress $\tau_{all}$, tensile stress $\sigma_{all}$)
- **Process parameters** (welding process, joint preparation, accessibility)
- **Quality parameters** (defect tolerance, inspection criteria)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A weldment is *not* just a fabricated joint — it is a **system** where every decision (material, joint type, weld size, sequence) directly governs the structural reliability, fatigue life, distortion behavior, and total cost of the product.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

Imagine you are stitching two pieces of cloth using a needle and thread. Now, suppose the cloth is made of **steel**, the thread is **molten metal**, and the needle is an **electric arc**. Suddenly, every tiny detail matters:

- The **length of the stitch** (weld length) determines how much the joint can hold.
- The **thickness of the thread** (weld size) decides whether it tears or holds.
- The **direction of the stitch** (weld orientation) — parallel or perpendicular to the load — changes the strength drastically.
- **Pulling too hard during stitching** (weld distortion) wrinkles the cloth (the part warps).

In the same way, **General Design Guidelines for Weldments** are like the **"master tailoring instructions"** for fabricating strong, reliable, distortion-free, and economical welded assemblies.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Engineering Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit | Governing Code |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Throat thickness (equal leg) | $t$ | $0.707\,h$ | AWS D1.1 |
| Minimum fillet weld size | $h_{min}$ | Function of thicker plate thickness | AWS D1.1 Table 5.8 |
| Maximum fillet weld size | $h_{max}$ | Plate thickness (for edge welds) | AWS D1.1 |
| Effective weld length | $L_e$ | Actual length $- 2h$ | AWS D1.1 |
| Minimum pitch of plug/slot welds | $p_{min}$ | $4 \times$ center-to-center of holes | AWS D1.1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 0.707?** For an equal-leg fillet weld with leg size $h$, the throat $t$ lies along the 45° bisector, so $t = h \cos 45° = h / \sqrt{2} \approx 0.707\,h$. This is the **shortest distance** from the weld root to the face — the plane where failure typically initiates.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Welds and Their Design Roles

Welds are broadly classified into two categories:

### A. **Fillet Welds** (Most Common in Design)
- Triangular cross-section joining two surfaces at right angles (or at an angle between 60°–120°).
- Failure occurs by **shear** along the throat plane.
- Governed by **shear strength** (typically the weakest mode).
- Common types:
  - **Transverse fillet weld** — load perpendicular to weld axis → higher strength.
  - **Parallel (longitudinal) fillet weld** — load parallel to weld axis → lower strength.
  - **Inclined fillet weld** — load at angle $\theta$ to weld axis.

### B. **Groove (Butt) Welds**
- Full penetration welds with beveled or square edge preparation.
- Failure occurs by **tension or compression** in the parent metal.
- **Strongest weld type**; throat = plate thickness.
- Types: Square, V, U, J, bevel (single or double).

### C. **Plug & Slot Welds**
- Holes/slots filled with weld metal — used for **secondary attachments** and **stiffeners**.
- Limited load transfer; design strength is significantly lower than fillet or groove welds.

### D. **Spot, Seam, Projection Welds** (Resistance Welding)
- Used in **sheet metal** applications (automotive bodies, cabinets).
- Weld size = nugget diameter.

## 2.2 Core Design Logic — The Six Pillars of Weldment Design

### Pillar 1: **Strength Adequacy**
The weld must transfer the applied load without exceeding allowable stress. For a fillet weld of leg $h$ and length $L$:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{0.707\,h\,L} \le \tau_{all}$$

### Pillar 2: **Minimum Weld Size (Prevents Brittle Failure)**
To prevent **under-sized welds** that crack under thermal stresses, AWS D1.1 prescribes minimum sizes based on the **thickness of the thicker plate joined** $t_{max}$:

| Thicker Plate Thickness $t$ (mm) | Minimum Fillet Weld Size $h_{min}$ (mm) |
| :---: | :---: |
| $t \le 6$ | **3** |
| $6 < t \le 13$ | **5** |
| $13 < t \le 19$ | **6** |
| $19 < t \le 38$ | **8** |
| $38 < t \le 57$ | **10** |
| $57 < t \le 150$ | **12** |
| $t > 150$ | **16** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This rule ensures the weld has enough **mass** to resist the rapid cooling stresses that occur upon solidification. A thin weld on a thick plate solidifies too quickly → cracks.

### Pillar 3: **Maximum Weld Size (Prevents Plate Melt-Through)**
- For welds **along edges of plates less than 6 mm thick**: $h_{max} = t$
- For welds **along edges of plates 6 mm or thicker**: $h_{max} = t - 1.5$ mm (or $t - 1/16$ inch)

### Pillar 4: **Effective Weld Length**
The **start** and **end** of a weld are inherently weaker due to crater defects. Hence:

$$L_e = L_{actual} - 2h$$

If $L_e \le 4h$, the weld is considered a **small weld** and its strength is further reduced by 50% (AWS D1.1 §2.3.2.7).

### Pillar 5: **Distortion Control**
Welds shrink on cooling, causing **angular distortion**, **longitudinal shrinkage**, and **bowing**. Design guidelines:
- Use **intermittent welds** instead of continuous welds (where strength permits).
- Place welds about the **neutral axis** (balanced welds).
- Use **double-V** or **double-bevel** grooves instead of single-V for thick plates.
- Specify **backing strips** or **weld tabs** to control start/end defects.
- Sequence welds to balance shrinkage.

### Pillar 6: **Accessibility & Inspection**
- Provide **minimum 150 mm clearance** for manual welding electrodes (or 50 mm minimum for robotic welding).
- Design for **double-sided access** wherever possible.
- Include **weld access holes** at intersecting joints to prevent lamellar tearing.

## 2.3 Weld Orientation Effects — Parallel vs. Transverse

This is one of the **most critical** concepts in KTU examinations.

- **Transverse fillet welds** develop **direct shear + bending** on the throat → they are **stronger per unit length** (typically 30–50% stronger than parallel).
- **Parallel fillet welds** develop **pure shear** on the throat → weaker per unit length, but the **end returns** (wrapping the weld around the corner) can add up to 50% strength by eliminating end-crater defects.

For an **inclined load** at angle $\theta$ to the weld axis, the resultant force per unit length is:

$$f_R = \sqrt{f_p^2 + f_t^2}$$

where $f_p$ is the parallel component per unit length and $f_t$ is the transverse component per unit length.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In bridge girders, **transverse stiffener welds** are the primary load paths — they are designed to be full-strength fillets. In ship hulls, **longitudinal welds** dominate the shear flow between plates and must be continuous for watertight integrity.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $t = 0.707\,h$ | Throat thickness of equal-leg fillet weld | Geometry |
| 2 | $t = h\,\cos(45°)$ | Same as above (alternate form) | Geometry |
| 3 | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{0.707\,h\,L}$ | Average shear stress in fillet weld | Strength check |
| 4 | $F = 0.707\,h\,L\,\tau_{all}$ | Allowable load on fillet weld | Sizing the weld |
| 5 | $h = \dfrac{F}{0.707\,L\,\tau_{all}}$ | Required leg size | Sizing the weld |
| 6 | $L_e = L - 2h$ | Effective weld length | End crater allowance |
| 7 | $f_R = \sqrt{f_p^2 + f_t^2}$ | Resultant force per unit length (inclined load) | Combined loading |
| 8 | $f_R = (1 - \sin\theta)f_p + 0.707\,\sin\theta\,f_t$ | Combined strength (AWS ultimate method) | Ultimate strength |
| 9 | $h_{min}$ from table (Pillar 2) | Minimum fillet weld size per AWS | Weld specification |
| 10 | $h_{max} = t - 1.5$ mm | Maximum fillet weld along edge | Weld specification |
| 11 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{t_p \cdot L}$ | Stress in groove (butt) weld | Groove weld design |
| 12 | $A_{throat} = t \cdot L$ | Shear area of fillet weld | Shear strength |
| 13 | $A_{plug} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$ | Shear area of single plug weld | Plug weld design |
| 14 | $P_{plug} = \tau_{all} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$ | Allowable load on plug weld | Plug weld capacity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always use `cos 45° = 0.707`** (not 0.5, not 0.71) — this is the **AWS standard rounded value** expected in KTU answer sheets.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Throat Thickness in an Equal-Leg Fillet Weld

### Step 1: Geometry Setup
Consider an equal-leg fillet weld with leg size $h$, joining two perpendicular plates. The throat $t$ is the perpendicular distance from the **root of the weld** (at the corner of the plates) to the **face (hypotenuse)** of the weld triangle.

The weld cross-section is an **isosceles right triangle** with:
- Two equal legs of length $h$ meeting at 90° at the root.
- The hypotenuse (face of the weld) is the slanted top surface.

### Step 2: Identify the Geometry of the Throat Plane
The throat plane is the plane of minimum area through the weld. For an equal-leg fillet weld, this plane is oriented at **45°** to both plates.

**Why 45°?** Because both legs are equal ($h = h$), the failure plane bisects the angle between them, which is the line at 45° to the vertical plate and 45° to the horizontal plate.

### Step 3: Apply Trigonometry
In the right triangle formed by leg $h$, leg $h$, and throat $t$ (as the hypotenuse of the half-triangle):

$$\cos 45° = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{t}{h}$$

### Step 4: Solve for Throat
$$t = h \cos 45°$$

$$\boxed{t = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707\,h}$$

### Step 5: Shear Area Derivation
The shear failure occurs along this 45° throat plane. Its area is:

$$A_{throat} = t \times L$$

Substituting $t = 0.707\,h$:

$$A_{throat} = 0.707\,h \times L$$

### Step 6: Shear Stress Equation
By definition of average shear stress:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A_{throat}} = \frac{F}{0.707\,h\,L}$$

### Step 7: Allowable Load Equation
Rearranging for the safe design load $F_{all}$:

$$F_{all} = \tau_{all} \times 0.707\,h \times L$$

### Step 8: Required Weld Size Equation
Rearranging for required leg size $h$ given known load $F$ and length $L$:

$$h = \frac{F}{0.707\,L\,\tau_{all}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Logic:** The factor 0.707 is the **fundamental reason** fillet welds are designed in **shear**, not tension. The throat plane is the smallest load-bearing cross-section, and it is inclined at 45°, so the full load $F$ creates only a shear component on it.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Combined Load on an Inclined Fillet Weld

### Step 1: Set Up the Problem
A fillet weld group is loaded by an **inclined force** $F$ acting at an angle $\theta$ to the weld axis. The force can be decomposed into:
- **Parallel component:** $F_p = F \cos\theta$
- **Transverse component:** $F_t = F \sin\theta$

### Step 2: Compute Force per Unit Length
Dividing by the effective weld length $L_e$:

$$f_p = \frac{F_p}{L_e} = \frac{F \cos\theta}{L_e}$$

$$f_t = \frac{F_t}{L_e} = \frac{F \sin\theta}{L_e}$$

### Step 3: Resultant Force per Unit Length (Vector Sum)
Since $f_p$ and $f_t$ are **perpendicular** to each other, the resultant is:

$$f_R = \sqrt{f_p^2 + f_t^2} = \frac{F}{L_e}\sqrt{\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta} = \frac{F}{L_e}$$

> **Surprise!** The resultant per unit length is **independent of the load angle** $\theta$ — it always equals $F/L_e$. This means for a **uniform strength** design, the weld size does not depend on orientation (the difference arises from **failure mode**, not the load itself).

### Step 4: Design Check
The fillet weld size must satisfy:

$$h \ge \frac{f_R}{0.707\,\tau_{all}} = \frac{F}{0.707\,L_e\,\tau_{all}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Weld Selection Decision Table

| Loading Condition | Recommended Weld Type | Design Stress | Direction Effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pure static tension | **Groove (butt) weld** | $\sigma = F/(t \cdot L)$ | Full strength |
| Pure static shear | **Fillet weld** | $\tau = F/(0.707 h L)$ | Transverse > Parallel |
| Cyclic/fatigue loading | **Groove weld, ground flush** | Reduced allowable | Avoid parallel |
| Secondary attachment (gusset, stiffener) | **Fillet (intermittent)** | $\tau = F/(0.707 h L)$ | Either direction OK |
| Sheet metal overlap (thin sheets) | **Spot / projection / seam** | Nugget shear / pull-out | Lap shear |
| Leak-tight joint (vessel, pipe) | **Full-penetration groove** | $\sigma$ in parent metal | Either direction |
| Edge of plate (no edge access) | **Plug / slot weld** | $\tau = F/(\pi d^2/4)$ | Either direction |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (For Practice)

**Problem:** A bracket is welded to a column using two parallel fillet welds, each 80 mm long, on opposite sides of a 100 mm-wide bracket plate. The leg size of each weld is 8 mm. The allowable shear stress is 110 MPa. Calculate the **safe load** that can be applied.

**Step 1: Throat thickness**

$$t = 0.707 \times h = 0.707 \times 8 = 5.66 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Effective length per weld**

$$L_e = L_{actual} - 2h = 80 - 2(8) = 80 - 16 = 64 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Shear area per weld**

$$A = 0.707 \times h \times L_e = 0.707 \times 8 \times 64 = 362 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Safe load per weld**

$$F_{one} = \tau_{all} \times A = 110 \times 362 = 39,820 \text{ N} = 39.82 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Total safe load (two welds)**

$$\boxed{F_{total} = 2 \times 39.82 = 79.64 \text{ kN}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always check $L_e \ge 4h$** in any weld design. Here, $L_e = 64$ mm and $4h = 32$ mm. Since $64 > 32$, the weld is **not a small weld** — no 50% penalty applies.

```mermaid
    J --> K

    K3 --> L
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Stress Path in a Fillet Weld

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Applied Load F] --> A2[Decomposed Along Weld Axis]
    A2 --> A3[Parallel Component Fp]
    A2 --> A4[Transverse Component Ft]
    A3 --> A5[Pure Shear on Throat]
    A4 --> A6[Direct Shear + Bending on Throat]
    A5 --> A7[Throat Plane at 45 degree]

    A7 --> A8{tau actual less than tau allowable?}
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Q: Define a fillet weld. State the formula for the throat thickness of an equal-leg fillet weld.**

**Model Answer:**

A **fillet weld** is a weld of approximately triangular cross-section joining two surfaces lying in different planes (typically at 60°–120° to each other). The two surfaces to be joined are not beveled, and filler metal is deposited in the corner formed by their intersection.

For an equal-leg fillet weld of leg size $h$, the **throat thickness** $t$ is the perpendicular distance from the root of the weld to its face (hypotenuse). It is given by:

$$t = h \cos 45° = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707\,h$$

The throat plane is the plane of minimum cross-section through the weld, oriented at 45° to both plates, and it is the **critical plane along which the weld fails by shear**.

**Why this matters:** A 50 mm weld with leg $h = 8$ mm has throat $t = 5.66$ mm — the **effective load-bearing area is only 5.66 mm wide per millimeter of length**, not 8 mm. Designers must size welds based on throat area, not leg size, for strength calculations.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Q: What are the factors that govern the minimum size of a fillet weld as per AWS D1.1?**

**Model Answer:**

The **minimum size of a fillet weld** is governed by the **thickness of the thicker plate joined**, not by strength requirements. This rule prevents the formation of **brittle, under-sized welds** that crack under rapid cooling.

Key factors:

1. **Thicker plate thickness** ($t_{max}$): As $t_{max}$ increases, $h_{min}$ increases in steps (3, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16 mm) per AWS D1.1 Table 5.8.
2. **Heat dissipation rate**: A thick plate acts as a **massive heat sink**, causing the weld metal to cool rapidly → brittle martensite can form → cracking risk.
3. **Code compliance**: Specifying $h < h_{min}$ violates the code and results in rejection during inspection.
4. **Practical reason**: Welds below $h_{min}$ are difficult to deposit reliably — they are prone to **lack of fusion** at the root.

**Example:** If two plates of thickness 10 mm and 14 mm are joined, the thicker plate is 14 mm, so $h_{min} = 6$ mm (from the table: for $13 < t \le 19$ mm, $h_{min} = 6$ mm).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the different types of welds used in design for manufacturing. State the formula for the strength of a fillet weld loaded in parallel and transverse directions.**

**Model Answer:**

Weldments employ several types of welds, classified by **geometry, penetration, and load path**:

1. **Fillet Weld** — Triangular cross-section, deposited in the corner of two perpendicular (or angled) plates. **Most common in design** because it requires **no edge preparation**. Loaded in **shear** along the throat.
   - *Parallel fillet weld:* Load parallel to weld axis. Pure shear on throat.
   - *Transverse fillet weld:* Load perpendicular to weld axis. Shear + bending on throat. ~30–50% stronger per unit length than parallel.

2. **Groove (Butt) Weld** — Plates are beveled and welded full-depth. **Strongest** weld type. Loaded in tension/compression. Throat = plate thickness.
   - Types: Square, V, Bevel, U, J — single or double.

3. **Plug and Slot Welds** — Holes or slots cut in one plate, filled with weld metal to join a second plate. Used for **secondary attachments** and **stiffeners**. Limited load capacity.

4. **Spot, Seam, Projection Welds** — Resistance welding processes for **sheet metal**. Weld size = nugget diameter.

**Strength Formula for Fillet Weld:**

For a fillet weld of leg $h$, length $L$, allowable shear stress $\tau_{all}$:

$$F = 0.707 \cdot h \cdot L \cdot \tau_{all}$$

- **Parallel loading:** $F$ acts along weld axis. Strength is the full value above.
- **Transverse loading:** $F$ acts perpendicular to weld axis. The same formula applies, but the **actual failure load is 30–50% higher** than the parallel case (experimentally observed). For design conservatism, the same formula is used.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] A circular bar of diameter 60 mm is welded to a plate by two parallel fillet welds as shown. Each weld is 100 mm long, leg size is 10 mm. The allowable shear stress in the weld is 94 MPa. Determine the safe tensile load that the joint can carry.**

**Given Data:**
- Bar diameter: $d = 60$ mm
- Number of welds: 2 (parallel)
- Weld length (each): $L = 100$ mm
- Leg size: $h = 10$ mm
- Allowable shear stress: $\tau_{all} = 94$ MPa

**Step 1: Compute Throat Thickness**

$$t = 0.707 \cdot h = 0.707 \times 10 = 7.07 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute Effective Length per Weld**

$$L_e = L - 2h = 100 - 2 \times 10 = 80 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Check if Weld is "Small"**

$$4h = 4 \times 10 = 40 \text{ mm} \quad ; \quad L_e = 80 \text{ mm}$$

Since $L_e (80) > 4h (40)$, the weld is **not small** → no 50% penalty.

**Step 4: Shear Area per Weld**

$$A = 0.707 \cdot h \cdot L_e = 0.707 \times 10 \times 80 = 565.6 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5: Safe Load per Weld**

$$F_{one} = \tau_{all} \cdot A = 94 \times 565.6 = 53,166.4 \text{ N} = 53.17 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Total Safe Load (Two Welds)**

$$\boxed{F_{total} = 2 \times 53.17 = 106.33 \text{ kN}}$$

**Validation Check:**
- Min weld size for $t_{max} = 60$ mm plate: $h_{min} = 12$ mm (per AWS table, $57 < t \le 150$).
- **Selected $h = 10$ mm < $h_{min} = 12$ mm — violates code!** ✗

**Correction:** Use $h = 12$ mm minimum.
- New throat: $t = 0.707 \times 12 = 8.484$ mm
- New effective length: $L_e = 100 - 24 = 76$ mm
- New shear area: $A = 0.707 \times 12 \times 76 = 644.78$ mm²
- New safe load per weld: $F_{one} = 94 \times 644.78 = 60,609$ N = 60.6 kN
- **Corrected total safe load: $F_{total} = 121.2$ kN** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mistake:**
> Students often compute the safe load using $h = 10$ mm without checking the **AWS minimum size rule** (Pillar 2). This is a **mandatory check** — the design must comply with the code-specified minimum, not just satisfy strength. A weld that is strong enough but smaller than $h_{min}$ is **NOT acceptable** and will be rejected in the workshop. The valuation key awards full marks only when the design satisfies **both strength AND code-compliance**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks] Discuss the general design guidelines for weldments with respect to: (i) weld size, (ii) weld length, and (iii) weld orientation.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Weld Size Guidelines:**

- **Minimum size** ($h_{min}$): Governed by thicker plate thickness per AWS D1.1 Table 5.8. Prevents brittle welds that crack on rapid cooling. For example, for plate thickness 20 mm, $h_{min} = 8$ mm.
- **Maximum size** ($h_{max}$):
  - For plate edge < 6 mm: $h_{max} = t$ (plate thickness).
  - For plate edge ≥ 6 mm: $h_{max} = t - 1.5$ mm.
  - This prevents melting the plate edge entirely.
- **Practical size**: $h$ should be the **larger** of (a) strength requirement $h_{req} = F/(0.707 L \tau_{all})$ and (b) code minimum $h_{min}$.

**(ii) Weld Length Guidelines:**

- **Effective length:** $L_e = L_{actual} - 2h$ (excluding two crater zones).
- **Minimum length:** $L_e \ge 4h$ to be considered a "normal" weld; otherwise, strength is reduced by 50%.
- **Minimum number**: For dynamically loaded structures, return welds (wrap-around at ends) of length $\ge 2h$ are recommended at the ends of parallel welds.
- **Intermittent welds** can be used to reduce distortion and cost, but minimum length of each segment = $4h$, and maximum center-to-center pitch = $16t$ (for compression) or $24t$ (for tension) where $t$ is plate thickness.

**(iii) Weld Orientation Guidelines:**

- **Transverse fillet welds** carry load perpendicular to the weld axis and are **stronger** than parallel welds (typically 30–50% higher strength per unit length). They develop direct shear + bending on the throat.
- **Parallel (longitudinal) fillet welds** carry load parallel to the weld axis, develop pure shear, and have **lower unit strength** but distribute load over a longer length.
- **Inclined welds** combine both effects; the resultant force per unit length $f_R = \sqrt{f_p^2 + f_t^2}$ is independent of the angle $\theta$, but the **strength reduction** at certain angles must be considered.
- **Balanced design:** Place welds symmetrically about the line of action of the load to avoid **eccentric loading** and secondary bending stresses.
- **Avoid welds in regions of high tensile stress concentration** — use groove welds (which develop the full plate strength) in such regions.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] A tie-rod of rectangular cross-section 40 mm × 20 mm is welded to a gusset plate using three transverse fillet welds, two at the top and one at the bottom (or symmetric equivalent). Each weld is 80 mm long and 8 mm leg size. The allowable shear stress is 100 MPa. Find the safe load on the joint.**

**Given Data:**
- Tie-rod cross-section: 40 mm × 20 mm
- Number of transverse welds: 3
- Weld length (each): $L = 80$ mm
- Leg size: $h = 8$ mm
- Allowable shear stress: $\tau_{all} = 100$ MPa

**Step 1: Throat Thickness**

$$t = 0.707 \cdot h = 0.707 \times 8 = 5.656 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Effective Length per Weld**

$$L_e = L - 2h = 80 - 16 = 64 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Check Small Weld Condition**

$$4h = 32 \text{ mm} < L_e = 64 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Normal weld, no penalty}$$

**Step 4: Shear Area per Weld**

$$A = 0.707 \cdot h \cdot L_e = 0.707 \times 8 \times 64 = 362 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5: Safe Load per Weld**

$$F_{one} = 100 \times 362 = 36,200 \text{ N} = 36.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Total Safe Load (Three Welds)**

$$\boxed{F_{total} = 3 \times 36.2 = 108.6 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 7: Verification — Is Plate Stronger than Weld?**

Tie-rod tensile capacity at $\sigma_{all} = 0.6 \sigma_y$ (typical for structural steel with $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa, $\sigma_{all} = 150$ MPa):

$$F_{rod} = \sigma_{all} \cdot A_{rod} = 150 \times (40 \times 20) = 150 \times 800 = 120,000 \text{ N} = 120 \text{ kN}$$

Since $F_{weld} (108.6 \text{ kN}) < F_{rod} (120 \text{ kN})$, the **weld governs** → design is safe at **108.6 kN**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's Insight:** It is **good practice** to have the weld slightly weaker than the plate so that in case of overload, the **weld fails first** (a ductile, visible failure) rather than the **plate fails suddenly** (a brittle, catastrophic failure). This is the philosophy of **"weakest link"** design in weldments.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam**

### A. Fundamental Geometry

- **Throat thickness** $t = 0.707 h$ (equal-leg fillet weld)
- **Effective length** $L_e = L - 2h$
- **Small weld condition**: $L_e \le 4h$ → strength × 0.5
- **Throat plane angle**: 45° to both plates

### B. Strength Formulas (Memorize the Derivation Path)

- Average shear: $\tau = F / (0.707 h L)$
- Allowable load: $F = 0.707 h L \tau_{all}$
- Required leg size: $h = F / (0.707 L \tau_{all})$
- Combined load: $f_R = \sqrt{f_p^2 + f_t^2}$

### C. AWS D1.1 Mandatory Checks (Examiner's Favorites)

- **Min weld size** from plate thickness (Table 5.8)
- **Max weld size** along plate edges ($t - 1.5$ mm)
- **Effective length** $L_e = L - 2h$ (NOT the actual length)
- **End returns** for parallel welds in fatigue
- **Weld access holes** for intersecting welds (to prevent lamellar tearing)

### D. Design Wisdom

- **Transverse > Parallel** welds per unit length (30–50% stronger)
- **Groove > Fillet** welds for full strength
- **Symmetric welding** about neutral axis reduces distortion
- **Double-V grooves** for thick plates to halve weld metal volume
- **Intermittent welds** reduce cost and distortion but are unsuitable for fatigue
- **Bigger welds are not always better** — they increase heat input → distortion → residual stress

### E. KTU Pitfalls to Avoid

1. Using the **actual length** $L$ instead of the **effective length** $L_e = L - 2h$.
2. Forgetting the **0.707 factor** in shear stress calculations.
3. Specifying $h$ smaller than the AWS minimum, even if strength is satisfied.
4. Confusing the **angle of throat plane** (45° for equal-leg) with the **angle of the load** $\theta$.
5. Not checking the **small weld condition** ($L_e \le 4h$).
6. Omitting **end returns** for parallel welds in fatigue-critical joints.

### F. Real-World Context

- **Bridges, buildings, ships, pressure vessels, automotive chassis, railway bogies, cranes** — all depend on these guidelines.
- The **AWS D1.1** and **IS 816** codes are universally applicable to mild steel weldments.
- **Aluminum, stainless steel, and titanium** weldments have modified guidelines (lower $h_{min}$, special preheat, no zinc near weld).

> **End of Module 2 — General Design Guidelines for Weldments**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pre and Post Treatment of Welds
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pre and Post Treatment of Welds

# Pre and Post Treatment of Welds — A Comprehensive Engineering Treatise

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Pre and Post Weld Treatments?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Pre-weld treatment** refers to the sequence of preparatory operations performed *before* the actual welding arc strikes the parent metal — including joint edge preparation, surface cleaning, fit-up, tacking, backing selection, and controlled preheating. **Post-weld treatment (PWT)** encompasses all operations performed *after* the weld bead is deposited — including slag removal, inspection, grinding, peening, and **Post Weld Heat Treatment (PWHT)** such as stress relieving, normalising, tempering, or annealing — to restore metallurgical integrity and dimensional stability of the welded assembly.

### 1.2 The Engineering "Why" — A Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are baking a cake in a heavy cast-iron pan. Would you:
- (a) Throw a frozen, grease-laden, lumpy batter directly into a **cold** oven and then expect a smooth, stress-free cake? or
- (b) Carefully **preheat** the oven, **clean** the pan, level the batter, and then *after* baking, let the cake **rest** in a warm environment so the internal stresses equalise without cracking?

Welds behave the same way. The arc is a *localised*, intense heat source that creates steep thermal gradients, residual stresses, and brittle microstructures. **Pre-treatment prepares** the joint for the thermal shock; **post-treatment heals** the joint after the shock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2 (PEBRT524):**
> *"Welding in design — selection of welding process, joint design, pre and post weld treatments, and design for weldability."*
> Expect 7–10 mark questions on preheat temperature derivation, PWHT cycles, and residual stress relief.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Heat input (H)** is expressed in **kilojoules per millimetre (kJ/mm)** — the energy deposited per unit length of weld.
- **Preheat temperature (T₀)** is measured in **degrees Celsius (°C)**.
- **Carbon equivalent (CE)** is a **dimensionless number** (typically 0.1 – 0.8).
- **Interpass temperature (T_ip)** is the maximum temperature of the previously deposited bead before the next pass — critical in multi-pass welds.
- **t₈/₅** — cooling time between **800 °C** and **500 °C** — governs the HAZ microstructure.

### 1.4 The Three Engineering Objectives of Pre + Post Treatment

| # | Objective | Physical Mechanism |
|---|-----------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Prevent cracking** (cold, hot, HAZ) | Reduce cooling rate; diffuse hydrogen out |
| 2 | **Restore mechanical properties** | Refine grain structure; temper martensite |
| 3 | **Relieve residual stresses** | Allow plastic creep at elevated temperature |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pre-Weld Treatment — Process Architecture

The pre-weld sequence can be broken into **five functional stages**, each addressing a specific failure mode:

### Stage 1 → Joint Edge Preparation
- **Beveling** (single-V, double-V, J-groove, U-groove) — to ensure **full penetration** and adequate access for the electrode.
- **Root gap** (typically 2–3 mm) and **root face** (1–2 mm) — control dilution and root fusion.
- **Included angle (θ)** — usually **60° ± 5°** for arc welding processes.

### Stage 2 → Surface Cleaning
- Removal of **rust, oil, paint, moisture** within **20 mm** of the joint line.
- **Acetone/wire-brush cleaning** for stainless steels to avoid carbon contamination.
- Purpose: prevent **porosity**, **slag inclusions**, and **hydrogen embrittlement**.

### Stage 3 → Preheating (T₀)
- **Definition:** Raising the base metal temperature *before* and *during* welding to a controlled value.
- **Mechanism:** Reduces the cooling rate (dt/dτ), allowing hydrogen to diffuse out, suppresses martensite formation.
- **Applicable when:** CE > 0.45 (carbon steel), section thickness > 25 mm, or service temperature < –30 °C.

### Stage 4 → Fit-Up and Alignment
- **Root spacing**, **misalignment tolerance** ≤ 10% of thinner plate thickness.
- Use of **jigs, clamps, and strong-backs** to restrain distortion.

### Stage 5 → Backing and Tacking
- **Backing** (ceramic, copper, gas) — supports the molten root pool.
- **Tack welds** — short, properly fused beads to hold the joint; tacks must be *removed or fully consumed* in the final weld.

---

## 2.2 Post-Weld Treatment — Process Architecture

### Stage 1 → Slag and Spatter Removal
- **Chipping + wire brushing** for shielded metal arc welds (SMAW).
- Critical before the next pass to prevent **slag entrapment**.

### Stage 2 → Peening
- Light **hammering of each bead** (except the final cap) to impart compressive stresses and counteract shrinkage.
- **Not** recommended on the last bead or on austenitic stainless steels (work-hardening risk).

### Stage 3 → Grinding and Profiling
- Remove **undercut, overlap, excessive reinforcement**.
- Achieves required **weld profile** for fatigue-critical joints (e.g., toe grinding to 1 mm transition radius).

### Stage 4 → Inspection (NDT)
- **Visual (VT)** → always first.
- **Dye Penetrant Test (DPT)** → surface cracks.
- **Magnetic Particle Test (MPT)** → surface/sub-surface (ferromagnetic only).
- **Radiographic Test (RT)** → volumetric defects (porosity, lack of fusion).
- **Ultrasonic Test (UT)** → volumetric + sub-surface; preferred for thick sections.

### Stage 5 → Post-Weld Heat Treatment (PWHT)
This is the **most important** PWT for KTU examinations.

| PWHT Type | Temperature Range | Purpose | Typical Application |
|-----------|------------------|---------|---------------------|
| **Stress Relieving** | 550 – 650 °C | Reduce residual stress | Pressure vessels, bridges |
| **Normalising** | 850 – 950 °C | Refine grain structure | Heavy forgings, castings |
| **Annealing (Full)** | 850 – 900 °C + slow cool | Soften, homogenise | High-carbon steels |
| **Tempering** | 200 – 600 °C | Reduce hardness of martensite | Hardenable alloy steels |
| **Solution Annealing** | 1000 – 1100 °C (water quench) | Dissolve carbides | Austenitic SS (SS 304, 316) |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **board-examination essentials**. Memorise the units and the boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$ | **Heat input** (η = arc efficiency) | kJ/mm |
| 2 | $\mathrm{CE}_{\text{IIW}} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}$ | **Carbon Equivalent (IIW)** | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\mathrm{CE}_{\text{Pcm}} = C + \dfrac{Si}{30} + \dfrac{Mn + Cu}{20} + \dfrac{Cr}{20} + \dfrac{Ni}{60} + \dfrac{Mo}{15} + \dfrac{V}{10} + 5B$ | **Pcm formula** (low-alloy) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $T_0 = 160 \cdot \sqrt{t} \cdot \left(0.1 + \mathrm{CE}\right)$ | **Preheat temp (low-carbon steel)** | °C |
| 5 | $T_0 \approx 160 \cdot (0.1 + \mathrm{CE})$ | **Simplified preheat** (t < 50 mm) | °C |
| 6 | $t_{8/5} = \dfrac{H}{2\pi k} \cdot \left[\dfrac{1}{500 - T_0} - \dfrac{1}{800 - T_0}\right]$ | **Cooling time 800→500 °C** (3-D heat flow, thick plate) | s |
| 7 | $\sigma_{R} = \dfrac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \nu}$ | **Thermal residual stress** (restrained) | MPa |
| 8 | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$ | **Free thermal expansion** | mm |
| 9 | $H_{\text{critical}} = \dfrac{f \cdot t \cdot (500 - T_0)}{...}$ | See derivation (Section 3) | kJ/mm |

> Symbol key: $V$ = arc voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $v$ = travel speed (mm/s), $t$ = plate thickness (mm), $E$ = Young's modulus (~2×10⁵ MPa), $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion (~12×10⁻⁶ /°C for steel), $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (~0.3), $k$ = thermal conductivity.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure vessels (ASME Sec VIII):** Mandatory PWHT for all C-Mn steels with thickness > **32 mm** to prevent *stress-corrosion cracking* in sour (H₂S) service.
- **Nuclear reactors (AISI 304 SS):** **Solution annealing at 1050 °C** followed by rapid quench to prevent *sensitisation* (chromium carbide precipitation at grain boundaries).
- **Shipbuilding (EH36, DH36 grades):** Preheating at **100–150 °C** mandatory for CE > 0.40 to prevent *lamellar tearing* in T-joints.
- **High-strength low-alloy (HSLA) bridges:** PWHT (590 ± 15 °C for 1 h per 25 mm) is mandated by AASHTO for fracture-toughness reasons.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Carbon Equivalent (IIW) and Preheat Temperature

The **International Institute of Welding (IIW)** carbon equivalent formula is a **weighted sum** of alloying elements, normalised against carbon's effect on hardenability.

### Step 1 → Express the Hardness Contribution of Each Element
Carbon (C) raises hardness **1×** per unit weight. Manganese is **1/6** as effective as C. Chromium, Mo, V combined contribute **1/5** each. Nickel and Cu together contribute **1/15** each.

$$
\mathrm{CE}_{\text{IIW}} = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{(Cr + Mo + V)}{5} + \frac{(Ni + Cu)}{15}
$$

### Step 2 → Physical Meaning
If two steels have the same CE, they will have **similar HAZ hardness and cracking tendency** regardless of the actual chemistry. This is why CE is the **single most important preheat decision variable**.

### Step 3 → Plug In Example Values
Consider a **medium-carbon steel with 0.25 % C, 1.0 % Mn, 0.5 % Cr, 0.2 % Mo, 0.3 % Ni, 0.1 % Cu**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{CE}_{\text{IIW}} &= 0.25 + \frac{1.0}{6} + \frac{(0.5 + 0.2 + 0)}{5} + \frac{(0.3 + 0.1)}{15} \\[4pt]
&= 0.25 + 0.1667 + 0.1400 + 0.0267 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5834
\end{aligned}
$$

> CE = 0.58 → **High** cracking susceptibility. Preheat is mandatory.

### Step 4 → Compute the Required Preheat Temperature
For low-carbon and C-Mn steels (BS 5135 / AWS D1.1 guidance), the simplified preheat equation is:

$$
T_0 \; [\!^{\circ}\mathrm{C}] = 160 \, \big(0.10 + \mathrm{CE}\big)
$$

Substituting CE = 0.5834:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0 &= 160 \times (0.10 + 0.5834) \\[4pt]
&= 160 \times 0.6834 \\[4pt]
&= 109.34 \;^{\circ}\mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

→ **Preheat to ≈ 110 °C** (rounded up to nearest 10 °C for shop practice).

### Step 5 → Generalised Formula Including Thickness (BS 5135)
For thicker plates, the **thermal mass effect** demands higher preheat:

$$
T_0 = 160 \cdot \sqrt{t} \cdot \big(0.10 + \mathrm{CE}\big)
$$

where $t$ = plate thickness in mm.

Example — same steel but $t = 25$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0 &= 160 \times \sqrt{25} \times 0.6834 \\[4pt]
&= 160 \times 5 \times 0.6834 \\[4pt]
&= 546.7 \;^{\circ}\mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This exceeds the lower critical temperature (A₁ ≈ 727 °C) only when combined with a longer hold; in practice, an interpass cap of **250 °C** is observed to avoid grain coarsening.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Heat Input and Cooling Time (t₈/₅)

### Step 1 → Define Heat Input
Heat input is the **arc energy per unit weld length**, with an efficiency factor $\eta$ (typically 0.6 – 0.85 depending on process).

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \quad [\mathrm{kJ/mm}]
$$

### Step 2 → 3-D Thick-Plate Cooling Model (Rosenthal, 1948)
For a moving point source on a thick plate, the cooling time between 800 °C and 500 °C is:

$$
t_{8/5} = \frac{H}{2 \pi k \rho c} \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{500 - T_0} - \frac{1}{800 - T_0} \right]^{-1} \quad \text{(simplified 3-D)}
$$

A more practically used empirical form is:

$$
t_{8/5} = \frac{H \cdot F}{F_0} \quad \text{where} \quad F = \left( \frac{1}{500 - T_0} - \frac{1}{800 - T_0} \right)
$$

### Step 3 → Numerical Example
Given: $H = 1.5$ kJ/mm, $T_0 = 110$ °C, $k = 0.04$ J/(mm·s·K), 2-D heat flow correction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \frac{1}{500 - 110} - \frac{1}{800 - 110} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{390} - \frac{1}{690} \\[4pt]
&= 0.002564 - 0.001449 \\[4pt]
&= 0.001115
\end{aligned}
$$

For low-alloy steel with $2\pi k \rho c \approx 9.5 \times 10^{-3}$ (J/mm·s·K²):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{8/5} &= \frac{1.5 \times 1000}{0.0095 \times 0.001115} \\[4pt]
t_{8/5} &\approx 7.1 \; \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> t₈/₅ ≈ 7 s falls in the bainitic region for typical C-Mn steel → produces **toughness of ≥ 27 J at –20 °C**, satisfying most structural code requirements.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Residual Stress from Restrained Cooling

### Step 1 → Free Thermal Strain
If unrestrained, the weld contraction would be:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{th}} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

For carbon steel ($\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, $\Delta T = 600$ °C from 800 to 200 °C):

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{th}} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 600 = 7.2 \times 10^{-3}
$$

### Step 2 → Elastic Stress (Hooke's Law in Biaxial Constraint)
If the joint is **fully restrained** (K = 1), the residual stress reaches the **yield strength**:

$$
\sigma_{R,\text{max}} = \frac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \nu} = \frac{2 \times 10^5 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 600}{1 - 0.3}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{R,\text{max}} &= \frac{1440}{0.7} \\[4pt]
&= 2057 \; \mathrm{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 → Real Behaviour
Since $\sigma_{R}$ cannot exceed yield, plastic flow caps it at $\sigma_y \approx 250$ MPa for mild steel. This is the **dangerous** level that PWHT must reduce to **~25–35 % of yield** (~60–90 MPa) to prevent *stress-corrosion cracking* and *dimensional distortion* during service.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Selection of PWHT Cycle

| Steel Type | Typical CE | Preheat T₀ | PWHT Cycle | Inspection |
|------------|-----------|------------|------------|------------|
| **Mild Steel (IS 2062)** | 0.25–0.40 | RT – 75 °C | 600 °C / 1 h | VT, UT |
| **C-Mn (IS 2002)** | 0.40–0.55 | 100 – 175 °C | 620 °C / 1 h per 25 mm | VT, RT, UT |
| **Low-alloy (15CDV6)** | 0.50–0.70 | 175 – 250 °C | 650 °C / 2 h + temper | RT, UT, MPT |
| **Austenitic SS (SS 304)** | < 0.30 | None | 1050 °C / 30 min WQ | DPT, RT |
| **HSLA (EH36)** | 0.40–0.50 | 100 °C | 580 °C / 1 h | UT |
| **Quench-tempered (HY-80)** | 0.65+ | 150 – 200 °C | **Not** PWHT-able; use as-welded | RT, UT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Austenitic stainless steels (300-series) DO NOT receive sub-critical PWHT** in the 550–650 °C range. This causes **sensitisation** (chromium carbide precipitation), resulting in intergranular corrosion. The correct treatment is **solution annealing at 1050 °C followed by water quench**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow — Pre-Weld Treatment Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Design Drawing] --> B[Verify Weldability]
    B --> C{Carbon Equivalent?}
    C -->|CE less than 0.30| D[Light Cleaning]
    C -->|CE 0.30 to 0.45| E[Cleaning plus Preheat 75 to 150 C]
    C -->|CE greater than 0.45| F[Cleaning plus Preheat 150 to 250 C]
    D --> G[Edge Preparation Beveling]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Fit up and Tacking]
    H --> I[Backing Selection]
    I --> J[Ready for Welding]
    J --> K[Arc Strikes]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Post-Weld Treatment Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Weld Bead Deposited] --> B[Slag and Spatter Removal]
    B --> C{Peening Required?}
    C -->|Yes intermediate pass| D[Light Hammer Peening]
    C -->|No final cap| E[Skip Peening]
    D --> F[NDT Inspection]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Defects Found?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Repair Weld]
    G -->|No| I{PWHT Required?}
    H --> F
    I -->|Yes Carbon steel| J[Stress Relief 600 to 650 C]
    I -->|Yes Austenitic SS| K[Solution Anneal 1050 C WQ]
    I -->|No| L[Final Grinding and Dispatch]
    J --> M[Hardness and Final NDT]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Effect of Preheat on HAZ Microstructure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base Metal Pearlite and Ferrite] --> B[Arc Heat Input H]
    B --> C{High T0 Preheated?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Slow Cooling]
    C -->|No| E[Fast Cooling]
    D --> F[Coarse Pearlite and Ferrite Tough HAZ]
    E --> G[Martensite Brittle HAZ]
    G --> H[Cold Cracking]
    F --> I[Sound Weld]
    H --> J[Rework or Scrap]
    I --> K[PWHT Optional]
```

## 4.4 Block Architecture — PWHT Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Welded Joint Complete] --> B{Service Condition?}
    B -->|Pressure Vessel ASME| C[PWHT Mandatory 620 C]
    B -->|Structural IS 800| D{PWHT if t greater than 32 mm}
    B -->|Austenitic SS| E[Solution Anneal 1050 C]
    B -->|Quenched and Tempered| F[No PWHT Allowed]
    C --> G[Soak 1 h per 25 mm]
    D -->|Yes| G
    D -->|No| H[Visual plus UT]
    E --> I[Water Quench immediately]
    F --> J[Strict As Welded Inspection]
    G --> K[Furnace Cool to 300 C]
    I --> L[Pickle and Passivate]
    J --> M[Dispatch]
    H --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.5 Block Architecture — Defect → Treatment Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Detected Defect] --> B{Defect Type}
    B --> C[Porosity]
    B --> D[Slag Inclusion]
    B --> E[Undercut]
    B --> F[Crack]
    C --> G[Grind Out plus Reweld plus Reduce H]
    D --> H[Grind plus Reweld plus Improve Cleaning]
    E --> I[Grind and Refill]
    F --> J[Excavate plus Preheat plus Reweld plus PWHT]
    G --> K[Re inspect NDT]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define preheating in arc welding. List the metallurgical benefits of preheating the base metal prior to welding.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (1 mark):** Preheating is the controlled heating of the base metal to a specified temperature (called the **preheat temperature, T₀**) in and around the joint area, *before* the welding arc strikes. It is maintained during welding as the interpass temperature.
>
> **Metallurgical benefits (2 marks):**
> (i) **Reduces the cooling rate** of the weld and HAZ, suppressing the formation of hard, brittle **martensite** in hardenable steels.
> (ii) **Promotes hydrogen diffusion** out of the weld metal, mitigating **hydrogen-induced cold cracking (HICC)**.
> (iii) **Reduces residual stress** by lowering the thermal gradient between the weld pool and the surrounding base metal.
> (iv) **Removes surface moisture**, thereby reducing **porosity** risk.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional II]**
**What is PWHT? Why is stress-relieving heat treatment not recommended for austenitic stainless steels?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **PWHT (1 mark):** Post-Weld Heat Treatment is the controlled heating of a welded assembly to a temperature *below* the lower critical temperature (A₁), holding (soaking) for a specified time, and then cooling at a controlled rate — primarily to relieve residual stresses and improve toughness.
>
> **Austenitic stainless steel (2 marks):** In the **550 – 850 °C** sensitisation range, chromium combines with carbon to form **chromium carbides (Cr₂₃C₆)** at grain boundaries, depleting the surrounding matrix of Cr below the **12 % passivation threshold**. This causes **intergranular corrosion** (sensitisation). Hence, the correct PWHT for austenitic SS is **solution annealing at 1050 °C followed by water quench**, NOT sub-critical stress relief.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. You must answer EITHER the whole of Question A OR Question B.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the various **pre-weld treatments** with their objectives. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A C-Mn steel plate of thickness **25 mm** has the following composition: **C = 0.18 %, Mn = 1.2 %, Si = 0.3 %, Cr = 0.25 %, Mo = 0.10 %, Ni = 0.20 %, Cu = 0.15 %**. Using the **IIW carbon equivalent formula** and the **BS 5135 simplified preheat equation**, determine:
> (i) The carbon equivalent of the steel.
> (ii) The required preheat temperature.
> (iii) Whether PWHT is recommended. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Pre-Weld Treatments (7 marks)

The pre-weld sequence consists of the following operations:

1. **Joint Edge Preparation (1.5 marks):**
   - **Beveling** of plate edges to ensure full penetration — single-V (θ = 60°), double-V, J, or U groove depending on thickness.
   - **Root face** of 1–2 mm; **root gap** of 2–3 mm; **land** of 1.5 mm.
   - For thin plates (< 6 mm) square butt joint suffices; for > 6 mm, V-groove is necessary.

2. **Surface Cleaning (1 mark):**
   - Removal of **rust, oil, grease, paint, moisture** within 20 mm of the joint line using **wire brush, grinder, acetone, or chemical solvents**.
   - Purpose: prevent **porosity, slag inclusion, hydrogen embrittlement**.

3. **Preheating (2 marks):**
   - Heating the joint region to a controlled temperature (typically 100 – 250 °C) before welding.
   - Objectives:
     - Reduce **cooling rate** (suppress martensite).
     - Drive off **diffusible hydrogen**.
     - Reduce **thermal gradient and residual stress**.
     - Remove **moisture**.
   - Computed from **CE**, plate thickness, and restraint level.

4. **Fit-Up and Alignment (1 mark):**
   - Maintain **root gap, alignment, and tack welding**.
   - Misalignment ≤ 10 % of thinner plate thickness; tack length 30–50 mm, spacing 200–300 mm.

5. **Backing and Fixturing (1.5 marks):**
   - **Backing** (ceramic, copper, gas) supports the root pool for full penetration.
   - **Jigs and strong-backs** restrain distortion in long welds.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** t = 25 mm, C = 0.18 %, Mn = 1.2 %, Si = 0.3 %, Cr = 0.25 %, Mo = 0.10 %, Ni = 0.20 %, Cu = 0.15 %.

**Step (i) — Carbon Equivalent (IIW):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{CE}_{\text{IIW}} &= C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{(Cr + Mo + V)}{5} + \frac{(Ni + Cu)}{15} \\[4pt]
&= 0.18 + \frac{1.2}{6} + \frac{(0.25 + 0.10 + 0)}{5} + \frac{(0.20 + 0.15)}{15} \\[4pt]
&= 0.18 + 0.2000 + 0.0700 + 0.0233 \\[4pt]
&= 0.4733
\end{aligned}
$$

**→ CE = 0.47 (3 marks)**

**Step (ii) — Preheat Temperature (BS 5135):**

For plate thickness 25 mm, using the thickness-corrected equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0 &= 160 \cdot \sqrt{t} \cdot (0.10 + \mathrm{CE}) \\[4pt]
&= 160 \times \sqrt{25} \times (0.10 + 0.4733) \\[4pt]
&= 160 \times 5 \times 0.5733 \\[4pt]
&= 458.6 \;^{\circ}\mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

However, this value exceeds the **safe interpass limit** (typically ≤ 250 °C) and would lead to **grain coarsening**. In practice, the **interpass cap of 250 °C** is used, with a minimum preheat of 150 °C.

**→ Recommended Preheat T₀ = 150 – 200 °C; Interpass cap = 250 °C (3 marks)**

**Step (iii) — PWHT Recommendation:**

Since CE > 0.40 and thickness > 20 mm with C-Mn chemistry, the welded joint is in the **high-restraint, moderate-hardenability** regime. **PWHT (stress relief at 600 ± 15 °C for 1 h per 25 mm thickness)** is **mandatory** as per ASME Sec VIII and IS 2825.

**→ PWHT REQUIRED — Stress relief at 600 °C, soak 1 h, furnace cool to 300 °C (1 mark)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Discuss in detail the various **post-weld heat treatment (PWHT)** processes, their temperature ranges, and the specific applications for which each is recommended. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** During SMAW welding of a 30 mm thick structural plate, the following data was recorded: **Arc voltage V = 24 V, Current I = 220 A, Travel speed v = 4 mm/s, Arc efficiency η = 0.8.** Determine:
> (i) The **heat input** in kJ/mm.
> (ii) Whether the heat input is within the **acceptable range (≤ 2.0 kJ/mm)** for the chosen C-Mn steel.
> (iii) The probable **HAZ microstructure** if no preheating is performed. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — PWHT Processes (7 marks)

1. **Stress Relieving (2 marks):**
   - **Temperature:** 550 – 650 °C (below A₁ ≈ 727 °C).
   - **Mechanism:** Creep-induced stress relaxation without phase transformation.
   - **Application:** Pressure vessels, bridges, pipelines (C-Mn and low-alloy steels).
   - **Soak time:** 1 h per 25 mm thickness; furnace cool to 300 °C.

2. **Normalising (1.5 marks):**
   - **Temperature:** 850 – 950 °C (above A₃) + air cool.
   - **Mechanism:** Refines coarse-grained HAZ, restores uniform ferrite-pearlite structure.
   - **Application:** Heavy castings and forgings welded into service.

3. **Annealing (Full) (1 mark):**
   - **Temperature:** 850 – 900 °C + furnace cool (~ 50 °C/h).
   - **Purpose:** Maximum softening, homogenisation.
   - **Application:** High-carbon tool steel weldments.

4. **Tempering (1 mark):**
   - **Temperature:** 200 – 600 °C.
   - **Purpose:** Reduce hardness of martensite, improve toughness.
   - **Application:** Hardenable low-alloy steels (e.g., 15CDV6, HY-80).

5. **Solution Annealing (Austenitic SS) (1.5 marks):**
   - **Temperature:** 1000 – 1100 °C + **water quench** (rapid).
   - **Purpose:** Dissolve chromium carbides, restore corrosion resistance.
   - **NOT** to be confused with sub-critical stress relief → 1050 °C WQ is the only safe PWHT for SS 304/316.

#### Part (b) — Heat Input Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** V = 24 V, I = 220 A, v = 4 mm/s, η = 0.8.

**Step (i) — Heat Input (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.8 \times 24 \times 220}{4 \times 1000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4224}{4000} \\[4pt]
&= 1.056 \; \mathrm{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**→ H = 1.056 kJ/mm**

**Step (ii) — Acceptability Check (2 marks):**

Since **H = 1.056 kJ/mm < 2.0 kJ/mm**, the heat input is **within the acceptable range**. ✓

If it exceeded 2.0 kJ/mm, the coarse HAZ grains and the risk of **underbead cracking** would increase. In such case, the welder must **reduce current, increase travel speed, or use multi-pass stringer beads**.

**Step (iii) — Probable Microstructure (2 marks):**

For a 30 mm thick C-Mn plate with no preheat and H ≈ 1.06 kJ/mm, the **cooling time t₈/₅** is short (≈ 5–8 s). The HAZ will form **untempered martensite + bainite** in the coarse-grained region adjacent to the fusion line.

**→ Probable microstructure: brittle martensite in coarse-grained HAZ → high susceptibility to HAZ hydrogen cracking (HICC).** Preheat at **100 – 150 °C** is therefore strongly recommended.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU Examiner's Perspective):**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Heat Input:** Many students write $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$ but **forget the arc efficiency $\eta$** in the numerator. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly includes $\eta$, so missing it costs **2 out of 7 marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing Preheat with Interpass:** Preheat is applied *before* welding; interpass is the *maximum allowed* temperature of the previously deposited bead. Conflating them loses 1 mark.
>
> 3. **PWHT for Stainless Steel = Stress Relief (WRONG):** A very common error. Austenitic SS **must not** receive 600 °C stress relief. Use **1050 °C solution anneal + WQ**. This is a **3-mark deduction** in any PWHT question.
>
> 4. **Using Pcm Instead of CE IIW without justification:** The two formulas give different numerical answers. Always state the formula used explicitly.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the Furnace-Cool Step in PWHT:** Rapid air cooling after stress-relief soaking can re-introduce residual stress. The cycle must end with **furnace cool to ≤ 300 °C** before opening.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Preheat** is governed by **CE and thickness**; formula $T_0 = 160\sqrt{t}(0.1 + \mathrm{CE})$.
- ✅ **Heat input** $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v \cdot 1000}$ in **kJ/mm**; keep ≤ 2.0 kJ/mm for C-Mn steels.
- ✅ **PWHT types:** Stress relief (550–650 °C), Normalising (850–950 °C), Tempering (200–600 °C), Solution anneal (1050 °C + WQ for SS).
- ✅ **PWHT soak rule:** **1 h per 25 mm** of thickness (minimum 30 min).
- ✅ **Cool slowly** from PWHT temperature: **furnace cool to 300 °C** at ≤ 150 °C/h.
- ✅ **Austenitic SS → NO 600 °C PWHT** (sensitisation); use 1050 °C WQ.
- ✅ **Quenched and tempered steels → NO PWHT** (temper embrittlement risk).
- ✅ **NDT sequence:** VT → DPT/MPT → RT/UT (always follow this order).
- ✅ **Peening** is for **intermediate beads only**; never on final cap or austenitic SS.
- ✅ **Backing materials:** ceramic (one-time), copper (water-cooled, reusable), gas (Ar backing for SS pipes).
- ✅ **Root gap** 2–3 mm; **included angle** 60° ± 5°; **root face** 1–2 mm.
- ✅ **Interpass temperature cap** typically 250 °C; exceeding it coarsens HAZ grains.
- ✅ **t₈/₅** of 5–10 s for C-Mn steel gives optimal bainitic-tough HAZ.
- ✅ **Fit-up tolerance:** misalignment ≤ 10 % of thinner plate thickness.
- ✅ **Tack welds** must be either **fully consumed** in the final weld or **removed**; do not leave them as stress concentrators.
- ✅ **Always clean** within 20 mm of the joint line before striking the arc.

---

**End of Notes — Module 2, Topic: Pre and Post Treatment of Welds**
*Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | 2024 Scheme | PEBRT524*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of Thermal Stresses in Weld Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effects of Thermal Stresses in Weld Joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermal Stresses in Weld Joints** are the internal residual stresses generated in and around a welded joint due to the non-uniform, transient thermal cycle of the welding process. When a localized region of the parent metal is rapidly heated to melting temperature and subsequently allowed to cool, differential thermal expansion and contraction occur. Because the surrounding base metal restrains this dimensional change, internal elastic and plastic strains are locked into the structure, producing a self-equilibrating residual stress field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Thermal stress is classified as the *primary driving force* behind welding-induced distortion, cracking (hot cracking, cold cracking, lamellar tearing), and reduction in fatigue life. KTU examiners expect students to link the *cause* (non-uniform ΔT), the *mechanism* (restraint), and the *consequence* (distortion/cracking) in a logical chain.

## 1.2 The Three Principal Stress Components in a Weldment

| Stress Type | Direction | Primary Cause |
|---|---|---|
| Longitudinal (σₓ) | Along weld axis | Restraint of longitudinal thermal contraction by cooler parent metal |
| Transverse (σᵧ) | Perpendicular to weld axis | Restraint of transverse contraction; multi-pass effect |
| Through-thickness (σ_z) | Through plate depth | Restraint in thick plates where Z-direction contraction is blocked |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tug-of-War on a Sun-Heated Bridge"

Imagine a long iron rod with one end clamped to a rigid wall. Now imagine pouring boiling water only over the middle section of the rod. The middle wants to expand, but the two cool ends (and the wall) refuse to let it. The middle pushes outward, the ends push inward — this invisible **tug-of-war** is exactly what happens during welding. Once everything cools down, the rod never returns to its original stress-free state; it carries *residual* "memories" of the heating, much like how a sun-baked bridge deck remains slightly compressed after sunset.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants for Steel (must be memorized for KTU):**
> - Coefficient of thermal expansion: **α ≈ 12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**
> - Young's Modulus: **E ≈ 200 GPa (2 × 10⁵ MPa)**
> - Yield strength of mild steel: **σᵧ ≈ 250 MPa**
> - Specific heat: **c ≈ 0.46 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - Thermal conductivity: **k ≈ 50 W/(m·K)**

## 1.4 Source of Thermal Stresses — The Welding Thermal Cycle

A point in the Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) experiences a complete thermal cycle:

1. **Rapid heating** to peak temperature (T_peak) — strain rate is high, expansion is severely restrained.
2. **Brief hold** at peak — austenite grain coarsening occurs.
3. **Rapid cooling** — the previously expanded zone wants to shrink, but the surrounding metal is now cooler and itself contracting. Incompatible contractions produce tensile residual stresses in the weld zone and balancing compressive stresses in the base metal.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Understanding thermal stresses is critical in design of pressure vessels, ship hulls, railway bridges, nuclear reactor cores, and aerospace structures where welded joints govern structural integrity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Thermal Stresses Arise — The Three Necessary Conditions

A thermal stress will develop in a structure **only if ALL three** conditions are satisfied:

1. **A temperature change (ΔT ≠ 0)** exists in the body.
2. **The body (or part of it) is restrained** from freely expanding or contracting.
3. **The temperature change causes a strain** that, if unrestrained, would be incompatible with the boundary conditions.

If even one condition is missing, thermal stress = 0.

## 2.2 Mechanics of Stress Generation — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 — Free Thermal Strain:** If a small element is free, heating by ΔT causes strain:
  
  $$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

- **Step 2 — Restrained Mechanical Strain:** If the element is *fully* restrained (zero net strain), the mechanical strain must exactly oppose the thermal strain:
  
  $$\varepsilon_{mech} = -\varepsilon_{th} = -\alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

- **Step 3 — Induced Stress:** By Hooke's Law for uniaxial restraint:
  
  $$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon_{mech} = -E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

- **Step 4 — Sign Convention:** Compressive stress during heating, tensile stress during cooling. **Residual stresses after cooling are predominantly tensile in the weld zone**, which is dangerous because they superimpose on applied service loads.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU high-yield and appear frequently in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | α = thermal expansion coefficient; ΔT = temperature change | °C⁻¹; °C | Free thermal strain |
| 2 | $\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | E = Young's modulus | MPa; °C⁻¹; °C | Stress under full restraint |
| 3 | $\delta_{th} = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | L = original length | mm; mm; °C⁻¹; °C | Free thermal expansion |
| 4 | $\sigma_{eq} = \frac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \nu}$ | ν = Poisson's ratio | dimensionless | Biaxial restraint (2D) |
| 5 | $\sigma_{v} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}$ | σ₁,σ₂,σ₃ = principal stresses | MPa | Von Mises yield check |
| 6 | $F = E \cdot A \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | A = cross-sectional area | N; mm² | Restraint force |
| 7 | $\Delta T_{crit} = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{E \cdot \alpha}$ | σᵧ = yield strength | °C | Temperature for first yield |

## 2.4 Types of Restraint (Exam-Relevant)

| Restraint Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal (Inherent) Restraint** | Restraint provided by the cooler parent metal surrounding the hot weld pool | Single-pass butt weld in a thick plate |
| **External (Mechanical) Restraint** | Restraint provided by clamping fixtures, jigs, or adjacent stiff structure | Plate clamped to a welding table |
| **Self-Restraint** | Restraint due to non-uniform temperature distribution within the same part | Fillet weld on a T-joint |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Implications

- **Pressure Vessels:** Residual tensile stresses near the weld reduce the effective fatigue life; ASME code requires PWHT (Post Weld Heat Treatment) to relieve them.
- **Bridges:** Longitudinal weld shrinkage on stiffened plates causes **buckling distortion** in compression zones.
- **Shipbuilding:** Block fabrication welds accumulate triaxial stresses → risk of **lamellar tearing** in T-joints.
- **Aerospace:** Welded titanium components in jet engines require precise thermal management to avoid stress-corrosion cracking.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Mitigation:** Engineers use techniques like **peening, backstep welding, symmetrical welding sequences, preheating, and PWHT** to reduce thermal stress magnitudes by 30–70%.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thermal Stress in a Fully Restrained Bar

**Problem Setup:** A steel bar of length L and cross-section A is rigidly clamped between two immovable walls. Its temperature is uniformly raised by ΔT. Find the reaction force and stress developed.

### Step 1: Free Thermal Expansion (If Walls Were Removed)

The bar would freely expand by an amount:

$$\delta_{th} = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

This is the displacement the bar *wants* to make.

### Step 2: Compatibility Condition (Walls Do Not Move)

Since the walls are rigid, the net displacement of the bar ends must be **zero**. Therefore, the mechanical compression caused by the wall reaction must cancel the free thermal expansion:

$$\delta_{mech} + \delta_{th} = 0$$

### Step 3: Express Mechanical Compression in Terms of Force

By Hooke's Law, the wall reaction force P produces a mechanical compression:

$$\delta_{mech} = -\frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E}$$

The negative sign indicates compression (opposing the thermal expansion).

### Step 4: Apply the Compatibility Equation

Substitute Step 1 and Step 3 into Step 2:

$$-\frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E} + L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 0$$

### Step 5: Solve for the Reaction Force P

$$L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = \frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E}$$

Cancel L from both sides (L ≠ 0):

$$\alpha \cdot \Delta T = \frac{P}{A \cdot E}$$

$$P = E \cdot A \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

### Step 6: Solve for the Stress σ

Divide both sides by A:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The stress is **compressive during heating** and becomes **tensile during cooling**. Because ΔT in welding is huge (often > 1000 °C near the weld), σ_th easily exceeds the yield strength, causing plastic flow. This is why the *residual* stress after cooling is bounded by σᵧ.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Temperature for Onset of Plastic Deformation

In real welds, the stress cannot exceed yield strength σᵧ. The temperature rise at which yielding first begins is:

### Step 1: Equate Thermal Stress to Yield Strength

At the threshold of yielding:

$$\sigma_{th} = \sigma_{y}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Thermal Stress Formula

$$E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T_{crit} = \sigma_{y}$$

### Step 3: Solve for ΔT_crit

$$\Delta T_{crit} = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{E \cdot \alpha}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example for Mild Steel

Using E = 200 × 10³ MPa, α = 12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C, σᵧ = 250 MPa:

$$\Delta T_{crit} = \frac{250}{(200 \times 10^3) \times (12 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$\Delta T_{crit} = \frac{250}{2.4} = 104.17 \ ^\circ C$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** In mild steel, a temperature differential of only **~104 °C** is sufficient to cause yielding. Since real welds exhibit ΔT of 500–1500 °C, plastic deformation is *unavoidable* — this is the fundamental reason welded joints always carry residual stress and some degree of distortion.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Biaxial Restraint (Thin Plate Welding)
