# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**What is a draft angle in forging? State the typical values of draft angles used for external and internal surfaces in a hot closed-die forging process.**

#### Model Answer (10 lines):
A **draft angle** is a small intentional taper given to the walls of a forging that are parallel to the direction of die motion. It allows the forged part to be easily ejected from the die cavity without scoring, reduces die wear, and compensates for thermal contraction of the forging on cooling.

**Typical values for hot closed-die forging:**
- **External draft:** $3°$ to $5°$
- **Internal draft:** $5°$ to $7°$ (approximately 1.5 times the external draft, because internal cavities cannot be lubricated as effectively).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only one value (e.g., "3 degrees") without distinguishing internal vs. external. Always give **both** values to earn full marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Why are fillet and corner radii specified in forging design? What happens if these radii are too small?**

#### Model Answer (8 lines):
- **Fillet radius ($R_f$)** is the rounded internal transition; **corner radius ($R_c$)** is the rounded external transition.
- They are required to:
  1. Ensure smooth metal flow into the cavity.
  2. Avoid stress concentration in the die (prevents die cracking).
  3. Prevent forging defects like **folds, laps, and cold shuts**.
- **General rule:** $R_f \geq 1.5\,t$ and $R_c \geq 1.5\,R_f$, where $t$ is the adjoining wall thickness.
- If radii are too small: die cracks rapidly, forging shows underfill or folding defects, and die life is drastically reduced.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse fillet (internal) and corner (external) radii. The two are different and serve different purposes. State the **minimum numerical relationship** to score full marks.

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (Choice 1): Design Factors – Comprehensive Treatment

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the following forging design factors with sketches:
1. Draft angle
2. Fillet and corner radii
3. Flash and flash-land

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hot closed-die forging of AISI 1020 steel is to be designed for a connecting rod. The maximum section thickness is 18 mm. Specify:
- (i) The minimum external and internal draft angles.
- (ii) The minimum fillet and corner radii.
- (iii) The recommended flash geometry with flash-land ratio.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) – Design Factor Explanations**

**1. Draft Angle:**
A small taper on the walls of a forging parallel to the die-movement direction. It enables easy part ejection, reduces die wear, and provides clearance for thermal contraction. Typical values: $3°$–$5°$ external, $5°$–$7°$ internal.

**2. Fillet and Corner Radii:**
- Fillet radius ($R_f$): Rounded internal transition between two surfaces. Prevents underfill, folds, and cold shuts.
- Corner radius ($R_c$): Rounded external edge. Prevents die cracking due to stress concentration.
- Rules: $R_f \geq 1.5\,t$ and $R_c \geq 1.5\,R_f$.

**3. Flash and Flash-Land:**
- Flash is the thin sheet of metal that escapes between the die halves at the parting line.
- Flash-land is the narrow region where the dies first contact, controlling the rate of metal escape.
- Flash-land ratio: $K = b/h$ typically between 4 and 8.
- Functions: pressure reservoir, flow baffle, trimming allowance.

**Part (b) – Numerical Specifications**

Given: $t_{\max} = 18$ mm, AISI 1020 steel, hot closed-die forging.

**(i) Draft angles:**
- External draft: $\theta_e = 3°$ (standard for hot steel, precision forging).
- Internal draft: $\theta_i = 1.5 \times 3° = 4.5°$ (round up to $5°$ for safety).

**(ii) Fillet and corner radii:**
Using $R_f \geq 1.5\,t$ at the thinnest section. The thinnest section in a connecting rod is typically the shank (around 8–10 mm). At the critical section:

$$R_f = 1.5 \times 8 = 12 \text{ mm}$$

$$R_c = 1.5 \times R_f = 1.5 \times 12 = 18 \text{ mm}$$

**At the maximum section (18 mm):**

$$R_f = 1.5 \times 18 = 27 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Flash geometry:**
- Flash thickness: $h = 1.5$ mm (standard for hot steel).
- Flash-land ratio: $K = 5$ (typical mid-range value).
- Flash width: $b = K \times h = 5 \times 1.5 = 7.5$ mm.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget to compute the *internal* draft as 1.5× the *external* draft. They also forget that $R_c \geq 1.5 R_f$—not the other way around. Many confuse the two formulas.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Forging Defects and Design Remedies

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the major forging defects—**underfill, cold shut, die shift, and flash truncation**—and explain how proper design of the forging can prevent each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel forging requires a rib of height 30 mm. Specify:
- (i) The minimum rib thickness to avoid buckling and folding.
- (ii) The minimum fillet radius where the rib meets the web.
- (iii) The expected forging temperature for AISI 1045 steel.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) – Forging Defects and Design Remedies**

**1. Underfill:**
- **Symptom:** Incomplete cavity filling; forging shows missing material in thin or deep regions.
- **Cause:** Insufficient forging pressure, too large a cavity, sharp internal corners.
- **Design remedy:** Increase fillet radii ($R_f \geq 1.5 t$), increase flash-land ratio ($K = b/h = 5$–$8$), use preform dies to distribute metal more uniformly.

**2. Cold Shut / Lap:**
- **Symptom:** A crack-like surface discontinuity caused by two streams of metal meeting but not bonding.
- **Cause:** Sharp internal corners, low forging temperature, excessive deformation speed.
- **Design remedy:** Use generous fillet radii, maintain proper forging temperature, and avoid abrupt thickness changes.

**3. Die Shift:**
- **Symptom:** Flash is thicker on one side than the other; forging is asymmetric.
- **Cause:** Asymmetric forging, off-center parting line, worn die guides.
- **Design remedy:** Use a symmetric parting line, balance the projected area in both die halves, and ensure tight die guiding.

**4. Flash Truncation:**
- **Symptom:** The flash is so thin that it breaks off and the cavity does not fill.
- **Cause:** Flash thickness too small, die wear.
- **Design remedy:** Specify $h \geq 1.5$ mm and maintain die maintenance schedule.

**Part (b) – Rib Design Specifications**

Given: Rib height $H = 30$ mm, AISI 1045 steel.

**(i) Minimum rib thickness:**
Rule: $H/t \leq 5$ (for steel).

$$t_{\text{rib}} = \dfrac{H}{5} = \dfrac{30}{5} = 6 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Minimum fillet radius where rib meets web:**

$$R_f = 1.5 \times t_{\text{rib}} = 1.5 \times 6 = 9 \text{ mm}$$

If the adjoining web is thicker (say 12 mm), use the **smaller** of the two calculated radii to avoid mismatch:

$$R_f(\text{from web}) = 1.5 \times 12 = 18 \text{ mm}$$

Use $R_f = 9$ mm (smaller value) to ensure both surfaces transition smoothly.

**(iii) Forging temperature for AISI 1045 steel:**
AISI 1045 is a medium-carbon steel. The standard hot forging temperature range is:
- **Start temperature:** $1200 °C$
- **Finish temperature:** $850 °C$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students commonly state only one temperature (e.g., "1200 °C") without specifying both the start and finish temperatures. The forging range is critical because cooling below $850 °C$ causes excessive die wear and incomplete filling.

---

## Examiner's Pitfall Summary

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Forging Design Questions:**
> 1. Confusing **fillet radius (internal)** with **corner radius (external)**.
> 2. Stating draft angle without **specifying internal vs. external** values.
> 3. Writing formulas without the **numerical substitution step**.
> 4. Omitting **units** (mm, °C, MPa) in numerical answers.
> 5. Forgetting the **1.5× ratio** rule between fillet and corner radii.
> 6. Not providing the **forging temperature range** (start + finish) for material-related questions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Draft angle** is mandatory for ejection: $3°$–$5°$ external, $5°$–$7°$ internal (hot steel).
- ✅ **Fillet radius rule:** $R_f \geq 1.5\,t$ (where $t$ is the thinner adjoining wall).
- ✅ **Corner radius rule:** $R_c \geq 1.5\,R_f$.
- ✅ **Web aspect ratio:** $H/t \leq 5$ to prevent buckling.
- ✅ **Rib rules:** Taper ribs; rib height $\leq 5 \times$ rib thickness; rib thickness $<$ web thickness.
- ✅ **Boss rules:** $h \leq 2.5 d$, $L \geq 3 d$ (spacing), $R = 0.07 d$.
- ✅ **Flash-land ratio:** $K = b/h = 4$ to $8$.
- ✅ **Flash thickness:** $h \geq 1.5$ mm for hot steel.
- ✅ **Parting line:** Symmetric, maximizes single-die-half use, follows contour, avoids trapped cores.
- ✅ **Grain flow:** Must follow service load direction (e.g., connecting rod shank, crankshaft journals).
- ✅ **Forging temperature (AISI 1045):** $1200 °C$ start, $850 °C$ finish.
- ✅ **Forging temperature (AISI 1020):** $1250 °C$ start, $900 °C$ finish.
- ✅ **Defects → Causes → Remedies:** Underfill, cold shut, die shift, flash truncation, fold, lap, buckle, scoring.
- ✅ **Symmetric forging** = balanced die force, longer die life, fewer die-shift defects.
- ✅ **Forging advantage:** Superior mechanical properties due to continuous, aligned grain flow.
- ✅ **Forging limitation:** Higher tooling cost; not economical for small production volumes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Closed Die Forging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Closed Die Forging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Closed Die Forging** (also called Impression Die Forging) is a hot or cold forging process in which the metal workpiece (billet) is plastically deformed between two die halves (upper and lower) that contain precisely machined cavities (impressions) replicating the final part geometry. The dies are mounted in a forging hammer or press and close upon the heated billet, forcing the metal to flow and fill the die cavity, producing a near-net shape component with controlled dimensions, superior mechanical properties, and excellent surface finish.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Syllabus Highlight):** Unlike *open die forging* (where metal flows freely as flash) and *flash-less forging* (where excess metal is precisely controlled), **closed die forging deliberately allows a thin ring of excess metal to escape through a flash gutter**, balancing die closure pressure, ensuring complete cavity fill, and accommodating material volume variations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of closed die forging like **using a waffle iron**:
- You pour batter (the heated billet) into the lower mold half.
- When the top mold closes, the batter is forced into every tiny pattern (the die impression).
- A small amount of batter (the **flash**) squeezes out around the edges through a designed channel.
- After cooling, you get a perfectly shaped waffle with crisp edges, every single time.

In engineering terms, the **flash gutter** acts like a safety valve — it absorbs the slight variations in billet volume so that the die closes fully and the cavity is completely filled.

### 1.3 Process Classification

| Type | Description | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Impression Die Forging (Closed)** | Metal fully confined in cavity except for flash | Gears, connecting rods, crankshafts |
| **Flash-less Forging** | Precise volume control, no flash | High-precision aerospace parts |
| **Multi-impression Forging** | Several stations (blocking → finishing) in one die set | Complex geometries |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Process Temperature for Steel:** **950 °C – 1250 °C** (austenitic range). For aluminum alloys: **350 °C – 500 °C**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Process Logic

**Step 1 — Billet Preparation:**
A cylindrical or pre-shaped billet is cut to a calculated volume, accounting for flash loss (**typically 20–40% extra material**) and scale formation.

**Step 2 — Heating:**
The billet is heated in a gas or induction furnace to the forging temperature. The choice of temperature window is critical — too low causes cracking; too high causes grain coarsening and burn.

**Step 3 — Placement & Forging Stroke:**
The heated billet is positioned in the lower die impression. The upper die descends, striking or pressing the billet. The metal is forced to:
1. Fill the cavity radially and vertically.
2. Eject a controlled amount through the **flash gutter**.

**Step 4 — Flash Formation — The "Why":**
The flash gutter is **deliberately narrow and thin** at its land, which creates a high resistance to metal flow. This throttles the metal back into the main cavity, ensuring complete fill. After escaping, the flash cools rapidly, solidifying first and forming a dam that further pressurizes the cavity.

**Step 5 — Trimming:**
The solidified flash is removed in a **trimming press** using a separate trimming die. This yields the final forged component.

### 2.2 Critical Design Parameters

- **Draft Angle (α):** Taper on vertical die walls to allow easy ejection of the part. Typical values: **3°–7° for hot forging**, **0.5°–1.5° for cold forging**.
- **Corner Radii (R):** Internal radii must be larger than external radii. Minimum R is typically **3 mm** for steel, **1.5 mm** for aluminum.
- **Flash Land (b):** The narrow land where flash is throttled. Width: **3–8 mm**. Thickness: **2–5 mm**.
- **Parting Line:** The plane where upper and lower dies meet — selected to maximize draft and minimize die wear.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` for absolute value (NOT pipe `|`) to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Forging Force (F) | $F = K_f \cdot A \cdot \sigma_{flow}$ | N; $K_f$ = forgeability factor, $A$ = projected area, $\sigma_{flow}$ = flow stress |
| 2 | Flash Thickness (t) | $t = 0.016 \sqrt{V}$ | mm; $V$ = part volume in mm³ |
| 3 | Flash Width (w) | $w = 5t + 0.10\sqrt{A_f}$ | mm; $A_f$ = flash area |
| 4 | Flash Land Factor | $K = w / t$ | Dimensionless, typically 4–8 |
| 5 | Die Wear Allowance (per 1000 parts) | $\Delta h = 0.003 \cdot s \cdot h$ | mm; $s$ = severity factor, $h$ = depth |
| 6 | Shrinkage Allowance (Steel) | $\Delta L = 0.015 \cdot L$ | 1.5% linear allowance |
| 7 | Draft Compensation | $D_c = D + 2L \tan(\alpha)$ | mm; adds taper to diameter |
| 8 | Billet Volume (with flash) | $V_b = V_p + V_f + V_{scale}$ | mm³; flash volume = 20–40% of $V_p$ |
| 9 | Maximum Projection Area Rule | $F_{press} \ge F_{forging} \times 1.25$ | Press capacity = 125% of required |
| 10 | Strain in Forging | $\epsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$ | True strain, dimensionless |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Closed die forging produces components that demand **high strength-to-weight ratio, fatigue resistance, and metallurgical integrity**:
- **Automotive:** Crankshafts, connecting rods, steering knuckles, transmission gears.
- **Aerospace:** Turbine discs, landing gear links, structural fittings.
- **Defense:** Gun barrels, projectile components.
- **Valves & Fittings:** High-pressure valve bodies, flanges.

The design decisions made on **flash geometry, draft, and radii** directly impact **die life, production rate, and post-forging machining cost** — making this topic central to *Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimum Flash Thickness (t)

The classic Viezel/Sabroff relationship balances **die wear** (favors thick flash) against **flash energy loss** (favors thin flash). The optimum is found by minimizing the total forging energy.

**Step 1 — Express Flash Energy Loss:**
The energy lost in squeezing flash through the land is proportional to the land area and the resistance of the land.

$$E_{flash} = K_1 \cdot t \cdot w \cdot \sigma_{flow}$$

where $K_1$ is a constant depending on land geometry.

**Step 2 — Express Die Wear Energy:**
Die wear increases as flash becomes thinner because more pressure is required.

$$E_{wear} = K_2 \cdot \frac{1}{t^2}$$

**Step 3 — Total Energy to Minimize:**

$$E_{total} = E_{flash} + E_{wear} = K_1 t w \sigma_{flow} + \frac{K_2}{t^2}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate with respect to t and set to zero:**

$$\frac{dE_{total}}{dt} = K_1 w \sigma_{flow} - \frac{2K_2}{t^3} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for optimum t:**

$$K_1 w \sigma_{flow} = \frac{2K_2}{t^3}$$

$$t^3 = \frac{2K_2}{K_1 w \sigma_{flow}}$$

$$t_{opt} = \left( \frac{2K_2}{K_1 w \sigma_{flow}} \right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 6 — Empirical Simplification (KTU Standard Form):**
For practical design, this reduces to the well-known formula relating flash thickness to part volume:

$$t = 0.016 \sqrt{V_p}$$

where $V_p$ is the finished part volume in mm³ and $t$ is in mm.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Forging Force (F)

**Step 1 — Basic Press Force Equation:**

$$F = \sigma_{flow} \cdot A_{projected} \cdot C_f$$

**Step 2 — Define the Forgeability Factor $C_f$:**
The factor $C_f$ accounts for:
- Material flow stress at forging temperature
- Die closure speed (hammer vs press)
- Friction at die–workpiece interface
- Geometric complexity

Typical values: **3,000 to 8,000 kg/cm²** for hot forging steel.

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Example:**
A connecting rod has projected area $A = 40 \text{ cm}^2$, $\sigma_{flow} = 600 \text{ MPa}$, and $C_f = 6000 \text{ kg/cm}^2$.

$$F = 6000 \cdot 40 = 240{,}000 \text{ kg} = 240 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 4 — Press Capacity Selection:**
Apply the **25% safety margin** (standard KTU/Kalpakjian rule):

$$F_{press} = 1.25 \times 240 = 300 \text{ tonnes}$$

Select the next standard press capacity (e.g., **400 tonne press**).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Flash Land Width (w) Analytical Form

**Step 1 — Flash Volume Constraint:**
The flash must accommodate the excess material $\Delta V$:

$$V_f = t \cdot w \cdot L_{flash}$$

where $L_{flash}$ is the perimeter of the flash.

**Step 2 — Express w in terms of t:**

$$w = \frac{\Delta V}{t \cdot L_{flash}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Empirical Correction (Includes Flash Area):**

$$w = 5t + 0.10 \sqrt{A_f}$$

where $A_f$ = projected flash area in mm².

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:**
Given $t = 3 \text{ mm}$ and $A_f = 1500 \text{ mm}^2$:

$$w = 5(3) + 0.10 \sqrt{1500}$$

$$w = 15 + 0.10(38.73)$$

$$w = 15 + 3.873 = 18.87 \text{ mm}$$

Round to **19 mm** for design drawing.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Influence Summary

| Design Parameter | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | KTU Trade-off Rule |
|------------------|-------------|-------------|---------------------|
| Flash thickness $t$ | Die life ↑ | Cavity pressure ↓ | Use optimum from $0.016\sqrt{V}$ |
| Flash width $w$ | Energy loss ↑ | Cavity fill ↑ | Maintain $w/t$ ratio of 4–8 |
| Draft angle $\alpha$ | Ejection ease ↑ | Machining stock ↑ | Hot: 5°–7°, Cold: 0.5°–1.5° |
| Corner radius $R$ | Die life ↑, Flow ↑ | Detail fidelity ↓ | Min R = 3 mm (steel) |
| Forging temp | Flow stress ↓ | Grain growth ↑ | Stay 100 °C below solidus |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Closed Die Forging Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Billet Cutting"] --> B["Heating Furnace"]
    B --> C["Scale Removal"]
    C --> D["Lower Die Placement"]
    D --> E["Upper Die Descent"]
    E --> F["Plastic Deformation"]
    F --> G["Flash Formation"]
    G --> H["Die Cavity Fill"]
    H --> I["Cooling in Die"]
    I --> J["Trimming Operation"]
    J --> K["Final Forged Part"]
    J --> L["Flash Scrap Recycle"]

    M["Billet Volume Calculator"] -.-> D
    N["Flash Thickness Optimizer"] -.-> F
    O["Press Capacity Selector"] -.-> E
    P["Draft Angle Validator"] -.-> D

    style A fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#5a1f1f,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#5a4a1f,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#1f5a3a,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#5a1f4a,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#5a1f1f,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#5a5a1f,color:#000000
    style H fill:#1f5a5a,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#1f1f5a,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#5a3a1f,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#1f5a4a,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#4a4a1f,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Die Geometry Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Upper Die Half"] --> A1["Impression Cavity"]
    A --> A2["Flash Land"]
    A --> A3["Draft Walls 5 deg"]

    B["Lower Die Half"] --> B1["Impression Cavity"]
    B --> B2["Flash Gutter"]
    B --> B3["Parting Line"]

    C["Workpiece"] --> C1["Heated Billet"]
    C --> C2["Flash Ring"]
    C --> C3["Finished Forging"]

    A1 --- B1
    A2 --- B2

    A --> C3
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*What is closed die forging? Differentiate between closed die forging and open die forging.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Closed die forging** is a forging process in which heated metal is plastically deformed between two die halves containing precisely shaped impressions of the desired part, producing near-net shape components.

**Key Differences:**

| Parameter | Open Die Forging | Closed Die Forging |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| Die geometry | Flat/simple dies | Impression dies |
| Material flow | Free lateral spread | Confined in cavity |
| Flash | Free flash | Controlled through gutter |
| Part complexity | Simple (shafts, rings) | Complex (gears, rods) |
| Tolerance | Loose (±3 mm) | Tight (±0.5 mm) |
| Production rate | Low | High |

(1 mark definition + 1 mark for the table + 1 mark for clarity of explanation = 3 marks)

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper PEBRT524]**
*Explain the function of a flash gutter in closed die forging.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **flash gutter** serves three critical functions:
1. **Pressure intensifier** (1 mark): The narrow flash land throttles metal flow, forcing the remaining material back into the cavity to ensure complete fill.
2. **Volume compensator** (1 mark): It absorbs excess material from variations in billet size, preventing incomplete die fill.
3. **Flash former** (1 mark): The solidified flash acts as a dam, sustaining high cavity pressure throughout the forging stroke.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Forging Force and Flash Design

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A steel component is to be closed-die forged. The part has a finished volume of **125,000 mm³** and a projected area of **50 cm²**. The flow stress of the material at forging temperature is **500 MPa**, and the forgeability factor is **6000 kg/cm²**. Determine:
   (i) Optimum flash thickness
   (ii) Flash land width
   (iii) Required forging force
   (iv) Recommended press capacity

**(b)** Explain the influence of flash thickness on (i) die life, and (ii) cavity pressure. Draw a typical flash gutter cross-section.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a)** [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Finished part volume $V_p = 125{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$
- Projected area $A = 50 \text{ cm}^2 = 5000 \text{ mm}^2$
- Flow stress $\sigma_{flow} = 500 \text{ MPa}$
- Forgeability factor $K_f = 6000 \text{ kg/cm}^2$

**(i) Optimum Flash Thickness [2 marks]:**

$$t = 0.016 \sqrt{V_p}$$

$$t = 0.016 \sqrt{125{,}000}$$

$$t = 0.016 \times 353.55$$

$$t = 5.66 \text{ mm} \approx 5.7 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Flash Land Width [2 marks]:**

$$w = 5t + 0.10 \sqrt{A_f}$$

Assuming flash area $A_f \approx 0.3 \times A_{part} = 1500 \text{ mm}^2$:

$$w = 5(5.66) + 0.10 \sqrt{1500}$$

$$w = 28.3 + 3.87 = 32.17 \text{ mm} \approx 32 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Required Forging Force [2 marks]:**

Convert $K_f$ to MPa: $6000 \text{ kg/cm}^2 \approx 588 \text{ MPa}$ (since $1 \text{ kg/cm}^2 \approx 0.098 \text{ MPa}$).

$$F = K_f \times A = 6000 \times 50 = 300{,}000 \text{ kg} = 300 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(iv) Press Capacity [1 mark]:**

$$F_{press} = 1.25 \times 300 = 375 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Select standard 400-tonne press.**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b)** [7 marks]

**(i) Influence on Die Life [3 marks]:**
- **Thicker flash** → Lower cavity pressure → Less die wear → **Longer die life** (1 mark)
- **Thinner flash** → Higher cavity pressure → More die wear → **Shorter die life** (1 mark)
- This is the **primary reason for an optimum** flash thickness (1 mark)

**(ii) Influence on Cavity Pressure [2 marks]:**
- Flash thickness has an **inverse cubic relationship** with cavity pressure.
- Small reduction in $t$ causes disproportionately large pressure increase, ensuring complete fill.
- Hence the empirical formula $t = 0.016 \sqrt{V_p}$ is a *compromise* between die life and fill pressure.

**(iii) Flash Gutter Cross-Section [2 marks]:**

```
  Cavity  │   t (land thickness)
  ────────┤━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ w (land width)
          │   ↓ gutter well
          │   ↓
          └────────── Parting Line
```
Components: **land (thickness $t$, width $w$)**, **gutter well (depth = 0.5w to 0.75w)**, **parting line**, **relief angle (typically 3° to 5°)**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice: Die Design Philosophy

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss the design considerations for die cavities in closed die forging. Explain draft, fillet, and parting line selection with diagrams.

**(b)** A forged steel connecting rod has the following details:
- Maximum length = 250 mm
- Maximum depth = 50 mm
- Material = Medium carbon steel

Calculate:
   (i) Linear shrinkage allowance
   (ii) Draft compensation for each end
   (iii) Total length including draft

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a)** [7 marks]

**Die Cavity Design Considerations:**

1. **Parting Line Selection [2 marks]:**
   - Plane where upper and lower die meet.
   - Preferable at the **largest cross-section** to reduce die wear.
   - Should minimize flash volume and ensure balanced die loads.

2. **Draft Angle [2 marks]:**
   - Taper on vertical walls to facilitate part ejection.
   - **External draft:** 5° to 7° (hot), 0.5° to 1.5° (cold).
   - **Internal draft:** At least 7° to compensate for contraction.

3. **Fillet and Corner Radii [2 marks]:**
   - Fillets improve metal flow, reduce stress concentration, and extend die life.
   - **Internal radius $R_i \ge 1.5 R_e$** (typical rule: $R_i = 1.5$ to $3 \times R_e$).
   - Minimum R = 3 mm for steel, 1.5 mm for aluminum.

4. **Web and Rib Design [1 mark]:**
   - Maximum web thickness = 3 × adjacent rib height.
   - Avoid thin, tall ribs (cause buckling and uneven flow).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b)** [7 marks]

**Given:** Length $L = 250 \text{ mm}$, depth = 50 mm, medium carbon steel.

**(i) Linear Shrinkage Allowance [2 marks]:**

$$\Delta L = 0.015 \times L = 0.015 \times 250 = 3.75 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Draft Compensation [3 marks]:**
Assuming standard draft $\alpha = 5°$ (hot forging medium carbon steel):

For a depth $h = 50 \text{ mm}$ at each end:
$$\Delta h = h \times \tan(\alpha) = 50 \times \tan(5°) = 50 \times 0.0875 = 4.375 \text{ mm per end}$$

Total draft compensation (both ends): $2 \times 4.375 = 8.75 \text{ mm}$.

**(iii) Total Forged Length [2 marks]:**

$$L_{total} = L + \Delta L + 2 \times \Delta h_{end}$$

$$L_{total} = 250 + 3.75 + 8.75 = 262.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Note:** The actual part after trimming would be approximately **250 mm** (within ±0.5 mm tolerance for closed die forging).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Closed Die Forging Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently mix mm³, cm³, cm², mm². *Always standardize* before substituting in formulas.
> 2. **Forgeability Factor Units:** $K_f$ is in **kg/cm²** — do NOT convert it to MPa before using with projected area in cm².
> 3. **Forgetting the 25% Safety Factor:** Press capacity selection **always** requires a 1.25 multiplier. Omitting it = loss of 1 mark.
> 4. **Flash vs. Trim Allowance:** *Flash* is a forging process feature; *trim allowance* is an additional design margin. Do not confuse them.
> 5. **Draft Angle Direction:** Draft is *always added* to the forged (oversized) die cavity, not subtracted. Easiest mark to lose!
> 6. **Skipping Optimum-Flash Justification:** Examiners award marks for the *reasoning* behind choosing $t = 0.016\sqrt{V}$, not just the formula substitution.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print and Paste!)**

- [x] **Closed die forging** = impression die forging with controlled flash via a gutter.
- [x] **Flash thickness formula:** $t = 0.016 \sqrt{V_p}$ (V in mm³, t in mm).
- [x] **Flash width formula:** $w = 5t + 0.10 \sqrt{A_f}$.
- [x] **Forging force:** $F = K_f \cdot A$, with $K_f$ in **kg/cm²** and $A$ in **cm²**.
- [x] **Press capacity** = $1.25 \times$ calculated forging force.
- [x] **Draft angle:** 3°–7° (hot), 0.5°–1.5° (cold).
- [x] **Minimum fillet radius:** 3 mm (steel), 1.5 mm (aluminum).
- [x] **Linear shrinkage:** **1.5% for steel**, **1.0% for aluminum**.
- [x] **Flash land** acts as a throttling valve — narrow land = high pressure, complete fill.
- [x] **Parting line** preferred at largest cross-section for die life.
- [x] **Die material** standard: **AISI H13 hot-work tool steel**.
- [x] **Forging temperature for steel:** 950 °C – 1250 °C; for aluminum: 350 °C – 500 °C.
- [x] **Flash volume** typically **20–40%** of finished part volume.
- [x] **Web thickness rule:** max web = 3 × rib height.
- [x] **Trimming is mandatory** to remove the flash after forging.

---

**End of Notes — Module 3 / Topic: Closed Die Forging**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Parting Lines of Dies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Parting Lines of Dies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parting Line (of a Die):** The parting line is the precise geometric interface (line, curve, or surface) along which the two mating halves of a forging die — namely the **upper die (top half)** and the **lower die (bottom half)** — separate when the die is opened after the forging stroke. It is the boundary on the workpiece that corresponds exactly to the die-parting plane.

In **drop forging, press forging, upset forging, and closed-die forging operations**, the parting line is arguably the single most influential feature in die design. It governs the **flash gutter geometry, draft direction, trimming allowance, grain flow continuity, die-block machining, and forging stock orientation**.

### Related Terminology
| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Die-parting plane** | The horizontal (or inclined) plane that physically separates the upper and lower die halves. |
| **Flash** | The thin metal sheet that escapes radially into the gutter when the dies close. |
| **Flash gutter (flash land)** | The machined recess around the die cavity that accepts escaping metal. |
| **Trim line** | The line on the forged part where the flash is later sheared off in a trimming die. |
| **Draft angle** | The taper (typically **5°–7°** for forging) on vertical die walls that allows the part to be ejected. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Cookie Cutter & Mould**
> Imagine a sandwich toaster or a cookie press: the top mould and bottom mould meet at a flat seam, squeezing the dough (hot metal) into a shaped cavity. The seam where they meet is exactly the *parting line*. If you place that seam in the wrong place, the dough squirts out unevenly, the imprint is incomplete, and removing the cookie becomes a struggle. Hot steel behaves the same way: the **parting line decides where the flash (extra metal) forms, which way the part is pulled out, and which face will be cosmetically "good" or "rough"**.

The **golden rule of parting-line placement**:

> *"Place the parting line at the **largest cross-section** of the forging, on a single plane if possible, so that the metal flows easily into both die halves and the flash volume is minimized."*

---

## 1.3 Significance in KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus

For the **PEBRT524 – Design for Manufacturing and Assembly** course under the **2024 NEP-aligned scheme**, the design of parting lines of dies is a **high-weightage topic** in **Module 3 (Die Design)** because:

1. It directly determines **DFA (Design for Assembly)** — the forged part must mate with mating components along a known reference face.
2. It influences **DFM (Design for Manufacturing)** — the easier the die, the lower the per-piece forging cost.
3. It links directly to **Course Outcomes (CO3 / CO4)** — *design dies for forging, press working, and casting processes*.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Pattern Tip:** Parting-line design appears frequently as either a **3-mark short question** (definition + types) or a **7-mark sub-part** of a 14-mark die-design problem. Always draw the **sketch** alongside the answer — a labelled parting-line diagram is worth **2–3 easy marks**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — How the Parting Line Functions

When a hot billet (typically heated to **1100 °C – 1250 °C** for carbon-steel forging) is placed in the lower die cavity and the upper die descends:

1. **Die closure (approach)** — the upper die approaches the billet; the dies have a small gap equal to the **flash thickness (≈ 3 % – 5 % of the forging thickness)**.
2. **Metal flow (fill stage)** — the plasticized steel flows radially outward into the die cavity.
3. **Flash formation (final stage)** — when the cavity is full, surplus metal squeezes into the **flash gutter**, raising the die-closure pressure and forcing the cavity to fill completely.
4. **Knockout & ejection** — the upper die retracts; the forging remains in the lower die (or sticks to upper, depending on geometry) and is lifted out by the **knockout pin**, then sent for **trimming**.

The **parting line** is what *directs* the metal flow during step 2, *locates* the flash during step 3, and *dictates* the trim plane during trimming.

---

## 2.2 Types of Parting Lines (With Sketches)

### (a) Straight (Flat) Parting Line
- Located on a **single horizontal plane**.
- **Easiest to machine** on the die block; simplest to trim.
- Used when the forging has its **largest cross-section in the middle**, symmetrically.

### (b) Irregular (Curved / Stepped) Parting Line
- Follows the **contour of the forging** when the largest cross-section is **not** at one plane.
- Requires **match-lapping** of the two die halves to obtain a perfect fit.
- Used for **crankshafts, connecting rods, gear blanks**.

### (c) Locked Parting Line
- A more complex irregular line with **vertical locking surfaces** to prevent lateral die shift.
- Used for **long, slender, asymmetric forgings**.

### (d) Angular (Inclined) Parting Line
- The parting plane is inclined (e.g., for a **double-end gear blank** where draft is required on both faces).
- More complex to set up; chosen only when functional requirements demand it.

### (e) Multiple Parting Lines
- Used in **multi-impression dies** where each impression may have a *different* parting plane, achieved with a **counter-blow hammer** or a die that splits the workpiece across two horizontal planes.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Design Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All standard equations used in KTU board examinations on parting-line design are consolidated below.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flash thickness (typical) | $t_f = (0.03 \text{ to } 0.05)\,h$ | mm | $h$ = forging thickness in cavity |
| 2 | Flash width (rule of thumb) | $w_f = 5\,t_f \text{ to } 8\,t_f$ | mm | Wider gutter → less forging pressure |
| 3 | Flash land width | $b = 0.15\,D$ to $0.3\,D$ | mm | $D$ = cavity depth at the parting line |
| 4 | Flash gutter volume (approx.) | $V_g = t_f \cdot w_f \cdot L$ | mm³ | $L$ = mean perimeter of the gutter |
| 5 | Trim allowance (radial) | $\delta_t = 1.0 \text{ to } 3.0$ | mm | Material left for the trim die |
| 6 | Draft angle (forging) | $\alpha = 5° \text{ to } 7°$ (typically **7°**) | degrees | Standard for steel; **3°** for aluminium |
| 7 | Parting-line offset (for asymmetric forgings) | $y = \dfrac{A_1 - A_2}{P}$ | mm | $A_1$, $A_2$ = projected areas above & below; $P$ = perimeter |
| 8 | Die-block length estimate | $L_{block} = D_{max} + 2\,(w_f + s)$ | mm | $s$ = safety margin (≈ 10–20 mm) |
| 9 | Die-block width | $W_{block} = B_{max} + 2\,(w_f + s)$ | mm | $B_{max}$ = widest forging dimension |
| 10 | Die-block height (top + bottom) | $H = 2\,\big(h + t_{flash} + c\big)$ | mm | $c$ = die-shoe thickness |

> **CRITICAL LaTeX NOTE:** Every `|`-shaped absolute value or modulus in the formulas above is rendered as `\vert` to keep the markdown tables intact.

---

## 2.4 Selection Rules for the Parting Line (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!TIP]
> **The "Six Golden Rules" — memorize for the 7-mark questions:**

1. **Largest cross-section rule:** Place the parting line at the **largest projected area** of the forging to minimize flash.
2. **Symmetry rule:** Keep the parting plane on the **centerline** of a symmetric forging to balance die forces.
3. **Single-plane rule:** Always prefer a **straight horizontal parting line** when geometry permits — cheaper die manufacture.
4. **Draft rule:** Ensure the parting line is **perpendicular to the direction of draft** on all vertical walls.
5. **Functional-face rule:** Place the parting line so that the **most critical functional face** of the part lies entirely in **one die half** (no flash on functional surfaces).
6. **Grain-flow rule:** Orient the parting line so that **grain flow follows the contour of the part**, especially for fatigue-loaded components (e.g., connecting rods).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Typical Parting-Line Application |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Crankshafts, connecting rods, steering knuckles — irregular/locked parting lines. |
| **Aerospace** | Turbine blades (upset forging), structural fittings — straight parting in precision dies. |
| **Agricultural machinery** | Tine forgings, plough points — straight parting with heavy flash gutters. |
| **Hand tools** | Pliers, hammers, spanners — straight/irregular parting with single impression. |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Locating the Parting Line for an Asymmetric Forging

Consider a forging whose projected area above the candidate parting line is $A_1$ and below is $A_2$, with a total perimeter $P$. The **center of pressure** (which dictates where the die must be located to balance forces) is offset by $y$ from the geometric centerline.

The forging is supported by the metal pressure acting on both halves. Equating the moments of the pressure forces about the proposed parting line gives:

$$
F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_1}{2} = F_2 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{2}
$$

Because the forging pressure is uniform (final-stage of die closure), the area-weighted balance simplifies to:

$$
A_1 \cdot \bar{x}_1 = A_2 \cdot \bar{x}_2
$$

By the parallel-axis theorem applied to the combined projected area:

$$
y = \dfrac{A_1 \, \bar{x}_1 - A_2 \, \bar{x}_2}{A_1 + A_2}
$$

For a simpler practical case where the centroidal distances of $A_1$ and $A_2$ from the geometric centerline are equal in magnitude, the expression collapses to the **standard KTU formula** memorized by students:

$$
y = \dfrac{A_1 - A_2}{P}
$$

**Where:**
- $y$ = perpendicular offset of the parting line from the die-block centerline (mm)
- $A_1$ = projected area above the proposed parting line (mm²)
- $A_2$ = projected area below the proposed parting line (mm²)
- $P$ = perimeter of the forging at the parting line (mm)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Flash-Gutter Volume Required

The **flash gutter** is a rectangular cross-section recess of width $w_f$, depth $t_f$, and mean perimeter $L$ around the cavity. The volume it must absorb is the difference between the **billet volume** and the **cavity volume** (a design margin of **15 %–25 %** is standard).

$$
V_{gutter} = t_f \cdot w_f \cdot L
$$

The depth of flash (which equals the die-closure gap) is set so that the **average die-closure pressure** $p_{cl}$ is reached:

$$
p_{cl} = \dfrac{4 F}{\pi \, d^2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{K_{ratio}}
$$

where $K_{ratio}$ is a constant that links flash thickness to the forging thickness $h$:

$$
t_f = K_{ratio} \cdot h
$$

For a **first-order design estimate** (the form asked in KTU problems), the relationship reduces to the empirical rule:

$$
t_f = 0.04 \, h
$$

**Worked numeric example (KTU-style):**

> **Problem:** A steel forging has a maximum thickness $h = 50$ mm. Compute the flash thickness, flash width, and flash-gutter volume if the mean perimeter around the cavity is $L = 400$ mm.

**Step 1 — Flash thickness**
$$t_f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Flash width (use mid-range 6 × flash thickness)**
$$w_f = 6 \times t_f = 6 \times 2.0 = 12 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Flash-gutter volume**
$$V_{gutter} = t_f \cdot w_f \cdot L = 2.0 \times 12 \times 400 = 9600 \text{ mm}^3 = 9.6 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Billet volume allowance (add 20 % safety)**
$$V_{billet,min} = V_{cavity} + 1.20 \times V_{gutter}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 20 % volume buffer in the billet ensures that the gutter is **never the limiting factor** during final die closure — the cavity will always fill completely before the flash metal runs out.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Die-Block Overall Dimensions from the Parting Line

The parting line sets the **mid-height** of the forging inside the die. The total die-block stack height is therefore:

$$
H_{stack} = h_{top} + h_{bot} = \big(h_{cav,top} + t_{flash,top} + c_{shoe}\big) + \big(h_{cav,bot} + t_{flash,bot} + c_{shoe}\big)
$$

Since the parting line is typically chosen at the **mid-plane** of the forging ($h_{cav,top} = h_{cav,bot} = h/2$), this simplifies to:

$$
H_{stack} = h + 2\,t_f + 2\,c_{shoe}
$$

The **length and width** of the die block must accommodate the cavity plus the flash gutter on all four sides plus a **machining safety margin** $s$:

$$
L_{block} = D_{cav,max} + 2\,(w_f + s)
$$

$$
W_{block} = B_{cav,max} + 2\,(w_f + s)
$$

with typical $s = 10$ mm to $20$ mm.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Parting-Line Types

| Property | Straight | Irregular | Locked | Angular |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Die cost** | **Lowest** | High | Highest | Medium |
| **Die-machining complexity** | Simple | Match-lapping required | Match-lapping + locks | Match-lapping + angled shim |
| **Flash control** | Excellent (uniform) | Good | Excellent | Difficult |
| **Trimming ease** | Easiest | Moderate | Moderate | Difficult |
| **Draft flexibility** | Limited | Good | Excellent | Good |
| **Typical application** | Symmetric short parts | Crankshafts, levers | Long shafts | Double-end gear blanks |
| **Forging tolerance achievable** | ±0.4 mm | ±0.6 mm | ±0.5 mm | ±0.8 mm |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Parting Line in a Closed-Die Forging Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Upper Die Shoe"]
    nodeB["Upper Die Block with Cavity Top Half"]
    nodeC["Flash Gutter Recess at PL"]
    nodeD["Horizontal Parting Line Plane"]
    nodeE["Lower Die Block with Cavity Bottom Half"]
    nodeF["Lower Die Shoe with Knockout Pin"]
    nodeG["Hot Billet Placed in Lower Cavity"]
    nodeH["Flash Metal Escaping Radially"]
    nodeI["Finished Forging with Flash Trim Line"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The two die halves (upper and lower) meet at the **Horizontal Parting Line Plane (D)**. Hot metal (G) is squeezed, escapes radially as **Flash (H)**, and the resulting forging (I) is later trimmed along the parting line.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Parting-Line Type

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Start: Identify Forging Geometry"]
    q1{"Is largest cross section at one plane"}
    q2{"Is forging symmetric about a horizontal plane"}
    q3{"Is forging long and asymmetric"}
    q4{"Is forging a double ended gear blank"}

    s1["Use STRAIGHT horizontal PL"]
    s2["Use IRREGULAR PL following contour"]
    s3["Use LOCKED PL with vertical locks"]
    s4["Use ANGLED PL with shim block"]

    start --> q1
    q1 -- Yes --> q2
    q1 -- No --> s2
    q2 -- Yes --> s1
    q2 -- No --> q3
    q3 -- Yes --> s3
    q3 -- No --> q4
    q4 -- Yes --> s4
    q4 -- No --> s2
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Functional Architecture — Inputs / Outputs / Constraints of Parting-Line Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp1["Forging 3D Geometry"]
    inp2["Material Type and Forgeability Rating"]
    inp3["Draft Angle Constraints"]
    inp4["Functional Faces and Tolerances"]

    proc1["Calculate Centroid and Projected Areas"]
    proc2["Apply Six Golden Rules"]
    proc3["Locate Parting Plane and Compute Offset y"]
    proc4["Size Flash Gutter t_f and w_f"]
    proc5["Define Die Block Overall Dimensions"]

    out1["Final Parting Line Geometry"]
    out2["Flash Gutter Drawing"]
    out3["Die Block General Assembly Drawing"]
    out4["Trim Die Specification"]

    con1["Die Cost Target"]
    con2["Production Volume"]
    con3["Available Die Machining Facilities"]

    inp1 --> proc1
    inp2 --> proc1
    inp1 --> proc2
    inp3 --> proc2
    inp4 --> proc2
    proc1 --> proc3
    proc2 --> proc3
    proc3 --> proc4
    proc4 --> proc5
    con1 --> proc5
    con2 --> proc5
    con3 --> proc5
    proc5 --> out1
    proc5 --> out2
    proc5 --> out3
    proc5 --> out4
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic Description (Text Form) of a Typical Parting-Line Section

> **Cross-section through a forging die showing the parting line in elevation:**

```
  ┌─────────────────────┐
  │   UPPER DIE SHOE    │   ← mounted in press slide
  ├─────────────────────┤
  │   UPPER CAVITY      │
  │     (depth h/2)     │
  │         ╲           │   ← draft angle 7°
  │   ┌──────────┐  ┌───┴── ← flash gutter (depth t_f)
  │   │          │  │~~~~~│ ← flash land (width b)
  │   │  CAVITY  │~~│     │ ← horizontal PL at the widest section
  │   │          │  │~~~~~│
  │   └──────────┘  └───┬── ← flash gutter
  │         ╱           │
  │     (depth h/2)     │
  ├─────────────────────┤
  │   LOWER DIE SHOE    │   ← mounted on press bolster
  └─────────────────────┘
         ↑
     knockout pin (lifts forging upward out of lower die)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this sketch in a KTU answer sheet:** Always label (i) upper die, (ii) lower die, (iii) cavity, (iv) flash gutter, (v) flash land, (vi) draft angle, and (vii) the parting-line plane itself. A correctly labelled sketch alone can secure **3 out of 7 marks** on a parting-line design question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**Q1. Define "parting line" in a forging die. State the most important rule for locating it.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The parting line is the **interface between the upper and lower halves of a forging die**, corresponding to the line on the forged part where the two die halves separate.

**Most important rule:** The parting line should be located at the **largest cross-section (largest projected area)** of the forging so that the flash volume is minimized, the metal flow is balanced, and the die is easy to machine.

> **Key-words to include for full marks:** *largest cross-section, flash, die halves, projected area, balance.*

---

**Q2. List any three types of parting lines used in forging dies with one typical application each.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Type of Parting Line | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Straight (flat) horizontal** | Symmetric short forgings such as **hex nuts, hand-hammer heads** |
| 2 | **Irregular (curved/stepped)** | **Connecting rods, levers, crankshafts** |
| 3 | **Locked parting line** | **Long shafts, axle-beam forgings** where lateral die shift must be prevented |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer (ESE Module 3, 14 Marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`**

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** A steel forging has the shape of a **rectangular block 80 mm × 50 mm with a central boss 30 mm diameter and 25 mm height** (the boss projects symmetrically on both faces). The forging thickness is **40 mm**, the maximum cavity depth is **20 mm** (boss height), and the mean perimeter of the cavity at the parting line is **260 mm**. Determine the **flash thickness, flash width, flash-gutter volume**, and the **overall die-block length and width** for a straight horizontal parting line. Take machining safety margin $s = 15$ mm. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **straight, irregular, locked, and angular parting lines** in a tabular form covering die cost, machining complexity, flash control, and typical application. State **two situations** where a straight horizontal parting line is unsuitable. *(7 marks)*

#### 🔹 SOLUTION — Question A

### Part (a) — Numerical Design

**Given:**
- Forging maximum thickness $h = 40$ mm
- Cavity depth $h_{cav} = 20$ mm
- Mean perimeter of cavity at PL, $L = 260$ mm
- Maximum cavity dimension, $D_{max} = 80$ mm, $B_{max} = 50$ mm
- Machining safety margin $s = 15$ mm

**Step 1 — Flash thickness** (using 4 % rule of thumb)

$$
t_f = 0.04 \times h = 0.04 \times 40 = 1.6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Flash width** (mid-range 6 × flash thickness)

$$
w_f = 6 \times t_f = 6 \times 1.6 = 9.6 \text{ mm} \;\; (\text{adopt } 10 \text{ mm})
$$

**Step 3 — Flash-gutter volume**

$$
V_{gutter} = t_f \cdot w_f \cdot L = 1.6 \times 10 \times 260 = 4160 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Die-block length**

$$
L_{block} = D_{max} + 2\,(w_f + s) = 80 + 2\,(10 + 15) = 80 + 50 = 130 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Die-block width**

$$
W_{block} = B_{max} + 2\,(w_f + s) = 50 + 2\,(10 + 15) = 50 + 50 = 100 \text{ mm}
$$

**Result Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Flash thickness $t_f$ | **1.6 mm** |
| Flash width $w_f$ | **10 mm** |
| Flash-gutter volume $V_{gutter}$ | **4160 mm³** |
| Die-block length $L_{block}$ | **130 mm** |
| Die-block width $W_{block}$ | **100 mm** |

---

### Part (b) — Comparison Table & Discussion

| Property | Straight | Irregular | Locked | Angular |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Die cost | Lowest | High | Highest | Medium |
| Machining | Simple | Match-lapping | Match-lapping + locks | Shim block needed |
| Flash control | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Difficult |
| Typical use | Symmetric parts | Crankshafts | Long shafts | Gear blanks |

**Two situations where a straight horizontal parting line is UNSUITABLE:**

1. **Long, slender, asymmetric shafts (e.g., axle beams):** the largest cross-section is *not* on a single horizontal plane, so a straight PL would require deep die pockets that cause excessive draft and non-uniform flash.
2. **Double-end gear blanks with draft required on both faces:** the parting plane must be inclined to provide draft on both top and bottom — only an **angular parting line** can achieve this.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **functional anatomy of a forging die showing the parting line, cavity, flash gutter, flash land, and draft angle**. Explain **how the parting line is located at the largest cross-section of the forging** and why this rule is critical for die life. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A forging has projected area $A_1 = 1800 \text{ mm}^2$ above the proposed horizontal parting plane and $A_2 = 1200 \text{ mm}^2$ below it. The perimeter at the proposed parting line is **P = 320 mm**. Calculate the **perpendicular offset $y$** of the parting line from the geometric centerline so that the die is force-balanced. If the billet is steel heated to **1200 °C** and the mean cavity depth is **25 mm**, estimate the **flash thickness** and the **flash-gutter volume**. *(7 marks)*

#### 🔹 SOLUTION — Question B

### Part (a) — Descriptive Answer with Sketch

**Sketch (described in text — reproduce on answer sheet):**

```
  ┌─────────────────────┐
  │  UPPER DIE SHOE     │
  ├─────────────────────┤
  │  UPPER CAVITY (h/2) │
  │       ╲             │  ← draft 7°
  │  ┌────┴──────┐~~~~~ │  ← flash land (PL)
  │  │  CAVITY   │~~~~~ │
  │  └────┬──────┘~~~~~ │
  │       ╱             │
  │  LOWER CAVITY (h/2) │
  ├─────────────────────┤
  │  LOWER DIE SHOE     │
  └─────────────────────┘
```

**Explanation — Why largest cross-section?**

Placing the parting line at the largest cross-section ensures:

- **Minimum flash volume** → less metal wasted, less flash-trimming load.
- **Balanced metal flow** → cavity fills evenly; no "cold-shut" defects.
- **Reduced die wear** → flash metal does not erode the cavity edges.
- **Easier ejection** → both halves of the die release the part symmetrically.

For these reasons, the rule **"PL at the largest cross-section"** is called the *first principle of forging-die design* and is critical for **die life** (typically **5 000 – 50 000 strokes** depending on material).

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $A_1 = 1800 \text{ mm}^2$, $A_2 = 1200 \text{ mm}^2$, $P = 320$ mm, $h = 25$ mm.

**Step 1 — Perpendicular offset of parting line**

$$
y = \dfrac{A_1 - A_2}{P} = \dfrac{1800 - 1200}{320} = \dfrac{600}{320} = 1.875 \text{ mm}
$$

The parting line is to be **shifted by 1.875 mm toward the smaller-area side ($A_2$)** so that the two halves of the die carry equal projected load.

**Step 2 — Flash thickness** (using 4 % rule)

$$
t_f = 0.04 \times h = 0.04 \times 25 = 1.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Flash width**

$$
w_f = 6 \times t_f = 6 \times 1.0 = 6.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Flash-gutter volume**

$$
V_{gutter} = t_f \cdot w_f \cdot P = 1.0 \times 6.0 \times 320 = 1920 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Parting-line offset $y$ | **1.875 mm (toward $A_2$ side)** |
| Flash thickness $t_f$ | **1.0 mm** |
| Flash width $w_f$ | **6.0 mm** |
| Flash-gutter volume $V_{gutter}$ | **1920 mm³** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Parting-Line Questions**
>
> 1. **No sketch (loses 2–3 marks):** Even a hand-drawn block diagram showing the parting plane is worth 2–3 marks. *Always draw.*
> 2. **Units omitted:** $y$, $t_f$, $w_f$, $V_{gutter}$ must carry **mm, mm, mm, mm³** respectively. Marks are deducted for missing or wrong units.
> 3. **Direction of offset forgotten:** The offset $y$ is towards the **smaller-area side** (so the larger area is balanced against the smaller). Many students compute the magnitude but forget to state the direction → **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Wrong formula for flash thickness:** Board uses **$t_f = 0.04\,h$** for steel forgings. Using $t_f = 0.10\,h$ (a casting-die assumption) is a common error → **−1 to 2 marks**.
> 5. **Confusing forging-die parting lines with casting-die parting lines:** Casting die parting is influenced by the **core** and **draft**; forging die parting is influenced by **flash control and grain flow**. Examiners deduct marks for this mix-up.
> 6. **Skipping the "Six Golden Rules":** A 7-mark question often asks *why* the parting line is placed where it is. Quoting the **largest-cross-section rule** explicitly is mandatory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (memorize for last-day exam prep):**

- ☐ **Definition:** Parting line = interface between upper and lower die halves on the forging.
- ☐ **Golden Rule #1:** Place at the **largest cross-section** of the forging.
- ☐ **Golden Rule #2:** Keep on the **centerline of symmetry** whenever possible.
- ☐ **Golden Rule #3:** Prefer **straight horizontal PL** if geometry permits.
- ☐ **Golden Rule #4:** PL must be **perpendicular to draft direction** on all vertical walls.
- ☐ **Golden Rule #5:** No flash should appear on a **functional/precision face**.
- ☐ **Golden Rule #6:** PL orientation should follow the **grain flow** of the part.
- ☐ **Five types:** Straight, Irregular, Locked, Angular, Multiple (multi-impression).
- ☐ **Standard flash thickness for steel forgings:** $t_f = 0.04\,h$.
- ☐ **Standard flash width:** $w_f = 6\,t_f$ (mid-range; $5\,t_f$ to $8\,t_f$ allowed).
- ☐ **Standard draft for steel forgings:** **7°**; for aluminium: **3°**.
- ☐ **Offset formula:** $y = \dfrac{A_1 - A_2}{P}$ → shift **towards smaller area** $A_2$.
- ☐ **Die-block overall size:** $L_{block} = D_{max} + 2\,(w_f + s)$, with $s = 10$–$20$ mm.
- ☐ **Always draw a labelled sketch** (cavity + flash gutter + draft + PL) — minimum 2–3 free marks.
- ☐ **Trim allowance:** keep **1–3 mm** radial material on the forging for the trim die to bite.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory aid for the exam hall:**
> *"Largest face, on the centerline, straight if possible, no flash on the working face, draft perpendicular — die survives."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drop Forging Die Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Drop Forging Die Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Drop Forging Die Design** is the systematic engineering process of planning, dimensioning, and detailing a closed impression die used in a drop hammer (board hammer or air-lift hammer) to plastically deform a heated metal billet into a desired forged shape. The design integrates **parting line selection**, **draft angles**, **fillets and corner radii**, **flash gutter geometry**, **web and rib proportions**, **shrinkage and machining allowances**, and **die block sizing** such that the forging can be ejected cleanly, the die has adequate life, the metal flow is sound, and the component meets metallurgical and dimensional specifications.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Paraphrased):** *Design of forging dies involves selection of parting line, draft, flash gutter, web, rib, and fillet radii along with determination of die block dimensions for drop and press forging operations.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mould of a Cake

Imagine you are baking a fancy shaped cake. You have two halves of a mould (top and bottom), and you pour hot batter between them. The batter flows into every corner, takes the shape of the mould, and the little excess that escapes is trimmed away. The mould (die) must:

- Have a **slope on the sides** (otherwise the cake won't come out) → this is the **draft angle**.
- Have a **line where the two halves meet** → the **parting line**.
- Have a small **overflow channel** for the extra batter → the **flash gutter**.
- Have **rounded corners** (sharp corners break) → **fillet radii**.

Drop forging die design applies the same logic, but with **red-hot steel**, **thousands of kilograms of impact force**, and tolerances of **±0.5 mm or tighter**.

## 1.3 Key Design Inputs and Outputs

| Input (Drawing Specs) | Output (Die Engineering) |
|---|---|
| Finished part shape | Cavity impression geometry |
| Material of forging | Die material, preform temperature |
| Annual production volume | Die hardness, expected die life |
| Tolerances and surface finish | Machining allowance, draft angles |
| Adjacent machining operations | Trim and piercing die layout |
| Inspection / NDT requirements | Parting line position, draft location |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Metric (KTU Board Favourite):** The *flash ratio* (cross-sectional area of flash gutter to forging cavity area) is a standard 3:1 to 5:1 for most steels. This single ratio governs whether the metal fills the cavity completely or escapes prematurely.

## 1.4 Classification of Drop Forging Dies

1. **Impression (Closed) Die** – Cavity completely confines the metal except through the flash gutter.
2. **Open Die (Flat Die)** – No flash gutter; metal extrudes laterally freely.
3. **Combination Die** – Multiple cavities (e.g., blocker + finisher) in one die set.
4. **Multi-Impression Die** – Forging + trimming/piercing stations combined.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Design Procedure (The "10-Step KTU Logic Chain")

A drop forging die is designed by moving from the **forging drawing** outward to the **die block boundary**, in this exact logical order:

1. **Locate the parting line** – Choose a plane that maximizes a single, symmetric impression, minimizes side thrust, and avoids deep pockets in one die half.
2. **Apply shrinkage allowance** – Enlarge the cold part dimensions by 1.5 % to 2.5 % (for steel) to compensate for thermal contraction.
3. **Apply machining allowance** – Add 1.5 mm to 3 mm on machined surfaces so that surface defects, decarburization, and scale can be removed.
4. **Add draft angles** – Provide 3°–7° external draft and 5°–10° internal draft to enable ejection.
5. **Insert fillets and corner radii** – Replace all sharp corners to prevent crack initiation in the die and in the forging.
6. **Design flash gutter** – Select flash thickness *t*, flash land width *b*, and gutter depth such that metal flow is restricted only after cavity fill.
7. **Design webs and ribs** – Ensure web thickness ≥ 3 × adjacent rib thickness, and rib thickness ≥ 1.5 × adjacent wall thickness.
8. **Add excess material / mismatch allowance** – Compensate for die shift and wear.
9. **Size the die block** – Width and length must accommodate impression, flash gutter, draft space, locking elements, and bolster alignment.
10. **Select die material and hardness** – 5CrNiMo, H11, H13, with HRC 38–45 for hot work.

## 2.2 Physical Reasoning Behind Each Step

- **Parting line** controls flash flow and forging symmetry. Wrong PL = unbalanced lateral thrust = die shift.
- **Shrinkage** is volumetric: linear shrink ≈ 1/3 of volumetric shrink. Steel shrinks ≈ 1.5 % linearly.
- **Machining allowance** removes the decarburized skin that is brittle and has poor fatigue strength.
- **Draft** is required because forging cools and shrinks onto die walls; without draft, the forging is locked in.
- **Fillets** reduce stress concentration; in dies, sharp internal corners crack first under cyclic thermal load.
- **Flash gutter** acts as a "controlled leak" – high resistance forces metal to fill thin features first.
- **Webs/ribs** govern metal flow direction and final grain flow pattern.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Flash Gutter Matters:** The flash gutter is the most critical part of a forging die. It is the **throttling zone** that ensures complete cavity fill. A thin flash thickens forging ribs; a thick flash thins them. This is why a **properly designed flash is the single biggest contributor to forging quality**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Standard Value | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear shrinkage allowance | $S = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.5) \times 10^{-2} \times L$ | mm | Steel 2 %, Al 1.5 %, Cu 1.5 % |
| 2 | External draft angle | $\alpha_{ext} = 3^\circ \text{ to } 7^\circ$ | degrees | $5^\circ$ most common |
| 3 | Internal draft angle | $\alpha_{int} = 5^\circ \text{ to } 10^\circ$ | degrees | Higher due to shrinkage grip |
| 4 | Draft length on height | $d = h \tan \alpha$ | mm | $h$ = depth of cavity |
| 5 | Fillet radius (general) | $r = (0.07 \text{ to } 0.10) \times t$ | mm | $t$ = adjacent thickness |
| 6 | Web thickness (minimum) | $W \ge 3 \times T_{rib}$ | mm | Prevents hot-spot cracking |
| 7 | Rib thickness (minimum) | $T_{rib} \ge 1.5 \times T_{wall}$ | mm | Maintains metal flow |
| 8 | Flash thickness | $t_f = 1.0 \text{ to } 3.5$ | mm | Depends on forging mass |
| 9 | Flash land width | $b = (3 \text{ to } 5) \times t_f$ | mm | Throttling zone |
| 10 | Flash gutter depth | $h_g = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.5) \times t_f$ | mm | Provides flash volume |
| 11 | Flash gutter volume ratio | $V_{gutter} \approx 0.7 V_{part} \text{ to } 1.0 V_{part}$ | — | Critical for fill |
| 12 | Die block width | $W_{die} = W_{cavity} + 2 b + 2 \times 25 \text{ mm}$ | mm | Add bolster/lock margins |
| 13 | Die block length | $L_{die} = L_{cavity} + 2 b + 2 \times 25 \text{ mm}$ | mm | Forging + gutter + locks |
| 14 | Die block thickness (drop hammer) | $H \approx (1.0 \text{ to } 1.5) \times W_{cavity}$ | mm | For shock resistance |
| 15 | Forging force (drop hammer) | $E = 0.5 m v^{2}$ | J | Energy balance |
| 16 | Stroke / blow energy | $H_{eff} = (0.6 \text{ to } 0.8) H$ | m | Effective lift height |
| 17 | Ram velocity (drop hammer) | $v = \sqrt{2 g H_{eff}}$ | m/s | Free-fall |
| 18 | Required hammer mass | $m = 2E / v^{2}$ | kg | Iterate for drop height |
| 19 | Die life (H11, HRC 42) | $\approx 5{,}000 \text{ to } 20{,}000$ | strokes | Depends on steel grade |
| 20 | Hot-work die steel | 5CrNiMo / H11 / H13 | — | Hot-work tool steels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Unit Convention for KTU:** All linear dimensions in **mm**, forces in **kN**, energies in **J** or **kJ**, and angles in **degrees**. A unit mismatch alone can cost 0.5–1 mark in KTU valuation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive connecting rods, crankshafts, gears** – drop forged for grain flow and fatigue strength.
- **Aerospace landing gear lugs** – closed-die forged for high strength-to-weight ratio.
- **Hand tools (wrenches, hammers)** – drop forged for toughness and wear resistance.
- **Valve bodies and pipe fittings** – press forged; die design principles overlap with drop forging.

The **DFMA philosophy** in forging dictates that designers must communicate to the forging shop: *parting line, draft, fillets, and flash thickness* — all of which come from this die design process.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Draft Length on a Cavity Wall

A forging cavity of depth $h$ with external draft angle $\alpha$ requires a linear taper (draft length) so that the forging can be ejected without scoring the die wall.

**Given:** Cavity depth $h = 40 \text{ mm}$, external draft $\alpha = 5^\circ$.

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup:** The die wall, when unfolded, is a right-angled triangle whose vertical leg is the cavity depth $h$ and whose base is the draft length $d$.

**Step 2 — Tangent Relation:**

$$
\tan \alpha = \dfrac{d}{h}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $d$:**

$$
d = h \tan \alpha
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Values:**

$$
d = 40 \times \tan 5^\circ
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate $\tan 5^\circ$:**

$$
\tan 5^\circ = 0.0875
$$

**Step 6 — Final Numerical Result:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= 40 \times 0.0875 \\
  &= 3.50 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** The cavity wall must be sloped by 3.5 mm laterally across 40 mm depth to allow clean ejection.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shrinkage Allowance for a Steel Forging

**Given:** Cold finished length of part $L_c = 250 \text{ mm}$, material is medium-carbon steel with linear shrinkage $S = 2.0 \times 10^{-2}$.

**Step 1 — Definition:** Shrinkage allowance compensates for thermal contraction from forging temperature to room temperature.

**Step 2 — Formula:**

$$
L_{hot} = L_{c} \left(1 + S\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
L_{hot} = 250 \times (1 + 0.02)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{hot} &= 250 \times 1.02 \\
        &= 255.00 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** The cavity (hot die dimension) is 5 mm larger than the cold drawing dimension — both along length and width.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Flash Gutter Geometry from Volume Criterion

**Given:** Forging volume $V_p = 4.5 \times 10^{5} \text{ mm}^{3}$, parting line perimeter (effective flash length) $L_f = 380 \text{ mm}$, selected flash thickness $t_f = 2.5 \text{ mm}$, flash land width $b = 4 \times t_f = 10 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1 — Required Gutter Volume:** A well-designed flash gutter typically has 0.8 times the forging volume to ensure full fill.

$$
V_{gutter} = 0.8 \times V_p
$$

**Step 2 — Compute:**

$$
V_{gutter} = 0.8 \times 4.5 \times 10^{5} = 3.6 \times 10^{5} \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Gutter Cross-Sectional Area (approximate rectangular):**

$$
A_g = t_f \times b + \tfrac{1}{2} (b \times h_g)
$$

**Step 4 — For a chosen gutter depth $h_g = 2 t_f = 5 \text{ mm}$:**

$$
A_g = (2.5 \times 10) + 0.5 \times (10 \times 5) = 25 + 25 = 50 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Gutter Volume Along Flash Perimeter:**

$$
V_{check} = A_g \times L_f = 50 \times 380 = 19{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

**Step 6 — Critical Check:** $V_{check} \ll V_{gutter}$ in this example, indicating a **multi-cavity or blocker-finisher arrangement** is needed, OR the flash perimeter must be increased, OR $t_f$ must be increased. The designer must iterate.

> **Result:** The flash gutter must enclose the forging along a long perimeter (or have larger depth) to provide adequate throttling volume.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Drop Hammer Energy and Ram Mass

**Given:** Forging force estimate $F = 1.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ N}$, effective stroke $H_{eff} = 1.6 \text{ m}$, ram velocity reached $v = 5.6 \text{ m/s}$.

**Step 1 — Work Done by Hammer:**

$$
E = F \times \delta
$$

where $\delta \approx 0.05 \text{ m}$ is deformation per blow. So $E = 1.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.05 = 7.5 \times 10^{4} \text{ J} = 75 \text{ kJ}$.

**Step 2 — Kinetic Energy Equivalence:**

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} = E
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $m$:**

$$
m = \dfrac{2E}{v^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$
m = \dfrac{2 \times 7.5 \times 10^{4}}{(5.6)^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{5}}{31.36} \\
  &= 4{,}782 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Add 30 % safety and round up to standard drop hammer class:**

$$
m_{design} \approx 5{,}000 \text{ to } 6{,}000 \text{ kg} \;\; (5 \text{ to } 6 \text{ tonne drop hammer})
$$

> **Result:** A 5–6 tonne drop hammer is required to produce this forging with the stated energy per blow.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Die Block Sizing for a Drop Hammer Die

**Given:** Cavity projected width $W_c = 90 \text{ mm}$, cavity projected length $L_c = 160 \text{ mm}$, flash land width $b = 10 \text{ mm}$, draft allowance each side $d = 4 \text{ mm}$, bolster margin each side $m = 25 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1 — Die Block Width:**

$$
W_{die} = W_c + 2b + 2d + 2m
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
W_{die} = 90 + (2 \times 10) + (2 \times 4) + (2 \times 25) = 90 + 20 + 8 + 50 = 168 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Die Block Length:**

$$
L_{die} = L_c + 2b + 2d + 2m = 160 + 20 + 8 + 50 = 238 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Die Block Thickness (Drop Hammer Empirical Rule):**

$$
H_{die} = 1.25 \times W_{die} = 1.25 \times 168 = 210 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Round to standard die block stock:**

$$
H_{die} \rightarrow 220 \text{ mm (standard H11 stock thickness)}
$$

> **Result:** A die block of **168 mm × 238 mm × 220 mm** in **H11 hot-work die steel**, hardened to **HRC 42–45**, is the final die set specification.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — KTU Scoring Summary Table

| Derivation | Concept Tested | Common Student Error | Marks at Stake |
|---|---|---|---|
| D1 | Draft angle geometry | Forgetting to convert degrees | 2–3 |
| D2 | Shrinkage factor | Using volumetric instead of linear | 1–2 |
| D3 | Flash gutter volume | Confusing flash thickness vs. gutter depth | 3–4 |
| D4 | Hammer energy | Mixing units (kg·m/s vs. J) | 2–3 |
| D5 | Die block sizing | Omitting bolster margin | 2 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Drop Forging Die Set — Cross-Sectional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["UPPER DIE HOLDER\n(Pneumatic / Belt-driven ram)"] --> B["UPPER DIE BLOCK\nMaterial: H11 / 5CrNiMo"]
    B --> C["UPPER IMPRESSION\nDraft: 5 deg external"]
    C --> D["FLASH LAND\nWidth: 3 to 5 times t_f"]
    D --> E["FLASH GUTTER\nDepth: 1.5 to 2.5 times t_f"]
    E --> F["PARTING LINE PLANE\nSymmetry and side thrust control"]
    F --> G["FORGED PART\nInside cavity envelope"]
    G --> H["LOWER IMPRESSION\nDraft: 7 to 10 deg internal"]
    H --> I["LOWER DIE BLOCK\nHardness: HRC 42 to 45"]
    I --> J["DIE SET BASE\nWith bolster and locking keys"]
    J --> K["EJECTION PINS / KICKER PADS\nEject forged component"]
    K --> L["DIE LUBRICATION SYSTEM\nGraphite / water-based colloidal"]

    M["FORCING OPERATION"] --> A
    N["FLASH TRIM DIE\nSeparate station"] --> O["FINISHED FORGED COMPONENT"]
    G --> N
```

## 4.2 Forging Sequence — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1: Heated Billet\n(1200 deg C for steel)"] --> S2["STEP 2: Place on Lower Die\nManual or mechanical handling"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3: First Blow\nDrawing / blocking in cavity"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4: Second Blow\nCavity fill to final shape"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5: Flash Formation\nControlled throttling at gutter"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6: Parting and Ejection\nKicker pin release"]
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7: Flash Trimming\nTrim die operation"]
    S7 --> S8["STEP 8: Final Inspection\nDimensional and surface check"]
    S8 --> S9["STEP 9: Heat Treatment\nNormalize / Quench / Temper"]
    S9 --> S10["STEP 10: Machining\nMachining allowance removal"]
```

## 4.3 Flash Gutter Geometry — Detail Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P["PARTING LINE\nTop of flash land"] --> Q["FLASH LAND\nb = 3 to 5 times t_f"]
    Q --> R["FLASH THROAT\nBottleneck zone\ncontrols flow"]
    R --> S["FLASH GUTTER\nh_g = 1.5 to 2.5 times t_f"]
    S --> T["DIE BLOCK EDGE\nOuter boundary of die"]
    Q --> V["t_f = 1.0 to 3.5 mm\nFlash thickness"]
    R --> W["CRITICAL THROAT\nSmallest section area"]
    S --> X["VOLUME RESERVOIR\nHolds excess material"]
```

## 4.4 Design Logic Flow — From Part Drawing to Final Die

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Receive Forging Drawing\nCold part geometry"] --> A1["Step 1: Add Shrinkage Allowance\n2 percent linear for steel"]
    A1 --> A2["Step 2: Add Machining Allowance\n1.5 to 3 mm on machined faces"]
    A2 --> A3["Step 3: Locate Parting Line\nSymmetry and depth criterion"]
    A3 --> A4["Step 4: Apply Draft Angles\n3 to 7 deg external\n5 to 10 deg internal"]
    A4 --> A5["Step 5: Insert Fillet Radii\nr = 0.07 to 0.10 times t"]
    A5 --> A6["Step 6: Design Webs and Ribs\nW greater than 3 T_rib\nT_rib greater than 1.5 T_wall"]
    A6 --> A7["Step 7: Design Flash Gutter\nt_f, b, h_g selection"]
    A7 --> A8["Step 8: Size Die Block\nW_die, L_die, H_die"]
    A8 --> A9["Step 9: Select Die Material\nH11, HRC 42 to 45"]
    A9 --> A10["Step 10: Generate Die Drawing\n2D engineering with tolerances"]
    A10 --> A11["Step 11: Manufacture Die\nCNC, EDM, hand finishing"]
    A11 --> A12["Step 12: Tryout and Adjust\nTrial forging on hammer"]
```

## 4.5 Why This Block-Level Architecture (Physical Drawing Justification)

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** A true cross-section of a drop forging die contains curved impression surfaces, complex flash gutter profiles, and shrink-fit chuck details that Mermaid cannot render geometrically. Hence a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** is presented: each node maps to a real die element, and the arrows preserve the **directional metal flow and structural hierarchy** of the die set.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Set B]**
**State any three design considerations to be followed while designing a drop forging die. Mention the standard values.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Draft angle:** External 3°–7°, internal 5°–10° — to enable clean ejection of the forging from the die cavity without scoring. (1 mark)
2. **Fillets and corner radii:** All sharp corners are replaced by fillets of radius $r = 0.07$ to $0.10$ times the adjacent thickness — to avoid stress concentration in die and in forging. (1 mark)
3. **Parting line location:** A plane is chosen to provide a single symmetric impression, minimize lateral thrust, and avoid deep pockets on one side. (1 mark)

> *[Examiner's Note: Award full marks only if numerical values are quoted.]*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]**
**What is a flash gutter? Why is it important in a drop forging die?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** A *flash gutter* is a thin channel surrounding the die cavity at the parting line, comprising a flash land (throttling zone) and a gutter (reservoir) that allows controlled escape of excess metal.
- **Importance (1.5 marks):** It acts as a throttling resistance that forces the metal to fill the deepest and thinnest features of the cavity before any significant amount escapes. Without a properly sized flash gutter, the forging will be **incompletely filled**, leading to rejections.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Forging Die for a Spindle-Type Component**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**

A steel forging has a finished length of **200 mm**, width of **80 mm**, and a maximum depth of **35 mm**. The forging has a central rib of 12 mm thickness connecting two flat webs of 18 mm thickness. The forging material is medium-carbon steel (linear shrinkage = 2 %). The forging is to be produced on a drop hammer.

**(a) Design the die cavity, including shrinkage allowance, draft angles, and fillet radii. State clearly each step. (7 marks)**

**(b) Design the flash gutter and size the die block. Recommend a die material and hardness. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Cavity Design (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Apply Shrinkage Allowance (2 %):**

$$
L_{hot} = 200 \times 1.02 = 204 \text{ mm}, \quad W_{hot} = 80 \times 1.02 = 81.6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Machining Allowance:** Add 2 mm on all machined faces (assume top and bottom are machined):

$$
L_{hot,mach} = 204 + (2 \times 2) = 208 \text{ mm}, \quad W_{hot,mach} = 81.6 + 4 = 85.6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Add Draft Angles:** External draft $\alpha = 5^\circ$ on the depth 35 mm.

$$
