d = 35 \times \tan 5^\circ = 35 \times 0.0875 = 3.06 \approx 3.1 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Fillet Radii:** For rib thickness 12 mm, fillet radius

$$
r = 0.08 \times 12 = 0.96 \approx 1.0 \text{ mm}
$$

For web thickness 18 mm,

$$
r = 0.08 \times 18 = 1.44 \approx 1.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Parting Line:** Choose the centre plane of the part horizontally — symmetric about PL, equal depth on both dies (17.5 mm each).

**Step 6 — Web/Rib Check:** Central rib 12 mm and webs 18 mm satisfy $T_{web} = 18 \geq 3 \times T_{rib}/2$ — geometrically sound. Rib-to-wall ratio $12 / 18 = 0.67 \geq 0.5$ — OK.

*(Total 7 marks: 1 mark for shrinkage, 1 for draft, 1 for fillet, 1 for PL, 2 for full layout, 1 for web/rib check.)*

---

**Part (b) — Flash Gutter and Die Block (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Flash Thickness:** For a forging of projected area ≈ 200 × 80 = 16,000 mm², choose $t_f = 2.0 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 2 — Flash Land Width:**

$$
b = 4 \times t_f = 4 \times 2 = 8 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Flash Gutter Depth:**

$$
h_g = 2 \times t_f = 2 \times 2 = 4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Die Block Sizing:** With cavity width 85.6 mm and length 208 mm:

$$
W_{die} = 85.6 + 2(8) + 2(3.1) + 2(25) = 85.6 + 16 + 6.2 + 50 = 157.8 \approx 160 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_{die} = 208 + 16 + 6.2 + 50 = 280.2 \approx 280 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
H_{die} = 1.25 \times 160 = 200 \text{ mm (round to standard 200 mm stock)}
$$

**Step 5 — Die Material:** **H11 hot-work die steel**, hardened to **HRC 42–45**, tempered at 600 °C for hot-work toughness.

*(Total 7 marks: 1 for $t_f$, 1 for $b$, 1 for $h_g$, 2 for die block, 1 for material, 1 for hardness.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Open-Die vs Closed-Die Analysis)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Set A]**

**(a) Compare the design features of open-die forging and closed-die forging. Discuss the applicability of each in industry with examples. (7 marks)**

**(b) For a steel connecting rod forging of finished mass 4.2 kg, design the flash gutter given that the flash perimeter around the cavity is 540 mm. The flash thickness may be taken as 2.5 mm. Recommend drop hammer capacity. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)**

| Feature | Open-Die Forging | Closed-Die Forging |
|---|---|---|
| Cavity definition | No cavity; flat or simple shape dies | Full impression cavity in both halves |
| Material flow | Free lateral flow, no restriction | Restricted by cavity and flash gutter |
| Tolerance | Loose (±2 mm or more) | Tight (±0.25 to ±0.5 mm) |
| Draft angle | Not required | Mandatory (3°–10°) |
| Machining allowance | Larger (3–6 mm) | Smaller (1.5–3 mm) |
| Die cost | Low | High (H11/H13, CNC machined) |
| Production rate | Low | High |
| Typical parts | Shafts, rings, discs, slabs | Connecting rods, gears, hand tools |
| Industries | Heavy forging (power, ship) | Automotive, aerospace, hand tools |

*(2 marks for the table, 2 marks for additional design feature differences like draft and tolerance, 3 marks for industry examples with at least two examples per type.)*

---

**Part (b) — Flash Gutter and Hammer Capacity (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Required Flash Gutter Volume:** For complete cavity fill, $V_{gutter} \approx 0.8 V_{part}$.

For steel, mass = 4.2 kg, density = 7850 kg/m³.

$$
V_{part} = \dfrac{4.2}{7850} = 5.35 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3} = 5.35 \times 10^{5} \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Required Gutter Volume:**

$$
V_{gutter} = 0.8 \times 5.35 \times 10^{5} = 4.28 \times 10^{5} \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Flash Land and Depth:**

$$
b = 4 \times 2.5 = 10 \text{ mm}, \quad h_g = 2 \times 2.5 = 5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Approximate Cross-Sectional Area of Gutter:**

$$
A_g \approx b \times t_f + 0.5 \times b \times h_g = (10 \times 2.5) + 0.5 \times (10 \times 5) = 25 + 25 = 50 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Available Gutter Volume from Perimeter:**

$$
V_{available} = A_g \times L_f = 50 \times 540 = 27{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

**Step 6 — Comparison:** $V_{available} = 27{,}000 \ll V_{gutter,required} = 4.28 \times 10^{5}$.

> **Conclusion:** A **single-cavity finisher is not sufficient**; the design must use a **blocker + finisher two-cavity die**, OR the flash thickness must be increased to about $t_f = 8 \text{ mm}$, OR the flash perimeter must be extended by adding a second parting region.

**Step 7 — Drop Hammer Capacity:** Empirical rule: hammer mass ≈ 6 × forging mass for a 4.2 kg steel connecting rod.

$$
m_{hammer} \approx 6 \times 4.2 = 25.2 \text{ kg (blow energy per kg forging)}
$$

Total hammer energy for one blow:

$$
E \approx 25.2 \times 4.2 \times 9.81 \approx 1038 \text{ J per blow}
$$

> **Recommended drop hammer: 500–1000 kg ram with 1.5–2.0 m drop height** delivering 8–16 kJ per blow, sufficient for a 4.2 kg connecting rod in 2–3 blows.

*(Total 7 marks: 1 for volume calc, 1 for cross-section, 1 for perimeter check, 1 for design conclusion, 1 for hammer energy, 1 for recommendation, 1 for units.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Drop Forging Die Design Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit mixing in hammer energy (–1 mark):** Mixing kg, m, and J without consistency. Always start with $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^{2}$ in **SI units** and stick to **Joules** until the final answer.
> 2. **Confusing flash thickness with flash land width (–1 mark):** $t_f$ is the *thinnest* dimension at the parting line; $b$ is the *land width* along the parting line. They are not the same.
> 3. **Omitting bolster margin in die block sizing (–1 to 2 marks):** KTU expects $W_{die}$ and $L_{die}$ to include bolster/lock margins. A common error is to give the cavity size as the die size.
> 4. **Forgetting draft angle sign (–0.5 mark):** Draft is *always* measured from the cavity wall (not the part axis). Internal draft and external draft differ in sign convention.
> 5. **Wrong shrinkage reference temperature (–1 mark):** Shrinkage must be computed from *forging temperature* (≈1200 °C for steel) to *room temperature* (≈25 °C), not from a generic "hot" state.
> 6. **Not stating die material and hardness (–1 mark):** The question almost always expects **H11 / 5CrNiMo** and **HRC 38–45** as the answer.
> 7. **Skipping the web/rib check (–0.5 mark):** The $W \ge 3 T_{rib}$ rule is a board favourite. Mentioning it without applying it loses a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drop Forging Die Design — 60-Second Revision**

- **Shrinkage (steel):** 2 % linear (1.5 % Al, 1.5 % Cu).
- **Draft:** External **3°–7°**, internal **5°–10°**; compute draft length as $d = h \tan \alpha$.
- **Fillets:** $r = 0.07$ to $0.10 \times$ adjacent thickness.
- **Webs:** $W \ge 3 T_{rib}$ (for hot-spot prevention).
- **Ribs:** $T_{rib} \ge 1.5 T_{wall}$.
- **Flash thickness $t_f$:** 1.0 to 3.5 mm depending on forging mass.
- **Flash land width $b$:** 3 to 5 times $t_f$.
- **Flash gutter depth $h_g$:** 1.5 to 2.5 times $t_f$.
- **Flash gutter volume:** 0.7 to 1.0 times the part volume — *the single most important design rule*.
- **Die block sizing:** $W_{die} = W_{cavity} + 2b + 2d + 2 \times 25$ mm bolster margin (and similarly for $L_{die}$).
- **Die block thickness (drop hammer):** $H_{die} \approx 1.25 \times W_{die}$.
- **Die material:** **H11** (or H13 / 5CrNiMo) hot-work tool steel, **HRC 38–45**.
- **Hammer energy:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$; ram velocity $v = \sqrt{2 g H_{eff}}$.
- **Hammer mass rule of thumb:** $m_{hammer} \approx 6 \times m_{forging}$ (rough indicator only).
- **Parting line (PL):** Centre plane, symmetric, no deep pockets in one half.
- **Machining allowance:** 1.5 to 3 mm on all machined surfaces.
- **DFMA mantra:** *"If you cannot forge it, you cannot design it"* — die design drives the part design, not the other way around.
- **Fillet rule of thumb:** Never put a sharp corner in a die; never draw a sharp corner on a forging drawing.
- **Flash is a feature, not a defect:** It controls quality, not waste.
- **Die life:** 5,000–20,000 strokes in H11 at HRC 42 for typical carbon-steel forgings.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Design Recommendations for Forging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** General Design Recommendations for Forging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: GENERAL DESIGN RECOMMENDATIONS FOR FORGING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forging** is a manufacturing process in which metal is shaped by plastic deformation under compressive forces, typically using dies, hammers, or presses. The design of a forging directly determines its manufacturability, die life, material utilization, and final mechanical performance.

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
**General Design Recommendations for Forging** constitute a set of geometric, metallurgical, and process-oriented design rules that govern the shape, proportions, draft angles, fillet radii, parting line placement, rib-web-boss relationships, and tolerance specifications of a forged component. These recommendations ensure the metal flows uniformly during deformation, minimize die wear, prevent forging defects (laps, folds, underfills, cracks), and promote favorable grain flow for enhanced mechanical strength.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Clay Vase Principle
Imagine shaping a wet clay vase on a potter's wheel. If you try to make a vase with razor-sharp edges, deep narrow cavities, and ultra-thin walls, the clay tears, cracks, or simply refuses to flow. The clay demands:
- **Generous curves** (radii/fillets) where walls meet — like rounding off a corner so stress flows smoothly.
- **A slight outward slope** (draft angle) so the vase slides out of the mold instead of sticking — just like a baking cake needs tapered sides for easy release.
- **Balanced thickness** so one part dries/contracts at the same rate as the other.

Forging behaves identically. The heated metal "wants" to flow, and the designer's job is to **shape the geometry so that the metal's natural flow is encouraged, not fought against**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes three pillars of forging design:
> 1. **Process-driven geometry** (draft, fillet, parting line)
> 2. **Section uniformity** (avoid abrupt changes in cross-section)
> 3. **Grain flow continuity** (load-bearing paths follow fiber direction)

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Minimum fillet radius (R_min):** **3 mm** (impression die forging)
- **Draft angle (α):** **3°–7°** (outside surfaces), **5°–10°** (inside surfaces)
- **Forging temperature (carbon steel):** **800 °C–1250 °C**
- **Flash-to-stock thickness ratio (t_f / t):** **2 : 3** (typical)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Forging Design

| # | Design Parameter | Purpose | Consequence if Ignored |
|---|------------------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Draft Angle** | Eject forging from die | Trapped part, die damage, scoring |
| 2 | **Fillet / Corner Radii** | Smooth metal flow | Laps, folds, die-crack initiation |
| 3 | **Web & Rib Proportions** | Maintain uniform flow | Underfill, buckles, cold shuts |
| 4 | **Parting Line Location** | Balanced die closure | Flash defects, material waste |
| 5 | **Section Thickness Ratio** | Predictable cooling & deformation | Quench cracks, residual stresses |

## 2.2 Draft Angle — The Release Geometry

The **draft angle (α)** is the taper applied to vertical walls of a forging so that the part can be ejected from the die cavity without binding.

**Relationship between draft and depth:**

$$d = D + 2h \tan \alpha$$

where:
- $d$ = dimension at the open (back) face of the die
- $D$ = dimension at the parting line
- $h$ = depth of the cavity
- $\alpha$ = draft angle (degrees)

> [!NOTE]
> For **impression-die forging**, $\alpha$ outside = **3°–7°**; $\alpha$ inside = **5°–10°**.
> For **drop forging**, $\alpha$ outside = **5°–7°**; $\alpha$ inside = **7°–10°**.

## 2.3 Fillet and Corner Radii — The Stress & Flow Softeners

Fillet radii are critical because metal flows **around corners**, not through them. An insufficient radius forces the metal to fold over itself, creating **laps** and **folds**.

**Standard Rules (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

$$R = 0.5 \times h, \quad R_{\min} = 3 \text{ mm}$$

$$r = 1.5R \text{ to } 2R$$

where:
- $R$ = fillet radius (junction of rib/web with base)
- $r$ = corner radius (intersection of two ribs)
- $h$ = height of the adjoining rib/web

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **larger fillet** improves metal flow but **increases die wear** and consumes material. KTU examiners expect students to articulate this **trade-off**.

## 2.4 Web and Rib Design — The Flow Distribution Rules

Webs and ribs must be **proportionally thinner** than the base to ensure complete die fill. Excessive web thickness traps gas, prevents flow, and causes **underfill defects**.

$$t_{w} \leq 3 t_{b}$$

$$h_{r} \leq 2.5 t_{b}$$

where:
- $t_w$ = web thickness
- $t_b$ = rib base thickness
- $h_r$ = rib height

## 2.5 Boss Design — Localized Mass Concentration

A **boss** is a cylindrical protrusion on a forging (e.g., a hub for gear mounting). Improperly designed bosses create **cold shuts** and **lapping** at the boss-base junction.

$$h_{b} \leq 0.5 \, d_{b}$$

$$D_{b} = d_{b} + 2h_{b} \tan \alpha$$

where:
- $h_b$ = boss height
- $d_b$ = boss diameter
- $D_b$ = boss diameter at the parting line (with draft)

## 2.6 Forging Force and Pressure — The Energy Equation

For closed-die forging in a press, the **maximum forging force** is given by:

$$F = K \, A \, \sigma_{f}$$

where:
- $K$ = die constraint factor (1.5 to 3.0; **K = 2.0** for typical impression die)
- $A$ = projected area of the forging including flash (m²)
- $\sigma_f$ = flow stress of the material at forging temperature (Pa)

The **flash thickness** is governed by the gutter design and die closure:

$$\frac{t_f}{t} = 2 \text{ to } 3$$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Variables | Units |
|---------|-------------|-----------|-------|
| $d = D + 2h\tan\alpha$ | Draft angle relation | d, D = dimensions; h = depth; α = draft | mm, mm, mm, ° |
| $R = 0.5h,\; R_{\min}=3\text{mm}$ | Fillet radius | R = radius; h = rib height | mm |
| $r = 1.5R \text{ to } 2R$ | Corner radius | r = corner; R = fillet | mm |
| $t_w \leq 3 t_b$ | Web thickness rule | t_w, t_b = thicknesses | mm |
| $h_r \leq 2.5 t_b$ | Rib height rule | h_r = rib height; t_b = base | mm |
| $h_b \leq 0.5 d_b$ | Boss proportion | h_b, d_b = boss height, diameter | mm |
| $F = K A \sigma_f$ | Forging force | K = factor; A = area; σ_f = flow stress | N, m², Pa |
| $t_f / t = 2 \text{ to } 3$ | Flash ratio | t_f = flash; t = stock | mm |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility
These rules directly govern the manufacture of **crankshafts, connecting rods, gears, flanges, valves, and aerospace structural brackets**. A part designed without these recommendations will fail during the forging stage itself — long before any service loading test.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Draft Angle Geometry

Consider a forging cavity of depth $h$ with a draft angle $\alpha$ on the outside wall.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometric configuration:**

The cavity wall is inclined at angle $\alpha$ from the vertical parting plane. The horizontal offset of the wall over the cavity depth $h$ is:

$$\Delta x = h \tan \alpha$$

**Step 2 — Compute the back-face dimension:**

The dimension at the open back face of the die is the parting-line dimension plus the inward taper on **both sides** of the cavity:

$$d = D + 2h \tan \alpha$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation (worked example):**

Given: $D = 80$ mm, $h = 50$ mm, $\alpha = 5°$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan 5^\circ &= 0.0875 \\
d &= 80 + 2(50)(0.0875) \\
d &= 80 + 8.75 \\
d &= 88.75 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is a classic KTU-style 3-mark computation. Note the conversion: angles in degrees must be converted using the tangent function correctly.

## 3.2 Derivation: Fillet Radius as a Function of Section Height

**Step 1 — Empirical rule statement:**

For impression-die forging, the fillet radius at the base of a rib/web of height $h$ is:

$$R = 0.5 \, h$$

with an absolute lower bound: $R_{\min} = 3$ mm.

**Step 2 — Apply the lower bound rule:**

For a rib of height $h = 4$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 0.5 \times 4 = 2 \text{ mm} \\
&\Rightarrow R_{\text{use}} = 3 \text{ mm (lower bound governs)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Corner radius calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= 1.5R \text{ to } 2R \\
r &= 1.5 \times 3 = 4.5 \text{ mm (minimum)} \\
r &= 2 \times 3 = 6 \text{ mm (preferred)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **3 mm minimum fillet** is an absolute floor in KTU problem statements. Always state this explicitly in your answer to score full marks.

## 3.3 Derivation: Web & Rib Thickness Limits

**Step 1 — Web thickness constraint:**

The web of a forging must be thin enough that the metal can flow from the base into the ribs without trapping air or forming laps.

$$t_w \leq 3 \, t_b$$

**Step 2 — Rib height constraint:**

Ribs must be short relative to their base width to prevent buckling and to allow the die to fill completely.

$$h_r \leq 2.5 \, t_b$$

**Step 3 — Numerical check (worked example):**

For a base thickness $t_b = 6$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{w,\max} &= 3 \times 6 = 18 \text{ mm} \\
h_{r,\max} &= 2.5 \times 6 = 15 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Forging Force Estimation

**Step 1 — Identify inputs:**

For a steel flange-type forging:
- Projected area (including flash): $A = 0.025$ m²
- Flow stress at forging temperature: $\sigma_f = 80$ MPa
- Die constraint factor: $K = 2.0$

**Step 2 — Apply the force equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= K \cdot A \cdot \sigma_f \\
F &= 2.0 \times 0.025 \times 80 \times 10^{6} \\
F &= 4 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} \\
F &= 4 \text{ MN (Mega-Newtons)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Press tonnage selection:**

The selected hydraulic press must have a capacity ≥ 1.25 × $F$ (with a 25% safety margin):

$$
F_{\text{press}} = 1.25 \times 4 = 5 \text{ MN}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Defect-Mechanism-Design-Fix

| Defect | Visual Symptom | Root Cause | Design Fix |
|--------|----------------|------------|------------|
| **Lap / Fold** | Seam at radius | Insufficient fillet radius | Increase $R \geq 0.5h$ |
| **Underfill** | Incomplete rib | Web too thick / rib too tall | Reduce $t_w$; reduce $h_r$ |
| **Cold Shut** | Surface discontinuity | Poor metal flow at boss junction | Increase boss fillet; add draft |
| **Flash Pinch** | Thin flash region | Gutter too thin | Increase gutter depth |
| **Die Cracking** | Die fracture at corner | Sharp internal corner | Apply $r = 1.5R$ minimum |
| **Scale Pit** | Surface pock-mark | Excessive flash / hot spots | Trim flash; control temperature |
| **Buckle** | Web distortion | Web too thin | Increase $t_w$ to ≥ 4 mm |
| **Crack** | Radial crack | Cold forging / abrupt section | Pre-heat; smooth transition |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners often frame questions as **"Identify the forging defect and recommend the design change."** Memorize the **Defect → Cause → Fix** chain above — it is the single highest-yield topic in Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Forging Design Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Forging Part] --> B{Is the part symmetric about the parting plane?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Locate parting line at center of mass]
    B -- No --> D[Choose parting line to balance die lock]
    C --> E{Is section thickness uniform?}
    D --> E
    E -- No --> F[Add fillet radius R = 0.5h, R min 3 mm]
    E -- Yes --> G[Proceed to draft check]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Is depth greater than 50 mm?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Apply draft 5 to 7 deg outside, 7 to 10 deg inside]
    H -- No --> J[Apply draft 3 to 5 deg outside, 5 to 7 deg inside]
    I --> K{Rib or web present?}
    J --> K
    K -- Yes --> L[Check t w less than 3 t b, h r less than 2.5 t b]
    K -- No --> M[Skip rib check]
    L --> N{Boss present?}
    M --> N
    N -- Yes --> O[Check h b less than 0.5 d b, add boss fillet]
    N -- No --> P[Finalize forging drawing with tolerances]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[End: Design complete and forgeable]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Forging Defect-to-Design Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Design Inputs]
        A1[Part Geometry] --> A2[Material Selection]
        A2 --> A3[Forging Process Type]
    end

    subgraph RULES[Design Rules Engine]
        B1[Draft Angle Module] --> RULESCORE[Rule Evaluator]
        B2[Fillet Radius Module] --> RULESCORE
        B3[Web Rib Module] --> RULESCORE
        B4[Boss Module] --> RULESCORE
    end

    INPUT --> RULES

    subgraph OUTPUT[Design Output]
        RULESCORE --> C1[Validated Forging Drawing]
        RULESCORE --> C2[Defect Risk Report]
        RULESCORE --> C3[Die Cost Estimate]
    end

    C2 --> D1{Defect Risk High?}
    D1 -- Yes --> D2[Redesign Loop]
    D2 --> INPUT
    D1 -- No --> D3[Release to Production]
```

## 4.3 Forging Cross-Section — Annotation Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CS[Cross-Section of a Typical Closed-Die Forged Part]
        P1[Parting Line at Horizontal Midplane]
        P2[Draft Angle alpha: 3 to 7 deg]
        P3[Fillet Radius R: 0.5 h, min 3 mm]
        P4[Corner Radius r: 1.5 R to 2 R]
        P5[Web Thickness t w: less than 3 t b]
        P6[Rib Height h r: less than 2.5 t b]
        P7[Boss: h b less than 0.5 d b]
        P8[Flash gutter: t f slash t equals 2 to 3]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams serve as **conceptual blueprints** for KTU examination sketches. In a 14-mark Part B answer, drawing a **labeled cross-section** with at least 6 of the above annotations typically earns 3–4 additional marks even if the numerical portion is incomplete.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define draft angle in forging. Why is it necessary, and what are typical values for impression-die forging?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Draft angle (α)** is the small taper applied to the vertical walls of a forging cavity to enable easy ejection of the forged part from the die.
>
> **Necessity:**
> - Prevents the forging from binding in the die cavity.
> - Avoids scoring and surface damage during ejection.
> - Compensates for thermal contraction on cooling.
>
> **Typical values for impression-die forging:**
> - **Outside surfaces: 3° – 7°**
> - **Inside surfaces: 5° – 10°**
>
> The formula relating draft to cavity depth $h$ and back-face dimension $d$ is $d = D + 2h \tan \alpha$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is a fillet radius? Why is a minimum fillet radius of 3 mm recommended in impression-die forging?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **fillet radius (R)** is a rounded transition between two intersecting surfaces of a forging (e.g., where a rib meets the base).
>
> **Reasons for the 3 mm minimum:**
> 1. Prevents **laps and folds** by allowing smooth metal flow around corners.
> 2. Reduces **stress concentration** in the die, preventing die-crack initiation.
> 3. Standard rule: $R = 0.5 h$, with an absolute floor of **R_min = 3 mm** because smaller radii are practically impossible to machine in hardened die steel and cause premature die failure.
>
> The corresponding **corner radius** $r$ is taken as **1.5 R to 2 R**. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Q. A forging component has a rib of height 12 mm and a base thickness of 6 mm. The cavity depth is 50 mm and the parting-line dimension is 80 mm. The forging is to be produced in impression-die forging of low-carbon steel.**

**(a)** Determine the **back-face dimension** of the cavity and the **fillet radius** to be provided. Discuss why the **3 mm minimum** rule exists.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **web thickness limit, rib height limit, and boss proportion check** for a boss of diameter 30 mm. Also estimate the **forging force** required if the projected area is 0.03 m² and the flow stress at forging temperature is 75 MPa. Take the die constraint factor as 2.0.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Draft angle selection:**

For an outside wall in impression-die forging, take the lower bound $\alpha = 5°$ (conservative).

**Step 2 — Back-face dimension calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan 5^\circ &= 0.0875 \\
d &= D + 2h \tan \alpha \\
d &= 80 + 2(50)(0.0875) \\
d &= 80 + 8.75 \\
d &= 88.75 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Fillet radius calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 0.5 \times h_{\text{rib}} = 0.5 \times 12 = 6 \text{ mm} \\
R_{\min} &= 3 \text{ mm (governs only if } R < 3 \text{ mm)} \\
\Rightarrow R_{\text{use}} &= 6 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Discussion of 3 mm minimum rule:**

> The 3 mm minimum fillet radius exists because:
> 1. **Die machinability** — radii below 3 mm are difficult to machine accurately in hardened die steel (HRC 58–62).
> 2. **Die life** — sharp corners act as crack-initiation sites under repeated forging loads.
> 3. **Standardization** — 3 mm is the industry-accepted practical floor for impression-die forging.
> 4. **Defect prevention** — smaller radii promote laps, folds, and underfill defects.
> **(Full 7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Web thickness limit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{b} &= 6 \text{ mm} \\
t_{w,\max} &= 3 \, t_b = 3 \times 6 = 18 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Rib height limit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{r,\max} &= 2.5 \, t_b = 2.5 \times 6 = 15 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the actual rib height = 12 mm ≤ 15 mm, **the design is acceptable**.

**Step 3 — Boss proportion check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_b &= 30 \text{ mm} \\
h_{b,\max} &= 0.5 \, d_b = 0.5 \times 30 = 15 \text{ mm} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Boss height must be} &\leq 15 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Forging force estimation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= K \cdot A \cdot \sigma_f \\
F &= 2.0 \times 0.03 \times 75 \times 10^6 \\
F &= 4.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 4.5 \text{ MN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Press selection:**

$$
F_{\text{press}} = 1.25 \times 4.5 = 5.625 \text{ MN} \Rightarrow \text{Select a 6 MN press}
$$

**(Full 7 marks)**

---

### Question B — Alternative (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Q. A forged lever arm is to be designed for an automotive application. The forging is to be made from medium-carbon steel with a flow stress of 90 MPa at forging temperature. The projected area including flash is 0.04 m².**

**(a)** Explain the **parting line selection criteria** for a non-symmetric forging. List the **typical forging defects** and the **corresponding design rules** to prevent them.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Estimate the **forging force and press capacity** required. Also calculate the **flash-to-stock thickness ratio** and discuss its significance on die life and material utilization.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Parting line selection for non-symmetric parts:**

The parting line is the plane along which the two die halves meet. For non-symmetric parts, it should be located so that:
- It passes through the **largest cross-section** (to minimize die complexity).
- It creates **balanced die lock** (equal material on each side).
- It avoids **undercuts** (which would require side-action dies).
- It lies along a **plane that allows complete ejection** without trapped geometry.

**Step 2 — Typical forging defects and design rules:**

| Defect | Cause | Design Rule |
|--------|-------|-------------|
| Lap / Fold | Sharp corner | $R \geq 0.5h$ (min 3 mm) |
| Underfill | Excess web/rib thickness | $t_w \leq 3 t_b$, $h_r \leq 2.5 t_b$ |
| Cold Shut | Poor boss flow | $h_b \leq 0.5 d_b$ |
| Flash Pinch | Thin gutter | $t_f / t = 2$ to $3$ |
| Buckle | Thin web | $t_w \geq 4$ mm |
| Crack | Cold forging | Adequate pre-heat, smooth transitions |

**(Full 7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Forging force estimation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= K \cdot A \cdot \sigma_f \\
F &= 2.0 \times 0.04 \times 90 \times 10^6 \\
F &= 7.2 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 7.2 \text{ MN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Press capacity:**

$$
F_{\text{press}} = 1.25 \times 7.2 = 9.0 \text{ MN} \Rightarrow \text{Select a 10 MN press}
$$

**Step 3 — Flash ratio:**

$$
\frac{t_f}{t} = 2 \text{ to } 3
$$

If stock thickness $t = 10$ mm, then flash thickness $t_f = 20$–$30$ mm.

**Step 4 — Significance on die life and material utilization:**

- **Higher flash ratio** → more material in flash gutter → **worse material utilization** (typical 30–50% material is wasted as flash).
- **Lower flash ratio** → less waste but **higher die wear** at the gutter, since the die must contain higher pressures.
- The **optimum** is $t_f / t = 2.5$ for most ferrous impression-die forgings.
- Excessive flash also increases **trimming cost** and secondary machining operations.

**(Full 7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 3 mm minimum fillet floor** — if your formula gives R < 3 mm, write "But $R_{\min} = 3$ mm governs". This single line is worth 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up draft for inside vs. outside walls** — inside walls need *more* draft (5°–10° vs 3°–7°) because of die-cooling differential.
> 3. **Forgetting the 25% press safety margin** — the press tonnage must be ≥ 1.25 × F.
> 4. **Converting flow stress to Pa incorrectly** — given in MPa, you must multiply by 10⁶.
> 5. **Not stating units explicitly** in intermediate steps — KTU markers deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 6. **Omitting the defect → cause → design-fix link** when asked about forging defects — this is worth 2–3 marks in a 14-mark question.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Draft angle (α):** Outside 3°–7°, Inside 5°–10° (impression die). Formula: $d = D + 2h \tan \alpha$.
- **Fillet radius (R):** $R = 0.5h$, **R_min = 3 mm** — an absolute floor.
- **Corner radius (r):** $r = 1.5R$ to $2R$.
- **Web thickness rule:** $t_w \leq 3 t_b$.
- **Rib height rule:** $h_r \leq 2.5 t_b$.
- **Boss rule:** $h_b \leq 0.5 d_b$.
- **Flash ratio:** $t_f / t = 2$ to $3$ (optimum ≈ 2.5).
- **Forging force:** $F = K A \sigma_f$ with $K = 2.0$ typical, **K = 1.5 to 3.0** range.
- **Press selection:** $F_{\text{press}} = 1.25 \times F$ (25% safety margin).
- **Parting line:** locate at largest cross-section; balance die lock; avoid undercuts.
- **Defect chain:** Lap → small fillet; Underfill → thick web/tall rib; Cold Shut → bad boss; Buckle → thin web; Crack → abrupt section change.
- **Material utilization:** typically **50–70%** for impression-die forging (rest lost to flash, scale, and trim).
- **Grain flow:** design ribs and webs so that the **load path follows the grain direction** — this is a recurring 3-mark question in KTU exams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Scheme Tip:** For any 14-mark question on forging design, structure your answer as **(1) Definition / Selection criterion, (2) Numerical calculation with units, (3) Defect-and-fix table, (4) Sketched cross-section with 5–6 labels**. This 4-part structure is exactly what KTU board examiners reward with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extrusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Extrusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extrusion** is a **compressive plastic deformation manufacturing process** in which a solid or heated **billet (workpiece)** is forced to flow through a shaped **die orifice** under the action of a **ram (punch) or hydraulic pressure**, producing a continuous product of uniform cross-section whose profile conforms to the die geometry.

The defining mathematical relationship that governs the process is the **Extrusion Ratio (R)**, given by:

$$R = \frac{A_{0}}{A_{f}} = \frac{\text{Cross-sectional area of the billet}}{\text{Cross-sectional area of the extruded product}}$$

The complementary parameter is the **Reduction in Area (%)**:

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \left(1 - \frac{A_{f}}{A_{0}}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{1}{R}\right) \times 100$$

| Parameter | KTU Board Standard Symbol | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Extrusion Pressure | $P$ | **MPa** or **N/mm²** |
| Mean Flow Stress of Billet | $\sigma_{f}$ | **MPa** |
| Extrusion Ratio | $R$ | **Dimensionless** |
| Ram Force | $F$ | **kN** or **MN** |
| Die Angle | $2\alpha$ | **Degrees** |
| Billet Length | $L_{0}$ | **mm** |
| Billet Diameter | $D_{0}$ | **mm** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Toothpaste Tube" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine squeezing a tube of toothpaste from the back with your thumb.** The toothpaste (billet) does not change its chemistry, but it **flows** continuously out of the small circular opening (die) as a long, uniform cylindrical strip (extrudate). The harder you press, the faster it flows, and the smaller the nozzle, the more pressure your thumb must apply.

**Engineering Translation of the Analogy:**

| Everyday Action | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Thumb pressing the tube | Hydraulic **ram** applying axial force |
| Tube body | **Container** holding the billet |
| Nozzle (tube mouth) | Shaped **die** with desired profile |
| Toothpaste coming out | **Extrudate** (finished product) |
| Resistance felt by thumb | **Extrusion Pressure (P)** required |
| Tube shape vs nozzle shape | **Billet cross-section** vs **die geometry** |

This simple intuition is the foundation of *forward (direct)* extrusion. If you instead pushed a hollow rod *into* the toothpaste tube from the mouth-side so the paste flowed *backwards around the rod*, that would be the conceptual equivalent of **indirect (backward) extrusion**, where friction between billet and container is eliminated.

---

## 1.3 KTU 2024 Syllabus Classification Snapshot

Extrusion, in the KTU 2024 Scheme's DFMA module, is classified by three independent axes:

1. **Direction of metal flow** → Direct / Indirect (Reverse) / Hydrostatic / Impact
2. **Temperature of working** → **Hot Extrusion**, **Warm Extrusion**, **Cold Extrusion**
3. **Workpiece integrity** → **Solid** (most metals) vs **Hollow** (tube) billets

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Frequently Tested Phrasing:** "Differentiate between direct and indirect extrusion." or "Explain extrusion ratio and its effect on extrusion pressure." Both appear in the **3-mark and 14-mark slots** of Module 3 every academic year.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Physics of Extrusion

The extrusion cycle consists of **four distinct physical stages**, each with its own characteristic force–displacement signature. Understanding these is essential because the KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to plot and interpret the **ram force vs. ram stroke curve**.

### Stage 1 — Billet Upsetting (Heading)
- The cold or pre-heated billet is placed in the **container**.
- The ram approaches and applies a small initial load, upsetting the billet against the die face.
- The material **fills the die cavity completely** and eliminates air gaps.
- Force rises rapidly to a peak — this is the **Breakthrough Pressure (P_bt)**.

### Stage 2 — Steady-State Flow (Steady Extrusion)
- The metal flows plastically through the die orifice as a steady stream.
- The ram force either **remains roughly constant** (ideal, frictionless) or **gradually increases** (direct extrusion with growing friction interface as the billet shortens... actually as a *slug* scrap remains, the friction-bearing surface area changes).
- Most of the extruded length is produced in this stage.

### Stage 3 — Piping Defect Onset
- As the remaining billet becomes thin, the dead-metal zone (DMZ) in front of the die is no longer supported.
- A **central pipe (piping defect)** starts forming inside the remaining slug.
- The ram force often **dips** at this point.

### Stage 4 — Scrap Separation & Ram Retraction
- The remaining thin **slug (butt)** is sheared off by a **die-face knife** or by stopping the ram.
- The ram retracts, the container is recharged with a fresh billet, and the cycle repeats.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** The **steady-state extrusion pressure** governs the design of the press (tonnage rating). The **peak breakthrough pressure** governs the structural strength design of the **container, stem, and die assembly**.

---

## 2.2 The Five Governing Variables of the Extrusion Process

| S.No. | Variable | Engineering Significance | KTU Board Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Extrusion Ratio (R)** | Determines total plastic strain $\varepsilon = \ln R$ | ⭐⭐⭐ Highest |
| 2 | **Billet Temperature (T_b)** | Controls $\sigma_f$; high T → low $\sigma_f$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | **Ram Speed (V_r)** | Higher speed → lower $\sigma_f$ (strain-rate softening in hot work) | ⭐⭐ |
| 4 | **Die Angle (2α)** | Affects redundant work and DMZ geometry | ⭐⭐ |
| 5 | **Friction Factor (m)** | Distinguishes direct from indirect extrusion | ⭐⭐⭐ |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete mathematical toolkit** required to solve every KTU 2024 Scheme numerical problem on extrusion. Master these six equations and the associated boundary conditions.

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Application / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Extrusion Ratio** | $R = A_0/A_f$ | Always $R > 1$; typical range $4$ to $400$ |
| 2 | **True (Logarithmic) Strain** | $\varepsilon = \ln R$ | For ideal homogeneous deformation |
| 3 | **Ideal Extrusion Pressure (no friction, no redundant work)** | $P_{id} = \sigma_f \cdot \ln R$ | Hypothetical lower bound |
| 4 | **Direct Extrusion Pressure (with sticking friction)** | $P_{dir} = \sigma_f \cdot \left(a + b \cdot \ln R\right)$ | $a \approx 1.0,\ b \approx 1.5$ (conical die) |
| 5 | **Indirect (Reverse) Extrusion Pressure (no container friction)** | $P_{ind} = \sigma_f \cdot b \cdot \ln R$ | $b \approx 1.0$ to $1.2$; $a$ term vanishes |
| 6 | **Ram Force** | $F = P \cdot A_0$ | $A_0$ = container/billet cross-section |
| 7 | **Johnson Approximation (friction + redundant, direct)** | $P = \sigma_f \left[\dfrac{2L_0}{D_0} + \ln R\right]$ | Simplified; assumes $\mu \approx 0.5$ |
| 8 | **Breakthrough Pressure (cold extrusion)** | $P_{bt} \approx 1.6 \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \ln R$ | Empirical; used for press selection |
| 9 | **Container Pressure (radial)** | $\sigma_r = P \cdot \dfrac{\nu}{1-\nu}$ | $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio; governs container shrink-fit design |
| 10 | **Maximum Velocity at Die Exit** | $V_{exit} = V_{ram} \cdot R$ | Mass conservation; $V_{exit} > V_{ram}$ always |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX/Markdown Rule Applied:** Notice the formula for $P_{dir}$ above — it uses square brackets `[ ]` and parentheses `( )` only. **Never write $P_{dir} = \sigma_f \cdot \left(a + b \cdot \ln R \vert \right)$** with vertical pipes inside a table row, as it will break the markdown table parser. Use $\lvert \rvert$ or $\mid$ if absolute value is required.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Extrusion is the **backbone of mass-produced structural profiles** worldwide:

- **Architectural & Civil:** Window/door frames, curtain wall sections, heat-sink fins (aluminium 6063).
- **Automotive:** Gear blanks, drive shafts, bumper reinforcements, crash-rails, battery cooling channels for EVs.
- **Aerospace:** Stringers, longerons, structural stiffeners in aircraft wing-boxes.
- **Electrical & Electronics:** Aluminium conductor wires, heat-sink extrusions, lead sleeves for cable joints.
- **Food & Polymer Industry:** Macaroni, pasta, snack pellets, plastic pipes (PVC, HDPE) — same kinematic principle, different material.

The **DFMA (Design for Manufacturing and Assembly) link** is that extruded sections minimize **part count** (no fasteners needed for hollow channels) and **assembly time**, directly supporting the KTU Module 3 learning outcome of **"designing products that are easy to manufacture and assemble."**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step is written out in full. No step is skipped. A KTU 2024 examiner will look for **clear variable definitions, clean algebraic manipulation, and explicit numerical substitution** — exactly the structure followed below.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ideal Homogeneous Extrusion Pressure $P_{id} = \sigma_f \ln R$

### Physical Setup
A cylindrical billet of original cross-section $A_0$ and length $L_0$ is extruded through a die to a final cross-section $A_f$ and a new length $L_f$. The deformation is **homogeneous** — every fiber of the billet strains by the same amount in the axial direction.

### Step 1 — Apply Volume Constancy
The volume of plastic material does not change (incompressibility of plastic deformation).

$$V_0 = V_f \implies A_0 \cdot L_0 = A_f \cdot L_f$$

### Step 2 — Express the True Strain
The **true (logarithmic) strain** in the axial direction is defined as:

$$\varepsilon = \int_{L_0}^{L_f} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln \left( \frac{L_f}{L_0} \right)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Volume Constancy into the Strain Equation
From Step 1, $L_f / L_0 = A_0 / A_f = R$. Substituting:

$$\varepsilon = \ln \left( \frac{A_0}{A_f} \right) = \ln R$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Plastic Work Equation
In homogeneous deformation, the **ideal plastic work per unit volume** equals the product of flow stress and true strain:

$$W_{ideal} = \sigma_f \cdot \varepsilon = \sigma_f \cdot \ln R$$

### Step 5 — Relate Work to Pressure
This work per unit volume is supplied by the ram pressure $P_{id}$ pushing the billet through the die. Equating:

$$P_{id} = \sigma_f \cdot \ln R$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The ideal extrusion pressure depends **only on the material's flow stress** and the **logarithm of the extrusion ratio**. It is *independent* of die angle, friction, and billet length. This is the **thermodynamic lower bound** for any real extrusion process.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Direct Extrusion Pressure with Friction — Slab Method

### Physical Setup
In **direct (forward) extrusion**, the billet is pushed by the ram *in the same direction* as the metal flow. The metal slides against the container wall, generating **frictional shear stress** $\tau_f = \mu \cdot p$, where $p$ is the local container pressure.

### Step 1 — Define the Friction Shear on the Container Wall
The container wall has surface area $\pi D_0 L$ (at any instant during compression). The frictional force opposing the ram is:

$$F_{friction} = \tau_f \cdot \pi D_0 L = \mu p \cdot \pi D_0 L$$

### Step 2 — Apply Slab Force Balance (Differential Element)
Consider an infinitesimal slab of billet of thickness $dL$ at pressure $p$. Force balance in the axial direction:

$$(p + dp) \cdot A_0 = p \cdot A_0 + \tau_f \cdot \pi D_0 \cdot dL$$

The flow-stress term (yielding) is implicit in $p$. Simplifying:

$$dp \cdot A_0 = \mu p \cdot \pi D_0 \cdot dL$$

### Step 3 — Solve the Differential Equation
Separating variables and integrating from the die end (where $p = P_{die}$) to the ram end (where $p = P_{ram}$):

$$\int_{P_{die}}^{P_{ram}} \frac{dp}{p} = \int_{0}^{L_0} \frac{\mu \pi D_0}{A_0} \, dL$$

Since $A_0 = \pi D_0^{2}/4$, the ratio $\dfrac{\mu \pi D_0}{A_0} = \dfrac{4 \mu}{D_0}$. Integrating:

$$\ln \left( \frac{P_{ram}}{P_{die}} \right) = \frac{4 \mu L_0}{D_0}$$

### Step 4 — Express the Ram Pressure
Rearranging:

$$P_{ram} = P_{die} \cdot e^{4\mu L_0 / D_0}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the Die-Exit Pressure
At the die exit, ignoring redundant work for a first approximation, $P_{die} \approx \sigma_f \cdot \ln R$. Therefore:

$$P_{ram} = \sigma_f \cdot \ln R \cdot e^{4\mu L_0 / D_0}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to a Linearized Form (Johnson Approximation)
Using $e^x \approx 1 + x$ for moderate $4\mu L_0/D_0$:

$$P_{ram} \approx \sigma_f \cdot \ln R \cdot \left(1 + \frac{4\mu L_0}{D_0}\right)$$

With the conventional assumption $\mu \approx 0.5$ (sticking friction at hot-work temperatures):

$$P_{ram} \approx \sigma_f \cdot \ln R + \sigma_f \cdot \frac{2 L_0}{D_0}$$

### Step 7 — Final Closed-Form Result

$$\boxed{P_{ram} = \sigma_f \left[\frac{2 L_0}{D_0} + \ln R\right]}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is the most-tested KTU formula in Module 3.** A 14-mark problem will almost always present you with $L_0$, $D_0$, $R$, and $\sigma_f$ and ask for the ram force. Memorize the *exact* form above.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Indirect (Reverse) Extrusion Pressure

In **indirect extrusion**, the die is mounted on a hollow ram that pushes *backward* through the container. The billet does **not** slide against the container wall, so the friction term **vanishes**.

Setting the friction term to zero in the slab analysis (or simply removing the $2L_0/D_0$ term):

$$\boxed{P_{ind} = \sigma_f \cdot \ln R}$$

with only a **redundant-work correction** of $b \approx 1.0$ to $1.2$ added in practice:

$$P_{ind} = \sigma_f \cdot b \cdot \ln R, \quad \text{where } b \approx 1.1$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Question:** A cylindrical aluminium billet of diameter **80 mm** and length **200 mm** is extruded to a final product diameter of **20 mm**. The mean flow stress of aluminium at the working temperature is **80 MPa**. The friction coefficient on the container wall is **0.10**. Calculate:
> (a) The extrusion ratio and percentage reduction.
> (b) The ideal (frictionless) extrusion pressure.
> (c) The actual ram pressure for **direct extrusion**.
> (d) The ram force required.
> (e) The breakthrough pressure.
> (f) Comment on whether indirect extrusion would reduce the pressure.

### Solution

#### (a) Extrusion Ratio and Percentage Reduction
$A_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_0^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(80)^2 = 5026.55 \text{ mm}^2$

$A_f = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2$

$$R = \frac{5026.55}{314.16} = 16.0$$

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{16}\right) \times 100 = 93.75\%$$

#### (b) Ideal Extrusion Pressure
$$P_{id} = \sigma_f \cdot \ln R = 80 \cdot \ln(16) = 80 \cdot 2.7726 = 221.81 \text{ MPa}$$

#### (c) Direct Extrusion Pressure
$$P_{dir} = \sigma_f \left[\frac{2 L_0}{D_0} + \ln R\right] = 80 \left[\frac{2 \times 200}{80} + 2.7726\right] = 80 \left[5.0 + 2.7726\right] = 80 \times 7.7726$$

$$P_{dir} = 621.81 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pressure above ~700 MPa is approaching the practical upper limit for conventional hot-extrusion presses.** This is why the process engineer must check whether indirect extrusion is viable.

#### (d) Ram Force
$$F = P_{dir} \cdot A_0 = 621.81 \times 5026.55 = 3{,}125{,}896 \text{ N} \approx 3125.9 \text{ kN} \approx 3.13 \text{ MN}$$

#### (e) Breakthrough Pressure
$$P_{bt} = 1.6 \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \ln R = 1.6 \times 80 \times 2.7726 = 354.89 \text{ MPa}$$

#### (f) Indirect Extrusion Comparison
For indirect extrusion (no friction term):

$$P_{ind} = \sigma_f \cdot b \cdot \ln R = 80 \times 1.1 \times 2.7726 = 243.99 \text{ MPa}$$

**Reduction in pressure:** $\dfrac{621.81 - 243.99}{621.81} \times 100 = 60.76\%$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** Indirect extrusion reduces the ram pressure by **~60%** for this case, but it requires a hollow ram and a stiffer die-pressing system — a classic **DFMA trade-off** the KTU board loves to ask about.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!NOTE]

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **2-mark to 3-mark direct-conceptual questions** that appear in KTU University's internal assessments, mid-semester modules, and as warm-up sub-parts of 14-mark questions.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** — Define extrusion ratio and explain its significance in the extrusion process.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **extrusion ratio (R)** is defined as the ratio of the cross-sectional area of the billet ($A_0$) to the cross-sectional area of the extruded product ($A_f$):

$$R = \frac{A_0}{A_f}$$

It is a **dimensionless number** that represents the **degree of plastic deformation** imposed on the material. Its significance is twofold:

1. **It directly governs the extrusion pressure.** From $P = \sigma_f \ln R$, a higher R demands exponentially higher pressure. As R approaches 400, the pressure requirement becomes impractical for conventional presses.
2. **It determines the product's length-to-billet-length ratio.** A billet of length $L_0$ produces an extrudate of length $L_f = R \cdot L_0$, so R is also a **length multiplication factor** for the process.

A typical industrial R lies between **10 and 100** for most non-ferrous metals. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** — Differentiate between hot extrusion and cold extrusion.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | **Hot Extrusion** | **Cold Extrusion** |
|---|---|---|
| Working Temperature | Above recrystallization temperature (typically 0.6–0.85 × $T_m$) | Room temperature (or slightly elevated) |
| Flow Stress $\sigma_f$ | Low (thermal softening dominates) | High (work hardening dominates) |
| Ram Pressure | Low to moderate (50–250 MPa typical) | Very high (700–1500 MPa typical) |
| Surface Finish | Oxidized, dull; needs post-machining | Bright, smooth, oxide-free |
| Dimensional Accuracy | Lower (±0.5 mm) | Higher (±0.05 mm) |
| Mechanical Properties | Annealed, ductile | Work-hardened, high strength |
| Typical Materials | Aluminium, copper, magnesium, lead | Steel, stainless steel, aluminium impact parts |
| Equipment Tonnage | Lower | Much higher (requires high-tonnage presses) |
| Application | Long structural profiles, heat-sinks, tubes | Precision shafts, gear blanks, cartridge cases |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Following the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, each 14-mark question has two sub-parts: **Part (a) carrying 7 marks** and **Part (b) carrying 7 marks**. Two alternative full-length questions are provided below so the student can practice either choice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — With Worked Solution

> **Question:** A copper billet of diameter **100 mm** and length **300 mm** is to be extruded at a temperature where the mean flow stress of copper is **150 MPa**. The friction factor on the container wall is **0.08**. The die is conical with a semi-angle of **45°**. Compute:
> (i) The extrusion ratio.
> (ii) The ideal extrusion pressure.
> (iii) The actual ram pressure for direct extrusion using Johnson's formula.
> (iv) The ram force.

**Model Solution:**

**Step (i): Extrusion Ratio**
Assume the final diameter is $D_f = 25$ mm (standard problem assumption for copper extrusion).

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}(100)^2 = 7853.98 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}(25)^2 = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$R = \frac{7853.98}{490.87} = 16.0$$

**Step (ii): Ideal Extrusion Pressure**
$$P_{id} = \sigma_f \cdot \ln R = 150 \cdot \ln(16) = 150 \cdot 2.7726 = 415.89 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step (iii): Direct Extrusion Pressure (Johnson's Formula)**
$$P_{dir} = \sigma_f \left[\frac{2 L_0}{D_0} + \ln R\right] = 150 \left[\frac{2 \times 300}{100} + 2.7726\right] = 150 \left[6.0 + 2.7726\right]$$

$$P_{dir} = 150 \times 8.7726 = 1315.89 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step (iv): Ram Force**
$$F = P_{dir} \cdot A_0 = 1315.89 \times 7853.98 = 10{,}334{,}500 \text{ N} \approx 10.33 \text{ MN}$$

> The required press tonnage is approximately **10.33 MN (≈1055 tonnes)**, indicating that hot extrusion is essential for this part. **(7 Marks — full step marks awarded for clear substitution and unit consistency)**

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — With Worked Solution

> **Question:** Compare **direct and indirect (reverse) extrusion** with neat functional diagrams and explain **why indirect extrusion requires a hollow ram and stiffer die set-up**. Discuss the resulting DFMA implications.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Direct Extrusion | Indirect Extrusion |
|---|---|---|
| Ram Position | Solid ram pushes billet from behind | Hollow ram carries the die and pushes from front |
| Friction on Container Wall | **Yes** — major energy loss | **No** — billet is stationary relative to container |
| Ram Force | $P_{dir} = \sigma_f\left[\frac{2L_0}{D_0} + \ln R\right]$ | $P_{ind} = \sigma_f \cdot b \cdot \ln R$ (≈ 30–60% lower) |
| Billet Length | Long, limited by container length | Short, since friction is not length-dependent |
| Product Length | Limited by R × billet length | Can be very long (small R acceptable) |
| Tooling Cost | Lower | Higher (hollow ram, dummy block, stiffer die set) |
| Defect: Piping | More common | Less common (no DMZ growth issue) |
| Speed of Operation | Slower (billet charging time) | Faster (no billet–container interface sliding) |

**Why a Hollow Ram is Required (DFMA Implication):**

In indirect extrusion, the **die is mounted on the ram itself**. For the extrudate to emerge *backward* out of the container, the ram must be **annular (hollow)**, allowing the extruded product to pass through its central bore. This **inverted loading** means:

1. The hollow ram experiences **tensile hoop stress** in addition to compression — it must be made of **high-grade forged alloy steel** with greater wall thickness.
2. The **die is not supported by the press frame** from the front, so it is structurally weaker. The container must be **longer and stiffer** to react the extrusion load.
3. **Billets must be shorter** (typically $L_0/D_0 \le 2$) to avoid buckling inside the container.

**DFMA Conclusion:** Indirect extrusion is **DFMA-favourable for high-tonnage, brittle alloys (e.g., titanium, magnesium)** and very long products (e.g., nuclear fuel cladding tubes) where the **60% pressure reduction** outweighs the **tooling cost penalty**. For routine aluminium sections, **direct extrusion is more cost-effective and is the global industry default.** **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Anticipated]

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]

> **Question:** With a labelled functional block diagram, describe the **hydrostatic extrusion process**. State **two industrial applications** and explain why it is suitable for **brittle materials**.

**Model Solution Outline (for student self-practice):**
- Definition: billet sits in a pressure chamber; pressurized fluid (castor oil or glycerine) drives the billet through the die.
- Functional block: Pressure Vessel → High-Pressure Pump → Intensifier → Fluid Medium → Billet → Die → Extrudate.
- Applications: zirconium nuclear cladding, superconducting niobium-tin tubes, tungsten rods.
- Suitability for brittle materials: **no container-wall friction** + **isostatic (uniform) compressive stress state** suppresses crack propagation in brittle alloys.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]

> **Question:** A **cold-extrusion** operation on a 0.35% C-steel billet of diameter **50 mm** requires a final product of diameter **15 mm**. The mean yield stress of the work-hardened steel is **600 MPa**. The friction coefficient is **0.10**. Calculate the ram force required using the **direct extrusion formula with redundant work correction** ($a = 1.0$, $b = 1.5$). The billet length is **75 mm**.

**Model Solution:**

$R = (50/15)^2 = 11.11$

$$P_{dir} = \sigma_f (a + b \ln R) = 600 \times (1.0 + 1.5 \times \ln 11.11) = 600 \times (1.0 + 1.5 \times 2.4079)$$

$$P_{dir} = 600 \times (1.0 + 3.6119) = 600 \times 4.6119 = 2767.16 \text{ MPa}$$

$A_0 = \pi/4 \times 50^2 = 1963.5 \text{ mm}^2$

$F = 2767.16 \times 1963.5 = 5{,}432{,}820 \text{ N} \approx 5.43 \text{ MN} \approx 554 \text{ tonnes}$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places KTU students lose marks on Extrusion problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the logarithm** — writing $P = \sigma_f \cdot R$ instead of $P = \sigma_f \cdot \ln R$. This is the **#1 valuation pitfall**; the examiner will deduct 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Confusing R and % reduction** — they are *not* the same. $R = A_0/A_f$ is dimensionless, while % reduction = $(1 - 1/R) \times 100$.
> 3. **Mixing up direct and indirect formulas** — the friction term $\frac{2L_0}{D_0}$ exists **only** for direct extrusion. Writing it for indirect will cost full marks.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency** — using $D_0$ in cm and $L_0$ in mm in the same equation. Always normalize to **mm** before substituting.
> 5. **Forgetting to multiply $P$ by $A_0$** when the question asks for the *ram force* (kN/MN), not the *pressure* (MPa).
> 6. **Wrong area formula** — using $D^2$ instead of $\frac{\pi}{4}D^2$. Two marks lost.
> 7. **Not commenting on the engineering significance** of the result (e.g., "this pressure exceeds the 700 MPa practical limit, so hot extrusion is recommended"). A KTU examiner rewards the **interpretive remark** with 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Module 3 viva and end-semester:**

- ☐ **Extrusion Ratio $R = A_0 / A_f$** — the single most important number; controls pressure, strain, and product length.
- ☐ **True strain $\varepsilon = \ln R$** — always the natural logarithm, never base 10.
- ☐ **Ideal Pressure $P_{id} = \sigma_f \cdot \ln R$** — the frictionless, redundant-work-free lower bound.
- ☐ **Direct Extrusion $P_{dir} = \sigma_f\left[\frac{2L_0}{D_0} + \ln R\right]$** — includes friction; used for ~90% of industrial cases.
- ☐ **Indirect Extrusion $P_{ind} = \sigma_f \cdot b \cdot \ln R$** — no friction term; $b \approx 1.1$; saves 30–60% pressure.
- ☐ **Breakthrough Pressure $P_{bt} \approx 1.6 \sigma_f \ln R$** — governs press tonnage selection.
- ☐ **Ram Force $F = P \cdot A_0$** — final step in every numerical problem.
- ☐ **Hot vs Cold:** Hot → low pressure, oxidized surface; Cold → high pressure, bright surface, work-hardened.
- ☐ **Defects to remember:** Piping (central pipe), die lines, surface cracking (from overheating), tearing (from low temperature).
- ☐ **DFMA angle:** Extrusion reduces **part count and assembly time** — always cite this in 14-mark answers to demonstrate Module 3 context awareness.
- ☐ **Types to enumerate in short answers:** Direct, Indirect, Hydrostatic, Impact, Continuous (Conform process).
- ☐ **Practical extrusion ratio limit:** $R_{\max} \approx 400$ for non-ferrous metals; $R_{\max} \approx 6$ for steels in cold extrusion.
- ☐ **Exit velocity $V_{exit} = V_{ram} \cdot R$** — comes straight from mass conservation; sometimes asked in 3-mark questions.

> **End of Module 3 — Extrusion Notes** | *Prepared under KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sheet Metal Work and Plastics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Sheet Metal Work

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
*Sheet Metal Work* refers to a category of manufacturing processes in which thin, flat sheets of metal (typically **0.4 mm to 6 mm** in thickness) are cut, bent, drawn, or stretched into useful products without adding or removing material. It is a *chipless* (or mass-conserving) forming process performed at room temperature (cold working) or at elevated temperatures (hot working) using presses, dies, and rollers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters in DFMA:** Sheet metal parts dominate appliance, automotive, aerospace, and electronics enclosures. Designing them with manufacturing in mind drastically reduces tooling cost and assembly operations.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Origami + Cookie Cutter" View:**
Think of sheet metal work as making *shapes out of a flat pancake of dough*. The **cookie-cutter** analogy captures *cutting* operations (blanking, piercing, shearing) — you stamp out a shape. The **origami** analogy captures *forming* operations (bending, drawing, stretching) — you fold and stretch the same flat sheet into a 3D shape *without tearing it*. The material is conserved: the same atoms that started flat end up in the final product, just redistributed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core sub-categories:**
> 1. **Cutting (Shearing-type):** Blanking, Piercing, Notching, Trimming
> 2. **Bending (Linear deformation):** V-bending, U-bending, Roll bending, Hemming
> 3. **Drawing (Surface-area redistribution):** Deep drawing, Shallow drawing, Ironing, Spinning

---

### 1.2 Plastics in Manufacturing

**Formal Definition:**
*Plastics* are a broad class of **synthetic or semi-synthetic organic polymers** that can be molded, extruded, or cast into solid shapes. In DFMA, plastics are essential *substitute materials* for metals when the design requires **corrosion resistance, light weight, electrical insulation, color integration, or low-cost high-volume production**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key advantage for design engineers:** Plastics allow **part consolidation** — what once took 8 metal parts welded together can become a single injection-molded plastic component, eliminating fasteners and assembly labor.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Boiled Noodles vs. Boiled Egg" View:**
Plastics come in two fundamentally different families:
- **Thermoplastics = Boiled noodles 🍜**: Heat them and they *soften repeatedly* (you can re-melt and reshape them). Examples: Polyethylene (PE), Polypropylene (PP), PVC, ABS.
- **Thermosets = Boiled egg 🥚**: Once cured, an irreversible chemical reaction locks them into a permanent 3D network. Re-heating causes *decomposition*, not melting. Examples: Phenolic, Epoxy, Melamine.
- **Elastomers = Rubber band 🩹**: Cross-linked but with loosely tied networks, so they stretch and snap back. Examples: Natural rubber, Silicone, Neoprene.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus-Highlight Families:**
> - **Thermoplastics:** PE, PP, PS, PVC, ABS, Nylon (PA), Polycarbonate (PC)
> - **Thermosets:** Phenolics (Bakelite), Epoxy, Polyester, Melamine, Urea-formaldehyde
> - **Elastomers:** Natural Rubber, SBR, Neoprene, Silicone
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sheet Metal Operations — Theory Breakdown

#### A. Cutting (Shearing) Operations
Cutting is achieved by **plastic shear failure** along a narrow band between punch and die. The clearance between punch and die (typically **5–10 % of sheet thickness per side**) governs:
- **Too tight** → secondary burnished zone, tool wear
- **Too large** → large rollover, burrs, distortion
- **Just right** → clean fracture with minimal rollover

**Operations:**
- **Blanking** → the *removed* piece is the useful product; scrap remains in the sheet.
- **Piercing** → the *removed* piece is scrap; the sheet with the hole is the product.
- **Notching** → removes material from the edge of a strip.
- **Trimming** → removes excess material from a previously drawn or formed part.

#### B. Bending Operations
Bending is the **linear plastic deformation** of a sheet about a straight axis. The sheet on the *inside* of the bend is **compressed**, while the *outside* is **stretched**. Somewhere between lies the **neutral axis** — a fiber that neither stretches nor compresses.

- **Minimum bend radius** = a function of material ductility and sheet thickness. For low-carbon steel, R_min ≈ t (one sheet thickness).
- **Spring-back**: when the punch is released, the elastic portion of the strain *recovers*, causing the part to open up by a small angle Δθ. This must be **over-bent** by a compensated angle to achieve the desired final angle.

#### C. Deep Drawing
A flat circular *blank* is forced by a punch through a die cavity to form a *cup-shaped* part. The sheet metal **flows radially inward** and is **redrawn** into a deeper shape. Wall thickness remains nearly equal to the original blank thickness in the *first draw*, but accumulates strain that limits the maximum draw ratio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limiting Draw Ratio (LDR):** The maximum blank-to-punch diameter ratio achievable in a single draw without failure. For low-carbon steel, **LDR ≈ 2.0 to 2.2**.

---

### 2.2 Plastics — Theory Breakdown

#### A. Polymer Behavior
Plastics deform **viscoelastically**: their mechanical response is a combination of viscous (fluid-like) flow and elastic (spring-like) recovery. This gives them:
- **Creep** — slow continuous deformation under sustained load
- **Stress relaxation** — stress decays with time at constant strain
- **High specific strength** (strength-to-weight ratio)
- **Anisotropy** in flow direction vs. cross-flow direction (especially in fiber-filled grades)

#### B. Plastics Processing (Manufacturing View)

| Process | Best For | Typical Materials | Key Design Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Injection Molding** | Complex 3D parts, high volume | Thermoplastics, some thermosets | Uniform wall thickness, draft angle ≥ 1° |
| **Blow Molding** | Hollow vessels, bottles | PE, PET, PP | Parison design controls wall thickness |
| **Extrusion** | Continuous profiles, pipes, sheets | Thermoplastics | Constant cross-section only |
| **Thermoforming** | Trays, enclosures, large panels | PS, PVC, ABS | Male/female die, vacuum or pressure |
| **Compression Molding** | Heat-resistant parts, electrical | Thermosets (Phenolic, Melamine) | Charge mass, cure time critical |
| **Rotational Molding** | Large hollow tanks, toys | PE | Wall thickness controlled by mold rotation speed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design for Assembly (DFA) for Plastics:** Snap-fits, living hinges, and self-tapping boss features are *integral* to molded plastic parts, eliminating fasteners and adhesives.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bending Allowance (arc length at neutral axis) | $BA = \dfrac{\pi}{180}\,(R + K\,t)\,\theta$ | R = inner bend radius (mm), t = sheet thickness (mm), K = neutral-axis K-factor, θ = bend angle (deg) | K ranges from 0.33 (soft, R/t small) to 0.50 (hard, R/t large) |
| 2 | Outside Setback (OSS) | $OSS = (R + t)\,\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$ | R, t, θ as above | Distance from tangent to outer apex of bend |
| 3 | Bend Deduction (BD) | $BD = 2\,(OSS) - BA$ | All above | Used to compute flat-pattern length |
| 4 | Flat Pattern / Developed Length | $FL = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 - \text{BD}$ | L₁, L₂, L₃ = straight leg lengths (mm) | Apply BD once per bend |
| 5 | Blank Diameter (cylindrical cup, first draw) | $D = \sqrt{d^{2} + 4\,d\,h}$ | D = blank diameter (mm), d = cup diameter (mm), h = cup height (mm) | Derived from volume conservation |
| 6 | Limiting Draw Ratio (LDR) | $\text{LDR} = \dfrac{D_{\max}}{d_{\min}}$ | D_max = largest blank that can be drawn in one operation; d_min = smallest punch | For low-carbon steel ≈ 2.0–2.2 |
| 7 | Spring-back Compensation Angle | $\theta_{c} = \theta \left(1 - \dfrac{3\,Y}{4\,E}\cdot\dfrac{t}{2R_{i}+t}\right)^{-1}$ | θ = target bend angle, Y = yield stress, E = Young's modulus, t = thickness, R_i = inner bend radius | Use larger R to reduce spring-back |
| 8 | Bending Strain (outer fiber) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{2R_{i}}{t} + 1}$ | R_i = inner bend radius, t = thickness | Indicates ductility demand |
| 9 | Volume Conservation (general) | $A_{1}\,t = A_{2}\,t_{2}$ | A₁, A₂ = surface areas; t, t₂ = thicknesses | Foundation of all deep-drawing equations |
| 10 | Injection Molding Clamp Tonnage | $F_{c} = A_{p}\,P_{\text{inj}}$ | A_p = projected area (mm²), P_inj = injection pressure (MPa) | Select press with 1.1×–1.3× safety factor |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units & Conversion Pitfall:** KTU problems are almost always in **mm** and **degrees**. If a problem uses **radians**, convert using $\theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\text{deg}}\times\pi/180$ before substituting.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive bodies** (doors, hoods, fenders) → sheet metal *stamping* and *hemming* of low-carbon steel and aluminum alloys.
- **Aircraft fuselage skins** → aluminum sheet *stretch forming* and *roll forming*.
- **Kitchen mixer, food processor housings** → injection-molded **ABS** or **polycarbonate**.
- **Beverage bottles** → PET (thermoplastic) *blow molding*; recycled PET (rPET) is industry standard.
- **Electrical switchgear panels** → phenolic or melamine *compression molding* for heat/flame resistance.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bending Allowance (BA)

We consider a sheet of thickness *t* bent around a cylindrical punch of inner radius *R* through a bend angle *θ* (in degrees).

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**
Place the bend origin at the center of curvature O. The sheet has three reference fibers:
- Inner fiber at radius $R$
- Neutral fiber at radius $R_{n} = R + K\,t$
- Outer fiber at radius $R + t$

**Step 2 — Arc length of the neutral fiber.**
The neutral fiber does not stretch (by definition of K-factor). Its arc length $L_n$ subtends the same angle θ at O:

$$L_{n} = R_{n}\cdot\theta_{\text{rad}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert θ from degrees to radians.**
Since KTU problems give θ in degrees, substitute $\theta_{\text{rad}} = \dfrac{\pi}{180}\,\theta$:

$$L_{n} = (R + K\,t)\,\dfrac{\pi}{180}\,\theta$$

**Step 4 — Identify the result as the Bending Allowance.**
The flat blank must provide exactly this length along the neutral axis to "feed" into the bend. Therefore:

$$\boxed{BA = \frac{\pi}{180}\,(R + K\,t)\,\theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary conditions to remember:**
> - K = 0 → arc is at the inner surface (over-estimates length)
> - K = 0.5 → arc is at the mid-thickness (assumes neutral axis is at centerline, valid for very large R/t)
> - K = 0.33 → typical for sharp bends in soft materials (e.g., aluminum, mild steel at small R/t)

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Flat Pattern Length (FL) & Bend Deduction (BD)

**Step 1 — Define Outside Setback (OSS).**
When the sheet is bent, the *intersection* of the two flat legs (extended) lies *outside* the bend. The perpendicular distance from the apex of the bend (outer surface) to that intersection is the OSS.

By the geometry of a right triangle with adjacent side (R + t) and angle θ/2:

$$OSS = (R + t)\,\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the over-counting.**
The straight legs (L₁ and L₂), measured from the leg intersection, each include the OSS twice (once on each leg). So the *apparent* leg length is *too long* by 2·OSS total.

**Step 3 — Apply the correction.**
The flat blank length must equal the sum of the legs *minus* the over-counted material beyond the neutral arc:

$$FL = L_{1} + L_{2} - \bigl[2\,(OSS) - BA\bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Define the Bend Deduction.**

$$\boxed{BD = 2\,(R + t)\,\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) - \frac{\pi}{180}(R + K\,t)\,\theta}$$

For a part with multiple bends:

$$FL = \sum L_{i} - \sum BD_{j}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Blank Diameter for a Cylindrical Cup (Deep Drawing)

**Step 1 — State the physical principle.**
Mass (and therefore volume, since density is unchanged) is conserved in cold working. The volume of the *flat circular blank* equals the volume of the *drawn cup* (wall + bottom).

**Step 2 — Volume of the blank.**
Blank is a flat disk of diameter D and thickness t:

$$V_{\text{blank}} = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}\,t$$

**Step 3 — Volume of the cup.**
A cup has two regions:
- **Bottom** (circular disk): diameter = d, thickness = t → $V_b = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}\,t$
- **Wall** (cylindrical shell): diameter = d, height = h, thickness = t → $V_w = \pi d h t$

Total cup volume:

$$V_{\text{cup}} = \frac{\pi d^{2} t}{4} + \pi d h t$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two volumes.**

$$\frac{\pi D^{2} t}{4} = \frac{\pi d^{2} t}{4} + \pi d h t$$

**Step 5 — Divide both sides by πt.**

$$\frac{D^{2}}{4} = \frac{d^{2}}{4} + d h$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by 4 and rearrange.**

$$D^{2} = d^{2} + 4 d h$$

**Step 7 — Take the positive square root (diameters are positive).**

$$\boxed{D = \sqrt{d^{2} + 4 d h}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity:** This is a *first-draw* formula. It assumes:
> (a) wall thickness remains ≈ t (no thinning),
> (b) no flange is left on the cup,
> (c) the bottom is flat and circular of diameter d.
> If a flange is left, an extra $\dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_f^{2} - d^{2})\,t$ term is added.

**Worked Numerical Example:**
A cylindrical cup with **d = 50 mm** and **h = 75 mm** is to be drawn from a steel sheet. Find the blank diameter.

$$D = \sqrt{(50)^{2} + 4 \times 50 \times 75}$$

$$D = \sqrt{2500 + 15000} = \sqrt{17500}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 132.3 \text{ mm}}$$

Check the draw ratio: $\text{DR} = D/d = 132.3 / 50 = 2.65$ — this *exceeds* the LDR of ~2.2 for low-carbon steel, so the part **cannot be drawn in one operation** and would need *re-drawing* (or annealing between draws).

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Spring-back Compensation

**Step 1 — Identify elastic recovery.**
When a sheet is bent plastically, both *plastic* and *elastic* strains are imposed. Upon release, the elastic strain recovers, opening the bend by an angle Δθ.

**Step 2 — Relate elastic strain to geometry.**
The maximum elastic bending strain at the outer fiber is:

$$\varepsilon_{e} = \frac{Y}{E}$$

(For most metals, this is on the order of 0.001 to 0.005, i.e., **0.1 % to 0.5 %**.)

**Step 3 — Geometric spring-back angle.**
For pure bending of a linear-elastic-perfectly-plastic material, the spring-back angle is:

$$\Delta\theta = \theta - \theta' = \frac{3\,Y}{4\,E}\cdot\frac{t}{2R_{i}+t}\,\theta$$

**Step 4 — Compensate by over-bending.**
To achieve the *target* angle θ after spring-back, the punch must be set to a *larger* compensated angle:

$$\boxed{\theta_{c} = \frac{\theta}{1 - \dfrac{3\,Y}{4\,E}\cdot\dfrac{t}{2R_{i}+t}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Higher **Y/E** ratio (e.g., high-strength steel) and *smaller R/t* (sharp bends) cause **more spring-back** — exactly the cases where compensation is most critical.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Plastic Process Selection

| Design Requirement | Recommended Process | Wall Thickness Range | Draft Angle Required? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Complex 3D, high volume | Injection Molding | 0.5 – 6 mm | **Yes** (≥ 1°) |
| Hollow bottles, tanks | Blow Molding | 0.3 – 6 mm | No |
| Long uniform profiles | Extrusion | 0.5 – 25 mm | N/A |
| Large thin enclosures | Thermoforming | 0.2 – 6 mm | Yes (≥ 2°) |
| Heat-resistant electrical | Compression Molding | 1 – 50 mm | Yes (≥ 1°) |
| Large hollow toys | Rotational Molding | 2 – 12 mm | No |

The *sub-modules* P4a, P5a, P6a represent the three *critical quality windows* — **pack-and-hold pressure** (prevents sink marks), **gate-freeze timing** (controls crystallinity), and **runner regrinding** (recyclability, supporting sustainable design).
