$$F_d = \pi \cdot 80 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 380 \cdot 2.646 \cdot 0.9730$$
$$F_d = 288{,}845 \text{ N} \approx 288.8 \text{ kN}$$

**(iv) Feasibility Check:**
$$\sigma_{uts}/\sigma_f = 520/380 = 1.368$$
$$LDR_{\text{ideal}} = e^{1.368} = 3.93$$
$$LDR_{\text{practical}} \approx 0.7 \cdot e^{0.684} = 0.7 \cdot 1.982 = 1.387$$
**Adjusted LDR for steel with anisotropy $\approx$ 2.0–2.2.**

Since $DR = 2.646 > LDR \approx 2.0$ → **Single draw is NOT feasible**. Use **two-stage redrawing**:
- Stage 1: $D_1 = 212$ mm, $d_1 = 212/2.0 = 106$ mm, intermediate cup.
- Stage 2: $D_2 = 106$ mm, $d_2 = 106/1.6 = 66.25$ mm (redraw ratio).
- Stage 3 (trim/redraw): reach 80 mm.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Question]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of neat sketches, describe the various *defects* encountered in deep drawing. Explain how they are *prevented or controlled*.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the *effect of blank-holder force* ($F_b$) on the quality of the drawn cup. Illustrate with a graph of $F_b$ versus defect type.

**Model Solution (a) — Defects in Deep Drawing:**

| # | Defect | Description | Cause | Prevention |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wrinkling** | Buckling in flange | Insufficient $F_b$ | Increase $F_b$; use draw beads |
| 2 | **Tearing** | Wall fracture at punch corner | Excess $F_b$ or high $DR$ | Reduce $F_b$; redraw in stages |
| 3 | **Earing** | Wavy top edge with peaks | Planar anisotropy $\Delta R \neq 0$ | Use balanced blank rotation; select low-$\Delta R$ material |
| 4 | **Orange peel** | Rough surface | Coarse grain size | Use fine-grain sheet (low ASTM No.) |
| 5 | **Stretcher strains** | Lüders bands on yield | Discontinuous yielding | Pre-temper rolling (skin pass) |
| 6 | **Bottom cracking** | Fracture at punch radius | Sharp $r_p$; high $F_b$ | Use $r_p = (5\text{ to }10) \cdot t$ |
| 7 | **Die marks / scoring** | Surface scratches | Poor lubrication, die wear | Use proper lubricant; polish die |
| 8 | **Springback / distortion** | Out-of-round cup | Elastic recovery | Overbend; use restrain ring |
| 9 | **Thinning / necking** | Localized wall thinning | Excessive DR | Lower DR; iron separately |

> **Mark distribution for (a):** 7 marks = 4 marks for naming/sketches + 2 marks for causes + 1 mark for prevention strategies.

**Model Solution (b) — Effect of Blank-Holder Force:**

The blank-holder force $F_b$ has a **non-monotonic** relationship with cup quality. A typical $F_b$ vs. defect type graph is:

- **At very low $F_b$:** The flange *wrinkles*. The compressive hoop stress in the flange is unopposed, leading to buckling of the unsupported sheet.
- **At optimum $F_b$:** A wrinkle-free, tear-free cup is produced. This is the *process window* — typically narrow (5–10% of theoretical).
- **At high $F_b$:** The wall *tears* near the punch corner. Excessive $F_b$ increases the radial stress needed to pull material past the binder, which raises wall stress beyond $\sigma_{uts}$.

**Empirical rule of thumb for the optimum $F_b$:**

$$F_{b,\text{opt}} \approx (0.005 \text{ to } 0.10) \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D^2 - d^2) \cdot \sigma_y$$

- Lower coefficient (0.005–0.02) for **thick, low-strength** sheets
- Higher coefficient (0.05–0.10) for **thin, high-strength** sheets

> **Graph (qualitative):** Plot $F_b$ on x-axis. Left region: wrinkling. Central region: defect-free. Right region: tearing. The boundary is a U-shaped curve.

**Mark distribution for (b):** 7 marks = 3 marks for explanation + 2 marks for the U-shaped graph + 2 marks for the empirical formula and process window discussion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up drawing ratio and reduction:** A student who writes $DR = d/D$ instead of $D/d$ loses 1 full mark on average.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $\pi$:** The drawing force equation *must* include $\pi$ — students often write $F_d = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_f \cdot DR \cdot \ln(DR)$ and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing cup wall thickness with average cup thickness:** The bottom is *thicker* than $t$, the wall is *thinner* than $t$. Confusing these two facts leads to 0.5–1 mark deduction.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions in derivation:** KTU board explicitly requires *boundary conditions* and *assumptions* to be listed — skipping them forfeits 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Wrong LDR formula:** Some students write $LDR = e^{\sigma_f/\sigma_{uts}}$ (inverted) — this is a classic 1-mark error.
> 6. **Units mismatch:** Mixing mm and m in the same numerical calculation without conversion. Always use SI (m, Pa) in the formula, then express final answer in kN.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (10-marker weightage for KTU):**

- **Core Definition:** Deep drawing = forming a hollow, seamless shell from a flat blank by *radial drawing* across a die. *No intentional wall thinning* (in pure form).
- **Key Geometric Parameter:** $DR = D/d$ — the single most important number in the entire topic.
- **LDR (Mild Steel):** Approximately 2.0 in single draw; approximately 5.0 in two-stage (cumulative).
- **Drawing Force Formula (most-used):** $F_d = \pi \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_f \cdot (DR - 0.5)$ — Siebel's empirical.
- **Drawing Force Formula (ideal):** $F_d = \pi \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_f \cdot DR \cdot \ln(DR)$.
- **LDR Theoretical Limit:** $LDR = e^{\sigma_{uts}/\sigma_f}$ — but *practice values are 50–70% of theoretical*.
- **Blank-Holder Force:** Optimum lies in a narrow window — too low causes *wrinkling*; too high causes *tearing*.
- **Defects to Memorize:** Wrinkling, tearing, earing, orange peel, stretcher strains — at least 5 must be remembered.
- **Ironing vs. Deep Drawing:** Ironing = clearance $c < t$ (intentional thinning). Pure drawing = $c \geq t$.
- **Volume Constancy:** $V_{\text{blank}} = V_{\text{cup}}$ — leads to $t_f \approx t \cdot \sqrt{DR}$ for thin walls.
- **Drawing Efficiency:** 60–80% practical; losses are due to friction (40%) and bending (10–20%).
- **Anisotropy Matters:** Normal anisotropy $R > 1$ improves drawability; planar anisotropy $\Delta R \approx 0$ prevents earing.
- **Die Clearance:** $c = (1.05 \text{ to } 1.10) \cdot t$ for pure drawing; $c = t_f$ for ironing.
- **Punch Corner Radius:** $r_p \geq 4 \cdot t$ (for steels); larger for stainless / aluminum to prevent bottom cracking.
- **Strain-Hardening Exponent $n$:** Higher $n$ (e.g., austenitic SS, deep-drawing grade steel) $\Rightarrow$ better drawability.

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always *state assumptions before the derivation*, *use SI units in the formula*, and *convert the final answer to kN or MPa* — these three habits alone recover 2–3 marks that students routinely lose.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Keeler-Goodman Formability Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Keeler-Goodman Formability Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Keeler–Goodman Formability Diagram**, more commonly known in modern manufacturing literature as the **Forming Limit Diagram (FLD)**, is a graphical tool used in **sheet metal forming analysis** to predict the onset of **localized necking** and subsequent **fracture** during a stamping or deep-drawing operation. It was pioneered in the **1960s** by **S.P. Keeler** (left side / tension–compression zone) and **G.M. Goodwin** (right side / biaxial tension zone), building on the earlier work of Keeler and Backofen.

The diagram is a two-dimensional Cartesian plot where:
- The **ordinate (y-axis)** represents the **major principal true strain** $\varepsilon_1$ (in the direction of maximum stretch).
- The **abscissa (x-axis)** represents the **minor principal true strain** $\varepsilon_2$ (perpendicular to $\varepsilon_1$).

A characteristic **bell-shaped curve** (the FLC — Forming Limit Curve) divides the diagram into a **safe region (below the curve)** where the sheet can be deformed without failure, and a **fracture/necking region (above the curve)** where localized thinning and splitting will occur.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):** The Keeler–Goodman diagram is the *single most important graphical tool* in DFMA for evaluating the *formability* of sheet metal parts. KTU questions are frequently asked on (a) labeling the FLD, (b) identifying safe/unsafe strain states, and (c) computing the **FLD₀** (plane-strain intercept) for given materials.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are stretching a **square rubber sheet** between your fingers:
- If you pull it **equally in both directions** (biaxial tension), it is very hard to deform — it might tear quickly.
- If you pull it in **only one direction** while letting it freely shrink in the other (uniaxial tension), the deformation is easy.
- If you pull in one direction while **compressing** in the perpendicular direction (e.g., over a curved punch), deformation is even easier.

The Keeler–Goodman diagram is essentially a **"safety map"** for sheet metal under all these different stretching conditions. Each point on the diagram represents a specific combination of in-plane strains, and the curve tells the design engineer: *"If your part's strain state falls below this curve, you are SAFE; if it falls above, the metal will NECK and CRACK."*

Think of it as the **stress-strain curve's 2D cousin for sheet metal** — instead of one axis of stress, we now have two axes of in-plane strain.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Mild Steel) | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plane-strain intercept | **FLD₀** | **≈ 0.33 to 0.40** | Lowest point on FLC; most critical forming condition |
| Strain hardening exponent | **n** | **0.20 – 0.25** | Controls FLD₀ |
| Normal anisotropy | **r̄** | **1.5 – 2.0** | Affects left-side FLC behavior |
| Thickness sensitivity | **t** | **0.7 – 2.0 mm** | Thicker sheet → higher FLD |

> [!NOTE]
> The relationship **FLD₀ ≈ n** is a popular empirical thumb-rule taught in KTU examinations. However, the more accurate empirical relationship for **low-carbon steels** is **FLD₀ = n / 0.21**, as proposed by Hecker.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Strain State Fundamentals

In a sheet metal element undergoing forming, we identify two in-plane principal strains measured on the surface:
- $\varepsilon_1$ — **Major strain** (algebraically largest).
- $\varepsilon_2$ — **Minor strain** (algebraically smallest).

By **volume constancy** (incompressibility) of plastic deformation:

$$
\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 = 0
$$

Since the sheet is **thin** and surface strains are measured (out-of-plane strain $\varepsilon_3$ remains unmeasured but implicit):

$$
\varepsilon_3 = -(\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2)
$$

The **sign convention** in the FLD is:
- **Tensile strain → positive (+)**
- **Compressive strain → negative (−)**

## 2.2 Construction of the FLD Curve

The FLC has two distinct halves, derived separately:

### 2.2.1 Right Side — Keeler Curve (Biaxial Tension, $\varepsilon_2 > 0$)
Empirically developed by Keeler using strain measurements on stretched sheets:

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0 + 0.8 \, \varepsilon_2
$$

This describes the linear relationship in the **stretching/bulging region** (right of plane-strain).

### 2.2.2 Left Side — Goodwin Curve (Tension–Compression, $\varepsilon_2 < 0$)
Empirically developed by Goodwin for deep-drawing conditions:

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0 - 0.88 \, \varepsilon_2
$$

This describes the linear relationship in the **drawing region** (left of plane-strain).

> [!NOTE]
> Some KTU textbooks quote the Goodwin slope as **−0.88**, others as **−0.80**. The KTU-accepted convention is **−0.88** (per Keeler & Backofen's original 1963–1968 publications).

### 2.2.3 The Plane-Strain Point (FLD₀)
This is the **lowest, most critical point** on the FLC, occurring at $\varepsilon_2 = 0$. It corresponds to **uniaxial tension** (for negative $\varepsilon_2$) transitioning to **biaxial tension** (for positive $\varepsilon_2$). The empirical formula:

$$
\text{FLD}_0 = \frac{n}{0.21} \quad \text{(for steels, Hecker, 1975)}
$$

A simplified rule used in undergraduate texts: $\text{FLD}_0 \approx n$.

## 2.3 Influence of Material & Process Parameters on FLD₀

| Parameter | Effect on FLD₀ | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Strain hardening exponent ($n$) | **↑ n → ↑ FLD₀** (directly proportional) | High-strength steels with $n > 0.2$ are more formable |
| Normal anisotropy ($r̄$) | **↑ r̄ → ↑ FLD₀** (slight effect) | Deep-drawing quality sheets resist thinning |
| Sheet thickness ($t$) | **↑ t → ↑ FLD₀** | Thicker gauge → more formable |
| Strain rate ($\dot{\varepsilon}$) | **↑ $\dot{\varepsilon}$ → ↑ FLD₀** (mild effect) | High-speed forming slightly improves formability |
| Temperature ($T$) | **↑ T → ↓ FLD₀** (above recrystallization) | Warm forming reduces formability (counterintuitive but real) |
| Pre-strain | **Lowers FLD₀** | Pre-strained sheets have less reserve ductility |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 = 0$ | Volume constancy | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\sigma = K\varepsilon^n$ | Hollomon power law (true stress–strain) | MPa |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_1^* = n$ | Maximum uniform strain in uniaxial tension (Considère) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{FLD}_0 = \dfrac{n}{0.21}$ | Hecker's empirical relation (low-carbon steel) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0 + 0.8\,\varepsilon_2$ | **Keeler equation** (right side, $\varepsilon_2 \geq 0$) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0 - 0.88\,\varepsilon_2$ | **Goodwin equation** (left side, $\varepsilon_2 \leq 0$) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $r = \dfrac{\varepsilon_w}{\varepsilon_t}$ | Normal anisotropy (width strain / thickness strain) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\bar{r} = \dfrac{r_0 + 2r_{45} + r_{90}}{4}$ | Average normal anisotropy | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\Delta r = \dfrac{r_0 - 2r_{45} + r_{90}}{2}$ | Planar anisotropy (earing tendency) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The vertical bar character `\vert` is used in formulas above (e.g., $\vert\varepsilon_1\vert$) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Do **not** use the pipe symbol `|` inside table cells — it will corrupt the table rendering.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The FLD is a **production-floor diagnostic tool** used in the automotive, aerospace, and appliance industries. Practical applications include:

1. **Press-shop troubleshooting:** When a stamped part develops splits, engineers plot the actual measured strains (using circle-grid analysis or DIC — Digital Image Correlation) on the FLD. Points above the FLC identify problematic regions of the die design.
2. **Die tryout validation:** Before committing to a hard-tool die, FLD analysis predicts whether the geometry is feasible.
3. **Material selection:** When switching from mild steel to aluminum (e.g., for lightweight automotive bodies), the lower FLD₀ of aluminum (≈ 0.04) immediately flags formability concerns.
4. **Finite Element Analysis (FEA) post-processing:** Commercial codes like **PAM-STAMP, AutoForm, LS-DYNA, and ABAQUS** output FLD plots as standard post-processor overlays to validate forming simulations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volume Constancy in Sheet Metal

Consider a sheet metal element of initial dimensions $L_0 \times W_0 \times t_0$. After plastic deformation, dimensions become $L_f \times W_f \times t_f$.

**Step 1:** Define true (logarithmic) strains along each principal direction.

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right) \quad ; \quad \varepsilon_2 = \ln\!\left(\frac{W_f}{W_0}\right) \quad ; \quad \varepsilon_3 = \ln\!\left(\frac{t_f}{t_0}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **law of volume constancy** for plastic deformation (the volume is preserved since the elastic strain is negligible).

$$
L_0 \, W_0 \, t_0 \;=\; L_f \, W_f \, t_f
$$

**Step 3:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides.

$$
\ln(L_0) + \ln(W_0) + \ln(t_0) \;=\; \ln(L_f) + \ln(W_f) + \ln(t_f)
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to group the logarithmic ratios.

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right) + \ln\!\left(\frac{W_f}{W_0}\right) + \ln\!\left(\frac{t_f}{t_0}\right) \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the three terms as the principal true strains.

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 = 0\;}
$$

This is the **fundamental strain equation** of the FLD. For thin sheets where the in-plane strains are measured (e.g., via circle-grid analysis), the out-of-plane strain is simply the negative sum of the two in-plane strains.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Considère Criterion (Maximum Uniform Strain = n)

This derivation explains **why** the strain hardening exponent $n$ controls formability.

**Step 1:** Start with the **Hollomon power law** for true stress–strain behavior beyond yielding.

$$
\sigma = K\,\varepsilon^n
$$

**Step 2:** During uniaxial tension, the **true stress** in the specimen equals the applied load divided by the instantaneous cross-sectional area.

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply **force equilibrium** in the plastic regime (the load is carried entirely by plastic flow; elastic contributions are neglected at large strains).

$$
P = \sigma \, A = K\,\varepsilon^n \, A
$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate the load with respect to strain to find the condition for **load maximum (onset of necking)**.

$$
\frac{dP}{d\varepsilon} = K \left[ n\,\varepsilon^{n-1} A + \varepsilon^n \frac{dA}{d\varepsilon} \right]
$$

**Step 5:** Use volume constancy: $A \, L = A_0 \, L_0$. Differentiating logarithmically:

$$
\frac{dA}{A} + \frac{dL}{L} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{dA}{d\varepsilon} = -A
$$

(since $dL/L = d\varepsilon$ in true strain).

**Step 6:** Substitute back into the load derivative.

$$
\frac{dP}{d\varepsilon} = K \left[ n\,\varepsilon^{n-1} A - \varepsilon^n A \right] = K\,A\,\varepsilon^{n-1}\left(n - \varepsilon\right)
$$

**Step 7:** Set $dP/d\varepsilon = 0$ to find the **maximum-load (necking) condition**.

$$
n - \varepsilon = 0
$$

**Step 8:** Solve for the critical strain.

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon_1^* = n\;}
$$

This is the famous **Considère criterion (1885)**. It tells us that a higher $n$ value gives a higher uniform elongation before necking begins — directly linking material behavior to FLD₀.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A low-carbon steel sheet has $n = 0.25$ and $K = 530$ MPa. A square grid printed on the sheet is measured after forming, and a region of the part shows a major strain of $\varepsilon_1 = 0.45$ and a minor strain of $\varepsilon_2 = -0.20$. Determine:
1. The value of FLD₀.
2. Whether the deformation is SAFE or UNSAFE.
3. The thickness strain $\varepsilon_3$ at that point.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute FLD₀ using Hecker's formula.**

$$
\text{FLD}_0 = \frac{n}{0.21} = \frac{0.25}{0.21} = 1.190
$$

Wait — this value is **unrealistically high** for low-carbon steel (typical FLD₀ ≈ 0.33). This indicates that the simplified rule **FLD₀ ≈ n** is more appropriate here. KTU accepts both, but the **simplified rule** is the one most often used:

$$
\text{FLD}_0 \approx n = 0.25
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the relevant equation.** Since $\varepsilon_2 = -0.20 < 0$, we are on the **left (Goodwin) side** of the FLD.

$$
\varepsilon_{1,\text{crit}} = \text{FLD}_0 - 0.88\,\varepsilon_2 = 0.25 - 0.88 \times (-0.20)
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{1,\text{crit}} = 0.25 + 0.176 = 0.426
$$

**Step 3 — Compare actual vs. critical strain.**

$$
\varepsilon_{1,\text{actual}} = 0.45 \quad > \quad \varepsilon_{1,\text{crit}} = 0.426
$$

Since the actual major strain **exceeds** the FLC value, the deformation is **UNSAFE (necking/fracture expected)**.

**Step 4 — Compute the thickness strain.**

$$
\varepsilon_3 = -(\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2) = -(0.45 + (-0.20)) = -(0.25) = -0.25
$$

So the sheet has thinned by $1 - e^{-0.25} = 22.1\%$.

**Result Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value | Status |
|---|---|---|
| FLD₀ | 0.25 | — |
| $\varepsilon_2$ | −0.20 | Left side (drawing region) |
| $\varepsilon_{1,\text{crit}}$ (FLC value) | 0.426 | Threshold |
| $\varepsilon_{1,\text{actual}}$ | 0.45 | **ABOVE FLC** |
| $\varepsilon_3$ | −0.25 | 22.1% thinning |
| **Verdict** | **UNSAFE** | ❌ Part will fail in this region |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Keeler–Goodman FLD

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete functional architecture** of the FLD — its inputs (material/process parameters), construction (curve equations), regions (safe/unsafe), and outputs (formability decisions used by design engineers).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Material Parameters] --> A1[Strain Hardening Exponent n]
    A --> A2[Anisotropy r-bar]
    A --> A3[Thickness t0]
    A --> A4[Strength Coefficient K]

    B[Input Process Parameters] --> B1[Strain Path]
    B --> B2[Strain Rate]
    B --> B3[Temperature]
    B --> B4[Friction Condition]

    A1 --> C[FLD0 Computation]
    A2 --> C
    A3 --> C
    C --> C1[Hecker Formula FLD0 = n by 0.21]
    C --> C2[Approximate FLD0 approx n]

    C1 --> D[Construct FLC Curve]
    C2 --> D

    D --> E1[Right Side Keeler: epsilon1 = FLD0 + 0.8 epsilon2]
    D --> E2[Left Side Goodwin: epsilon1 = FLD0 - 0.88 epsilon2]
    D --> E3[Plane Strain Point epsilon2 = 0]

    F[Measured Strain State] --> F1[Major Strain epsilon1 from grid]
    F --> F2[Minor Strain epsilon2 from grid]

    F1 --> G[Overlay on FLD]
    F2 --> G

    G --> H1{Point lies BELOW FLC?}
    G --> H2{Point lies ABOVE FLC?}

    H1 --> I1[Safe Region - No Necking]
    H1 --> I2[Strain Reserve Available]

    H2 --> J1[Unsafe Region - Localized Necking]
    H2 --> J2[Fracture Risk]

    K[Design Decision Output] --> K1[Approve Die Geometry]
    K --> K2[Modify Die or Material]
    K --> K3[Adjust Lubrication or Blank Holder Force]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Map of FLD Regions and Critical Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FLD[Forming Limit Diagram]
        direction LR
        A1[Uniaxial Tension\n eps2 less than 0\n eps1 greater than 0] -->|Drawing| P[Plane Strain Point\n FLD0 = n\n eps2 = 0]
        P -->|Stretching| B1[Biaxial Tension\n eps2 greater than 0\n eps1 greater than 0]
        P -->|Equal Biaxial| B2[Pure Stretch\n eps1 = eps2]
    end

    style P fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style A1 fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333
    style B1 fill:#f8961e,stroke:#333
    style B2 fill:#f3722c,stroke:#333
```

**Key annotations for the schematic above:**

- The **green zone (left)** represents uniaxial/deep-drawing conditions — generally safe, large deformation possible.
- The **yellow star (center)** is the **plane-strain point** at FLD₀ — the most critical, lowest formability point.
- The **orange/red zones (right)** are biaxial tension conditions — formability drops as $\varepsilon_2$ becomes more positive.

## 4.3 Formability Decision Flowchart (Engineering Workflow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Sheet Metal Part Design]) --> M1[Identify Critical Strain Region]
    M1 --> M2[Compute FLD0 from Material n-value]
    M2 --> M3[Construct FLC using Keeler and Goodwin equations]
    M3 --> M4[Measure or Simulate epsilon1 and epsilon2]
    M4 --> Q1{Is epsilon2 greater than or equal to 0?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Apply Keeler equation]
    Q1 -- No --> R2[Apply Goodwin equation]
    R1 --> Cmp[Compare epsilon1_actual vs epsilon1_critical]
    R2 --> Cmp
    Cmp --> Q2{Actual less than or equal to Critical?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Safe([SAFE - Approve Design])
    Q2 -- No --> Unsafe([UNSAFE - Revise Design])
    Unsafe --> M1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the Forming Limit Diagram (FLD). What does the curve represent, and what is the significance of the FLD₀ point?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Forming Limit Diagram (FLD), also known as the **Keeler–Goodman Formability Diagram**, is a graphical plot of the **major principal true strain** ($\varepsilon_1$) on the y-axis versus the **minor principal true strain** ($\varepsilon_2$) on the x-axis for a sheet metal undergoing forming. **(1 mark)**

The FLC (Forming Limit Curve) divides the diagram into two regions: a **safe region below the curve** (where the sheet can be deformed without localized necking) and an **unsafe region above the curve** (where localized necking and fracture occur). **(1 mark)**

The **FLD₀** is the **plane-strain intercept** of the FLC, occurring at $\varepsilon_2 = 0$. It represents the most critical (lowest) formability condition and is empirically related to the strain hardening exponent: $\text{FLD}_0 \approx n$ or $\text{FLD}_0 = n/0.21$. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**State the Keeler and Goodwin equations used to construct the FLC. Why are there two different equations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For the **right side** of the FLD (biaxial tension, $\varepsilon_2 \geq 0$), the **Keeler equation** is: $\varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0 + 0.8\,\varepsilon_2$. **(1 mark)**

For the **left side** of the FLD (tension–compression, $\varepsilon_2 \leq 0$), the **Goodwin equation** is: $\varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0 - 0.88\,\varepsilon_2$. **(1 mark)**

Two different equations are used because the strain states on either side of the plane-strain point are physically different — the right side is governed by **stretching/bulging** (positive minor strain), while the left side is governed by **deep drawing** (negative minor strain). Each region exhibits a different slope of the FLC. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Keeler–Goodman Formability Diagram (FLD)**. Label the axes, the safe region, the necking/fracture region, the plane-strain point (FLD₀), and the uniaxial and biaxial tension regions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A low-carbon steel sheet has a strain hardening exponent $n = 0.22$ and strength coefficient $K = 510$ MPa. In a stamped automotive panel, a critical region shows $\varepsilon_1 = 0.35$ and $\varepsilon_2 = 0.15$. Determine (i) the FLD₀, (ii) whether the deformation is safe or unsafe, and (iii) the percentage thickness reduction. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Sketch (3 marks):** A clearly drawn FLD with:
- Y-axis labeled **"Major Strain $\varepsilon_1$"**.
- X-axis labeled **"Minor Strain $\varepsilon_2$"**.
- Origin O at the center.
- A bell-shaped curve passing through FLD₀ on the y-axis.
- Labels: **Safe region** (below curve), **Necking/Fracture region** (above curve).
- Labels: **Uniaxial tension** (lower-left), **Plane strain** (at FLD₀), **Biaxial tension** (upper-right).

**Explanation (4 marks):**
- The FLD is a tool to evaluate sheet metal formability (1 mark).
- Plot of $\varepsilon_1$ vs $\varepsilon_2$ — major and minor principal strains (1 mark).
- The FLC separates safe from unsafe forming zones (1 mark).
- FLD₀ is the plane-strain intercept, the most critical point (1 mark).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $n = 0.22$, $K = 510$ MPa, $\varepsilon_1 = 0.35$, $\varepsilon_2 = 0.15$.

**Step 1: Compute FLD₀ (2 marks).**
Using the simplified rule (accepted in KTU exams):

$$
\text{FLD}_0 \approx n = 0.22
$$

**Step 2: Identify the side of the FLD (1 mark).**
Since $\varepsilon_2 = 0.15 > 0$, we are on the **right side (Keeler region, biaxial tension)**.

**Step 3: Compute the critical major strain (2 marks).**

$$
\varepsilon_{1,\text{crit}} = \text{FLD}_0 + 0.8\,\varepsilon_2 = 0.22 + 0.8 \times 0.15
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{1,\text{crit}} = 0.22 + 0.12 = 0.34
$$

**Step 4: Compare and conclude (1 mark).**

$$
\varepsilon_{1,\text{actual}} = 0.35 > \varepsilon_{1,\text{crit}} = 0.34
$$

The point lies **marginally above** the FLC → **UNSAFE** (necking expected).

**Step 5: Percentage thickness reduction (1 mark).**

$$
\varepsilon_3 = -(\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2) = -(0.35 + 0.15) = -0.50
$$

Thickness reduction percentage:

$$
\%\text{reduction} = \left(1 - e^{\varepsilon_3}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - e^{-0.50}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\%\text{reduction} = (1 - 0.6065) \times 100 = 39.35\%
$$

**Final Answer:** FLD₀ = 0.22; **UNSAFE**; thickness reduction ≈ **39.35%**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the **factors affecting FLD₀** in sheet metal forming. How does the strain hardening exponent $n$ and sheet thickness influence the FLC? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An aluminum alloy sheet has $n = 0.12$ and $K = 290$ MPa. During a deep-drawing operation, a strain measurement gives $\varepsilon_1 = 0.20$ and $\varepsilon_2 = -0.10$. Determine (i) the FLD₀, (ii) the critical major strain using the appropriate equation, and (iii) whether the forming operation is safe. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Discussion of factors affecting FLD₀ (5 marks):**

1. **Strain hardening exponent $n$** — FLD₀ is **directly proportional** to $n$. Higher $n$ → higher FLD₀ → better formability. From Considère's criterion, $\varepsilon_1^* = n$ in uniaxial tension. **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Sheet thickness $t$** — Thicker sheets have **higher FLD₀** because through-thickness strain gradients are smaller and there is more material reserve to resist localized thinning. **(1 mark)**
3. **Normal anisotropy $\bar{r}$** — Higher $\bar{r}$ (deep-drawing quality) → slightly higher FLD₀, especially on the left (Goodwin) side. **(0.5 mark)**
4. **Strain rate** — Increasing strain rate generally slightly increases FLD₀. **(0.5 mark)**
5. **Strain path** — A two-stage strain path can either raise or lower the apparent FLD₀ depending on direction. **(0.5 mark)**
6. **Temperature and pre-strain** — Higher temperature (warm forming) reduces FLD₀; pre-strain also reduces it. **(1 mark)**

**Effect of $n$ and thickness on FLC (2 marks):**
- A higher $n$ shifts the **entire FLC upward** (parallel translation in the y-direction).
- Increasing thickness shifts the FLC upward, and also makes the FLC slightly **flatter** (less pronounced curvature).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $n = 0.12$, $K = 290$ MPa, $\varepsilon_1 = 0.20$, $\varepsilon_2 = -0.10$.

**Step 1: Compute FLD₀ (2 marks).**

$$
\text{FLD}_0 \approx n = 0.12
$$

**Step 2: Identify the side (1 mark).**
Since $\varepsilon_2 = -0.10 < 0$, we use the **Goodwin equation (left side)**.

**Step 3: Compute the critical major strain (2 marks).**

$$
\varepsilon_{1,\text{crit}} = \text{FLD}_0 - 0.88\,\varepsilon_2 = 0.12 - 0.88 \times (-0.10)
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{1,\text{crit}} = 0.12 + 0.088 = 0.208
$$

**Step 4: Compare and conclude (2 marks).**

$$
\varepsilon_{1,\text{actual}} = 0.20 < \varepsilon_{1,\text{crit}} = 0.208
$$

The point lies **just below** the FLC → **SAFE** (the operation is acceptable with a small safety margin).

**Final Answer:** FLD₀ = 0.12; critical $\varepsilon_1$ = 0.208; **SAFE**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in KTU Examinations:**
> 1. **Wrong equation selection** — Students frequently apply the Keeler equation when $\varepsilon_2 < 0$ or vice versa. **Always check the sign of $\varepsilon_2$ first.** (Loss: 2–3 marks)
> 2. **Using $\text{FLD}_0 = n/0.21$ instead of $\text{FLD}_0 \approx n$** — While Hecker's formula is more accurate, it gives unrealistically high FLD₀ for steel ($n=0.25$ gives 1.19, which is wrong). For KTU, the **simplified rule $\text{FLD}_0 \approx n$** is preferred. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting volume constancy** — When asked for thickness strain, students often skip the step $\varepsilon_3 = -(\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2)$. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 4. **Sign confusion in Goodwin equation** — Substituting a negative $\varepsilon_2$ into $\varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0 - 0.88\varepsilon_2$ and forgetting the double negative. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 5. **Not labeling axes on the sketch** — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for unlabeled axes on the FLD diagram. (Loss: 1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ The **Forming Limit Diagram (FLD)** plots **major strain ($\varepsilon_1$)** vs **minor strain ($\varepsilon_2$)**.
- ⭐ The FLC divides the plot into **safe region (below)** and **necking/fracture region (above)**.
- ⭐ The **lowest point** of the FLC is **FLD₀** (plane strain), which equals the strain hardening exponent $n$.
- ⭐ **Right side (biaxial tension, $\varepsilon_2 \geq 0$):** Keeler equation — $\varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0 + 0.8\,\varepsilon_2$.
- ⭐ **Left side (tension–compression, $\varepsilon_2 \leq 0$):** Goodwin equation — $\varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0 - 0.88\,\varepsilon_2$.
- ⭐ **Volume constancy:** $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 = 0$.
- ⭐ **Considère criterion:** Maximum uniform strain in uniaxial tension equals $n$.
- ⭐ Higher $n$, higher $\bar{r}$, and greater thickness → **higher FLD₀** → better formability.
- ⭐ For a point on the FLD: if $\varepsilon_{1,\text{actual}} > \varepsilon_{1,\text{crit}}$, the deformation is **UNSAFE**.
- ⭐ Aluminum typically has very low FLD₀ ($\approx 0.04$ to $0.12$) compared to mild steel ($\approx 0.25$ to $0.40$).
- ⭐ The FLD is used in **press-shop troubleshooting, die validation, material selection, and FEA post-processing**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forming Limit Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Forming Limit Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Forming Limit Diagram (FLD) — Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Forming Limit Diagram (FLD)**, also known as the **Forming Limit Curve (FLC)** plot, is a fundamental graphical tool in sheet metal forming analysis that delineates the boundary between **safe (deformation without failure)** and **unsafe (necking/fracture)** forming regimes for a given sheet metal material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The FLD is a two-dimensional plot of **major principal true strain ($\varepsilon_1$)** on the vertical axis versus **minor principal true strain ($\varepsilon_2$)** on the horizontal axis. It predicts formability of sheet metals under various strain paths encountered in real stamping operations.

The curve is constructed by experimentally deforming sheet metal specimens through multiple strain paths (uniaxial tension, plane strain, biaxial stretching, equibiaxial, and deep drawing) and recording the strain combinations at the onset of **localized necking** (the precursor to fracture).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Danger Zone Map"

Imagine you are driving a car along a winding road. Your speedometer reads one value (major strain), and the road's curvature is another (minor strain). The **Forming Limit Diagram is essentially a "danger-zone map"** of the sheet metal's "driving conditions":

- **Safe Green Zone:** Smooth driving, no problem — sheet metal deforms without rupture.
- **Yellow Caution Zone:** Approaching the edge — minor surface roughening (orange peel), localized thinning begins.
- **Red Critical Zone:** Past the FLC boundary — **localized necking and fracture**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strain Ratio ($\rho$):** The ratio of minor to major strain ($\rho = \varepsilon_2 / \varepsilon_1$) defines the **strain state**:
> - $\rho = -0.5$ → Uniaxial Tension
> - $\rho = 0$ → Plane Strain (most critical for FLD₀)
> - $\rho = 1$ → Equibiaxial Stretching
> - $-0.5 < \rho < 0$ → Stretch-Draw region
> - $0 < \rho < 1$ → Biaxial Stretching region

## 1.3 Historical Context

- **1965 — Keeler & Backofen:** Established the **right half** (stretching side) of the FLC for steels.
- **1968 — Goodwin:** Extended the FLC to the **left half** (drawing side, $\varepsilon_2 < 0$) for aluminum alloys.
- Together, this is termed the **Keeler-Goodwin Diagram**, the foundation of modern FLD analysis.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Steel) |
|---|---|---|
| Plane-strain intercept | $\text{FLD}_0$ | $0.30$ – $0.45$ |
| Strain hardening exponent | $n$ | $0.15$ – $0.25$ |
| Strength coefficient | $K$ | $400$ – $700$ MPa |
| Strain ratio | $\rho$ | $-0.5$ to $+1.0$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU:** "**FLD₀ ≈ n**" — The plane-strain intercept approximately equals the strain hardening exponent for many steels. This is a frequently-tested relationship.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Coordinate System and Strain Conventions

In sheet metal forming, principal strains are measured on the **deformed surface** of the sheet:

$$\varepsilon_1 \geq \varepsilon_2 \geq \varepsilon_3$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_1$ = **Major principal true strain** (maximum, in the direction of greatest elongation)
- $\varepsilon_2$ = **Minor principal true strain** (intermediate, in-plane perpendicular direction)
- $\varepsilon_3$ = **Thickness strain** (negative, since sheet thins during forming)

> **Volume constancy (incompressibility) for plastic deformation:**
> $$\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 = 0$$

## 2.2 Standardized Strain Paths on the FLD

The FLD is divided into **two halves** based on the sign of the minor strain:

| Region | Minor Strain | Physical Process | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Right Half (Stretching)** | $\varepsilon_2 \geq 0$ | Stretching dominates | Car roof panel, hood outer |
| **Left Half (Drawing)** | $\varepsilon_2 \leq 0$ | Drawing (compression) dominates | Cup drawing, door inner panel |
| **Plane Strain** | $\varepsilon_2 = 0$ | Most critical — gives $\text{FLD}_0$ | Cylindrical punch corner |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All critical FLD equations, conservatively presented for KTU 2024 evaluation patterns:**

| # | Formula | Description | Domain / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 = 0$ | Volume constancy (incompressibility) | All strains dimensionless |
| 2 | $\rho = \varepsilon_2 / \varepsilon_1$ | Strain ratio | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{FLD}_0 \approx n$ | Keeler's empirical rule (steels) | $n$ is dimensionless |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0 \left(1 - 0.4 \dfrac{\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_1}\right)$ | Keeler's parabolic FLC (left/drawing side) | Valid for $\varepsilon_2 \leq 0$ |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 = \text{FLD}_0$ | Goodwin's linear FLC approximation (right/stretching side) | Valid for $0 \leq \varepsilon_2 \leq \varepsilon_1$ |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_1 = \dfrac{n}{0.21}$ | Modified Keeler for aluminum | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_f = n$ | Strain at fracture (uniform elongation) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $t_f = t_0 \exp(\varepsilon_3)$ | Final thickness | mm |
| 9 | $\text{FLD}_0 = \dfrac{\ln(1 + n)}{0.226}$ | Hecker/Aluminum refinement | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $n = \dfrac{\partial \ln \sigma}{\partial \ln \varepsilon}$ | Strain hardening exponent definition | Dimensionless |

## 2.4 Key Engineering Interpretation

- **Above the FLC (red zone):** Localized necking and fracture occur — **part rejected**.
- **Below the FLC (green zone):** Safe forming region — **part acceptable**.
- **Engineers' goal:** Design stamping operations so that the **strain path of every point in the sheet lies below the FLC**, with adequate safety margin (typically 10–15% below the curve).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is plane strain ($\rho = 0$) the most critical point?**
> At plane strain, the diffuse neck elongates without spreading sideways, and the localized neck forms earliest. This is why $\text{FLD}_0$ is the single most important point on the curve — it represents the **lowest forming limit** and is the engineering benchmark.

## 2.5 Factors Influencing the FLC

The FLC shifts **upward (improved formability)** when:
1. Higher strain hardening exponent ($n$)
2. Greater sheet thickness ($t_0$)
3. Higher normal anisotropy ($r$-value, drawing side only)
4. Higher temperature (warm/hot forming)
5. Finer grain size and cleaner microstructure

The FLC shifts **downward (reduced formability)** when:
1. Higher yield strength
2. Lower $n$-value
3. Pre-existing defects, scratches
4. Strain rate sensitivity effects
5. Hydrogen embrittlement (advanced high-strength steels)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Volume Constancy from Sheet Metal Incompressibility

**Starting premise:** Plastic deformation in metals occurs at constant volume (the density does not change appreciably).

Let the initial sheet dimensions be $L_0$, $W_0$, $t_0$, and final dimensions $L_f$, $W_f$, $t_f$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Initial Volume} = L_0 \cdot W_0 \cdot t_0 \\
&\text{Final Volume} = L_f \cdot W_f \cdot t_f \\
&\text{By volume constancy:} \quad L_0 W_0 t_0 = L_f W_f t_f \\
&\text{Dividing both sides:} \quad \dfrac{L_f}{L_0} \cdot \dfrac{W_f}{W_0} \cdot \dfrac{t_f}{t_0} = 1 \\
&\text{Take natural logarithm:} \quad \ln\!\left(\dfrac{L_f}{L_0}\right) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_f}{W_0}\right) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{t_f}{t_0}\right) = 0 \\
&\text{Recognizing true strains:} \quad \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 = 0 \\
&\therefore \quad \boxed{\varepsilon_3 = -(\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Since $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$ are *measured* from circle grid analysis on the sheet surface, the thickness strain $\varepsilon_3$ is **inferred** from this equation — never measured directly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Keeler's Empirical Relation for the Drawing Side (Left Half)

**Empirical starting observation (Keeler, 1965):** For steels in the drawing region ($\varepsilon_2 \leq 0$), the FLC can be approximated by a straight line, with the curve's slope empirically fitted to $-0.4$ when plotting $\varepsilon_1$ vs. $\varepsilon_2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{General line through } (\varepsilon_2 = 0,\, \varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0) \text{ with slope } m: \\
&\varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0 + m \cdot \varepsilon_2 \\
&\text{Substituting empirical slope } m = -0.4: \\
&\boxed{\varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0 - 0.4 \cdot \varepsilon_2} \quad \text{...(Keeler's Drawing Side)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Rearrangement Insight:** Sometimes the form is expressed as a ratio to make the parabolic/elliptical nature explicit:
> $$\varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0 \left(1 - 0.4 \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_1}\right)$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Goodwin's Linear Relation for the Stretching Side (Right Half)

**Empirical starting observation (Goodwin, 1968):** For aluminum and similar alloys in the stretching region, the FLC is nearly linear with a slope of $-1$ (i.e., $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 = \text{const}$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Linear form through } (\varepsilon_2 = 0,\, \varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0) \text{ with slope } -1: \\
&\varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0 - 1 \cdot \varepsilon_2 \\
&\therefore \quad \boxed{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 = \text{FLD}_0} \quad \text{...(Goodwin's Stretching Side)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical interpretation of the slope $-1$:** This implies that **localized necking occurs when the sum of the two in-plane strains equals a critical constant** (which equals $\text{FLD}_0$). This is equivalent to saying the **thickness strain** $\varepsilon_3 = -\text{FLD}_0$ at necking for all stretching paths.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Keeler's Fundamental Relation $\text{FLD}_0 \approx n$

**Starting premise:** Marciniak-Kuczynski (M-K) localized necking theory combined with power-law hardening $\sigma = K\varepsilon^n$ gives an analytical estimate.

For a power-law material in plane strain ($\varepsilon_2 = 0$, $\varepsilon_3 = -\varepsilon_1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Consid\'er's criterion for diffuse necking:} \quad \dfrac{d\sigma}{d\varepsilon} = \sigma \\
&\text{For } \sigma = K\varepsilon^n: \quad \dfrac{d\sigma}{d\varepsilon} = nK\varepsilon^{n-1} \\
&\text{Equating:} \quad nK\varepsilon^{n-1} = K\varepsilon^n \implies n = \varepsilon_{\text{uniform}} \\
&\text{Keeler's empirical result for steels (validating M-K theory):} \quad \boxed{\text{FLD}_0 \approx n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU style):**

A steel sheet has $n = 0.22$. Calculate the forming limit in plane strain.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Given: } n = 0.22 \\
&\text{Using Keeler's relation: } \text{FLD}_0 = n \\
&\text{FLD}_0 = 0.22 \quad \text{(dimensionless, true strain)} \\
&\text{Equivalent engineering strain: } e_0 = e^{0.22} - 1 = 0.246 \approx 24.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Thickness Strain at Necking on the Stretching Side

Using the Goodwin relation $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 = \text{FLD}_0$ and volume constancy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\varepsilon_3 = -(\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2) = -\text{FLD}_0 \\
&\text{Final thickness ratio:} \quad \dfrac{t_f}{t_0} = \exp(\varepsilon_3) = \exp(-\text{FLD}_0) \\
&\text{For } \text{FLD}_0 = 0.30: \quad \dfrac{t_f}{t_0} = e^{-0.30} = 0.741 \\
&\text{Therefore, thickness reduction} = (1 - 0.741) \times 100\% = 25.9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Note:** The **maximum allowable thickness reduction** before necking is approximately **$1 - e^{-\text{FLD}_0}$**. Beyond this, failure is imminent.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Problem Matrix (Typical KTU Question Pattern)

> **Common KTU Question Type:** A point with measured strains $(\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2)$ is given. Compute FLD₀, evaluate the safety factor, and recommend the forming condition.

**Methodology (Algorithm-style — to be used as a solution template, not as code):**

| Step | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify given $(\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2)$ | Strain state established |
| 2 | Compute $\rho = \varepsilon_2 / \varepsilon_1$ | Strain path identified |
| 3 | Use material $n$-value → $\text{FLD}_0 = n$ | Limit curve established |
| 4 | Compute theoretical $\varepsilon_{1,\text{limit}}$ at given $\rho$ via Keeler or Goodwin | Limit value |
| 5 | Compare actual $\varepsilon_1$ to $\varepsilon_{1,\text{limit}}$ | Safe or unsafe |
| 6 | Compute safety margin $= (\varepsilon_{1,\text{limit}} - \varepsilon_1) / \varepsilon_{1,\text{limit}} \times 100\%$ | Quantitative margin |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: FLD Concept Architecture

This diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the FLD determination process, from raw sheet to final formability decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sheet Metal Specimen] --> B[Apply Etched Circle Grid of Diameter d0]
    B --> C[Deform via Standardized Strain Path Tests]
    C --> D[Measure Deformed Ellipse: Major d1 and Minor d2]
    D --> E[Compute True Strains epsilon1 and epsilon2]
    E --> F[Plot Strain Points on FLD Axes]
    F --> G{Does Point Fall Below FLC Boundary?}
    G -- Yes --> H[SAFE ZONE - Part Acceptable]
    G -- No --> I[UNSAFE ZONE - Necking or Fracture Imminent]
    F --> J[Construct FLC by Curve Fitting]
    J --> K[Determine FLD0 at Plane Strain Intercept]
    K --> L[Compare with Material n-value for Validation]
    L --> M[Generate Final Formability Report]
    H --> M
    I --> N[Redesign Process: Adjust Blank Holder Force or Lubrication]
    N --> A
    M --> O[Store FLD Data for Future Production Reference]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Strain Path Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sheet Metal Subjected to Forming Load] --> B[Identify Strain Ratio rho]
    B --> C{rho equals negative half?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Uniaxial Tension State]
    C -- No --> E{rho equals zero?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Plane Strain State - Most Critical]
    E -- No --> G{rho between zero and one?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Biaxial Stretching]
    G -- No --> I{rho equals one?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Equibiaxial Stretching]
    I -- No --> K{rho between negative half and zero?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Stretch-Draw Region]
    K -- No --> M[Outside Standard FLD Domain]
    F --> N[Report Plane Strain FLD0 Value]
    H --> O[Apply Keeler Right Side Formula]
    L --> P[Apply Goodwin Left Side Formula]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: FLC Construction Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Begin FLC Construction] --> B[Select Multiple Test Specimens]
    B --> C[Apply Different Widths to Vary Strain Path]
    C --> D[Perform Tensile / Bulge / Nakajima Tests]
    D --> E[Record Strain at Onset of Localized Necking]
    E --> F[Plot All Critical Strain Pairs on Graph]
    F --> G[Draw Best-Fit Curve: Keeler-Goodwin Form]
    G --> H[Identify FLD0 at epsilon2 equals 0]
    H --> I[Validate: FLD0 approx equals n]
    I --> J[Publish FLC for Production Use]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each box represents a *functional step* in the FLD analysis workflow, not a physical object. The arrows indicate information/data flow direction, mirroring how an industrial sheet-metal formability engineer would proceed from raw material to final formability verdict.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style]**

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
**Define Forming Limit Diagram (FLD). What is the significance of the FLD₀ value?**

**Model Answer:**

The Forming Limit Diagram (FLD) is a graphical representation in sheet metal forming analysis that plots the **major principal true strain ($\varepsilon_1$)** on the vertical axis against the **minor principal true strain ($\varepsilon_2$)** on the horizontal axis. It represents the boundary between safe forming and failure (necking/fracture) for a given sheet material under various strain paths.

**Significance of FLD₀:**
1. FLD₀ is the **plane-strain intercept** of the FLC, representing the most critical (lowest) formability point.
2. It corresponds to the condition $\varepsilon_2 = 0$, which is encountered in many real stamping operations (e.g., cylindrical punch corner).
3. For steels, FLD₀ is empirically related to the strain hardening exponent: $\text{FLD}_0 \approx n$.
4. It serves as a **benchmark material property** for sheet metal selection and process design. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for significance)*

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
**Explain the difference between the Keeler side and the Goodwin side of the FLC.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Keeler Side (Right, $\varepsilon_2 \geq 0$) | Goodwin Side (Left, $\varepsilon_2 \leq 0$) |
|---|---|---|
| Strain region | Stretching region | Drawing region |
| Slope of FLC | Approximately parabolic | Approximately linear |
| Governing relation | $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 \approx \text{FLD}_0$ (approx. linear) | $\varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0 - 0.4\varepsilon_2$ |
| Material focus | Aluminum alloys (also applicable) | Steels |
| Physical process | Biaxial stretching | Deep drawing |

The two sides together constitute the **Keeler-Goodwin diagram**, providing a complete strain-path map of formability. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, 14-Mark Module Choice]**

---

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

**a) Derive the Keeler empirical relation for the forming limit curve on the stretching side. Discuss the engineering significance of FLD₀ ≈ n. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Establish the experimental basis:**
Keeler (1965) conducted experiments on sheet steel specimens of varying widths, subjected to uniaxial and biaxial tensile tests. By varying specimen geometry, the strain ratio $\rho = \varepsilon_2/\varepsilon_1$ was controlled from $-0.5$ (uniaxial) to $+1$ (equibiaxial).

**Step 2 — Empirical observations:**
- At plane strain ($\rho = 0$), the limiting major strain is $\text{FLD}_0$.
- As minor strain increases positively (biaxial stretching), the curve rises.

**Step 3 — Curve fitting:**
Empirically, Keeler found that for the stretching side:

$$
\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 = \text{FLD}_0
$$

This is a **straight line with slope $-1$** passing through $(\varepsilon_2 = 0,\, \varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0)$.

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:**
The relation $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 = \text{FLD}_0$ combined with volume constancy gives:
$$\varepsilon_3 = -(\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2) = -\text{FLD}_0 = \text{const.}$$

This implies that **localized necking initiates when the thickness strain reaches a critical constant**, independent of the strain path on the stretching side. This is a profound and practically useful result.

**Step 5 — Engineering significance of $\text{FLD}_0 \approx n$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Consid\`er's diffuse necking criterion:} \quad \dfrac{d\sigma}{d\varepsilon} = \sigma \\
&\text{For power-law hardening } \sigma = K\varepsilon^n: \\
&nK\varepsilon^{n-1} = K\varepsilon^n \implies \varepsilon_{\text{uniform}} = n
\end{aligned}
$$

- For steels, experimental data correlates $\text{FLD}_0$ with the strain hardening exponent $n$.
- This allows **predicting formability from a simple uniaxial tensile test**.
- A high-$n$ material (e.g., $n = 0.25$) will have $\text{FLD}_0 = 0.25$ → better formability.
- A low-$n$ material (e.g., $n = 0.08$, high-strength steel) will have $\text{FLD}_0 = 0.08$ → poor formability.

**Step 6 — Practical utility:**
- Material selection: Pick higher $n$-value for complex stampings.
- Process design: Use $\text{FLD}_0$ to compute **maximum allowable thickness reduction** $= 1 - e^{-\text{FLD}_0}$.

*(7 marks: 2 for derivation + 3 for FLD₀ ≈ n significance + 2 for engineering application)*

---

**b) A steel sheet has $n = 0.24$ and initial thickness $t_0 = 1.2$ mm. In a stamping operation, a critical point on the sheet experiences major strain $\varepsilon_1 = 0.18$ and minor strain $\varepsilon_2 = 0.05$. Determine:**
- **(i) The plane-strain forming limit (FLD₀).**
- **(ii) The maximum allowable major strain at the given strain ratio using Keeler's formula.**
- **(iii) Whether the point is safe, marginal, or unsafe. Calculate the safety margin in percent.**
- **(iv) The final thickness at this point.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — FLD₀:**
$$
\text{FLD}_0 \approx n = 0.24
$$

**Part (ii) — Maximum allowable major strain using Keeler's right-side formula:**

The right side of the FLC (stretching, $\varepsilon_2 \geq 0$) is governed by:
$$
\varepsilon_{1,\text{limit}} + \varepsilon_2 = \text{FLD}_0
$$
$$
\varepsilon_{1,\text{limit}} = \text{FLD}_0 - \varepsilon_2 = 0.24 - 0.05 = 0.19
$$

**Part (iii) — Safety evaluation:**

Compare actual $\varepsilon_1 = 0.18$ with limit $\varepsilon_{1,\text{limit}} = 0.19$.

$$
\varepsilon_1 = 0.18 < \varepsilon_{1,\text{limit}} = 0.19
$$

The point lies **just below the FLC** — it is **SAFE** (but with minimal margin).

$$
\text{Safety Margin} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{1,\text{limit}} - \varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_{1,\text{limit}}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.19 - 0.18}{0.19} \times 100\% = 5.26\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Although the point is technically "safe," a **5.26% safety margin is dangerously low** (recommended minimum is 10–15%). In a production environment, this would be flagged as **marginal** and the process should be redesigned. Students should **always comment on adequacy** of the safety margin, not merely state safe/unsafe.

**Part (iv) — Final thickness:**

Using volume constancy:
$$
\varepsilon_3 = -(\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2) = -(0.18 + 0.05) = -0.23
$$

$$
t_f = t_0 \cdot \exp(\varepsilon_3) = 1.2 \cdot \exp(-0.23) = 1.2 \cdot 0.7945 = 0.9534 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final thickness $t_f \approx 0.953$ mm** (approximately 20.5% reduction from 1.2 mm).

*(7 marks: 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 distribution)*

---

### **Question B — Option 2 / Alternative Choice (14 Marks)**

**a) Explain the experimental method to determine the Forming Limit Curve using circle grid analysis. List at least four standard strain path tests used. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept of Circle Grid Analysis:**
In circle grid analysis, a regular array of circles (typically of diameter $d_0 = 2.5$ mm or $5$ mm) is **electrochemically etched** or printed on the sheet metal surface before deformation. Upon deformation, the circles become ellipses, from which principal strains are computed.

**Step 2 — Strain calculation from deformed ellipses:**

Let $d_0$ = original circle diameter, and $d_1, d_2$ = major and minor axes of the deformed ellipse.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\varepsilon_1 = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{d_1}{d_0}\right) \\
&\varepsilon_2 = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{d_2}{d_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Standardized strain path tests (minimum four required):**

1. **Uniaxial Tensile Test:** Standard tensile specimen, gives $\rho = -0.5$ (left extreme of FLC).
2. **Plane Strain Tensile Test:** Wide specimen (typically width-to-gage-length ratio of 8:1 or greater) ensures $\rho \approx 0$ — produces the $\text{FLD}_0$ data point.
3. **Nakajima Test (Hemispherical Punch):** Circular blank deformed over a hemispherical punch; varying blank size produces different strain ratios from $\rho = 0$ to $\rho = 1$.
4. **Marciniak Test (Flat-Bottom Cylindrical Punch):** Cylindrical or flat-bottom punch with intermediate blank sizes to cover the $\rho$ range.
5. **Hydraulic Bulge Test:** Sheet clamped and pressurized by fluid on one side; produces equibiaxial strain state ($\rho = 1$).
6. **Swift Cup Drawing Test:** Deep drawing operation; produces left-side FLC data (negative $\varepsilon_2$).

**Step 4 — FLC construction procedure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{For each test: identify critical region (onset of necking)} \\
&\text{Measure } \varepsilon_1 \text{ and } \varepsilon_2 \text{ at the critical region (just before necking)} \\
&\text{Plot all critical } (\varepsilon_2,\, \varepsilon_1) \text{ points on the FLD axes} \\
&\text{Draw the FLC as the best-fit curve separating safe and failed points} \\
&\text{Identify } \text{FLD}_0 \text{ at } \varepsilon_2 = 0 \text{ (plane-strain intercept)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering significance:**
The resulting FLC is material-specific, thickness-specific, and temperature-specific. It is used to:
- Validate stamping simulations (FEA).
- Diagnose field failures (cracked stampings).
- Compare candidate materials for new product designs.

*(7 marks: 1 for circle grid concept + 2 for strain calculation + 2 for test methods + 2 for FLC construction)*

---

**b) A deep-drawing operation produces a strain state with major strain $\varepsilon_1 = 0.30$ and minor strain $\varepsilon_2 = -0.15$ at a critical region. The sheet has $n = 0.20$ and initial thickness $t_0 = 0.9$ mm. Using Keeler's drawing-side formula, evaluate safety and compute the final thickness. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Establish the FLD₀:**
$$
\text{FLD}_0 = n = 0.20
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the strain path:**
$\varepsilon_2 = -0.15 < 0$ → This is the **drawing side** (left half) of the FLC.

Strain ratio:
$$
\rho = \dfrac{\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_1} = \dfrac{-0.15}{0.30} = -0.5
$$

This is **uniaxial tension** ($\rho = -0.5$ is the left extreme of the FLC).

**Step 3 — Apply Keeler's drawing-side formula:**

$$
\varepsilon_{1,\text{limit}} = \text{FLD}_0 - 0.4 \cdot \varepsilon_2
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{1,\text{limit}} = 0.20 - 0.4 \cdot (-0.15) = 0.20 + 0.06 = 0.26
$$

**Step 4 — Safety evaluation:**

$$
\varepsilon_1 = 0.30 > \varepsilon_{1,\text{limit}} = 0.26
$$

**The point is UNSAFE** — it lies **above the FLC**, necking/fracture will occur.

$$
\text{Safety Margin} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{1,\text{limit}} - \varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_{1,\text{limit}}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.26 - 0.30}{0.26} \times 100\% = -15.38\%
$$

Negative safety margin confirms **failure expected**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often forget to check **which side of the FLC** ($\varepsilon_2 \geq 0$ or $\varepsilon_2 \leq 0$) the strain point lies on, and apply the wrong Keeler/Goodwin formula. **Always verify sign of $\varepsilon_2$ first.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** Some students compute safety margin as a positive number even when the part is unsafe. The correct convention is **negative margin = unsafe**, **positive margin = safe**.

**Step 5 — Final thickness:**

$$
\varepsilon_3 = -(\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2) = -(0.30 + (-0.15)) = -0.15
$$

$$
t_f = t_0 \cdot \exp(\varepsilon_3) = 0.9 \cdot \exp(-0.15) = 0.9 \cdot 0.8607 = 0.7746 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final thickness $t_f \approx 0.775$ mm** (approximately 13.9% reduction from 0.9 mm).

**Step 6 — Engineering recommendation:**

The deep-drawing process parameters (blank holder force, draw ratio, lubrication) must be modified to bring the strain path below the FLC. Options include:
- Reducing major strain $\varepsilon_1$ (smaller draw ratio).
- Increasing minor strain $\varepsilon_2$ toward zero (more stretching, less drawing).
- Using a material with higher $n$-value.
- Applying localized heating or improved lubrication.

*(7 marks: 1 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 distribution)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Forming Limit Diagram**

- **Definition:** FLD is a 2D plot of major strain $\varepsilon_1$ (Y-axis) vs. minor strain $\varepsilon_2$ (X-axis) showing formability limits.
- **FLD₀ = plane-strain intercept** ($\varepsilon_2 = 0$); the most critical point on the curve.
- **Keeler's rule:** $\text{FLD}_0 \approx n$ (strain hardening exponent) for steels.
- **Keeler's drawing-side (left, $\varepsilon_2 \leq 0$):** $\varepsilon_1 = \text{FLD}_0 - 0.4\varepsilon_2$.
- **Goodwin's stretching-side (right, $\varepsilon_2 \geq 0$):** $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 = \text{FLD}_0$.
- **Volume constancy:** $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 = 0 \implies \varepsilon_3 = -(\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2)$.
- **Final thickness:** $t_f = t_0 \cdot e^{\varepsilon_3}$.
- **Strain ratio:** $\rho = \varepsilon_2 / \varepsilon_1$; ranges from $-0.5$ (uniaxial) to $+1$ (equibiaxial).
- **Experimental method:** Circle grid analysis with Nakajima, Marciniak, bulge, and tensile tests.
- **Safety criterion:** Actual $\varepsilon_1$ must be **below** the FLC at the operating $\varepsilon_2$.
- **Safety margin:** $(\varepsilon_{1,\text{limit}} - \varepsilon_1)/\varepsilon_{1,\text{limit}} \times 100\%$; recommended **≥10–15%** for production.
- **Formability improves** with higher $n$, higher thickness $t_0$, higher $r$-value, finer grains.
- **Plane strain ($\rho = 0$)** is the most critical strain path because localized neck forms earliest.
- **Engineering goal:** Design stamping processes so that the entire sheet's strain map lies below the FLC with adequate safety margin.
- **FLC is material-, thickness-, and temperature-specific** — must be re-determined for each application.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Component Design for Blanking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Component Design for Blanking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Blanking

**Blanking** is a precision sheet-metal **shearing (cutting) operation** in which a punch and a die are used to separate a desired contour (the *blank*) from a larger sheet, strip, or coil of material. The piece that falls through the die opening is the finished product (the *blank*), while the remaining sheet with the holes is called the *strip* (or *scrap web*). Blanking is a *non-chip forming* cold-working process governed primarily by plastic shear failure along the cutting line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3)**
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, *Component Design for Blanking* falls under the DFM process-selection pillar. Students are expected to compute (i) the cutting force, (ii) the optimal clearance, (iii) the strip layout, and (iv) the press tonnage — all of which are high-yield for Part A & Part B questions.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU-Mandated Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Punch** | The upper tool that pushes the metal into the die |
| **Die** | The lower tool containing the cutting opening |
| **Blank** | The detached part that falls through (finished product) |
| **Slug** | The piece punched out in a piercing operation (counterpart of blanking) |
| **Strip** | The sheet/coil stock fed into the die |
| **Clearance (c)** | The radial gap between punch and die edges |
| **Shear (Cut) Angle (α)** | Inclination on the punch face to reduce cutting force |
| **Burnish Zone** | Polished smooth area on the cut edge |
| **Fracture Zone** | Rough, torn region on the cut edge |
| **Burr** | Unwanted projection on the cut edge |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cookie Cutter" Model

Imagine cutting cookies from a rolled-out dough sheet using a fluted cutter:
- The **cutter** is the *punch*; the **flat table opening** is the *die*.
- Press down vertically, and the shaped cookie (*blank*) drops through.
- The remaining dough sheet (with the hole) is the *strip* with its *scrap web*.

In blanking, the "dough" is a ductile metal sheet, the "pressure" is the press tonnage (measured in **kN** or **tonnes**), and the "flute" is governed by **clearance** — too tight and the cutter jams, too loose and the cookie edge looks ragged (analogous to **excess burr**). The force needed scales with the perimeter you are cutting, the thickness, and the metal's resistance to tearing.

## 1.4 Why Component Design for Blanking Matters in DFM

> [!NOTE]
> **DFM Philosophy:** *Design the part so that the manufacturing process is robust, economical, and repeatable.*
> 
> A well-designed blank:
> - Minimises material waste (↑ **material utilisation %**)
> - Reduces press tonnage (↓ energy + ↓ machine cost)
> - Produces burr-free, high-quality edges (↓ secondary operations)
> - Allows multiple-up layouts (↑ productivity)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Mechanics of the Blanking Process

When the punch descends onto the strip, the metal undergoes four sequential stages:

1. **Elastic Deformation Stage** — The strip elastically deflects. No permanent deformation.
2. **Plastic Deformation Stage** — Material flows plastically around the punch edge. The cut surface begins to *burnish* (mirror-like polish).
3. **Fracture / Shear Initiation** — Cracks nucleate at the punch and die edges and propagate inward.
4. **Final Separation** — The cracks meet; the blank is sheared off. A small *burr* remains on the bottom.

> [!TIP]
> The ratio of the *burnish zone* to *fracture zone* is a direct visual indicator of clearance correctness. Ideal blanking: ~30% burnish, ~70% fracture, **zero rollover** (burr).

## 2.2 Clearance — The Most-Critical DFM Parameter

**Clearance (c)** is the *one-sided* radial gap between the punch and die edges. It is the single most important design variable in blanking.

### 2.2.1 Empirical Rule (KTU Standard)

$$c \;=\; k_{\text{clr}} \cdot t \cdot \text{(factor per side)}$$

The recommended **per-side clearance as a percentage of stock thickness (t)** for common materials:

| Material | Clearance per side (% of t) |
|---|---|
| Mild Steel (≤ 0.1 %C) | 5 % – 7 % |
| Stainless Steel | 4 % – 6 % |
| Aluminium & Alloys | 5 % – 8 % |
| Copper & Brass | 4 % – 5 % |
| High-Carbon Steel | 7 % – 9 % |
| Phosphor Bronze | 3 % – 5 % |

### 2.2.2 Optimal Clearance (Fracture Mechanics Derivation)

For sharp tools and ductile material, the **optimum clearance per side** that yields a clean, vertical cut is given by:

$$c_{\text{opt}} \;\approx\; 0.10 \cdot t \quad \text{(general steels, sharp tools)}$$

The Shear formula most commonly adopted in KTU textbooks:

$$c \;=\; 0.10\,t \;\;(\text{approx. } 10\% \text{ of thickness per side, total diametral clearance } = 0.20\,t)$$

## 2.3 Cutting (Shear) Force — The Master Equation

The **maximum blanking force** is calculated as the perimeter being sheared multiplied by the sheet thickness multiplied by the **shear strength** of the material. The empirical form taught in KTU is:

$$F_{\text{blank}} \;=\; L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{u} \cdot k_{\text{sf}}$$

where:
- $F_{\text{blank}}$ = blanking / cutting force (**N**)
- $L$ = total cut perimeter (**mm**)
- $t$ = stock thickness (**mm**)
- $\sigma_{u}$ = ultimate tensile strength of material (**MPa** = **N/mm²**)
- $k_{\text{sf}}$ = shear correction factor (accounts for cut angle, edge radius, lubrication)
  - Sharp tool, full shear: $k_{\text{sf}} \approx 0.5$ to $0.7$ (since $\tau \approx 0.5\,\sigma_{u}$ for ductile metals)
  - KTU board convention: **$k_{\text{sf}} = 0.7$** (empirical, conservative)

### 2.3.1 Force-Reduction via Shear Angle

If the punch face is ground with a shear (cut) angle $\alpha$ (typically **1°–3°**), the peak force is reduced because the cutting progresses gradually rather than simultaneously around the perimeter.

$$F_{\text{blank,reduced}} \;=\; \dfrac{L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{u} \cdot k_{\text{sf}}}{1 + \dfrac{\mu \cot \alpha}{1.155}}$$

A simpler working formula used in KTU numericals:

$$F_{\text{blank}} \;=\; 0.7 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{u} \quad \text{(without shear angle)}$$

$$F_{\text{blank}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.7 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{u}}{1 + 0.25\,\alpha^{\circ}} \quad \text{(with shear angle α in degrees)}$$

## 2.4 Stripping Force

The **stripping force** is required to push the strip off the punch after the cut (prevent the strip from following the punch on the upstroke).

$$F_{\text{strip}} \;=\; k_{\text{strip}} \cdot L \cdot t$$

where $k_{\text{strip}} \approx 3\%\text{–}5\%$ of $\sigma_{u}$, typically taken as:

$$F_{\text{strip}} \;=\; 0.05 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{u} \quad \text{(KTU convention)}$$

## 2.5 Total Press Tonnage (Capacity Required)

$$\boxed{\;F_{\text{press}} \;=\; F_{\text{blank}} \;+\; F_{\text{strip}} \;+\; F_{\text{draw,if any}}\;}$$

For a pure blanking operation:

$$F_{\text{press}} \;\approx\; 1.30 \cdot F_{\text{blank}}$$

(i.e., a **30% safety margin** is added on top of $F_{\text{blank}}$ to account for stripper, friction, and material variability.)

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clearance per side | $c = 0.10\,t$ (general) | **mm** | Adjust per material table |
| 2 | Total diametral clearance | $C_{\text{total}} = 2c$ | **mm** | Used to size die opening |
| 3 | Blanking force | $F_{b} = 0.7\,L\,t\,\sigma_{u}$ | **N** | Empirical (KTU) |
| 4 | Blanking force with cut angle | $F_{b} = \dfrac{0.7\,L\,t\,\sigma_{u}}{1+0.25\alpha}$ | **N** | $\alpha$ in degrees |
| 5 | Stripping force | $F_{s} = 0.05\,L\,t\,\sigma_{u}$ | **N** | ~5% of blanking force |
| 6 | Press capacity | $F_{p} = 1.3\,F_{b}$ | **N** | Include 30% safety |
| 7 | Press tonnage (kN) | $T = F_{p} / 1000$ | **kN** | Or convert to tonnes |
| 8 | Shear angle max | $\alpha_{\max} \le 12^{\circ}$ (t $\le$ 1.5 mm) | — | Larger α distorts edge |
| 9 | Strip pitch (rectangular) | $p = D + s$ | **mm** | D = blank dimension, s = web |
| 10 | Strip pitch (circular) | $p = D + s$ | **mm** | $s = 1.5\,t$ typical |
| 11 | Strip width (single row) | $W = D + 2c + 2g$ | **mm** | $g$ = guide margin (1.5 t) |
| 12 | Material utilisation | $\eta = \dfrac{A_{\text{blank}}}{A_{\text{strip per part}}}$ | **%** | Target: $\eta > 70\%$ |
| 13 | Parts per strip length | $n = \dfrac{L_{\text{strip}}}{p}$ | — | Integer number |
| 14 | Centre of pressure (offset) | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum x_{i}\,F_{i}}{\sum F_{i}}$ | **mm** | From die centre |

## 2.7 Strip Layout (Nesting) — The DFM Optimisation Step

The **strip layout** determines how blanks are arranged on the strip to maximise material utilisation. Three canonical arrangements:

- **Single-row, straight-cut** — Simplest; one blank per pitch.
- **Single-row, staggered (zig-zag)** — Better utilisation for rectangular parts.
- **Multi-row / interleaved** — Two or more rows offset; maximum utilisation for asymmetric parts.

**Material utilisation** $\eta$ is the key DFM metric:

$$\eta \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Area of blank} \times \text{number of blanks}}{\text{Area of strip consumed}}$$

DFM target: $\eta \ge 70\%$. Below 50% indicates a poor layout → consider a different blank shape, alternative process (e.g., fineblanking, photochemical etching), or part redesign.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive body panels** (doors, fenders) — Trimming and piercing at line speed > 30 strokes/min
- **Electrical laminations** (motor cores) — Progressive dies blank silicon steel at micron-level tolerances
- **Washers, gears, clips** — High-volume blanking with multi-cavity dies, > 1000 parts/min
- **Battery cans, watch cases** — Deep-draw preceded by a blanking cup
- **DFM consequence:** Every 1% increase in $\eta$ on a 100,000-piece automotive run can save **several tonnes of steel** annually.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Blanking Force Equation

### 3.1.1 Starting Principle

When a punch of perimeter $L$ shears a sheet of thickness $t$, the **instantaneous shear area** is $A_{\text{shear}} = L \cdot t$. The stress resisting this shear is the **shear strength** $\tau$ of the material.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

By definition of shear stress:

$$\tau \;=\; \dfrac{F_{\text{blank}}}{A_{\text{shear}}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad F_{\text{blank}} \;=\; \tau \cdot A_{\text{shear}}$$

Substituting the shear area:

$$F_{\text{blank}} \;=\; \tau \cdot L \cdot t$$

For ductile sheet metals, empirical evidence (and Tresca's criterion) gives:

$$\tau \;\approx\; 0.57 \cdot \sigma_{u}$$

However, KTU board practice (which absorbs tool-wear, edge-radius, and lubrication effects) adopts a **conservative factor** $k_{\text{sf}} = 0.7$:

$$\tau_{\text{design}} \;=\; 0.7 \cdot \sigma_{u}$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{\;F_{\text{blank}} \;=\; 0.7 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{u}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Textbook vs. Board Convention:** While the theoretical factor is ~0.57, the KTU 2024 marking scheme expects **$k_{\text{sf}} = 0.7$** unless otherwise specified. Always re-read the question.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Circular Disc Blanking

> A circular disc of **diameter D = 50 mm** is to be blanked from a **mild-steel strip of thickness t = 3 mm** having $\sigma_{u} = 410$ MPa. Determine the blanking force, stripping force, and press tonnage required.

**Given:**
- $D = 50$ mm
- $t = 3$ mm
- $\sigma_{u} = 410$ N/mm²
- $L = \pi D = \pi \times 50 = 157.08$ mm

**Step 1 — Blanking Force:**

$$F_{b} \;=\; 0.7 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{u}$$

Substitute:

$$F_{b} \;=\; 0.7 \times 157.08 \times 3 \times 410$$

$$F_{b} \;=\; 0.7 \times 157.08 \times 1230$$

$$F_{b} \;=\; 0.7 \times 193208.4$$

$$F_{b} \;=\; 135245.88 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 135.25 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 2 — Stripping Force:**

$$F_{s} \;=\; 0.05 \cdot L \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{u}$$

$$F_{s} \;=\; 0.05 \times 157.08 \times 3 \times 410$$

$$F_{s} \;=\; 0.05 \times 193208.4$$

$$F_{s} \;=\; 9660.42 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 9.66 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Press Tonnage (with 30% safety):**

$$F_{p} \;=\; 1.30 \times F_{b} \;=\; 1.30 \times 135.25$$

$$F_{p} \;=\; 175.82 \;\text{kN}$$

Converting to tonnes (1 tonne ≈ 9.81 kN):

$$T \;=\; \dfrac{175.82}{9.81} \;\approx\; 17.92 \;\text{tonnes}$$

**Conclusion:** A press of at least **18 tonnes** capacity is required (round up to the next standard size, typically 20 tonnes).

## 3.2 Derivation of Strip Width and Pitch

### 3.2.1 Strip Pitch (p)

The **pitch** is the centre-to-centre distance between two consecutive blanks along the strip feed direction.

For a **circular blank of diameter D**, the pitch must clear:
- The blank diameter $D$
- A web (residual strip material) $s$ on either side

$$p \;=\; D \;+\; s$$

The web $s$ is empirically tied to the thickness to prevent distortion:

$$s \;=\; 1.5\,t \quad \text{(for circular blanks, KTU standard)}$$

### 3.2.2 Strip Width (W)

The strip width for a **single-row circular blank** layout:

$$W \;=\; D + 2c + 2g$$

where:
- $2c$ = total diametral clearance (one on each side of the blank perimeter)
- $g$ = lateral guide margin, $g \approx 1.5\,t$

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Strip Layout

> Using Example 1 data (D = 50 mm, t = 3 mm, $\sigma_{u} = 410$ MPa), determine: (i) optimum clearance, (ii) strip pitch, (iii) strip width, (iv) material utilisation, (v) number of parts per 2 m strip.

**Step 1 — Optimum Clearance per side:**

$$c \;=\; 0.10 \cdot t \;=\; 0.10 \times 3 \;=\; 0.30 \;\text{mm per side}$$

**Step 2 — Strip Pitch:**

$$s \;=\; 1.5\,t \;=\; 1.5 \times 3 \;=\; 4.5 \;\text{mm}$$

$$p \;=\; D + s \;=\; 50 + 4.5 \;=\; 54.5 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Strip Width:**

$$W \;=\; D + 2c + 2g$$

$$g \;=\; 1.5\,t \;=\; 4.5 \;\text{mm}$$

$$W \;=\; 50 + (2 \times 0.30) + (2 \times 4.5)$$

$$W \;=\; 50 + 0.6 + 9.0$$

$$W \;=\; 59.6 \;\text{mm} \;\approx\; 60 \;\text{mm (round up to standard size)}$$

**Step 4 — Material Utilisation $\eta$:**

Blank area:

$$A_{\text{blank}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,D^{2} \;=\; 0.7854 \times 50^{2} \;=\; 1963.5 \;\text{mm}^{2}$$

Strip area per part:

$$A_{\text{strip/part}} \;=\; p \times W \;=\; 54.5 \times 60 \;=\; 3270 \;\text{mm}^{2}$$

Utilisation:

$$\eta \;=\; \dfrac{A_{\text{blank}}}{A_{\text{strip/part}}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta \;=\; \dfrac{1963.5}{3270} \times 100\% \;\approx\; 60.04\%$$

**Step 5 — Number of Parts per 2 m Strip:**

$$n \;=\; \dfrac{L_{\text{strip}}}{p} \;=\; \dfrac{2000}{54.5} \;\approx\; 36.7 \;\Rightarrow\; 36 \text{ parts (floor)}$$

**Conclusion:** Utilisation is **60%** — acceptable but could be improved with a multi-row interleaved layout (potential $\eta \to 78\%$ for circular blanks using hexagonal nesting).

## 3.3 Centre of Pressure (Forces Off-Centre)

In multi-cavity dies, the **resultant cutting force** may not coincide with the die centre. This creates a tilting moment on the press slide, which must stay within the manufacturer's **permissible off-centre load** chart.

### 3.3.1 Derivation

For $n$ cuts at positions $(x_{i}, y_{i})$ with individual forces $F_{i}$:

$$\bar{X} \;=\; \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{i}\,F_{i}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{i}}, \quad \bar{Y} \;=\; \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_{i}\,F_{i}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{i}}$$

If the centre of pressure lies outside the **press manufacturer's permissible zone** (typically a circle of diameter ≈ 0.2 × bolster area), the press slide will tilt, causing uneven blanking and rapid die wear.

> [!TIP]
