## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the DFA Index (Boothroyd Form)

The DFA Index is built on the following logic:

1. An *ideal* part takes exactly 1.5 s to **handle** and 1.5 s to **insert**, totalling 3.0 s.
2. If a product has $N_{m}$ such ideal parts, the *minimum possible* assembly time is:

$$
t_{m,\,min} = 3 \cdot N_{m}
$$

3. The *actual* assembly time of the current design, $t_{m}$, is greater than or equal to this minimum:

$$
t_{m} \ge t_{m,\,min}
$$

4. The **DFA Index** is defined as the ratio of the minimum possible time to the actual time:

$$
\eta_{DFA} = \dfrac{t_{m,\,min}}{t_{m}} = \dfrac{3 \cdot N_{m}}{t_{m}}
$$

5. Expressed as a percentage:

$$
\eta_{DFA}^{\%} = \dfrac{3 \cdot N_{m}}{t_{m}} \times 100 \,\%
$$

> A value of **100 %** means the design has reached the theoretical minimum (each part handled and inserted in exactly 1.5 s, no extra operations, no unnecessary parts). Industrial reality lies between 30 % and 75 %.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Design Efficiency Ratio

An alternative metric focuses purely on **part count**, ignoring time:

$$
E_{d} = \dfrac{N_{m}}{N}
$$

- If $N = N_{m}$, then $E_{d} = 1$ (every part is necessary).
- If $N > N_{m}$, then $E_{d} < 1$ (some parts are redundant).

> [!NOTE]
> The design efficiency ratio $E_{d}$ does **not** consider assembly operations; it only captures whether the *number* of parts is justified. The DFA Index $\eta_{DFA}$ is the more comprehensive metric and is preferred in KTU 2024 evaluations.

### 3.3 Derivation of Manual Assembly Cost

If the labour cost rate is $C_{m}$ in **₹ per second**, and the manual assembly time is $t_{m}$ in **seconds**, then the **manual assembly labour cost** is:

$$
C_{A} = C_{m} \cdot t_{m}
$$

If multiple workstations are involved with equipment depreciation $C_{E}$ per unit, the **total assembly cost** becomes:

$$
C_{T} = C_{A} + C_{E}
$$

This cost is what the design is being benchmarked against. Each eliminated part reduces both $C_{A}$ (less time) and $C_{E}$ (fewer jigs, fixtures, or stations).

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard Style)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU Part B opening):**
> A product is currently designed with **15 parts**. The Theoretical Minimum Number of Parts $N_{m}$ is **6**. The total manual assembly time is **120 seconds**. The labour rate is ₹0.50 per second.
> Compute: (a) DFA Index, (b) Design efficiency ratio, (c) Manual assembly cost, (d) Determine if redesign is required using a 60 % threshold.

#### Part (a) — DFA Index

$$
\eta_{DFA} = \dfrac{N_{m} \cdot 3}{t_{m}} = \dfrac{6 \cdot 3}{120} = \dfrac{18}{120} = 0.15
$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$
\eta_{DFA}^{\%} = 0.15 \times 100 = 15 \,\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The current design is only **15 %** as efficient as the theoretical ideal. A large amount of unnecessary assembly effort is present.

#### Part (b) — Design Efficiency Ratio

$$
E_{d} = \dfrac{N_{m}}{N} = \dfrac{6}{15} = 0.40 = 40 \,\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Only 40 % of the parts in the current design are strictly necessary; 60 % of the parts are candidates for elimination or combination.

#### Part (c) — Manual Assembly Cost

$$
C_{A} = C_{m} \cdot t_{m} = 0.50 \times 120 = \text{₹}60
$$

#### Part (d) — Redesign Decision

The industrial redesign threshold is $\eta_{DFA} < 60 \,\%$. Since:

$$
\eta_{DFA} = 15 \,\% < 60 \,\%
$$

**Decision: Redesign is mandatory.** The designer should re-apply the Three-Question Test, eliminate redundant parts, and reduce the assembly time.

### 3.5 Counter-Check: Target Time for an Acceptable Design

If management insists on a target DFA Index of **60 %**, what is the *maximum acceptable* total assembly time for the same product?

$$
t_{m,\,target} = \dfrac{N_{m} \cdot 3}{\eta_{DFA,\,target}} = \dfrac{6 \cdot 3}{0.60} = \dfrac{18}{0.60} = 30 \text{ s}
$$

The current design (120 s) is **four times** slower than this target — confirming the magnitude of the redesign effort required.

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Two Iterations

| Metric | Original Design | Redesigned Concept |
|--------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Total parts, $N$ | 15 | 8 |
| Theoretical minimum, $N_{m}$ | 6 | 6 |
| Total assembly time, $t_{m}$ | 120 s | 28 s |
| DFA Index, $\eta_{DFA}$ | 15 % | 64 % |
| Design efficiency, $E_{d}$ | 40 % | 75 % |
| Labour cost (at ₹0.50/s) | ₹60 | ₹14 |
| Cost saving | — | ₹46 per unit (76.7 %) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The redesigned concept *eliminates 7 parts* (a 47 % reduction in part count) and *reduces assembly time by 77 %*. The DFA Index jumps from 15 % to 64 %, clearing the 60 % industrial threshold. This is the typical return on investment expected from a single round of systematic DFA analysis.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Flowchart of the Systematic DFA Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Product Concept and Customer Needs] --> B[Phase 1: Information Preparation]
    B --> B1[Identify Functional Requirements]
    B1 --> B2[Define Operating Environment and Constraints]
    B2 --> B3[Specify Target Production Volume and Cost]
    B3 --> C[Phase 2: Creative Conceptual Design]
    C --> C1[Generate Multiple Design Concepts]
    C1 --> C2[For each part, apply Three-Question Test]
    C2 --> Q1{Question 1: Does the part move during operation?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| KEEP1[Mark part: KEEP]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Question 2: Must part be different material?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| KEEP2[Mark part: KEEP]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Question 3: Must part be separated for assembly?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| KEEP3[Mark part: KEEP]
    Q3 -->|No| COMBINE[Mark part: CANDIDATE FOR COMBINATION]
    KEEP1 --> D[Phase 3: Embodiment Design]
    KEEP2 --> D
    KEEP3 --> D
    COMBINE --> D
    D --> D1[Generate Assembly Sequence]
    D1 --> D2[Estimate Handling Insertion Fastening Times]
    D2 --> D3[Compute Total Assembly Time tm]
    D3 --> D4[Compute DFA Index eta DFA]
    D4 --> E{Is eta DFA greater than or equal to target threshold of 60 percent?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Accept Design and Document]
    E -->|No| G[Redesign: revisit Part Mix and Assembly Sequence]
    G --> C
    F --> H[End: Finalised DFA Optimised Design]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Single Assembly Operation] --> H[Handling Stage]
    H --> H1[H1: 1.5 s Symmetric Easy Grasp]
    H --> H2[H2: 3.0 s Symmetric Awkward]
    H --> H3[H3: 4.5 s Asymmetric Awkward]
    P --> I[Insertion Stage]

    H1 --> SUM[Total ti equals tH plus tI plus tF]
    H2 --> SUM
    H3 --> SUM
    I1 --> SUM
    I2 --> SUM
    I3 --> SUM
    F1 --> SUM

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** *Define the Boothroyd–Dewhurst Three-Question Test used in the systematic DFA methodology. State the three questions precisely.*

**Model Answer:**
The Three-Question Test is a systematic decision rule applied to each candidate part of a product to determine whether it is essential or can be combined with another part. A part is retained as a separate component only if *at least one* of the following three questions is answered "Yes":

1. **Motion question:** Does the part move relative to all other parts during operation of the product?
2. **Material question:** Must the part be made of a different material from the other parts, or must it be isolated from them?
3. **Disassembly question:** Must the part be separated from the other parts to allow disassembly or assembly of the product?

If all three answers are "No", the part is a candidate for **elimination or combination**.

> **(2 marks for stating the three questions correctly, 1 mark for the rule that "Yes" to any one question is sufficient.)**

---

**Q2. (3 Marks)** *What is the DFA Index? Write its formula and explain the significance of the constant 3 in the Boothroyd formulation.*

**Model Answer:**
The **DFA Index** is a dimensionless measure of the assembly efficiency of a product design. It is the ratio of the minimum possible assembly time to the actual assembly time.

$$
\eta_{DFA} = \dfrac{N_{m} \cdot 3}{t_{m}}
$$

The **constant 3 seconds** represents the Boothroyd baseline for an *ideal* part: 1.5 s for handling and 1.5 s for insertion. It is the theoretical minimum time for one part to enter the assembly and be put into place.

> **(2 marks for the formula, 1 mark for the explanation of the constant.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4, 14-Mark Pattern with Internal Choice]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain in detail the three phases of the systematic Boothroyd–Dewhurst DFA methodology. State the inputs, activities, and outputs of each phase. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(b) (7 Marks)** A product consists of 12 parts. The Theoretical Minimum Number of Parts $N_{m}$ is 5. The individual handling, insertion, and fastening times (in seconds) for each part are tabulated below. Compute: (i) the total assembly time, (ii) the DFA Index, and (iii) the manual assembly cost at a labour rate of ₹0.75 per second. Comment on whether redesign is required.

| Part | $t_{H_i}$ (s) | $t_{I_i}$ (s) | $t_{F_i}$ (s) |
|------|---------------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | 1.5 | 1.5 | 1.5 |
| 2 | 3.0 | 3.0 | 1.5 |
| 3 | 3.0 | 1.5 | 0.0 |
| 4 | 1.5 | 3.0 | 1.5 |
| 5 | 4.5 | 3.0 | 3.0 |
| 6 | 1.5 | 1.5 | 0.0 |
| 7 | 3.0 | 3.0 | 1.5 |
| 8 | 1.5 | 1.5 | 1.5 |
| 9 | 1.5 | 4.5 | 1.5 |
| 10 | 3.0 | 3.0 | 1.5 |
| 11 | 1.5 | 1.5 | 0.0 |
| 12 | 3.0 | 3.0 | 1.5 |

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The systematic DFA methodology is executed in three sequential phases:

**Phase 1 — Information Preparation**
- **Inputs:** Customer requirements, product functional specifications, operating environment, target production volume, target cost, available manufacturing and assembly processes.
- **Activities:** Construct a product structure tree; identify the interfaces between parts; gather data on materials, tolerances, finishes; define the design constraints.
- **Outputs:** A clear *design brief* and a *functional block diagram* of the product.

**Phase 2 — Creative Conceptual Design**
- **Inputs:** Outputs of Phase 1.
- **Activities:** Generate multiple design concepts that satisfy the function; apply the Three-Question Test to every part of every concept; iterate; combine or eliminate redundant parts; rank concepts using part count and a first-cut DFA estimate.
- **Outputs:** A small set (2–3) of *DFA-evaluated conceptual designs* with a justified part list and a target DFA Index.

**Phase 3 — Embodiment Design and Verification**
- **Inputs:** The chosen concept from Phase 2.
- **Activities:** Build a detailed 3D CAD model; determine the assembly sequence; estimate each operation time using Boothroyd codes; compute the total assembly time $t_{m}$; compute the DFA Index; estimate assembly cost; compare against target.
- **Outputs:** A *fully detailed design*, a *completed DFA worksheet*, and a *costed assembly plan*. If the DFA Index is below the target threshold, the design is fed back to Phase 2.

> **(2 marks for naming the phases, 3 marks for inputs/outputs, 2 marks for the iterative feedback loop.)**

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Time per part:**
Computing $t_{i} = t_{H_i} + t_{I_i} + t_{F_i}$ for each of the 12 parts:

| Part | $t_{H_i}$ | $t_{I_i}$ | $t_{F_i}$ | $t_{i}$ (s) |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | 1.5 | 1.5 | 1.5 | 4.5 |
| 2 | 3.0 | 3.0 | 1.5 | 7.5 |
| 3 | 3.0 | 1.5 | 0.0 | 4.5 |
| 4 | 1.5 | 3.0 | 1.5 | 6.0 |
| 5 | 4.5 | 3.0 | 3.0 | 10.5 |
| 6 | 1.5 | 1.5 | 0.0 | 3.0 |
| 7 | 3.0 | 3.0 | 1.5 | 7.5 |
| 8 | 1.5 | 1.5 | 1.5 | 4.5 |
| 9 | 1.5 | 4.5 | 1.5 | 7.5 |
| 10 | 3.0 | 3.0 | 1.5 | 7.5 |
| 11 | 1.5 | 1.5 | 0.0 | 3.0 |
| 12 | 3.0 | 3.0 | 1.5 | 7.5 |

**Step 2 — Total Assembly Time:**

$$
t_{m} = \sum_{i=1}^{12} t_{i} = 4.5+7.5+4.5+6.0+10.5+3.0+7.5+4.5+7.5+7.5+3.0+7.5
$$

$$
t_{m} = 73.5 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — DFA Index:**

$$
\eta_{DFA} = \dfrac{N_{m} \cdot 3}{t_{m}} = \dfrac{5 \cdot 3}{73.5} = \dfrac{15}{73.5} \approx 0.2041
$$

$$
\eta_{DFA}^{\%} \approx 20.41 \,\%
$$

**Step 4 — Manual Assembly Cost:**

$$
C_{A} = C_{m} \cdot t_{m} = 0.75 \times 73.5 = \text{₹}55.125
$$

**Step 5 — Decision on Redesign:**

Industrial threshold = 60 %. Since $\eta_{DFA} \approx 20.41 \,\% < 60 \,\%$, **redesign is mandatory**. The designer should re-apply the Three-Question Test and explore part combinations to reduce both part count and operation times.

> **(2 marks for $t_{m}$, 2 marks for $\eta_{DFA}$, 1 mark for $C_{A}$, 2 marks for the redesign conclusion and comment.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative choice for Question A)*

**(a) (7 Marks)** Discuss the role of the DFA worksheet in the systematic methodology. Explain the Boothroyd time codes (H1, H2, H3 and I1, I2, I3) and their numerical values. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

**(b) (7 Marks)** A redesigned product concept has a Theoretical Minimum Number of Parts $N_{m} = 4$. The estimated total manual assembly time is 24 s. The original design had $N = 10$ parts and $t_{m} = 80$ s. The labour rate is ₹1.00 per second. Calculate the **DFA Index, design efficiency, and assembly cost saving** for the redesign. Comment on whether the new design clears the 60 % industrial threshold.

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The **DFA worksheet** is the formal data-capture document used throughout the systematic methodology. For each part, the designer records:
- Part name, identification code, and CAD reference.
- The Yes/No answer to each of the three Boothroyd questions.
- The decision (Keep / Combine) and the justification.
- The **Boothroyd handling code (H)**: H1 = 1.5 s, H2 = 3.0 s, H3 = 4.5 s.
- The **Boothroyd insertion code (I)**: I1 = 1.5 s, I2 = 3.0 s, I3 = 4.5 s.
- Any **additional operation time** (fastening, adhesive, testing, etc.).
- The computed $t_{i}$ for that part.

The **Boothroyd Handling Time Codes** are:

| Code | Description | Time (s) |
|------|-------------|----------|
| H1 | Symmetrical, easy to grasp | **1.5 s** |
| H2 | Symmetrical, awkward to grasp | **3.0 s** |
| H3 | Asymmetrical, awkward to grasp | **4.5 s** |

The **Boothroyd Insertion Time Codes** are:

| Code | Description | Time (s) |
|------|-------------|----------|
| I1 | Self-locating, no alignment needed | **1.5 s** |
| I2 | Requires visual or fixture alignment | **3.0 s** |
| I3 | Requires alignment plus hold-down | **4.5 s** |

**Role of the worksheet:** It provides a *traceable, auditable record* of every decision; it enables the team to discuss each part systematically rather than intuitively; it is the single source of truth used to compute the DFA Index and the assembly cost.

> **(3 marks for the worksheet contents, 2 marks for handling codes, 2 marks for insertion codes.)**

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — DFA Index of the New Design:**

$$
\eta_{DFA,new} = \dfrac{N_{m} \cdot 3}{t_{m,new}} = \dfrac{4 \cdot 3}{24} = \dfrac{12}{24} = 0.50
$$

$$
\eta_{DFA,new}^{\%} = 50 \,\%
$$

**Step 2 — DFA Index of the Original Design (for comparison):**

$$
\eta_{DFA,old} = \dfrac{N_{m} \cdot 3}{t_{m,old}} = \dfrac{4 \cdot 3}{80} = \dfrac{12}{80} = 0.15
$$

$$
\eta_{DFA,old}^{\%} = 15 \,\%
$$

**Step 3 — Design Efficiency of the New Design:**

$$
E_{d,new} = \dfrac{N_{m}}{N_{new}} = \dfrac{4}{4} = 1.00 = 100 \,\%
$$

> Since the new design has the theoretical minimum number of parts, the design efficiency is at the maximum value of 100 %.

**Step 4 — Assembly Cost Comparison:**

$$
C_{A,old} = 1.00 \times 80 = \text{₹}80
$$

$$
C_{A,new} = 1.00 \times 24 = \text{₹}24
$$

$$
\text{Cost Saving} = C_{A,old} - C_{A,new} = 80 - 24 = \text{₹}56 \text{ per unit}
$$

**Step 5 — Improvement Ratio:**

$$
\Delta\eta = \dfrac{\eta_{new} - \eta_{old}}{\eta_{old}} = \dfrac{0.50 - 0.15}{0.15} = \dfrac{0.35}{0.15} \approx 2.33 \;\;(\text{or } 233 \,\%)
$$

**Step 6 — Threshold Check:**

The new DFA Index is **50 %**, which is **below the 60 % industrial threshold**. Hence, although the redesign is a major improvement (a 233 % increase in DFA efficiency and a 70 % reduction in assembly cost), it is *not yet* at the industry-acceptable level. A further round of DFA analysis is recommended to combine any remaining parts and reduce the total time below **20 s** (which would give $\eta_{DFA} = 60 \,\%$).

> **(2 marks for DFA Index of new design, 2 marks for design efficiency, 2 marks for cost saving, 1 mark for the threshold comment.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the "3" in the DFA Index formula.** Many students write $\eta_{DFA} = N_{m}/t_{m}$ and lose 1 mark. Always remember the Boothroyd baseline of 3 s per ideal part.
> 2. **Mixing up $N_{m}$ and $N$.** $N_{m}$ is the *minimum* number of parts *after* applying the Three-Question Test; $N$ is the *current* number. They are different.
> 3. **Not converting seconds into the right unit** when the labour rate is given in ₹/minute or ₹/hour. Always convert to a consistent unit (per second is the cleanest in KTU papers).
> 4. **Skipping the redesign decision.** The question often asks "is redesign required?" — and students compute the index but forget to state the decision. The examiner's key explicitly expects a clear Yes/No statement with the 60 % threshold cited.
> 5. **Drawing the flowchart as a one-way path.** The systematic methodology has a *feedback loop*; if you draw it without the feedback, the examiner will deduct 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing the Boothroyd H and I codes.** Remember: H is about *grasping* the part, I is about *inserting* it. They are sequential, not interchangeable.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- The **Systematic DFA Methodology** was developed by **Boothroyd and Dewhurst** to minimise part count and assembly time.
- It is built around **three phases**: Information Preparation, Creative Conceptual Design, and Embodiment Design — with an *iterative feedback loop*.
- The **Three-Question Test** (motion, material, disassembly) decides whether a part is necessary; a "Yes" to *any one* question keeps the part.
- The **DFA Index** is $\eta_{DFA} = (N_{m} \cdot 3) / t_{m}$; it is the *single most important quantitative metric* in the topic.
- The **Design Efficiency Ratio** is $E_{d} = N_{m} / N$; it is a part-count-only metric and complements the DFA Index.
- The **DFA Worksheet** records, for every part: the three question answers, the handling code (H1, H2, H3), the insertion code (I1, I2, I3), and any extra operation time.
- The Boothroyd time values are **1.5 s, 3.0 s, 4.5 s** for both handling and insertion codes; the total per-part time is $t_{i} = t_{H_i} + t_{I_i} + t_{F_i}$.
- The **constant 3** in the DFA Index formula represents the *ideal* combined handling + insertion time for one part (1.5 + 1.5).
- The **assembly cost** is $C_{A} = C_{m} \cdot t_{m}$; redesigns are economically justified by computing the cost saving.
- The **industrial threshold** for an acceptable DFA Index is **60 %**; values below this trigger a redesign loop.
- The methodology is **iterative** — never present it as a strictly linear flow in your answer script.
- For KTU 2024 numerical problems, always **show every step of summation**; do not skip intermediate $t_{i}$ calculations.
- **Conversion discipline:** keep labour rate in ₹/s and assembly time in seconds for cleanest arithmetic.
- The methodology is **universal** — it applies to mechanical, electronic, and mechatronic products; exam questions may draw examples from any of these.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Assembly Efficiency # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Assembly Efficiency (AE)** is a fundamental quantitative metric in Design for Assembly (DFA) that evaluates the effectiveness of a product's design by comparing the **actual number of parts** in the assembly against the **theoretical minimum number of parts** required to satisfy the product's functional requirements. $$AE = \frac{N_m}{N_a} \times 100\%$$ Where: - $N_m$ = **Minimum (theoretical) number of parts** — the ideal count derived by systematically combining non-essential parts - $N_a$ = **Actual number of parts** — the count present in the current product design This is also referred to as the **Boothroyd DFA Index** (after Geoffrey Boothroyd, the pioneer of structured DFA methodology). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Assembly Efficiency is the *central evaluation metric* in Module 4. It directly supports the Course Outcome related to applying DFA principles to minimize part count and simplify assembly operations. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego Brick" Intuition Imagine you are building a small table using Lego bricks. A naïve design might use 12 individual bricks — one for each leg segment, one for the top, several for the side supports, etc. However, the **minimum functional requirement** is really just *one flat top* supported by *one solid base*. The smartest design might use only 4 bricks — each leg as one piece and the top as one piece. Assembly Efficiency is the **"smartness ratio"** of your Lego design: $$AE = \frac{4 \text{ (smart bricks)}}{12 \text{ (cluttered bricks)}} \times 100\% = 33.3\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** An AE of **100%** indicates a *theoretically perfect design* — no further parts can be eliminated without sacrificing function. Real-world mature products typically achieve **60% – 80%** AE. Below 50% suggests significant DFA improvement opportunities. ## 1.3 Why Assembly Efficiency Matters A higher Assembly Efficiency directly translates to: - **Lower production cost** (fewer parts = fewer operations) - **Faster assembly time** (fewer insertion and handling steps) - **Higher product reliability** (fewer interfaces = fewer failure points) - **Simplified supply chain** (fewer purchased components) - **Easier service and disassembly** (recyclability) > [!TIP] > A widely cited industry benchmark by Boothroyd, Dewhurst, and Knight states that **every 10% increase in DFA Index can reduce assembly cost by approximately 25%–30%** in mass-production contexts. ## 1.4 Time-Based Assembly Efficiency In the broader Boothroyd DFA framework, assembly efficiency is also evaluated in terms of **time**: $$AE_{time} = \frac{t_{min}}{t_a} \times 100\%$$ Where: - $t_{min}$ = Minimum theoretical assembly time (assuming ideal handling and insertion) - $t_a$ = Actual assembly time observed in production > [!NOTE] > For a comprehensive DFA analysis, BOTH the **part-count-based AE** and the **time-based AE** should be computed. They often correlate but can differ when individual parts have varying handling/insertion complexity. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Boothroyd's Three-Question Analysis (Core of DFA) The systematic way to determine $N_m$ is by applying Boothroyd's **Three-Question Test** to every part in the assembly. A part is treated as *essential* (i.e., counted in $N_m$) **only if** at least one of the following is answered "Yes": **Question 1 — Movement:** > *Does the part need to move relative to all other parts already assembled during operation of the product?* **Question 2 — Separation:** > *Is the part materially separate from other parts — i.e., is it made of a different material or must it be produced by a different process?* **Question 3 — Disassembly:** > *Does the part need to be separate to allow disassembly for maintenance or service access?* - If **at least one** answer is **YES** → Part is essential → Count it in $N_m$ - If **all three** answers are **NO** → Part is a candidate for **combination** → Exclude from $N_m$ ## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown ### Step 1: Functional Decomposition Decompose the assembly into its constituent parts. Create a **DFA Worksheet** with three columns: - Column A: Part identity - Column B: Three-question analysis - Column C: Decision (Keep / Combine) ### Step 2: Symmetry & Handling Classification For each retained part, classify by: - **Symmetry type**: - *Symmetric (rotational symmetry ≥ 2)*: Lower handling cost - *Asymmetric*: Higher handling cost - **Insertion requirements**: Vertical down, straight push, reorientation, etc. ### Step 3: Time Estimation Compute theoretical minimum time using Boothroyd standard times: $$t_i = t_{h} + t_{i,base}$$ Standard Boothroyd times (per part): | Operation Type | Standard Time (seconds) | |---|---| | Handling — Symmetric part | **1.50 s** | | Handling — Asymmetric part | **1.95 s** | | Insertion — Vertical, no reorientation | **1.50 s** | | Insertion — With one reorientation | **+0.50 s** | | Insertion — With reorientation (additional) | **+0.50 s each** | ### Step 4: Compute Efficiency Apply both the part-count and time-based formulas. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Description / Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Part-count AE | $AE = \dfrac{N_m}{N_a} \times 100\%$ | Primary DFA metric | | 2 | Time-based AE | $AE_t = \dfrac{t_{min}}{t_a} \times 100\%$ | Productivity-based metric | | 3 | Minimum assembly time | $t_{min} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_m} (t_{h,i} + t_{i,i})$ | Sum of ideal handling + insertion | | 4 | Number of parts eliminated | $\Delta N = N_a - N_m$ | Quantifies simplification potential | | 5 | Percentage reduction in parts | $\%\text{Reduction} = \dfrac{\Delta N}{N_a} \times 100\%$ | Measure of design improvement | | 6 | Three-question test | Q1 ∨ Q2 ∨ Q3 = TRUE | Determines if a part is essential | | 7 | DFA Index (alternate name) | $I_{DFA} = \dfrac{N_m}{N_a}$ | Same as AE expressed as a ratio | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Units:** $N_m$ and $N_a$ are dimensionless integers. Time $t$ is in **seconds (s)**. The percentage AE is unitless and bounded between **0% and 100%**. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Assembly Efficiency is widely used in: - **Automotive industry** (gearbox and engine sub-assemblies — typical AE 65–80%) - **Consumer electronics** (smartphone module assemblies) - **Appliance manufacturing** (washing machine sub-systems) - **Aerospace** (modular LRU — Line Replaceable Unit — design) - **DFMA audit reports** for vendor selection and supplier evaluation In production engineering, the **Bills-of-Material (BOM) level** often correlates with AE: a 2-level BOM is usually associated with AE > 70%, whereas a 5-level BOM typically indicates AE < 40%. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Part-Count Assembly Efficiency Metric The conceptual basis of Assembly Efficiency emerges from the **information-theoretic view** of a design. Consider an assembly with $N_a$ parts. Each part introduces a **separation interface** — a physical boundary where two surfaces must be mated, aligned, or fastened. The total number of interfaces in a generic assembly is approximated by: $$I = N_a - 1$$ (Essentially a tree-graph relationship: $N$ nodes yield $N-1$ edges.) Each interface contributes to assembly complexity through three sub-operations: 1. **Locating** the part 2. **Aligning** mating features 3. **Fastening or securing** the joint The *minimum* interface count achievable without sacrificing function is $N_m - 1$. The ratio of these gives the **interface efficiency**: $$\eta_{interface} = \frac{N_m - 1}{N_a - 1}$$ For large $N_a$, this approximates the part-count ratio used in practice: $$\frac{N_m - 1}{N_a - 1} \approx \frac{N_m}{N_a} = AE$$ The DFA Index $AE$ is therefore a **proxy for interface efficiency**, which is why eliminating parts (reducing $N_a$ toward $N_m$) is the *primary* lever of simplification. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Time-Based Assembly Efficiency The Boothroyd-Dewhurst model expresses the minimum theoretical assembly time as a sum of deterministic operation times: $$t_{min} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_m} (t_{h,i} + t_{i,i})$$ Where: - $t_{h,i}$ = Handling time of the $i^{th}$ part (depends on symmetry) - $t_{i,i}$ = Insertion time of the $i^{th}$ part (depends on reorientation) **Step-by-step expansion:** **Step 1:** Classify each retained part by handling class: $$ t_{h,i} = \begin{cases} 1.50 \text{ s}, & \text{if part } i \text{ is symmetric} \\ 1.95 \text{ s}, & \text{if part } i \text{ is asymmetric} \end{cases} $$ **Step 2:** Determine insertion class: $$ t_{i,i} = 1.50 \text{ s} + 0.50 \text{ s} \times r_i $$ Where $r_i$ is the number of reorientations required to mate part $i$ (typical $r_i \in \{0, 1, 2\}$). **Step 3:** Sum over all $N_m$ parts: $$ t_{min} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_m} 1.50 + \sum_{i=1}^{N_m} (1.50 + 0.50 r_i) + \sum_{i \in \text{asym}} 0.45 $$ The 0.45 s adjustment is the difference between asymmetric and symmetric handling. **Step 4:** Compute the time-based efficiency: $$ AE_{time} = \frac{t_{min}}{t_a} \times 100\% $$ ## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — "Bicycle Bell Assembly" Consider a small bicycle bell assembly that originally consists of **8 parts**: | Part # | Part Name | Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | Bell dome (cover) | Houses mechanism | | 2 | Striker arm | Rotates to hit dome | | 3 | Pivot pin | Axis for arm | | 4 | Spring | Provides striker force | | 5 | Mounting bracket | Attaches to handlebar | | 6 | Clamp bolt (2 halves) | Secures bracket | | 7 | Knob | User interface | | 8 | Trigger lever | User input | So $N_a = 8$. ### Stage 1: Apply Boothroyd's Three Questions | Part | Q1: Move? | Q2: Separate Material? | Q3: Disassemble? | Decision | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1. Bell dome | No | No (could be molded with bracket) | No | **Combine with bracket** | | 2. Striker arm | **Yes** (rotates) | Yes | Yes | **Keep** | | 3. Pivot pin | No (arm moves around it) | No | **Yes** (for service) | **Keep** | | 4. Spring | No (force on arm) | **Yes** (different material) | No | **Keep** | | 5. Mounting bracket | No | No (combined with dome) | No | **Combine with dome** | | 6. Clamp bolt (2 halves) | No | No | No | **Combine into 1 piece** | | 7. Knob | No | No | No | **Combine with trigger** | | 8. Trigger lever | **Yes** (rotates) | Yes | Yes | **Keep** | ### Stage 2: Count Retained Parts After combination: - Combined dome + bracket (1 part) - Striker arm (1 part) - Pivot pin (1 part) - Spring (1 part) - Combined clamp (1 part — was 2) - Combined knob + trigger (1 part — was 2) $$N_m = 5$$ ### Stage 3: Compute Assembly Efficiency $$ AE = \frac{N_m}{N_a} \times 100\% = \frac{5}{8} \times 100\% = 62.5\% $$ ### Stage 4: Time-Based Computation Assume all retained parts are symmetric except the spring (asymmetric): | Part | $t_h$ (s) | $r_i$ | $t_i$ (s) | Subtotal (s) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Dome+bracket | 1.50 | 0 | 1.50 | 3.00 | | Striker arm | 1.50 | 0 | 1.50 | 3.00 | | Pivot pin | 1.50 | 0 | 1.50 | 3.00 | | Spring | 1.95 | 0 | 1.50 | 3.45 | | Clamp | 1.50 | 1 | 2.00 | 3.50 | | Knob+trigger | 1.50 | 0 | 1.50 | 3.00 | $$ t_{min} = 3.00 + 3.00 + 3.00 + 3.45 + 3.50 + 3.00 = 18.95 \text{ s} $$ If actual measured assembly time is $t_a = 45$ s: $$ AE_{time} = \frac{18.95}{45} \times 100\% = 42.1\% $$ > [!NOTE] > The **gap** between part-count AE (62.5%) and time-based AE (42.1%) indicates that the remaining 5 parts are themselves **time-inefficient** in their handling/insertion. Further DFA refinement (better symmetry, snap-fits, etc.) is required. ### Stage 5: Parts Eliminated $$ \Delta N = N_a - N_m = 8 - 5 = 3 \text{ parts} $$ $$ \%\text{Reduction} = \frac{3}{8} \times 100\% = 37.5\% $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for the Student | Scenario | Action | Effect on AE | |---|---|---| | All 3 questions "NO" for a part | Combine with adjacent part | $N_m$ unchanged, $N_a$ decreases (if redesign implemented) → **AE increases** | | Part moves during operation | **Must keep** — functional necessity | No effect | | Part made of unique material | **Must keep** — process necessity | No effect | | Service-disassembly required | **Must keep** — maintainability | No effect | | Reducing reorientations | Improve insertion design | $t_{min}$ decreases, $AE_{time}$ increases | | Increasing part symmetry | Improve handling | $t_h$ decreases from 1.95 → 1.50 s | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Boothroyd's Three-Question Decision Flowchart The following Mermaid flowchart captures the core DFA decision logic used to determine whether a part is essential: ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA([Start: Examine Part P]) --> nodeB{Q1: Does P move
during operation?} nodeB -- YES --> nodeKeep1[Mark P as ESSENTIAL
Count in Nm] nodeB -- NO --> nodeC{Q2: Is P made of a
different material or
different process?} nodeC -- YES --> nodeKeep2[Mark P as ESSENTIAL
Count in Nm] nodeC -- NO --> nodeD{Q3: Must P be separate
for disassembly or
service access?} nodeD -- YES --> nodeKeep3[Mark P as ESSENTIAL
Count in Nm] nodeD -- NO --> nodeCombine[Mark P as CANDIDATE
for combination with
adjacent part] nodeKeep1 --> nodeNext([Move to Next Part]) nodeKeep2 --> nodeNext nodeKeep3 --> nodeNext nodeCombine --> nodeNext nodeNext --> nodeEnd([End: All Parts Analyzed]) ``` > [!NOTE] > The flowchart above is the **single most important diagram** for Module 4. Every KTU question on Assembly Efficiency begins with this logical decision sequence. ## 4.2 DFA Methodology — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow The following block diagram shows how Assembly Efficiency is computed as part of the overall DFA methodology: ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeIn1[Product Concept
and Functional Spec] --> nodeIn2[Generate Initial
Design Concept] nodeIn2 --> nodeIn3[Disassemble Concept
into Sub-Assemblies] nodeIn3 --> nodeIn4[Identify All Parts
in BOM - Na] nodeIn4 --> nodeProc1[Apply Three
Question Test
to Each Part] nodeProc1 --> nodeOut1[Determine Minimum
Part Count - Nm] nodeOut1 --> nodeProc2[Classify Each Retained Part
by Symmetry] nodeProc2 --> nodeProc3[Determine Handling
Time th] nodeProc3 --> nodeProc4[Determine Reorientation
Count r] nodeProc4 --> nodeProc5[Compute Insertion
Time ti] nodeProc5 --> nodeCalc1[Sum to Find
tmin] nodeOut1 --> nodeCalc2[Compute AE =
Nm / Na x 100] nodeCalc1 --> nodeCalc3[Compare with
Actual Time ta] nodeCalc3 --> nodeCalc4[Compute AE_time =
tmin / ta x 100] nodeCalc2 --> nodeOut2[DFA Report and
Design Recommendations] nodeCalc4 --> nodeOut2 ``` ## 4.3 Visualization of Efficiency Distribution (Block Concept) Below is a conceptual block mapping how the Assembly Efficiency percentage corresponds to design maturity: ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeZ1[AE less than 30 percent
POOR DESIGN
Heavy redundancy] --> nodeZ2[AE 30 to 50 percent
BELOW AVERAGE
Significant simplification possible] nodeZ2 --> nodeZ3[AE 50 to 70 percent
ACCEPTABLE
Typical industrial product] nodeZ3 --> nodeZ4[AE 70 to 90 percent
GOOD DESIGN
Well-optimized product] nodeZ4 --> nodeZ5[AE greater than 90 percent
EXCELLENT
Near-optimal simplification] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reference benchmark** (Boothroyd-Dewhurst industrial studies): A well-designed consumer product typically lies in the **60% – 75%** range. Striving for 100% is rarely practical due to material and service constraints. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **Define Assembly Efficiency. State the Boothroyd three-question test used in Design for Assembly.** **Model Answer:** **Assembly Efficiency (AE)** is defined as the ratio of the *minimum theoretical number of parts* required for a product's function to the *actual number of parts* in the design, expressed as a percentage: $$ AE = \frac{N_m}{N_a} \times 100\% $$ A higher AE indicates a more assembly-friendly design. An AE of 100% implies that no part can be eliminated without compromising function. **Boothroyd's Three-Question Test:** For every part in the assembly, the following three questions are asked. A part is treated as *essential* (counted in $N_m$) only if **at least one** answer is "Yes": 1. **Q1 — Movement:** Does the part need to move relative to other parts during product operation? 2. **Q2 — Material Separation:** Is the part made of a different material or produced by a different manufacturing process? 3. **Q3 — Disassembly:** Does the part need to be separable to allow maintenance, servicing, or recycling? If all three answers are "No", the part is a candidate for combination with an adjacent part. [3 marks] --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **Distinguish between part-count Assembly Efficiency and time-based Assembly Efficiency. State the standard Boothroyd handling times for symmetric and asymmetric parts.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Part-Count AE | Time-Based AE | |---|---|---| | Formula | $AE = (N_m / N_a) \times 100\%$ | $AE_t = (t_{min} / t_a) \times 100\%$ | | Measures | Structural simplification | Operational efficiency | | Considers handling/insertion? | No | Yes | | Best for | Initial screening | Detailed redesign | **Standard Boothroyd Handling Times:** - Symmetric part: **1.50 s** - Asymmetric part: **1.95 s** The base insertion time is **1.50 s** for vertical insertion with no reorientation, plus **0.50 s for each reorientation** required. [3 marks] --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Confusing $N_m$ and $N_a$ in the AE formula. Always remember: $N_m$ is the *minimum theoretical* count; $N_a$ is the *actual* count. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to include the **0.50 s reorientation adjustment** in insertion time — many students miss this and lose 2–3 marks. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Writing the AE formula but not converting to percentage (multiply by 100). Examiners explicitly award 1 mark for correct units/percentage. > - **Common Mistake #4:** In the three-question test, marking a part as "essential" because of a "Yes" to Q3 *only* — make sure you identify *which* question triggered the "essential" decision. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Based on Dec 2024 Pattern]** An industrial valve assembly currently consists of **12 parts**. Upon detailed DFA analysis using Boothroyd's three-question test, the design team has determined that the minimum essential part count is **6 parts**. The current production line records an actual average assembly time of **72 seconds** per valve. After redesign classification, the 6 retained parts have the following characteristics: | Part | Symmetry | Reorientations Required | |---|---|---| | 1. Valve body | Symmetric | 0 | | 2. Stem | Asymmetric | 1 | | 3. Bonnet | Symmetric | 0 | | 4. Disc | Asymmetric | 1 | | 5. Packing | Symmetric | 0 | | 6. Nut | Symmetric | 0 | **(a)** Compute the **part-count Assembly Efficiency** and the **percentage reduction in parts**. Comment on whether the design is well-optimized. [7 marks] **(b)** Compute the **minimum theoretical assembly time** and the **time-based Assembly Efficiency**. Discuss what the gap between part-count AE and time-based AE indicates. [7 marks] --- #### MODEL SOLUTION **Part (a) — Part-Count Analysis** Given: - $N_a = 12$ (actual parts) - $N_m = 6$ (minimum essential parts) **Step 1: Apply the AE formula:** $$ AE = \frac{N_m}{N_a} \times 100\% = \frac{6}{12} \times 100\% = 50\% $$ **Step 2: Number of parts eliminated:** $$ \Delta N = N_a - N_m = 12 - 6 = 6 \text{ parts} $$ **Step 3: Percentage reduction:** $$ \%\text{Reduction} = \frac{\Delta N}{N_a} \times 100\% = \frac{6}{12} \times 100\% = 50\% $$ **Comment:** An AE of 50% lies at the *lower boundary* of the "acceptable" range. The design has been simplified, but further optimization (improving symmetry, eliminating reorientations) is still possible. A target AE of **70–80%** would be industrially desirable. [**7 marks** — 1 mark formula, 2 marks substitution, 2 marks result, 2 marks comment] --- **Part (b) — Time-Based Analysis** **Step 1: Determine handling and insertion times for each part.** Using Boothroyd-Dewhurst standard times: - Handling: symmetric = 1.50 s, asymmetric = 1.95 s - Insertion: $t_i = 1.50 + 0.50 \times r$ | Part | Symmetry | $t_h$ (s) | $r_i$ | $t_i$ (s) | $t_h + t_i$ (s) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1. Body | Sym | 1.50 | 0 | 1.50 | 3.00 | | 2. Stem | Asym | 1.95 | 1 | 2.00 | 3.95 | | 3. Bonnet | Sym | 1.50 | 0 | 1.50 | 3.00 | | 4. Disc | Asym | 1.95 | 1 | 2.00 | 3.95 | | 5. Packing | Sym | 1.50 | 0 | 1.50 | 3.00 | | 6. Nut | Sym | 1.50 | 0 | 1.50 | 3.00 | **Step 2: Sum to find $t_{min}$:** $$ t_{min} = 3.00 + 3.95 + 3.00 + 3.95 + 3.00 + 3.00 = 19.90 \text{ s} $$ **Step 3: Compute time-based AE:** $$ AE_{time} = \frac{t_{min}}{t_a} \times 100\% = \frac{19.90}{72} \times 100\% \approx 27.6\% $$ **Step 4: Discussion of the gap:** The part-count AE is **50%**, while the time-based AE is only **27.6%** — a significant gap. This indicates that although half the parts have been eliminated, the **remaining 6 parts are still time-inefficient** in their handling/insertion. Two specific issues are evident: 1. **Two parts (Stem and Disc) are asymmetric**, each incurring an extra **0.45 s** in handling. 2. **Two parts require a reorientation** during insertion, each adding **0.50 s**. **Recommendations to close the gap:** - Redesign the stem and disc to be symmetric (or near-symmetric) — saves $2 \times 0.45 = 0.90$ s. - Use self-aligning features (chamfers, lead-ins) to eliminate reorientations — saves $2 \times 0.50 = 1.00$ s. Implementing these could reduce $t_{min}$ to about **18.0 s**, raising $AE_{time}$ to about **25.0%** while $AE_{part}$ stays at 50%. (Note: time-based AE may still appear low because the actual $t_a = 72$ s includes non-ideal real-world inefficiencies like operator delays and tool changes; the metric is a *theoretical upper bound*.) [**7 marks** — 2 marks table, 2 marks summation, 1 mark AE_t result, 2 marks discussion] --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Based on May 2024 Pattern]** **(a)** With the help of a **neat flowchart**, explain the **Boothroyd Three-Question Test** as applied in Design for Assembly. State the conditions under which a part is retained in the minimum part count. [7 marks] **(b)** An **electric switch assembly** has **15 parts** in its current design. After applying the DFA method, the design team identifies the following essential parts (which must be retained): switch housing, toggle lever, two contact springs (asymmetric), two terminal screws (symmetric), base plate, and rating label. Compute: - (i) The **part-count Assembly Efficiency**. - (ii) The **minimum theoretical assembly time** given the symmetry and reorientation data below: | Retained Part | Symmetry | Reorientations | |---|---|---| | Housing | Symmetric | 0 | | Toggle lever | Asymmetric | 1 | | Spring 1 | Asymmetric | 0 | | Spring 2 | Asymmetric | 0 | | Terminal screw 1 | Symmetric | 1 | | Terminal screw 2 | Symmetric | 1 | | Base plate | Symmetric | 0 | | Rating label | Symmetric | 0 | Assume $t_a = 60$ s. Comment on the time-based efficiency. [7 marks] --- #### MODEL SOLUTION **Part (a) — Three-Question Test Flowchart and Theory** The Boothroyd Three-Question Test is a structured DFA screening method. For every part in the assembly, the designer sequentially asks three questions: ``` [Start: Examine Part P] | v +---------------------------+ | Q1: Does P move during | | product operation? | +-------------+-------------+ | YES --->|----> Mark P ESSENTIAL | (count in Nm) NO v +---------------------------+ | Q2: Is P made of a | | different material | | or process? | +-------------+-------------+ | YES --->|----> Mark P ESSENTIAL | (count in Nm) NO v +---------------------------+ | Q3: Does P need to be | | separate for service | | or disassembly? | +-------------+-------------+ | YES --->|----> Mark P ESSENTIAL | (count in Nm) NO v +---------------------------+ | ALL THREE = NO | | => P is CANDIDATE for | | combination | +---------------------------+ ``` **Conditions for Retention:** A part is retained in $N_m$ if **at least one** of the following is true: - It must move during product operation (functional necessity). - It is manufactured from a different material or by a different process (manufacturing necessity). - It must be separable to enable disassembly, maintenance, or recycling (service necessity). A part that answers "No" to **all three** questions is a candidate for **combination with an adjacent part** and is excluded from $N_m$. [**7 marks** — 3 marks flowchart, 2 marks conditions, 2 marks explanation] --- **Part (b) — Numerical Computation** **Step 1: Identify $N_a$ and $N_m$.** - $N_a = 15$ parts (original) - $N_m = 8$ parts (after DFA: housing, toggle, 2 springs, 2 screws, base, label) **Step 2: Compute Part-Count AE:** $$ AE = \frac{N_m}{N_a} \times 100\% = \frac{8}{15} \times 100\% \approx 53.3\% $$ **Step 3: Build time table.** | Part | $t_h$ (s) | $r_i$ | $t_i = 1.50 + 0.50 r_i$ (s) | Subtotal (s) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Housing | 1.50 | 0 | 1.50 | 3.00 | | Toggle lever | 1.95 | 1 | 2.00 | 3.95 | | Spring 1 | 1.95 | 0 | 1.50 | 3.45 | | Spring 2 | 1.95 | 0 | 1.50 | 3.45 | | Screw 1 | 1.50 | 1 | 2.00 | 3.50 | | Screw 2 | 1.50 | 1 | 2.00 | 3.50 | | Base plate | 1.50 | 0 | 1.50 | 3.00 | | Rating label | 1.50 | 0 | 1.50 | 3.00 | **Step 4: Sum to find $t_{min}$:** $$ t_{min} = 3.00 + 3.95 + 3.45 + 3.45 + 3.50 + 3.50 + 3.00 + 3.00 = 26.85 \text{ s} $$ **Step 5: Compute time-based AE:** $$ AE_{time} = \frac{26.85}{60} \times 100\% \approx 44.75\% $$ **Step 6: Comment:** - $AE_{part} = 53.3\%$ — acceptable but not excellent. - $AE_{time} = 44.75\%$ — moderate. The gap (~8.5 percentage points) shows that, while part elimination was done well, the **remaining parts still incur time penalties** due to: 1. **Three asymmetric parts** (toggle + 2 springs) adding 0.45 s each. 2. **Three parts requiring reorientation** (toggle, screw 1, screw 2) adding 0.50 s each. To improve $AE_{time}$ further, designers should aim to make the springs symmetric (e.g., by adding features for orientation-agnostic insertion) and use captive/snap-fit screws that don't require manual reorientation. [**7 marks** — 1 mark AE_part, 3 marks table + summation, 1 mark AE_time, 2 marks comment] --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield KTU Module 4 Revision Points — Assembly Efficiency** - **Definition:** $AE = (N_m / N_a) \times 100\%$; bounded between 0% and 100%. - **Boothroyd's Three Questions:** Movement, Material Separation, Disassembly. **One "Yes" = Essential.** - **Combined Criterion:** Combine parts only when **all three** answers are "No". - **Standard Times (Boothroyd-Dewhurst):** Symmetric handling = **1.50 s**; Asymmetric handling = **1.95 s**; Base insertion = **1.50 s**; **+0.50 s per reorientation**. - **Time-based AE:** $AE_t = (t_{min} / t_a) \times 100\%$. - **Minimum assembly time:** $t_{min} = \sum (t_h + t_i)$ over all $N_m$ parts. - **DFA Index = AE** (synonymous terms in Boothroyd literature). - **Industry benchmark:** Good design → AE 60–80%; Excellent design → AE > 90%. - **Order of operations in DFA analysis:** (1) Identify all parts ($N_a$) → (2) Apply three questions → (3) Count essentials ($N_m$) → (4) Compute AE → (5) Refine using handling/insertion data → (6) Compute $AE_t$. - **Eliminate Before Refining:** Always first reduce part count; *then* optimize remaining parts' geometry. - **Common confusion trap:** $N_m \leq N_a$ always; hence $AE \leq 100\%$ always. - **Examiner's favorite trap:** Asking for the formula and getting the inverse ($N_a / N_m$) — **always divide minimum by actual**. - **Reorientation default:** If the problem does not specify reorientation count, default $r_i = 0$ (vertical drop insertion). - **Symmetry default:** If unspecified, assume **symmetric** unless the part is an obvious keyed/asymmetric feature (e.g., springs, D-shaped shafts). - **Memory hook for the three questions:** **"M-M-D"** → **M**ovement, **M**aterial, **D**isassembly. - **Relationship to cost:** Each 10% rise in AE ≈ 25–30% drop in assembly cost (mass-production contexts). - **Why this matters in industry:** AE directly drives **DFA audit scores**, **supplier selection**, and **BOM complexity** in PLM systems.

topics.title: Classification System for Manual Handling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification System for Manual Handling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Classification System for Manual Handling** is a structured ergonomic evaluation framework that categorizes manual material handling (MMH) and assembly tasks into distinct risk levels based on measurable biomechanical, physiological, and postural parameters. In the context of **Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)**, this classification acts as a quantitative decision-support tool that guides product designers in determining whether a handling task is **acceptable, conditionally acceptable, or hazardous**, thereby driving design changes *upstream* before the product reaches the shop floor.

The most universally adopted classification in modern engineering design is the **Revised NIOSH Lifting Equation (RNLE)**, published by the **National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH)** in 1994, which superseded the original 1981 equation. It computes a **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** and compares it against the actual load to derive a **Lifting Index (LI)** — a single dimensionless number that classifies the task.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 Scheme, Module 4 of PEBRT524 demands that students must be able to (CO-4) "evaluate a design from the perspective of manual handling and ergonomic safety," making the classification system a high-weightage, frequently-asked topic in Part B questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **delivery warehouse worker** in Kerala lifting a sack of rice from the ground onto a truck. The sack's weight alone does **not** tell you whether the lift is safe. What actually matters is:

- How **far** the sack is from your body? (Horizontal distance)
- How **high** must you lift it? (Vertical height)
- How **twisted** is your back? (Asymmetry)
- How **often** do you repeat this? (Frequency)
- How **good** is your grip? (Coupling)

The **Classification System for Manual Handling** works exactly like a **traffic signal**:

| Signal | Meaning | Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 🟢 **GREEN (LI < 1.0)** | Safe lift, no action needed | Proceed as designed |
| 🟡 **YELLOW (1.0 ≤ LI ≤ 3.0)** | Conditional / Increased risk | Redesign the task |
| 🔴 **RED (LI > 3.0)** | High risk of injury | Mandatory redesign |

> [!NOTE]
> Think of the Lifting Index (LI) as a **"risk thermometer."** Just as a thermometer reading of 102 °F is more dangerous than 99 °F, an LI of 3.5 demands immediate design intervention, while LI = 0.5 is comfortably safe.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The system uses a **load weight constant** and **reference posture benchmarks**:

- **Load Weight Constant (LC)** = **23 kg** (the maximum recommended weight under ideal conditions, equivalent to 51 lb)
- **Reference Vertical Height** = **75 cm** (29.5 in) from the floor
- **Reference Horizontal Distance** = **25 cm** (9.8 in) from the body
- **Reference Asymmetry Angle** = **0°** (no twist)
- **Reference Coupling** = **Good** (handles, cut-outs)
- **Reference Frequency** = **Once per 5 minutes**
- **Reference Duration** = **≤ 1 hour** of continuous lifting

These constants are derived from **psychophysical, biomechanical, and physiological data** gathered from industrial worker populations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Revised NIOSH Lifting Equation (RNLE) — Logic Decomposition

The RNLE computes two principal outputs:

### 2.1.1 Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)

The RWL is the **maximum weight** that nearly all healthy workers can lift under the specific conditions of the task **without an increased risk of musculoskeletal injury**. It is defined as:

$$
RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM
$$

Where each multiplier is a **dimensionless reduction factor** ranging from 0 to 1.

### 2.1.2 Lifting Index (LI)

$$
LI = \frac{\text{Actual Load Weight (L)}}{RWL}
$$

The LI categorizes the task risk as:

| Lifting Index | Risk Level | Design Implication |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **LI < 1.0** | **Acceptable** | No redesign required for most workers |
| **1.0 ≤ LI ≤ 2.0** | **Moderate Risk** | Redesign is *advisable*; some workers may be at risk |
| **2.0 < LI ≤ 3.0** | **High Risk** | Redesign is **strongly recommended** |
| **LI > 3.0** | **Very High Risk** | **Mandatory** redesign; immediate engineering control needed |

## 2.2 The Six Multipliers — Detailed Explanation

### 2.2.1 Horizontal Multiplier (HM)

Captures the **horizontal distance (H)** of the load's center of gravity from the midpoint of the line joining the inner ankle bones.

- **Reference value:** H = 25 cm (≈ 9.8 in)
- **Range:** Valid for **25 cm ≤ H ≤ 63 cm**
- Outside this range, set HM = 0 (unacceptable)

$$
HM = \frac{25}{H \ (\text{cm})}
$$

**Why it matters:** The further the load is from the spine, the greater the moment arm and lower-back compressive force.

### 2.2.2 Vertical Multiplier (VM)

Reflects the **vertical height (V)** of the hands above the floor at the origin of the lift.

- **Reference value:** V = 75 cm (≈ 29.5 in)
- **Range:** Valid for **0 cm ≤ V ≤ 175 cm**

$$
VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert
$$

**Why it matters:** Lifts originating near floor level (V ≈ 0) or above shoulder (V > 175 cm) generate extreme spine stress.

### 2.2.3 Distance Multiplier (DM)

Accounts for the **vertical travel distance (D)** of the lift from origin to destination.

- **Reference value:** D = 25 cm (≈ 9.8 in)
- **Range:** Valid for **25 cm ≤ D ≤ 175 cm**

$$
DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D \ (\text{cm})}
$$

**Why it matters:** Long vertical travel increases muscle fatigue and cumulative spine loading.

### 2.2.4 Asymmetry Multiplier (AM)

Quantifies the **angular twist (A)** of the body during the lift, measured at the origin.

- **Reference value:** A = 0° (sagittal plane)
- **Range:** Valid for **0° ≤ A ≤ 135°**

$$
AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A \ (\text{in degrees})
$$

**Why it matters:** Twisting under load creates **shear forces** on the L5/S1 intervertebral disc — the most common site of industrial back injury.

### 2.2.5 Frequency Multiplier (FM)

Considers the **lifting rate (F)** in lifts per minute and the **duration** of continuous lifting.

- **Range:** F from 0.2 to 15 lifts/min
- **Duration categories:** **Short (≤ 1 hr)**, **Medium (1–2 hr)**, **Long (2–8 hr)**

FM is read from a published NIOSH **Frequency Table**, not computed algebraically. Example values:

| Frequency (lifts/min) | Duration ≤ 1 hr | Duration 1–2 hr | Duration 2–8 hr |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0.2 | 0.95 | 0.87 | 0.81 |
| 1 | 0.91 | 0.84 | 0.75 |
| 4 | 0.84 | 0.72 | 0.55 |
| 12 | 0.68 | 0.45 | 0.20 |

**Why it matters:** Muscles and discs need recovery time; high frequency without rest is a primary fatigue driver.

### 2.2.6 Coupling Multiplier (CM)

Reflects the **quality of the grip** between the worker's hand and the object.

- **Categories:** **Good, Fair, Poor**
- **Values depend on vertical location V:**

| Vertical (V) | Good | Fair | Poor |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| V < 75 cm | 1.00 | 0.95 | 0.90 |
| V ≥ 75 cm | 1.00 | 0.95 | 0.90 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Link:** This is the most direct hook into DFMA — designers can shift a task from *Poor* coupling to *Good* by simply adding handles, flanges, or undercuts to the part.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Parameter | Range / Reference | Formula / Source |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **LC** | Load Constant | **23 kg** | Fixed |
| **H** | Horizontal distance | 25 ≤ H ≤ 63 cm | $HM = 25/H$ |
| **V** | Vertical height | 0 ≤ V ≤ 175 cm | $VM = 1 - 0.003 \vert V - 75 \vert$ |
| **D** | Travel distance | 25 ≤ D ≤ 175 cm | $DM = 0.82 + 4.5/D$ |
| **A** | Asymmetry angle | 0° ≤ A ≤ 135° | $AM = 1 - 0.0032 A$ |
| **F** | Frequency | 0.2–15 lifts/min | From FM Table |
| **Coupling** | Grip quality | Good / Fair / Poor | From CM Table |
| **RWL** | Recommended Weight Limit | kg | $RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$ |
| **LI** | Lifting Index | dimensionless | $LI = L / RWL$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive assembly lines:** Carried out in India and globally to justify robotic vs. manual stations.
- **Defense manufacturing:** Ammunition and weapon handling classification under Indian Factory Act ergonomics rules.
- **Warehouse design:** Amazon, Flipkart use it to size rack heights, picker zones, and tote weights.
- **Medical device packaging:** Ensures sterile barrier systems are not over-weighted.
- **DFMA product redesign:** *Before* tooling is cut, designers compute the LI for each manual step; if LI > 2, they redesign for **gravity-fed hoppers, snap-fits, or jigs** to eliminate the lift entirely.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Numerical Example — KTU Typical Problem Pattern

> **Problem Statement:**
> In a small-scale manufacturing unit in Ernakulam, an operator lifts a **gearbox housing weighing 18 kg** from a workbench (V = 90 cm) and places it on an assembly fixture (V = 130 cm). The horizontal distance of the hands from the ankles is **H = 40 cm**, the body twist angle is **A = 45°**, the lifting frequency is **2 lifts/min** sustained for **2 hours**, and the coupling is **Fair**. Classify the task using the NIOSH classification system.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Identify Given Quantities

$$
L = 18 \text{ kg}, \quad H = 40 \text{ cm}, \quad V_{\text{origin}} = 90 \text{ cm}, \quad D = 130 - 90 = 40 \text{ cm}
$$

$$
A = 45^{\circ}, \quad F = 2 \text{ lifts/min}, \quad \text{Duration} = 2 \text{ hr}, \quad \text{Coupling} = \text{Fair}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Compute Horizontal Multiplier (HM)

$$
HM = \frac{25}{H} = \frac{25}{40} = 0.625
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Compute Vertical Multiplier (VM)

Since VM uses a *single representative V*, we average:

$$
V_{\text{avg}} = \frac{90 + 130}{2} = 110 \text{ cm}
$$

$$
VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V_{\text{avg}} - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 110 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \cdot 35 = 1 - 0.105 = 0.895
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Compute Distance Multiplier (DM)

$$
DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D} = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{40} = 0.82 + 0.1125 = 0.9325
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Compute Asymmetry Multiplier (AM)

$$
AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot 45 = 1 - 0.144 = 0.856
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Look up Frequency Multiplier (FM)

For F = 2 lifts/min and Duration = 2 hr, consulting the NIOSH table:

$$
FM \approx 0.70
$$

### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Look up Coupling Multiplier (CM)

For Fair coupling, $V \geq 75$ cm:

$$
CM = 0.95
$$

### 3.1.8 Step 8 — Compute RWL

$$
RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
RWL = 23 \times 0.625 \times 0.895 \times 0.9325 \times 0.856 \times 0.70 \times 0.95
$$

Computing stepwise in $\begin{aligned}$ environment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RWL &= 23 \times 0.625 \times 0.895 \times 0.9325 \times 0.856 \times 0.70 \times 0.95 \\
    &= 14.375 \times 0.895 \times 0.9325 \times 0.856 \times 0.70 \times 0.95 \\
    &= 12.866 \times 0.9325 \times 0.856 \times 0.70 \times 0.95 \\
    &= 11.998 \times 0.856 \times 0.70 \times 0.95 \\
    &= 10.270 \times 0.70 \times 0.95 \\
    &= 7.189 \times 0.95 \\
    &= 6.830 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.9 Step 9 — Compute Lifting Index (LI)

$$
LI = \frac{L}{RWL} = \frac{18}{6.830} = 2.636
$$

### 3.1.10 Step 10 — Classification and Design Recommendation

| Output | Value | Interpretation |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| RWL | **6.83 kg** | Only 6.83 kg should be lifted under these conditions |
| LI | **2.64** | Falls in **2.0 < LI ≤ 3.0** → **HIGH RISK** |
| Action | **Redesign** | Add fixture with sliding tray, add handle (Good coupling), or reduce V by 20 cm |

## 3.2 Sensitivity Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Parameter on RWL

This matrix helps a DFMA designer prioritize which design change yields the **biggest RWL gain per rupee** invested.

| Design Change | Parameter Varied | ΔRWL Impact | Engineering Cost | Priority |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Add ergonomic handle | CM: 0.95 → 1.00 | **+5.3%** | Low | 🟢 High |
| Reduce horizontal reach by 10 cm | H: 40 → 30 cm | **+25%** | Medium | 🟢 High |
| Add jib crane / hoist | F: 2 → 0.2 lifts/min | **+30 to +40%** | High | 🟡 Medium |
| Reorient pallet to eliminate twist | A: 45° → 0° | **+14.4%** | Low | 🟢 High |
| Lower workbench height | V: 90 → 75 cm | **+4.5%** | Medium | 🟡 Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** A combination of **handle + reorientation + jig hoist** can push LI from 2.64 down to ~1.0, transforming the task from "high risk" to "acceptable" — *purely through design, with no operator training required.*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Classification System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Task Input Parameters"] --> B{"Parameter Identification"}
    B --> C1["H Horizontal distance"]
    B --> C2["V Vertical height"]
    B --> C3["D Travel distance"]
    B --> C4["A Asymmetry angle"]
    B --> C5["F Frequency"]
    B --> C6["Coupling Quality"]
    C1 --> D1["HM Computation"]
    C2 --> D2["VM Computation"]
    C3 --> D3["DM Computation"]
    C4 --> D4["AM Computation"]
    C5 --> D5["FM Table Lookup"]
    C6 --> D6["CM Table Lookup"]
    D1 --> E["Multiplier Aggregation Node"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    E --> F["RWL = LC x HM x VM x DM x AM x FM x CM"]
    G["Actual Load Weight L"] --> H["Lifting Index LI = L / RWL"]
    F --> H
    H --> I{"Risk Classification Engine"}
    I --> J1["LI less than 1.0 ACCEPTABLE GREEN"]
    I --> J2["LI between 1.0 and 2.0 MODERATE YELLOW"]
    I --> J3["LI between 2.0 and 3.0 HIGH RISK ORANGE"]
    I --> J4["LI greater than 3.0 VERY HIGH RED"]
    J1 --> K["Proceed with Design"]
    J2 --> L["Advisory Redesign"]
    J3 --> M["Strongly Recommended Redesign"]
    J4 --> N["Mandatory Engineering Control"]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree for Manual Handling Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start: New Product or Process Step"] --> Q1{"Does the task involve manual lifting?"}
    Q1 -->|No| OUT["No classification needed"]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{"Is H within 25 to 63 cm?"}
    Q2 -->|No| UN1["Unacceptable HM equals 0 Stop and Redesign"]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{"Is V within 0 to 175 cm?"}
    Q3 -->|No| UN2["Unacceptable VM equals 0 Stop and Redesign"]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{"Is A within 0 to 135 degrees?"}
    Q4 -->|No| UN3["Unacceptable AM equals 0 Stop and Redesign"]
    Q4 -->|Yes| CALC["Compute RWL and LI"]
    CALC --> CLS{"Classify Lifting Index"}
    CLS --> C1["Green LI below 1"]
    CLS --> C2["Yellow LI 1 to 2"]
    CLS --> C3["Orange LI 2 to 3"]
    CLS --> C4["Red LI above 3"]
```

## 4.3 Posture Reference Diagram (Block Architecture Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Operator Standing Posture"] --> P2["Hand at Origin Point V1"]
    P2 --> P3["Hand at Destination Point V2"]
    P3 --> P4["Horizontal Reach H"]
    P4 --> P5["Body Twist A"]
    P5 --> P6["Frequency of Cycle F"]
    P6 --> P7["Grip Coupling C"]
    P7 --> P8["Ergonomic Risk Profile"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above deliberately substitute the physical anatomy with **functional architecture** because Mermaid cannot render human postural sketches; however, the *input–output logic* of the ergonomic model is preserved exactly for KTU exam purposes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Question 1: Define the Lifting Index (LI) and state its classification bands.

**Model Answer:**
The **Lifting Index (LI)** is the dimensionless ratio of the **Actual Load Weight (L)** to the **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** as defined by the Revised NIOSH Lifting Equation.

$$
LI = \frac{L}{RWL}
$$

**Classification bands:**

1. **LI < 1.0** → Acceptable for most workers.
2. **1.0 ≤ LI ≤ 2.0** → Moderate risk; advisory redesign.
3. **2.0 < LI ≤ 3.0** → High risk; strongly recommended redesign.
4. **LI > 3.0** → Very high risk; mandatory redesign.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Question 2: List the six multipliers used in the NIOSH equation and state the parameter each one addresses.

**Model Answer:**
The six multipliers are:

1. **HM (Horizontal Multiplier)** — based on horizontal distance H.
2. **VM (Vertical Multiplier)** — based on vertical height V.
3. **DM (Distance Multiplier)** — based on travel distance D.
4. **AM (Asymmetry Multiplier)** — based on angular twist A.
5. **FM (Frequency Multiplier)** — based on lifts per minute and duration.
6. **CM (Coupling Multiplier)** — based on grip quality (Good / Fair / Poor).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part A):** Students frequently list the multipliers but *omit the parameter each one depends on*. Doing so forfeits 1 mark. Always state **both** the symbol **and** the governing physical quantity.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

### Option A — Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Revised NIOSH Lifting Equation** in detail. Define RWL and derive the expressions for the **Horizontal, Vertical, Distance, and Asymmetry multipliers**, including their valid ranges.

**Model Solution:**

The **Revised NIOSH Lifting Equation (RNLE)** is a quantitative ergonomic tool that determines a Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) for a manual lifting task, accounting for biomechanical, postural, and recovery factors.

$$
RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM
$$

The **Load Constant (LC) = 23 kg** represents the maximum recommended weight for an ideal lift.

**Horizontal Multiplier (HM):** Depends on the horizontal distance H of the load from the ankles.

$$
HM = \frac{25}{H \ (\text{cm})}, \quad 25 \le H \le 63 \text{ cm}
$$

**Vertical Multiplier (VM):** Depends on the vertical location V of the hands at origin.

$$
VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert, \quad 0 \le V \le 175 \text{ cm}
$$

**Distance Multiplier (DM):** Depends on the vertical travel distance D.

$$
DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D \ (\text{cm})}, \quad 25 \le D \le 175 \text{ cm}
$$

**Asymmetry Multiplier (AM):** Depends on the angular twist A of the body.

$$
AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A, \quad 0^{\circ} \le A \le 135^{\circ}
$$

**(b)** [7 Marks] An operator lifts a **12 kg component** with H = 35 cm, V = 60 cm, D = 50 cm, A = 30°, F = 1 lift/min (duration < 1 hr), and **Good coupling**. Compute RWL and LI. Classify the task.

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Multipliers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HM &= \frac{25}{35} = 0.714 \\
VM &= 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 60 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \cdot 15 = 1 - 0.045 = 0.955 \\
DM &= 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{50} = 0.82 + 0.090 = 0.910 \\
AM &= 1 - 0.0032 \cdot 30 = 1 - 0.096 = 0.904
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — From FM Table (F = 1, duration < 1 hr): **FM = 0.91**.

Step 3 — From CM Table (Good coupling, V < 75): **CM = 1.00**.

Step 4 — RWL computation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RWL &= 23 \times 0.714 \times 0.955 \times 0.910 \times 0.904 \times 0.91 \times 1.00 \\
    &= 23 \times 0.714 \times 0.955 \times 0.910 \times 0.904 \times 0.91 \\
    &= 16.42 \times 0.955 \times 0.910 \times 0.904 \times 0.91 \\
    &= 15.68 \times 0.910 \times 0.904 \times 0.91 \\
    &= 14.27 \times 0.904 \times 0.91 \\
    &= 12.90 \times 0.91 \\
    &= 11.74 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5 — Lifting Index:

$$
LI = \frac{L}{RWL} = \frac{12}{11.74} = 1.02
$$

Step 6 — Classification: **LI ≈ 1.02 → Yellow Band → Moderate Risk → Advisory Redesign** is recommended.

---

### Option B — Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **importance of the Coupling Multiplier (CM)** in the NIOSH equation and explain how a product designer can improve it during the DFMA stage.

**Model Solution:**
The **Coupling Multiplier (CM)** addresses the *quality of grip* between the worker's hand and the object. A secure grip reduces the **clutch force** (the force the hand must exert to hold the load), which in turn reduces forearm fatigue, accidental slippage, and reflex muscle contraction in the back.

**Standard CM values (for V < 75 cm and V ≥ 75 cm):**

- **Good** coupling → CM = 1.00 (e.g., handles, flanges, holes, smooth concave surfaces)
- **Fair** coupling → CM = 0.95 (e.g., sharp edges, partial handles)
- **Poor** coupling → CM = 0.90 (e.g., flat or slippery surfaces, awkward shapes)

**DFMA Design Strategies to Improve Coupling:**

1. **Add hand-grip features:** Moulded handles, side flanges, or thumb depressions on the part.
2. **Use textured surfaces:** Knurling, ribbing, or rubber overmoulding.
3. **Add mechanical aids:** Eyebolts, lifting lugs, or threaded bosses.
4. **Use packaging geometry:** Boxes with hand-holes or die-cut slots.

> For example, switching from **Poor (0.90)** to **Good (1.00)** improves RWL by **11%** without any other change — a *free ergonomic gain* achieved purely through design.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A worker in a furniture manufacturing unit lifts a **20 kg chair component** from the floor (V = 25 cm) to a table (V = 90 cm). H = 50 cm, A = 60°, F = 0.5 lifts/min, duration = 4 hours, Poor coupling. Compute and classify the lifting task. Suggest **two DFMA redesign actions** to bring LI below 1.0.

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Average V:

$$
V_{\text{avg}} = \frac{25 + 90}{2} = 57.5 \text{ cm}
$$

Step 2 — Compute multipliers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HM &= \frac{25}{50} = 0.500 \\
VM &= 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 57.5 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \cdot 17.5 = 1 - 0.0525 = 0.9475 \\
DM &= 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
D = 90 - 25 = 65 \text{ cm}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
DM &= 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{65} = 0.82 + 0.0692 = 0.8892 \\
AM &= 1 - 0.0032 \cdot 60 = 1 - 0.192 = 0.808
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — From FM Table (F = 0.5, duration 4 hr ≈ Long): **FM ≈ 0.80**.

Step 4 — Poor coupling, V = 57.5 cm (≥ 75? No, V < 75): **CM = 0.90**.

Step 5 — RWL:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RWL &= 23 \times 0.500 \times 0.9475 \times 0.8892 \times 0.808 \times 0.80 \times 0.90 \\
    &= 11.5 \times 0.9475 \times 0.8892 \times 0.808 \times 0.80 \times 0.90 \\
    &= 10.90 \times 0.8892 \times 0.808 \times 0.80 \times 0.90 \\
    &= 9.69 \times 0.808 \times 0.80 \times 0.90 \\
    &= 7.83 \times 0.80 \times 0.90 \\
    &= 6.26 \times 0.90 \\
    &= 5.64 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 6 — Lifting Index:

$$
LI = \frac{20}{5.64} = 3.55
$$

Step 7 — Classification: **LI > 3.0 → RED BAND → VERY HIGH RISK → Mandatory redesign**.

**Two DFMA Redesign Actions:**

1. **Eliminate the lift entirely** by introducing a tilting jig that slides the component from the floor pallet to the table, reducing H to ~25 cm, A to 0°, and frequency to 0.2 lifts/min.
2. **Add side handles** to the chair component, upgrading coupling from Poor (0.90) to Good (1.00) and reducing the operator's reach so H drops to 30 cm.

> Combined effect reduces LI from 3.55 to approximately 0.8 — well into the **safe green zone**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B – 14 Marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting to average V** between origin and destination → loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Using the wrong FM value** (e.g., taking 0.91 for all frequencies) → loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Not stating the LI band explicitly** after the calculation → loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Omitting design recommendations** when a "very high risk" LI is computed → loses 2 marks, as this defeats the *design for ergonomics* purpose of the question.
> 5. **Unit errors** — students sometimes write RWL in grams or pounds → 0 marks for that step.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **RNLE** classifies manual handling tasks into **four risk bands** based on the **Lifting Index (LI)**.
- ✅ **RWL** is computed using **seven terms**: Load Constant (23 kg) and **six multipliers** (HM, VM, DM, AM, FM, CM).
- ✅ **HM** and **DM** use **inverse formulas**; **VM** and **AM** use **linear-decay formulas**.
- ✅ **FM** and **CM** are **read from tables**, never computed algebraically.
- ✅ The **Load Constant (23 kg)** assumes an ideal, healthy worker under ideal conditions — it is **not** a recommended lift for all real tasks.
- ✅ In **DFMA**, the goal is to *eliminate* high-LI tasks via **redesign**, not to rely on worker training.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of controls** in design: (1) Eliminate lift, (2) Mechanize (jig/hoist), (3) Improve posture (reduce H, V, A), (4) Improve coupling, (5) Reduce frequency.
- ✅ A **LI of 1.0** is the **threshold** between *acceptable* and *not acceptable* — a value every KTU student must memorize.
- ✅ Always **average V** between the origin and destination heights when the lift is between two elevations.
- ✅ The **multipliers are dimensionless** and always lie between 0 and 1 (except CM = 1.00 for Good coupling).
- ✅ The most **powerful design levers** in DFMA are: **reducing H** (add a fixture), **eliminating A** (reorient the work), and **adding handles** (improve coupling).
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark problems, **always end with a classification band statement AND a redesign recommendation** — both are mandatory for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification System for Manual Insertion and Fastening
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification System for Manual Insertion and Fastening

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manual Insertion and Fastening Classification System** is a structured design-evaluation methodology, rooted in the **Boothroyd–Dewhurst DFMA (Design for Manufacture and Assembly)** framework, used to categorize hand-operated assembly operations into three tiers — **Preferred, Acceptable, and Eliminate** — based on quantitative insertion time thresholds and qualitative handling/manipulation criteria.

The classification system quantitatively assesses the **ease of human handling** of components and the **ease of fastening** (e.g., screwing, snapping, riveting) during manual assembly. It provides engineers with a **go / no-go decision rule** to determine whether a manual operation is efficient enough to retain in the final design or whether the part must be redesigned, automated, or eliminated altogether.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Luggage Conveyor Belt**
>
> Imagine you are a worker at an airport baggage handling station. Suitcases (parts) arrive on a belt and you must pick each one up, orient it correctly, and slide it into a locker (fasten it).
>
> - If a suitcase is **light, has a single top handle, and slides in from above** → you do it in under 1.5 seconds (Preferred).
> - If the suitcase is **heavier, needs both hands, and you must angle it slightly** → it takes 2 seconds (Acceptable).
> - If the suitcase is **bulky, requires rotating it 180°, and the locker opening is awkward** → it takes 4–5 seconds (Eliminate — you redesign the locker, the handle, or the orientation).
>
> The manual insertion classification system works exactly this way: it is a **stopwatch-based audit** of how fast a human can *handle + insert + fasten* a part. The faster the operation, the lower the labor cost and the lower the error rate.

### 1.3 Core Metrics in the Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The system uses three cardinal metrics:**
>
> 1. **Insertion Time (t)** — measured in **seconds (s)**
> 2. **Accessibility Index** — top-down vs. side vs. obstructed
> 3. **Manipulation Index** — one-handed vs. two-handed, and need for reorientation

These metrics are evaluated against **published threshold tables** to assign each operation to one of three categories.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Tier Classification Architecture

The manual insertion system is built on a hierarchical decision logic, applied in sequence:

**Step 1 — Identify the part geometry and fastening requirement.**
Determine the part's symmetry (rotational, reflective), thickness-to-diameter ratio (t/D), and rigidity.

**Step 2 — Measure the effective insertion time (t).**
Time starts when the operator picks up the part and ends when the part is fully seated and ready for fastening.

**Step 3 — Apply the classification threshold.**
Compare *t* against the time table (see 2.2).

**Step 4 — Apply qualitative handling penalties.**
If the part is asymmetric, requires reorientation, or is obstructed, escalate it one tier (from Preferred → Acceptable, or from Acceptable → Eliminate).

**Step 5 — Apply fastening time classification.**
Once inserted, evaluate the fastening step (screw, snap-fit, rivet, etc.) using the fastening threshold table (see 2.3).

### 2.2 Manual Insertion Time Classification Table

| Classification | Insertion Time (s) | Engineering Implication | Typical Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Preferred** | **t < 1.5 s** | Highly efficient, one-handed, top-down | Snap-fit cap, single-pin connector |
| **Acceptable** | **1.5 s ≤ t < 3.0 s** | Workable but suboptimal; consider design tweaks | Two-pin header, top-loaded screw |
| **Eliminate** | **t ≥ 3.0 s** | Inefficient; redesign, automate, or remove | Multi-pin blind mate, obstructed bolt |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 1.5-second and 3.0-second thresholds are *empirical benchmarks* derived from Boothroyd's industrial time studies on repetitive manual assembly. They are not arbitrary — they correspond to the *cycle time* at which a single operator can sustain assembly without fatigue over an 8-hour shift.**

### 2.3 Manual Fastening Classification Table

| Classification | Fastening Time (s) | Tool / Method | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Preferred** | **t < 2.0 s** | Single stroke, power tool, top-down | Power screwdriver, vertical rivet |
| **Acceptable** | **2.0 s ≤ t < 5.0 s** | Multiple strokes, slight angle | Hand screwdriver, angled screw |
| **Eliminate** | **t ≥ 5.0 s** | Multi-axis, obstructed, torque-controlled | Blind nut, captive washer stack |

### 2.4 Design Rules to Promote Operations Up the Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of these rules:** Every design feature that *reduces cognitive load*, *eliminates reorientation*, or *improves visibility* cuts insertion time. The Boothroyd system encodes these as a checklist:

1. **Symmetry First:** Make parts rotationally symmetric (≥ 1 axis) and reflectively symmetric (≥ 1 plane) to avoid forced reorientation.
