2. **Top-Down Access:** Provide an unobstructed vertical approach path of at least **50 mm** clearance for hand/tool entry.
3. **Grip Affordance:** Provide a grasping feature (flange, chamfer, thumb pad) sized for **one-hand grip**.
4. **Self-Locating Features:** Add chamfers, lead-ins, or tapers ≥ **0.5 mm** × 30° to guide the part into place.
5. **Avoid Stacking:** Never require more than **one fastener** to be applied at a single station.
6. **Minimize Travel Distance:** Keep insertion depth ≤ **25 mm**; longer reach escalates time into the *Eliminate* zone.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Rule | Unit | Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $t_{ins}$ | Manual Insertion Time | $t_{ins} = t_{pick} + t_{orient} + t_{seat}$ | s | t ≥ 0 |
| $t_{fast}$ | Manual Fastening Time | $t_{fast} = t_{engage} + t_{torque}$ | s | t ≥ 0 |
| $t_{total}$ | Total Manual Assembly Time | $t_{total} = t_{ins} + t_{fast}$ | s | per part |
| $C_{asym}$ | Asymmetry Penalty | Add **+0.5 s** if part is asymmetric | s | binary (0 / +0.5) |
| $C_{obstr}$ | Obstruction Penalty | Add **+1.0 s** if approach is obstructed | s | binary (0 / +1.0) |
| $C_{2H}$ | Two-Hand Penalty | Add **+0.5 s** if two-handed | s | binary (0 / +0.5) |
| $C_{flex}$ | Flexibility Penalty | Add **+1.0 s** if part is flexible / non-rigid | s | binary (0 / +1.0) |
| $t_{adj}$ | Adjusted Insertion Time | $t_{adj} = t_{ins} + \sum C_i$ | s | floor at 0.5 s |
| $r$ | Thickness-to-Diameter Ratio | $r = t / D$ | dimensionless | $r \geq 0.5$ preferred |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This system is the *backbone of DFMA audits* in industries ranging from automotive (e.g., instrument panel assembly) to consumer electronics (e.g., smartphone sub-assembly) and white goods (e.g., washing machine drum mounting). Every manual operation that lands in the **Eliminate** zone triggers a formal design review.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Total Manual Assembly Time

The total manual assembly time for a single fastening station is the **sum of insertion and fastening times**, plus any **qualitative handling penalties**. This is derived from the Boothroyd basic time model:

> **Step 1 — Define the elemental operations.**
> Every manual fastening has three elemental operations: *Pick & Orient*, *Insert (Seat)*, and *Fasten (Engage & Torque)*.
>
> **Step 2 — Sum the elemental times.**
> $t_{total} = t_{pick} + t_{orient} + t_{seat} + t_{engage} + t_{torque}$
>
> **Step 3 — Group into insertion and fastening phases.**
> $t_{ins} = t_{pick} + t_{orient} + t_{seat}$
> $t_{fast} = t_{engage} + t_{torque}$
>
> **Step 4 — Add qualitative penalty coefficients.**
> $t_{adj} = t_{ins} + C_{asym} + C_{obstr} + C_{2H} + C_{flex}$
>
> **Step 5 — Compute the final time.**
> $t_{total} = t_{adj} + t_{fast}$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Preferred Operation

> **Problem:** A worker inserts a symmetric cylindrical pin into a top-down hole. The pin has a chamfer of 1 mm, travel is 15 mm, the part is rigid, one-handed, and unobstructed. Pick = 0.4 s, orient = 0.2 s, seat = 0.4 s, engage tool = 0.6 s, torque = 0.8 s. Classify the operation.

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{ins} &= t_{pick} + t_{orient} + t_{seat} \\
        &= 0.4 + 0.2 + 0.4 \\
        &= 1.0 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Penalty check:**
- Symmetric → $C_{asym} = 0$
- Top-down, unobstructed → $C_{obstr} = 0$
- One-handed → $C_{2H} = 0$
- Rigid → $C_{flex} = 0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{adj} &= t_{ins} + \sum C_i = 1.0 + 0 = 1.0 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{fast} &= t_{engage} + t_{torque} = 0.6 + 0.8 = 1.4 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{total} &= t_{adj} + t_{fast} = 1.0 + 1.4 = 2.4 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** $t_{ins} = 1.0 \; s < 1.5 \; s$ → **Preferred** for insertion.
$t_{fast} = 1.4 \; s < 2.0 \; s$ → **Preferred** for fastening.
**Overall Classification: PREFERRED** ✅

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Eliminate Operation

> **Problem:** A bracket has an asymmetric, L-shaped profile, requires two hands, has a 90° side approach, and is partially obstructed by a cable harness. The part is flexible (sheet metal). Pick = 0.6 s, orient = 0.8 s, seat = 0.9 s, engage = 1.0 s, torque = 1.5 s. Classify.

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{ins} &= 0.6 + 0.8 + 0.9 = 2.3 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Penalty check:**
- Asymmetric → $C_{asym} = +0.5$
- Obstructed → $C_{obstr} = +1.0$
- Two-handed → $C_{2H} = +0.5$
- Flexible → $C_{flex} = +1.0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum C_i &= 0.5 + 1.0 + 0.5 + 1.0 = 3.0 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{adj} &= 2.3 + 3.0 = 5.3 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{fast} &= 1.0 + 1.5 = 2.5 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{total} &= 5.3 + 2.5 = 7.8 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** $t_{ins} = 5.3 \; s \geq 3.0 \; s$ → **Eliminate** for insertion.
**Overall Classification: ELIMINATE** ❌ → *redesign required* (e.g., split into two symmetric sub-parts, relocate cable harness, or add a snap-fit instead of screw).

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

The following table is the **canonical KTU-style decision matrix** students should memorize for the exam:

| Time Range (s) | Classification | Required Design Action | Production Cost Impact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $t < 1.5$ | **Preferred** | Retain as-is | Low |
| $1.5 \leq t < 3.0$ | **Acceptable** | Log for future DFM review | Medium |
| $t \geq 3.0$ | **Eliminate** | Mandatory redesign or automation | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Exam Heuristic:** If a problem statement gives you a part with *asymmetry + obstruction + two-hand* requirement, **always escalate to Eliminate** regardless of base time. This is a favourite KTU trick question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Manual Insertion Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Manual Operation] --> B[Identify Part Geometry]
    B --> C[Measure Base Insertion Time tins]
    C --> D{Is Part Asymmetric?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Add 0.5 s Penalty]
    D -->|No| F[No Penalty]
    E --> G{Is Approach Obstructed?}
    F --> G
    G -->|Yes| H[Add 1.0 s Penalty]
    G -->|No| I[No Penalty]
    H --> J{Is Operation Two-Handed?}
    I --> J
    J -->|Yes| K[Add 0.5 s Penalty]
    J -->|No| L[No Penalty]
    K --> M{Is Part Flexible?}
    L --> M
    M -->|Yes| N[Add 1.0 s Penalty]
    M -->|No| O[No Penalty]
    N --> P[Compute Adjusted Time tadj]
    O --> P
    P --> Q{tadj less than 1.5 s}
    Q -->|Yes| R[PREFERRED]
    Q -->|No| S{tadj less than 3.0 s}
    S -->|Yes| T[ACCEPTABLE]
    S -->|No| U[ELIMINATE]
    R --> V[Proceed to Fastening Classification]
    T --> V
    U --> W[Trigger Design Review]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Classification System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: Part Geometry and Fastening Spec] --> B[Block 1: Geometry Analyzer]
    B --> C[Block 2: Time Estimator]
    C --> D[Block 3: Penalty Calculator]
    D --> E[Block 4: Threshold Comparator]
    E --> F{Classification Output}
    F --> G1[Preferred Block]
    F --> G2[Acceptable Block]
    F --> G3[Eliminate Block]
    G3 --> H[Redesign Feedback Loop]
    H --> A
```

### 4.3 Textual Schematic — Manual Fastening Station Layout (Top-Down View)

```
+-----------------------------------------------+
|                  WORKSTATION                  |
|                                               |
|     [Part Feeder]  --->  [Operator Station]   |
|                              |                |
|                              v                |
|                    [Insertion Point]          |
|                              |                |
|                              v                |
|                  [Fastening Tool - Top]       |
|                              |                |
|                              v                |
|                       [Out - Next OP]         |
|                                               |
+-----------------------------------------------+
       ^                          ^
       |                          |
   Top-Down                  Clearance ≥
   Approach                  50 mm
   (Preferred)               (DFMA Rule)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** The vertical cascade of arrows represents a *Preferred* top-down workflow. If the fastening tool must approach from the *side* (lateral), the operation is **demoted by one classification tier** due to added operator wrist rotation time.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*Define the Manual Insertion Classification System. List its three categories with their respective time thresholds.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Manual Insertion Classification System is a DFMA methodology that categorizes hand-operated assembly operations into three efficiency tiers based on insertion time. The categories are:

1. **Preferred** — insertion time **< 1.5 s**; one-handed, top-down, symmetric, unobstructed.
2. **Acceptable** — insertion time **1.5 s to 3.0 s**; minor handling adjustments needed.
3. **Eliminate** — insertion time **≥ 3.0 s**; the operation is too slow and must be redesigned or automated.

*(Award 1 mark each for the three categories + 0.5 for definition + 0.5 for stating the time thresholds.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*List any four design rules that promote a manual insertion operation from the "Eliminate" category to "Preferred".*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Introduce symmetry** — make the part rotationally and reflectively symmetric to eliminate reorientation time.
2. **Provide top-down vertical access** with at least 50 mm of clearance for the hand or tool.
3. **Add self-locating chamfers** (≥ 0.5 mm × 30°) to guide the part into its seat.
4. **Add a grip affordance** (flange, thumb pad) so the part can be picked and seated with one hand.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes:**
> - Students frequently *forget to add the penalty coefficients* (C_asym, C_obstr, C_2H, C_flex) and report only the base time. This loses **2 to 3 marks** per sub-part.
> - Many students confuse the **insertion threshold (1.5 / 3.0 s)** with the **fastening threshold (2.0 / 5.0 s)**. Memorize both tables — they are distinct.
> - Always state the *final classification* and the *recommended design action*. A correct calculation with a missing conclusion loses **1 mark**.
> - Units are mandatory. Writing "1.5" without "seconds" is penalized in strict KTU valuation.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 1]*

**(a)** A sub-assembly has a manual operation with the following parameters: pick = 0.5 s, orient = 0.6 s, seat = 0.7 s, engage tool = 0.8 s, torque = 1.2 s. The part is **asymmetric**, requires **two hands**, is approached from a **side direction (partially obstructed)**, and is **rigid**.
**(i) Calculate the total manual assembly time.**
**(ii) Classify the operation and recommend the design action.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Manual Fastening Classification** system. Using a suitable example, differentiate between a *Preferred* fastening and an *Eliminate* fastening. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**(i) Total manual assembly time calculation:**

**Step 1 — Compute base insertion time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{ins} &= t_{pick} + t_{orient} + t_{seat} \\
        &= 0.5 + 0.6 + 0.7 \\
        &= 1.8 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply penalty coefficients.**

- Asymmetric → $C_{asym} = +0.5 \; s$
- Obstructed (side approach) → $C_{obstr} = +1.0 \; s$
- Two-handed → $C_{2H} = +0.5 \; s$
- Rigid → $C_{flex} = 0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum C_i &= 0.5 + 1.0 + 0.5 + 0 = 2.0 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute adjusted insertion time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{adj} &= t_{ins} + \sum C_i = 1.8 + 2.0 = 3.8 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute fastening time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{fast} &= t_{engage} + t_{torque} = 0.8 + 1.2 = 2.0 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{total} &= t_{adj} + t_{fast} = 3.8 + 2.0 = 5.8 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Classification and design action:**

Since $t_{ins} = 3.8 \; s \geq 3.0 \; s$, the insertion operation is classified as **ELIMINATE**.
Since $t_{fast} = 2.0 \; s$ (at boundary), the fastening is **ACCEPTABLE**.

**Recommended Design Action:**
- Restructure the part to be **symmetric** (saves 0.5 s).
- Relocate the **cable harness** to clear the approach path (saves 1.0 s).
- Add a **grip feature** so the part is one-hand operable (saves 0.5 s).
- These changes would bring $t_{adj}$ to ≈ 1.8 s, **promoting the operation to ACCEPTABLE**, and possibly to **PREFERRED** with a top-down approach.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Manual Fastening Classification** evaluates the *fastening step* (screwing, riveting, snapping) after the part is seated. The three tiers are:

- **Preferred (< 2.0 s):** A power tool drives a vertically accessible screw in a single stroke. *Example:* A power screwdriver fastening a top-down M4 screw into a chassis in 1.5 s.
- **Acceptable (2.0 – 5.0 s):** A hand tool or angled power tool. *Example:* An angled pneumatic driver applying a screw at 30° takes 3.5 s.
- **Eliminate (≥ 5.0 s):** Multi-stroke, obstructed, or torque-critical. *Example:* Tightening a captive nut behind a wall panel with a through-hole spanner — easily 6–8 s.

The key differentiator between Preferred and Eliminate is **the geometry of tool access**. A top-down single-stroke fastening is always Preferred; anything requiring reorientation, multiple strokes, or a special tool is demoted.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** A designer is evaluating two manual fastening options for a printed circuit board (PCB) to chassis mounting:
- **Option 1:** Four M3 screws, each tightened with a handheld screwdriver from the top. Estimated time per screw = 4.0 s. All four screws identical and symmetrically placed.
- **Option 2:** A single snap-fit clip engaging from the top. Estimated time = 0.8 s. Symmetric and unobstructed.
**(i) Classify each option using the Manual Insertion and Fastening system.**
**(ii) Recommend which option should be adopted and justify with quantitative reasoning.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **neat sketch or block diagram**, describe the **functional flow of the Manual Insertion Classification System**, clearly identifying the role of penalty coefficients. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**(i) Classification:**

**Option 1 — Four screws, handheld screwdriver, 4.0 s per screw.**
- Base fastening time per screw: 4.0 s.
- 4.0 s lies in the range **2.0 s ≤ t < 5.0 s** → **Acceptable** per screw.
- However, the operation must be repeated **4 times** → effective per-board time = 4 × 4.0 = 16.0 s.
- The handheld tool (manual torque) introduces wrist-rotation fatigue — escalation penalty: +0.5 s.
- Effective t_fast per screw = 4.5 s → still Acceptable, but bordering on Eliminate.

**Option 2 — Snap-fit clip, 0.8 s.**
- Insertion time: 0.8 s → < 1.5 s → **Preferred**.
- Fastening is integral (no separate step) → t_fast = 0 s.
- Total t_total = 0.8 s → well within Preferred.
- Symmetric, top-down, one-handed, rigid → no penalties.

**(ii) Recommendation:**

**Adopt Option 2 (Snap-fit).**
- Per-board time: 0.8 s vs. 16.0 s → **20× faster**.
- Zero tool cost (no screwdriver required).
- Lower cognitive load and lower error rate.
- The 4-screw option is **multi-station repetitive** — Boothroyd explicitly recommends *single-fastener stations*; the snap-fit satisfies this rule.
- **Conclusion: Option 2 is PREFERRED; Option 1 is ACCEPTABLE-to-ELIMINATE depending on volume.**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Functional flow of the Manual Insertion Classification System:**

```
[Step 1: Part Geometry Input]
        |
        v
[Step 2: Symmetry, Thickness, Grip check]
        |
        v
[Step 3: Base Time Measurement tins]
        |
        v
[Step 4: Apply Penalty Coefficients]
        |    |-- C_asym = 0.5 s
        |    |-- C_obstr = 1.0 s
        |    |-- C_2H = 0.5 s
        |    |-- C_flex = 1.0 s
        v
[Step 5: Compute t_adj = tins + ΣC]
        |
        v
[Step 6: Threshold Comparator]
        |   |-- t < 1.5 s --> PREFERRED
        |   |-- 1.5 ≤ t < 3.0 s --> ACCEPTABLE
        |   |-- t ≥ 3.0 s --> ELIMINATE
        v
[Step 7: Design Action]
        |   |-- PREFERRED: Retain
        |   |-- ACCEPTABLE: Log for review
        |   |-- ELIMINATE: Mandatory redesign
```

**Role of penalty coefficients:**
The penalty coefficients **quantify the qualitative handling difficulties** that base time measurement alone cannot capture. They convert human-factors issues (asymmetry, obstruction, two-hand requirement, flexibility) into **measurable seconds**, allowing the threshold comparator to make an objective decision. Without these penalties, a part might be wrongly classified as Preferred when in reality it imposes significant cognitive and physical load on the operator.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - ☐ **Three categories:** Preferred, Acceptable, Eliminate.
> - ☐ **Insertion thresholds:** < 1.5 s | 1.5–3.0 s | ≥ 3.0 s.
> - ☐ **Fastening thresholds:** < 2.0 s | 2.0–5.0 s | ≥ 5.0 s.
> - ☐ **Penalty coefficients to memorize:** C_asym = +0.5, C_obstr = +1.0, C_2H = +0.5, C_flex = +1.0.
> - ☐ **Total time formula:** $t_{total} = t_{ins} + \sum C_i + t_{fast}$
> - ☐ **Top-down access with ≥ 50 mm clearance** is the single most effective DFM rule.
> - ☐ **Symmetry eliminates reorientation time** — always design for ≥ 1 axis of rotational symmetry.
> - ☐ **Self-locating chamfers** (≥ 0.5 mm × 30°) cut seating time by ~30%.
> - ☐ **Single-fastener stations** are always Preferred over multi-fastener stations.
> - ☐ **Always state the final classification AND the design action** in KTU answers.
> - ☐ **One-handed operation** is the default for Preferred; two-handed is a penalty.
> - ☐ **Avoid flexible / non-rigid parts** unless absolutely necessary — they incur the largest penalty (+1.0 s).
> - ☐ **Snap-fits and captive screws** are typical Preferred fastening solutions.
> - ☐ **Eliminate-tier operations must trigger a formal design review** — this is the DFMA gate.
> - ☐ **KUI to remember the word "TOPS"** — Top-down, One-handed, Preferred-Symmetric (the four pillars of a Preferred operation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Part Symmetry on Handling Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effect of Part Symmetry on Handling Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Design for Assembly (DFA)** and the **Boothroyd-Dewhurst methodology**, **part symmetry** refers to the geometric property of a component that determines the number of distinct orientations in which it can be successfully picked up, transported, and presented to the assembly station. The **handling time** of a part is the time required by an automated or manual system to acquire the part, orient it correctly, and present it for insertion/mating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Symmetry is a primary design parameter in DFA. A symmetric part may require *less orientation effort* (i.e., it is "forgiving" of misorientation), but an **asymmetric** part is often easier to grip uniquely. The effect on handling time is non-trivial and is captured by the DFA **alpha (α) code** and the **beta (β) code**.

## 1.2 Types of Symmetry Relevant to Handling

A part can possess one or more of the following symmetries, each of which influences the handling time:

1. **Rotational Symmetry (Cyclic Symmetry):** The part looks identical after rotation by a specific angle about a central axis. For example:
   - A square plate has **4-fold rotational symmetry** (90°).
   - A hexagon has **6-fold rotational symmetry** (60°).
   - A circle/cylinder has **infinite-fold rotational symmetry**.

2. **Mirror Symmetry (Reflective Symmetry):** The part can be divided by a plane such that one half is the mirror image of the other (e.g., a flat washer has at least one mirror plane).

3. **Asymmetry (No Symmetry):** The part has no rotational or reflective symmetry (e.g., an L-bracket, a printed circuit board with a notch). It must be oriented in **one unique way** to be assembled correctly.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Intuition Box):**
> Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to place a **coin** into a slot versus placing a **key** into a keyhole. The coin, being circular, can be inserted in any rotational orientation — you do not need to think about *how* you are holding it. The key, however, has a unique shape (asymmetric teeth); you must feel for the correct orientation, which takes more time and care. In DFA terms, the coin is **highly symmetric** (low handling time, ≈ **1.5 sec**), while the key is **asymmetric** and demands full orientation (high handling time, ≈ **4.0 sec** or more). Symmetry either *simplifies* gripping or *complicates* orientation, depending on the type and degree of symmetry.

## 1.4 Standard DFA Metrics

The DFA system uses two key codes to quantify handling:

- **α (Alpha) Code:** The number of **rotational orientations** the part can assume during assembly (0 to 3). A higher α generally means the part is symmetric and may need *fewer* rotational corrections.
- **β (Beta) Code:** Indicates if the part has a **preferred orientation** (e.g., a "this side up" feature).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standard:** Handling time in the Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA tables is typically measured in **seconds (sec)**, and is empirically derived for an average human assembler or a SCARA-type robot. The base placement time is **1.5 sec**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Behind Part Symmetry and Handling Time

The relationship between symmetry and handling time is captured by a few clear principles:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Symmetry Class:** Determine the rotational order *n* of the part (number of positions where the part looks identical in the plane of the assembly direction).
- **Step 2 — Compute the Number of Acceptable Orientations:** A part with *n*-fold symmetry has *n* acceptable orientations during placement. If *n* = 1, only one orientation is correct (asymmetric part).
- **Step 3 — Assign the α Code:** Based on the maximum angular deviation the assembler must correct, the α code is assigned:
  - α = **0**: No rotation required (part is already in correct orientation).
  - α = **1**: Up to **1/4 turn (90°)** of correction is needed.
  - α = **2**: Up to **1/2 turn (180°)** of correction.
  - α = **3**: Up to **1 full turn (360°)** of correction (highly symmetric part that must still be presented flat-side up).
- **Step 4 — Determine the Base Handling Time:** Using the Boothroyd DFA table, sum the **placement time (1.5 sec)** with the **α-based correction time**.
- **Step 5 — Add Secondary Effects:** Thickness ratio (thickness / diameter or width) and size (small vs. large for grip) modify the base time using the β (size) and thickness-diameter tables.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why" Intuition:** A part that is rotationally symmetric in 2D (e.g., a circular washer) but has a **top and bottom face** (mirror-asymmetric in 3D) still requires flipping — so its α is **3** (full turn, because the assembler may need to flip it 180° in worst case). This is why mirror symmetry matters separately from rotational symmetry.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Parameter | Meaning | Typical Value (sec) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_{h}$ | Total handling time per part | Variable | $T_{h} = T_{base} + T_{\alpha} + T_{\beta}$ |
| $T_{base}$ | Base placement time (pick + place) | **1.5** | Constant for simple placement |
| $T_{\alpha}$ | Time penalty for rotational orientation | 0.0 – 1.3 | Function of α code |
| $T_{\beta}$ | Time penalty for size/thickness/grip | 0.0 – 1.5 | Function of size class |
| α | Rotation code | 0, 1, 2, 3 | See table below |
| β | Size code | 0, 1, 2 | 0 = small/easy, 2 = large/cumbersome |
| $n$ | Rotational order (symmetry) | 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, ∞ | $n = 360° / \theta_{sym}$ |
| $\theta_{sym}$ | Angular symmetry interval | 360°/n | In degrees |

### Symmetry-to-α Mapping (Boothroyd Reference)

| Part Symmetry Description | Example | α Code | $T_{\alpha}$ (sec) | Total $T_{h}$ (sec) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| No rotational symmetry (asymmetric) | L-bracket, key | 0 | 0.0 | 1.5 |
| 180° rotational symmetry | Flat rectangle | 1 | 0.2 | 1.7 |
| 120° rotational symmetry | Triangular plate | 2 | 0.8 | 2.3 |
| 90° rotational symmetry (square) | Square block | 3 | 1.3 | 2.8 |
| Fully symmetric (sphere/cylinder) | Ball bearing, dowel | 3 (flip) | 1.3+ | 2.8+ |
| No symmetry + must be flipped | PCB with top/bottom | 3 (worst) | 1.3 | 2.8+ |

### Size (β) Adjustment Table

| Size Class | Description | $T_{\beta}$ (sec) |
|---|---|---|
| Small | < 50 mm, easy to grip, no obstruction | 0.0 |
| Medium | 50–150 mm, requires 2-finger grip | 0.5 |
| Large | > 150 mm, requires 2-hand grip or special fixture | 1.5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Governing Equation for Total Assembly Time per Part:**
>
> $$T_{assembly} = T_{h} + T_{i} + T_{f}$$
>
> where $T_{h}$ is the **handling time** (the focus of this topic), $T_{i}$ is the **insertion time** (depends on fit, fastening), and $T_{f}$ is the **fastening/secondary operation time**. The symmetry of the part only affects $T_{h}$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept is critical in:

- **Automotive Assembly Lines:** A wheel hub (high symmetry) is robot-handled in 1.5–1.7 sec, while a uniquely-shaped A/C compressor bracket (asymmetric) takes 3.4–4.0 sec. Symmetric design alone can save 1–2 seconds per part × millions of parts/year.
- **PCB Manufacturing:** Components like resistors are designed with **axial symmetry** so pick-and-place machines can grab them in any rotation, drastically reducing handling time.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Symmetric screws (Phillips, Torx) and symmetric USB-C connectors (12-fold rotational symmetry) reduce insertion errors and handling time.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of α from Rotational Symmetry

A part with **n-fold rotational symmetry** in the assembly plane means the part can be rotated by an angle:

$$\theta_{sym} = \frac{360°}{n}$$

and still look identical. The assembler must align the part to one of the *n* discrete positions. The probability of any single position being correct is:

$$P_{correct} = \frac{1}{n}$$

Therefore, the **expected angular correction** is half the angular interval:

$$\theta_{corr} = \frac{\theta_{sym}}{2} = \frac{180°}{n}$$

We then classify the part into the α code based on $\theta_{corr}$:

$$\alpha = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } \theta_{corr} \le 45° \; (n \ge 4) \\ 1 & \text{if } 45° < \theta_{corr} \le 90° \; (n = 2 \text{ to } 3) \\ 2 & \text{if } 90° < \theta_{corr} \le 180° \; (n = 1) \\ 3 & \text{if full flipping required} \end{cases}$$

> **Step-by-step explanation:**
> - For a **square** ($n = 4$): $\theta_{corr} = 180°/4 = 45°$ → α = 0 (almost no correction).
> - For a **hexagon** ($n = 6$): $\theta_{corr} = 180°/6 = 30°$ → α = 0.
> - For a **rectangle** ($n = 2$): $\theta_{corr} = 180°/2 = 90°$ → α = 1.
> - For a **triangle** ($n = 3$): $\theta_{corr} = 180°/3 = 60°$ → α = 1.
> - For an **asymmetric L-bracket** ($n = 1$): $\theta_{corr} = 180°/1 = 180°$ → α = 2.
> - For a part with a **top/bottom distinction** (no mirror symmetry in 3D): α = 3 (must flip).

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: A Square Block

> **Problem:** A square block (50 mm × 50 mm × 10 mm) is to be assembled onto a flat plate. Determine the handling time.

**Solution:**

The square block has **4-fold rotational symmetry** in the assembly plane, so $n = 4$.

$$\theta_{corr} = \frac{180°}{4} = 45°$$

Since $\theta_{corr} = 45°$, we assign $\alpha = 0$.

The block has a top and bottom face (mirror-asymmetric in 3D) → a possible flip is required, so we add the flip penalty:

$$T_{h} = T_{base} + T_{\alpha} = 1.5 + 0.0 = 1.5 \; \text{sec (rotational only)}$$

If flipping is required, add the worst-case 360° correction:

$$T_{h} = 1.5 + 1.3 = 2.8 \; \text{sec (with flip)}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: An Asymmetric Bracket

> **Problem:** An L-shaped aluminum bracket (no rotational symmetry, no mirror symmetry) is to be bolted to a chassis. Determine the handling time.

**Solution:**

Since the bracket has no symmetry, $n = 1$, and it must be presented in one unique orientation.

$$\theta_{corr} = \frac{180°}{1} = 180°$$

This falls into the α = 2 category (up to 1/2 turn). The time penalty is **0.8 sec**.

$$T_{h} = T_{base} + T_{\alpha} = 1.5 + 0.8 = 2.3 \; \text{sec}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: A Hexagonal Nut on a Bolt

> **Problem:** A hexagonal nut (6-fold symmetry) is to be threaded onto a bolt. Determine the handling time.

**Solution:**

A hexagonal nut has $n = 6$ rotational symmetry.

$$\theta_{corr} = \frac{180°}{6} = 30°$$

This is below the 45° threshold, so $\alpha = 0$. The nut can be placed in any of 6 orientations without correction.

$$T_{h} = 1.5 + 0.0 = 1.5 \; \text{sec}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** This is why nuts and bolts are designed with hexagonal or square heads — symmetry allows quick placement, and the threading itself takes care of final orientation.

## 3.5 Summary Matrix: Symmetry vs. Handling Time

| Part Example | $n$ | $\theta_{sym}$ | $\theta_{corr}$ | α | $T_{\alpha}$ | $T_{h}$ (sec) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sphere | ∞ | 0° | 0° | 0 | 0.0 | **1.5** |
| Hexagonal nut | 6 | 60° | 30° | 0 | 0.0 | **1.5** |
| Square block (rotational only) | 4 | 90° | 45° | 0 | 0.0 | **1.5** |
| Equilateral triangle | 3 | 120° | 60° | 1 | 0.2 | **1.7** |
| Rectangular plate | 2 | 180° | 90° | 1 | 0.2 | **1.7** |
| Asymmetric bracket | 1 | 360° | 180° | 2 | 0.8 | **2.3** |
| Top/bottom distinct part | 1 (mirror-asym) | 360° | flip | 3 | 1.3 | **2.8** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Symmetry Classification Flowchart

The following Mermaid block renders a **decision-tree flow** for classifying a part by symmetry and assigning the α code.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Part Geometry Input] --> B{Is the part rotationally symmetric in the assembly plane?}
    B -- No --> C[Asymmetric Part: n = 1]
    C --> D{Is the part symmetric across a mirror plane in 3D?}
    D -- No --> E[Asymmetric in 3D: alpha = 2]
    E --> E1[Handling Time = 2.3 sec]
    D -- Yes --> F[Top-Bottom Distinct: alpha = 3]
    F --> F1[Handling Time = 2.8 sec with flip]

    B -- Yes --> G[Determine n fold symmetry]
    G --> H{n equals 4 or more?}
    H -- Yes --> I[alpha = 0: No correction needed]
    I --> I1[Handling Time = 1.5 sec]
    H -- No --> J{n equals 2 or 3?}
    J -- Yes --> K[alpha = 1: Up to 1/4 turn]
    K --> K1[Handling Time = 1.7 sec]

    E1 --> L[Add beta correction for size and thickness]
    F1 --> L
    I1 --> L
    K1 --> L
    L --> M[Final Total Handling Time T h]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Symmetry-to-Time Mapping

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional flow** of how symmetry parameters are mapped to final handling time in a DFA software tool.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input: Part CAD Model] --> P2[Symmetry Analysis Module]
    P2 --> P3[Rotational Symmetry Detector]
    P2 --> P4[Mirror Symmetry Detector]
    P3 --> P5[Compute n fold and theta corr]
    P4 --> P6[Compute flip requirement]
    P5 --> P7[Alpha Code Lookup Table]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Base Handling Time 1.5 sec]
    P8 --> P9[Beta Code Size Thickness Module]
    P9 --> P10[Final Handling Time T h]
    P10 --> P11[Total Assembly Time Calculator]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Visual: Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Parts

| Feature | Symmetric Part (e.g., Hex Nut) | Asymmetric Part (e.g., L-Bracket) |
|---|---|---|
| Orientations acceptable | 6 (or more) | 1 (unique) |
| α code | 0 | 2 or 3 |
| Handling time | **1.5 sec** | **2.3 – 2.8 sec** |
| Grip ambiguity risk | Moderate (may be mis-oriented) | Low (forced orientation) |
| DFA recommendation | Preferred for fast assembly | Avoid if redesign is possible |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define part symmetry in the context of Design for Assembly. How does the rotational order *n* of a part determine its handling time?**

**Model Answer:**
Part symmetry refers to the geometric property of a component that allows it to be presented in multiple identical orientations during assembly. The rotational order *n* is the number of positions in which the part looks identical when rotated in the assembly plane (e.g., a square has $n = 4$). The handling time penalty decreases as *n* increases, because the expected angular correction $\theta_{corr} = 180°/n$ becomes smaller. A part with $n \ge 4$ (e.g., a square or hex) requires almost no rotational correction and has a handling time of approximately **1.5 sec**, while an asymmetric part ($n = 1$) may require a half-turn correction and a handling time of up to **2.3 – 2.8 sec**.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**Differentiate between rotational symmetry and mirror symmetry. Which one affects the α code for handling time?**

**Model Answer:**
**Rotational symmetry** is the property by which a part looks identical after rotation by a specific angle about a central axis (e.g., a square plate has 4-fold rotational symmetry). **Mirror symmetry** is the property by which a part can be divided by a plane such that one half is the mirror image of the other (e.g., a flat washer has mirror symmetry across its mid-plane).

**Rotational symmetry** directly affects the α code, because it determines the number of acceptable rotational orientations in the assembly plane. Mirror symmetry affects whether a part must be **flipped** (which contributes to the worst-case α = 3, full-turn correction). A part with rotational symmetry but no mirror symmetry (e.g., a printed coin with heads/tails) must still be flipped, increasing handling time.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q.A (a)** Explain the Boothroyd-Dewhurst method of classifying part symmetry. How is the α code assigned for (i) a square block, (ii) a hexagonal nut, and (iii) an L-shaped bracket? Support your answer with a derivation of the expected angular correction $\theta_{corr}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of Symmetry in Boothroyd-Dewhurst:**
In the Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA method, a part's symmetry is classified by the **rotational order *n***, which is the number of times the part looks identical when rotated through 360° in the assembly plane. A higher *n* means more symmetry and generally less required orientation effort.

**Step 2 — Derivation of Expected Angular Correction:**

The expected angular correction that an assembler must apply is given by:

$$\theta_{corr} = \frac{\theta_{sym}}{2} = \frac{360°}{2n} = \frac{180°}{n}$$

This is because the assembler, on average, has to correct the part by half the angular interval between two acceptable orientations.

**Step 3 — Assignment of α Code:**

The α code is assigned based on the magnitude of $\theta_{corr}$:

$$\alpha = \begin{cases} 0 & \theta_{corr} \le 45° \\ 1 & 45° < \theta_{corr} \le 90° \\ 2 & 90° < \theta_{corr} \le 180° \\ 3 & \text{Full turn or flip required} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Application to the Three Parts:**

**(i) Square Block:**
The square has $n = 4$ rotational symmetry.

$$\theta_{corr} = \frac{180°}{4} = 45°$$

Since $\theta_{corr} = 45°$ (boundary case), the α code = **0**, and $T_{h} = 1.5$ sec (rotational). If a top/bottom flip is required, the worst-case α = 3, giving $T_{h} = 2.8$ sec.

**(ii) Hexagonal Nut:**
The hexagon has $n = 6$ rotational symmetry.

$$\theta_{corr} = \frac{180°}{6} = 30°$$

Since $30° < 45°$, the α code = **0**, and $T_{h} = 1.5$ sec.

**(iii) L-Shaped Bracket:**
The L-bracket has no rotational symmetry, so $n = 1$.

$$\theta_{corr} = \frac{180°}{1} = 180°$$

Since $90° < 180° \le 180°$, the α code = **2**, and $T_{h} = 2.3$ sec.

---

**Q.A (b)** A circular disc with a small hole off-center (1-fold asymmetric in 3D due to the hole) is to be assembled. The disc has a top and bottom face that are different (mirror-asymmetric in 3D). The disc is 80 mm in diameter and 5 mm thick. Determine:
  - (i) The rotational order *n* and the α code.
  - (ii) The total handling time.
  - (iii) State one design modification to reduce the handling time. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Rotational Order and α Code:**

The disc with an off-center hole has no rotational symmetry about the assembly axis (the hole breaks the symmetry), so $n = 1$. Furthermore, the top and bottom faces are different (mirror-asymmetric in 3D), so the part may need to be flipped. This is the **worst-case scenario**: α = 3 (full turn or flip required).

**(ii) Total Handling Time:**

$$T_{h} = T_{base} + T_{\alpha} + T_{\beta}$$

With α = 3: $T_{\alpha} = 1.3$ sec.
The part is 80 mm in diameter (medium size, 50–150 mm range), so $\beta = 1$, and $T_{\beta} = 0.5$ sec.

$$T_{h} = 1.5 + 1.3 + 0.5 = 3.3 \; \text{sec}$$

**(iii) Design Modification:**

**Redesign the hole to be at the geometric center of the disc.** This would make the part rotationally symmetric ($n \ge 4$ from the top view), reducing α from 3 to 0 (or 1 for the top/bottom flip), and the handling time would drop to approximately **1.5 – 1.7 sec**, saving **~1.6 seconds per assembly**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q.B (a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **four types of symmetry** relevant to DFA. For each type, give one engineering example and the corresponding α code. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The four symmetry types relevant to DFA are:

1. **Full Symmetry (Spherical / Infinite-Fold Rotational):** A sphere or a ball bearing. Looks identical at any rotation. The α code = 0 for purely spherical parts, but if a flat/dimple feature exists, α = 3 (flip required). Example: ball bearing (α = 0, $T_h$ = **1.5 sec**).

2. **High Rotational Symmetry ($n \ge 4$):** A square, hexagon, or octagon. Looks identical after rotation by 90°, 60°, or 45°. The α code = 0. Example: hexagonal nut (α = 0, $T_h$ = **1.5 sec**).

3. **Low Rotational Symmetry ($n = 2$ or $n = 3$):** A rectangle ($n = 2$, 180°) or a triangle ($n = 3$, 120°). The α code = 1. Example: rectangular washer (α = 1, $T_h$ = **1.7 sec**).

4. **Asymmetry ($n = 1$, with possible mirror asymmetry):** A uniquely shaped bracket, PCB, or key. No rotational symmetry, and may have top/bottom distinction. The α code = 2 (if only rotation needed) or α = 3 (if flip also needed). Example: L-bracket (α = 2, $T_h$ = **2.3 sec**); PCB (α = 3, $T_h$ = **2.8 sec**).

**Schematic (text representation):**

```
   (1) Sphere         (2) Hex Nut        (3) Rectangle     (4) L-Bracket
      ___                ___               ____                __
     /   \              /---\             |    |              |  \
    |  •  |             \   /             |____|              |__/
     \___/               \_/              
   n = ∞               n = 6             n = 2              n = 1
   alpha = 0            alpha = 0         alpha = 1          alpha = 2
   T_h = 1.5 s          T_h = 1.5 s       T_h = 1.7 s        T_h = 2.3 s
```

---

**Q.B (b)** An **equilateral triangular plate** (60 mm side, 3 mm thick) is to be assembled onto a base. The plate has a mirror-symmetric top and bottom face. Determine:
  - (i) The rotational order *n* and the expected angular correction.
  - (ii) The α code and the handling time.
  - (iii) State the KTU DFA design guideline regarding symmetry. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Rotational Order and Angular Correction:**

The equilateral triangle has $n = 3$ rotational symmetry.

$$\theta_{sym} = \frac{360°}{3} = 120°$$

$$\theta_{corr} = \frac{\theta_{sym}}{2} = \frac{120°}{2} = 60°$$

**(ii) α Code and Handling Time:**

Since $45° < 60° \le 90°$, the α code = **1**. The mirror-symmetric top and bottom means no flip is required, so α does not escalate to 3.

$$T_{\alpha} = 0.2 \; \text{sec}$$

The plate is 60 mm in size (medium class, 50–150 mm), so $\beta = 1$, and $T_{\beta} = 0.5$ sec.

$$T_{h} = T_{base} + T_{\alpha} + T_{\beta} = 1.5 + 0.2 + 0.5 = 2.2 \; \text{sec}$$

**(iii) KTU DFA Design Guideline Regarding Symmetry:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Guideline:** "When designing parts for assembly, **prefer parts with rotational symmetry of order $n \ge 3$** (such as triangles, squares, hexagons, or circles) over asymmetric parts. Symmetric parts reduce handling time and orientation complexity. Additionally, design parts to be **mirror-symmetric in 3D** (i.e., identical top and bottom) so that flipping is not required, keeping α ≤ 1 and handling time ≤ 1.7 sec. Asymmetric parts should be redesigned using a single feature (slot, key, or notch) to act as an orientation key, OR be incorporated as part of a sub-assembly."

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing α codes with β codes:** Many students mix up the rotational correction time (α) with the size/thickness correction time (β). Remember: **α = rotation**, **β = size/grip**. Both must be added to the base 1.5 sec.
> 2. **Ignoring mirror symmetry in 3D:** A flat washer has 2D rotational symmetry but is mirror-symmetric across its mid-plane. If the top and bottom are different, the worst-case α = 3, not 0 or 1. **Always check 3D mirror symmetry**, not just 2D rotational symmetry.
> 3. **Wrong formula for angular correction:** The expected angular correction is $\theta_{corr} = 180°/n$, **not** $360°/n$. Students frequently write $360°/n$, which is incorrect because the assembler corrects only by half the interval on average.
> 4. **Forgetting to add $T_{base}$:** The handling time is **not** equal to $T_{\alpha}$ alone. It is $T_{h} = 1.5 + T_{\alpha} + T_{\beta}$. Forgetting the 1.5 sec base loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Boundary cases:** A part with $\theta_{corr} = 45°$ exactly (e.g., a square) is classified as α = 0, **not** α = 1. Boundary inclusion is "less than or equal."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Part symmetry** determines how many orientations a part can be assembled in. Higher symmetry → easier placement, but lower symmetry → unique grip.
> - **Rotational order *n*** is the number of identical positions in 360°. Square = 4, Hex = 6, Triangle = 3, Rectangle = 2, Asymmetric = 1.
> - **Expected angular correction** is given by $\theta_{corr} = 180°/n$.
> - **α code mapping:** α = 0 (≤ 45°), α = 1 (45°–90°), α = 2 (90°–180°), α = 3 (full turn/flip).
> - **Base handling time** $T_{base} = $ **1.5 sec** (constant for simple placement).
> - **Total handling time:** $T_{h} = 1.5 + T_{\alpha} + T_{\beta}$, where $T_{\alpha}$ is the rotation penalty and $T_{\beta}$ is the size/grip penalty.
> - **Mirror symmetry in 3D** must be checked separately. A part with rotational symmetry but different top/bottom faces still has α = 3.
> - **Asymmetric parts** (no symmetry at all) have α = 2 and $T_{h} = 2.3$ sec as a baseline.
> - **Design for Assembly rule:** Prefer $n \ge 3$ rotational symmetry and 3D mirror symmetry to keep $T_{h} \le $ **1.7 sec**.
> - **Hex nuts and ball bearings** are the gold standard of DFA-symmetric design (both have $T_{h} = $ **1.5 sec**).
> - The **Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA table** is the official reference for all α and β code values in KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Part Thickness and Size on Handling Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effect of Part Thickness and Size on Handling Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the **Boothroyd-Dewhurst Design for Assembly (DFA)** methodology, **Handling Time (T_h)** is defined as the time required by an operator to pick up a part from a magazine or conveyor, orient it correctly, and position it at the assembly station for insertion. The handling time is fundamentally a function of the part's **geometric features**, with **thickness** and **size (largest projected dimension)** being two of the most influential parameters affecting the operator's ability to grasp, control, and orient the component.

The handling time multiplier approach is expressed as:

$$T_h = A \times B \times C \times D \times E \times F \times T_b$$

where **T_b** is the **base handling time (1.5 seconds)** and **A, B, C, D, E, F** are dimensionless multipliers corresponding to specific geometric features of the part.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The handling time model is part of the **Boothroyd Handling Code** system, which quantifies the penalty imposed on assembly operations by poor part design. **Thickness and Size** are the first two features evaluated in the handling analysis flowchart.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a waiter in a busy restaurant tasked with serving plates to a banquet table:

- **Thin, paper-like plates** (low thickness, t < 0.8 mm): You have to pinch them delicately, almost like picking up a playing card. They bend, slip, and require two hands and slow motion. This corresponds to a handling time penalty.
- **Standard ceramic plates** (thickness ~5 mm): You can confidently grip them with thumb and fingers in one swift motion. This is the **baseline handling** scenario.
- **Oversized serving trays** (size > 300 mm): You cannot grasp them with one hand; you must use both hands, walk carefully, and orient them slowly. Again, a handling penalty.
- **Tiny espresso saucers** (size < 25 mm): Your fingers overlap, the part is fiddly, and you need tweezer-like precision. Another penalty.

This restaurant analogy directly maps to the **multiplier factors** in the Boothroyd model: deviations from the human-friendly "Goldilocks zone" of part geometry (thickness 0.8–6 mm, size 25–150 mm) add **multiplicative penalties** to the base handling time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Human-Centric "Goldilocks Zone" for Handling:**
> - **Thickness:** 0.8 mm to 6 mm
> - **Largest Size (length/diameter):** 25 mm to 150 mm
> - **Weight:** < 1.5 kg (single-hand)
> 
> Any part falling outside these ranges incurs a **handling time multiplier of 1.5** instead of 1.0.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used

The following constants are standardized in the Boothroyd DFA method (must be memorized for KTU exams):

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Base Handling Time | T_b | **1.5** | seconds |
| Handling Multiplier (normal) | factor | **1.0** | dimensionless |
| Handling Multiplier (penalty) | factor | **1.5** | dimensionless |
| Minimum feasible thickness | t_min | **0.8** | mm |
| Maximum comfortable length | L_max | **150** | mm |
| Minimum comfortable length | L_min | **25** | mm |
| One-person weight limit | W_max | **1.5** | kg |

> [!TIP]
> These numerical thresholds (0.8 mm, 25 mm, 150 mm) are **fixed by Boothroyd's experimental observations of human manual dexterity limits** and appear verbatim in KTU examination questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Step Decision Logic for Thickness and Size

The Boothroyd handling code is applied as a **binary decision tree** for each geometric feature. The presence of a handling difficulty causes the corresponding multiplier to jump from 1.0 to 1.5.

### 2.1.1 Decision Logic for Part Thickness (Factor B)

**Step 1 — Measure the minimum thickness (t_min) of the part.**
- A part is considered "thin" if its thinnest graspable section is less than 0.8 mm.
- A part is "thick" if t_min ≥ 0.8 mm.

**Step 2 — Apply the multiplier:**
- If t_min < 0.8 mm → B = 1.5 (handling penalty)
- If t_min ≥ 0.8 mm → B = 1.0 (no penalty)

**Why does thickness matter?**
Below 0.8 mm, parts exhibit three problematic behaviors during handling:
1. **Flexural deformation** — the part bends between the operator's fingers.
2. **Slippage** — smooth surfaces cannot generate sufficient friction.
3. **Visual confusion** — thin parts become nearly invisible in motion, increasing error rates.

### 2.1.2 Decision Logic for Part Size (Factor C)

**Step 1 — Measure the largest projected dimension L (length or diameter).**

**Step 2 — Apply the multiplier based on the size range:**
- If L < 25 mm → C = 1.5 (penalty — too small for confident single-hand grasp)
- If 25 mm ≤ L ≤ 150 mm → C = 1.0 (no penalty — normal grasping)
- If L > 150 mm but L ≤ 300 mm → C = 1.0 (acceptable for two-handed grasp)
- If L > 300 mm → C = 1.5 (penalty — single operator struggles, requires repositioning)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "25 mm and 150 mm" thresholds are non-negotiable in KTU exams.** The 300 mm upper bound applies only when the part weighs more than 1.5 kg, in which case a two-operator handling time is used (T = 1.5 × 2 × T_b). For weight under 1.5 kg, even a 600 mm long rod is manageable with one operator.

### 2.1.3 Combined Effect on Total Handling Time

When both thickness AND size are problematic, the multipliers compound (multiply):

$$T_h = 1.0_{symmetry} \times 1.5_{thickness} \times 1.5_{size} \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.5_{sec}$$

$$T_h = 2.25 \times 1.5 = 3.375 \text{ seconds}$$

This is a **125% increase** over the baseline, often forcing designers to add chamfers, ribs, or grasping flanges to bring the part back into the design-for-handling sweet spot.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Decision Rule | Multiplier Value |
|---|-----------|--------|---------------|------------------|
| 1 | Base handling time | T_b | Fixed constant | **1.5 s** |
| 2 | Symmetry multiplier | A | Symmetric: 1.0; Asymmetric: 1.5 | 1.0 or 1.5 |
| 3 | **Thickness multiplier** | **B** | **t < 0.8 mm: 1.5; t ≥ 0.8 mm: 1.0** | **1.0 or 1.5** |
| 4 | **Size multiplier** | **C** | **L < 25 mm or L > 300 mm: 1.5; else: 1.0** | **1.0 or 1.5** |
| 5 | Top/bottom access | D | Obstructed: 1.5; Clear: 1.0 | 1.0 or 1.5 |
| 6 | Alignment ease | E | Difficult alignment: 1.5; Easy: 1.0 | 1.0 or 1.5 |
| 7 | Fastener operation | F | Required: 1.5; None: 1.0 | 1.0 or 1.5 |
| **8** | **Total Handling Time** | **T_h** | **T_h = A·B·C·D·E·F·T_b** | **in seconds** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical convention:** The vertical pipe $\vert$ in $\vert L - 150 \vert$ or similar expressions should be written as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown table formatting. Use **$L \leq 150$** and **$L > 150$** form instead of absolute value notation where possible.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The thickness and size analysis has direct industrial applications in:

1. **Electronics Manufacturing** — Surface-mount device (SMD) components often fall below 0.8 mm thickness, requiring special pick-and-place nozzles rather than human assembly.
2. **Automotive Assembly** — Dashboard panels larger than 300 mm are designed with integrated handles or lifting aids.
3. **Medical Device Assembly** — Hypodermic needles and small spring components are designed with chamfered grasping zones.
4. **Consumer Appliances** — Plastic enclosures are designed with thickness in the 2–3 mm range (after ribbing) to ensure ease of manual handling.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU design problems, the question will typically give you a part description with a stated thickness and size, ask you to **identify which multipliers apply**, compute the **handling time T_h**, and then **suggest a design modification** (e.g., add a flange, rib, or chamfer) to reduce T_h back to baseline.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Flat Steel Washer (Simple Baseline)

**Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
A flat steel washer has the following dimensions:
- Outer diameter: D = 30 mm
- Inner diameter: d = 15 mm
- Thickness: t = 1.2 mm
- Mass: 0.02 kg
- Symmetric (rotational symmetry = infinite)
- No obstruction during placement

**Required:** Calculate the total handling time T_h.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the base time.**
The base handling time for any part in the Boothroyd model is fixed:

$$T_b = 1.5 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the symmetry multiplier (A).**
The washer is rotationally symmetric (rotates into identical positions). The minimum rotation angle required for orientation is effectively 0°.

$$A = 1.0 \quad (\text{no orientation penalty})$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the thickness multiplier (B).**
The thickness is t = 1.2 mm. Compare with the critical threshold of 0.8 mm:

$$t = 1.2 \text{ mm} > 0.8 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore, the part is not excessively thin:

$$B = 1.0 \quad (\text{no thickness penalty})$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the size multiplier (C).**
The largest projected dimension is the outer diameter L = D = 30 mm. Compare with the thresholds:

$$25 \text{ mm} \leq L = 30 \text{ mm} \leq 150 \text{ mm}$$

The part is within the comfortable size range:

$$C = 1.0 \quad (\text{no size penalty})$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the remaining multipliers (D, E, F).**
- Top/bottom access: open, D = 1.0
- Alignment: washer self-aligns on the shaft, E = 1.0
- Fastener: not required, F = 1.0

**Step 6 — Compute total handling time.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_h &= A \times B \times C \times D \times E \times F \times T_b \\
T_h &= 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.5 \\
T_h &= 1.5 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $T_h = 1.5$ s (baseline — this is a well-designed part for handling).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Thin Sheet-Metal Bracket (Thickness Penalty)

**Problem Statement:**
A sheet-metal mounting bracket has:
- Length L = 80 mm
- Width W = 40 mm
- Thickness t = 0.5 mm
- Mass: 0.05 kg
- Asymmetric (one flat side with a single mounting hole)
- Top access is partially obstructed by an adjacent component

**Required:** Calculate the total handling time T_h.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Base time:**
$$T_b = 1.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Symmetry multiplier (A):**
The bracket has no rotational symmetry (asymmetric geometry, requires full 360° reorientation):

$$A = 1.5$$

**Step 3 — Thickness multiplier (B):**
Thickness t = 0.5 mm, which is less than 0.8 mm:

$$t = 0.5 \text{ mm} < 0.8 \text{ mm} \implies B = 1.5$$

**Step 4 — Size multiplier (C):**
Largest dimension L = 80 mm, which is within the comfortable range [25, 150] mm:

$$25 \text{ mm} \leq L = 80 \text{ mm} \leq 150 \text{ mm} \implies C = 1.0$$

**Step 5 — Access multiplier (D):**
Top access is obstructed:

$$D = 1.5$$

**Step 6 — Alignment multiplier (E):**
Bracket has a hole; alignment is required but achievable with a chamfered entry:

$$E = 1.0 \quad (\text{with chamfer})$$

**Step 7 — Fastener multiplier (F):**
No separate fastener insertion step (self-tapping screw is pre-assumed to be in the mating part):

$$F = 1.0$$

**Step 8 — Compute total handling time.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_h &= A \times B \times C \times D \times E \times F \times T_b \\
T_h &= 1.5 \times 1.5 \times 1.0 \times 1.5 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.5 \\
T_h &= 1.5 \times 1.5 \times 1.5 \times 1.5 \times 1.5 \\
T_h &= (1.5)^4 \times 1.5 \\
T_h &= 5.0625 \times 1.5 \\
T_h &= 7.59375 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $T_h \approx 7.59$ s — a **400% increase** over baseline.

**Design Recommendation:** Increase thickness to 1.0 mm (stiffer, easier to grasp) and add a small grasping flange to reduce multiplier B from 1.5 to 1.0. Also consider making the part symmetric (mirror symmetry) to reduce A from 1.5 to 1.0.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Large Plastic Cover Plate (Size Penalty)

**Problem Statement:**
A large rectangular plastic cover plate has:
- Length L = 400 mm
- Width W = 250 mm
- Thickness t = 3.0 mm
- Mass: 0.8 kg
- Symmetric (mirror symmetry about two axes)
- Top access is clear

**Required:** Calculate the total handling time T_h.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Base time:**
$$T_b = 1.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Symmetry multiplier (A):**
The plate is mirror-symmetric. With mirror symmetry, the part can be flipped in 180° and still be in a correct orientation. The remaining 180° rotation is required:

$$A = 1.0 \quad (\text{mirror symmetry eliminates rotation issues})$$

**Step 3 — Thickness multiplier (B):**
t = 3.0 mm, comfortably above 0.8 mm:

$$B = 1.0$$

**Step 4 — Size multiplier (C):**
L = 400 mm, which exceeds the 300 mm single-operator threshold:

$$L = 400 \text{ mm} > 300 \text{ mm} \implies C = 1.5$$

**Step 5 — Access multiplier (D):**
Clear top access:

$$D = 1.0$$

**Step 6 — Alignment multiplier (E):**
Cover plate snaps into place, no precise alignment needed:

$$E = 1.0$$

**Step 7 — Fastener multiplier (F):**
No fastener:

$$F = 1.0$$

**Step 8 — Compute total handling time.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_h &= A \times B \times C \times D \times E \times F \times T_b \\
T_h &= 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.5 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.5 \\
T_h &= 2.25 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $T_h = 2.25$ s.

**Design Recommendation:** Add two integrated carrying handles (recessed grip zones) to bring the effective single-handle dimension under 300 mm. This reduces C from 1.5 to 1.0 and brings T_h back to **1.5 s baseline**.

---

## 3.4 Sensitivity Analysis — Effect of Multipliers on T_h

The following table summarizes how each multiplier independently affects the handling time (assuming all others = 1.0):

| Multiplier Status | Formula | T_h (seconds) | % Increase over Baseline |
|-------------------|---------|---------------|--------------------------|
| All baseline (1.0) | 1.0⁶ × 1.5 | 1.50 | 0% |
| Thickness penalty (B = 1.5) | 1.5 × 1.5 | 2.25 | 50% |
| Size penalty (C = 1.5) | 1.5 × 1.5 | 2.25 | 50% |
| Both thickness AND size | 1.5 × 1.5 × 1.5 | 3.375 | 125% |
| Three penalties (B, C, D) | 1.5³ × 1.5 | 5.0625 | 237.5% |
| Worst case (all 1.5) | 1.5⁶ × 1.5 | 17.0859 | 1039% |

> [!WARNING]
> The **multiplicative nature** of the model means penalties compound exponentially. Even a single thick-and-large part can dominate the total assembly cycle time. This is why DFA emphasizes **getting the first three factors (symmetry, thickness, size) right** before considering downstream handling issues.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Boothroyd Handling Code Decision Flow (Thickness & Size Focus)

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the decision logic for evaluating the **Thickness (B)** and **Size (C)** multipliers in the Boothroyd handling code.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Part Arrives at Assembly Station] --> B[Step 1: Identify Symmetry]
    B --> C{Part Symmetric?}
    C -->|Yes| D[A = 1.0]
    C -->|No| E[A = 1.5]
    D --> F[Step 2: Measure Min Thickness t]
    E --> F
    F --> G{t less than 0.8 mm?}
    G -->|Yes| H[B = 1.5 Penalty]
    G -->|No| I[B = 1.0 Baseline]
    H --> J[Step 3: Measure Largest Size L]
    I --> J
    J --> K{L less than 25 mm OR L greater than 300 mm?}
    K -->|Yes| L[C = 1.5 Penalty]
    K -->|No| M[C = 1.0 Baseline]
    L --> N[Step 4: Evaluate Remaining Factors]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Compute T_h = A x B x C x D x E x F x T_b]
    O --> P[Output T_h in seconds]
    P --> Q[Apply Design Improvements if T_h exceeds 2.25 s]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Mapping of Handling Time Factors to Assembly Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT GEOMETRY]
        I1[Part Dimensions]
        I2[Mass Weight]
        I3[Material Properties]
    end
    
    subgraph HANDLING[HANDLING TIME COMPUTATION MODULE]
        H1[Symmetry Analysis - Factor A]
        H2[Thickness Analysis - Factor B]
        H3[Size Analysis - Factor C]
        H4[Access Analysis - Factor D]
        H5[Alignment Analysis - Factor E]
        H6[Fastener Analysis - Factor F]
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT METRICS]
        O1[Handling Time T_h seconds]
        O2[Assembly Cost Index]
        O3[DFA Score]
    end
    
    subgraph FEEDBACK[DESIGN FEEDBACK LOOP]
        F1[Suggest Flange]
        F2[Suggest Rib]
        F3[Suggest Chamfer]
        F4[Suggest Symmetry Fix]
    end
    
    I1 --> H1
    I1 --> H2
    I1 --> H3
    I2 --> H3
    I1 --> H4
    I1 --> H5
    I1 --> H6
    
    H1 --> O1
    H2 --> O1
    H3 --> O1
    H4 --> O1
    H5 --> O1
    H6 --> O1
    
    O1 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
    
    O1 --> F1
    O1 --> F2
    O1 --> F3
    O1 --> F4
```

## 4.3 Visual Map: The Human Dexterity "Goldilocks Zone"

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph THIN[THICKNESS ZONE - Vertical Axis]
        T1[t less than 0.8 mm: PENALTY]
        T2[t = 0.8 to 6 mm: OPTIMAL]
        T3[t greater than 6 mm: OK for most cases]
    end
    
    subgraph SIZE[SIZE ZONE - Horizontal Axis]
        S1[L less than 25 mm: PENALTY - too small]
        S2[L = 25 to 150 mm: OPTIMAL]
        S3[L = 150 to 300 mm: OK with two hands]
        S4[L greater than 300 mm: PENALTY - too large]
    end
    
    T2 --> S2
    T1 -.->|Compound Penalty| S1
    T1 -.->|Compound Penalty| S4
    T2 --> S3
    T3 --> S2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The intersecting "OPTIMAL" cell (thickness 0.8–6 mm AND size 25–150 mm) represents the **design-for-handling sweet spot** where T_h = 1.5 s baseline applies. Any part outside this zone incurs at least one multiplier of 1.5.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Q: State the Boothroyd-Dewhurst handling time equation and list the six geometric features (A, B, C, D, E, F) that act as multipliers on the base time T_b.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Boothroyd-Dewhurst handling time equation is:

$$T_h = A \times B \times C \times D \times E \times F \times T_b$$

where T_b = 1.5 seconds.

The six geometric feature multipliers are:
1. **A** — Symmetry (rotational or mirror)
2. **B** — Part thickness (penalty if t < 0.8 mm)
3. **C** — Part size / largest dimension (penalty if L < 25 mm or L > 300 mm)
4. **D** — Top/bottom access (obstructed vs. clear)
5. **E** — Alignment ease during insertion
6. **F** — Fastener operation requirement

Each multiplier is either **1.0** (no penalty) or **1.5** (penalty condition present).

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: For what values of part thickness and part size does the Boothroyd handling code assign a multiplier of 1.5? Justify each threshold with a brief reason.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Thickness threshold (B = 1.5):** When the minimum part thickness **t < 0.8 mm**.
- *Reason:* Parts thinner than 0.8 mm are flexible, slip easily, and cannot be grasped confidently, forcing the operator to use slower two-handed techniques.

**Size thresholds (C = 1.5):** Two cases trigger the penalty:
- When largest dimension **L < 25 mm**: Part is too small for comfortable finger grip; operator fumbles.
- When largest dimension **L > 300 mm**: Part is too large for a single operator to maneuver in confined assembly space.

Outside these limits, B = C = 1.0 (baseline).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [14 Marks] — KTU ESE December 2023 Pattern

**A rectangular sheet-metal lid is used in the assembly of an electronic enclosure. The lid has the following specifications:**
- Length L = 120 mm
- Width W = 80 mm
- Thickness t = 0.6 mm
- Mass = 0.07 kg
- The lid is asymmetric (single-sided flanges, no rotational symmetry, but has mirror symmetry along the length axis)
- The mating surface is partially obstructed by a wiring harness
- The lid must be aligned with four snap-fit posts during insertion
- No separate fasteners are used

**Solve the following sub-parts:**

**(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the total handling time T_h for the lid using the Boothroyd-Dewhurst method. Show every multiplier and the value of T_b used.

**(b) [7 marks]** The current design gives an unacceptably high T_h. Suggest **two specific design modifications** (one for thickness, one for size/symmetry) that would reduce the handling time. Recalculate the new T_h and discuss the percentage reduction achieved.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Calculation of T_h**

**Step 1: Base time.**
$$T_b = 1.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Symmetry multiplier A.**
The lid has mirror symmetry along the length axis but not rotational symmetry. A 180° flip is possible. The minimum rotation required is 180°.

In Boothroyd's coding, mirror symmetry (chiral flip) reduces the rotation angle by half, but does not fully eliminate it. Hence:

$$A = 1.0 \quad (\text{mirror symmetry is acceptable — no rotation penalty})$$

*(Note: Students may argue A = 1.5 if the part is fully asymmetric; both are accepted depending on interpretation, but mirror symmetry typically gives A = 1.0 in standard Boothroyd tables.)*

**Step 3: Thickness multiplier B.**
The lid thickness t = 0.6 mm, which is below the 0.8 mm threshold:

$$t = 0.6 \text{ mm} < 0.8 \text{ mm} \implies B = 1.5$$

**Step 4: Size multiplier C.**
Largest dimension L = 120 mm, which is within the comfortable range [25, 150] mm:

$$25 \text{ mm} \leq 120 \text{ mm} \leq 150 \text{ mm} \implies C = 1.0$$

**Step 5: Access multiplier D.**
The wiring harness obstructs the top mating surface:

$$D = 1.5$$

**Step 6: Alignment multiplier E.**
Four snap-fit posts require precise 4-way alignment:

$$E = 1.5$$

**Step 7: Fastener multiplier F.**
No fasteners (snap-fit only):

$$F = 1.0$$

**Step 8: Compute total handling time.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_h &= A \times B \times C \times D \times E \times F \times T_b \\
T_h &= 1.0 \times 1.5 \times 1.0 \times 1.5 \times 1.5 \times 1.0 \times 1.5 \\
T_h &= 1.5^4 \times 1.5 \\
T_h &= 5.0625 \times 1.5 \\
T_h &= 7.59375 \text{ seconds} \\
T_h &\approx 7.59 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (a) Final Answer:** $T_h \approx 7.59$ seconds.

---

**Part (b) — Design Improvements**

**Modification 1: Increase thickness (reduce B)**
- Add a small **embossed rib** or **stiffening bead** at the centre of the lid. This raises the effective grasping thickness from 0.6 mm to 1.5 mm.
- New B = 1.0 (eliminates thickness penalty).

**Modification 2: Improve access (reduce D)**
- Re-route the wiring harness to one side, providing clear top access to the mating surface.
- New D = 1.0 (eliminates access penalty).

**Recalculation with modifications:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{h,new} &= A \times B_{new} \times C \times D_{new} \times E \times F \times T_b \\
T_{h,new} &= 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.5 \times 1.0 \times 1.5 \\
T_{h,new} &= 2.25 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

**Percentage reduction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduction} &= \frac{T_h - T_{h,new}}{T_h} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{7.59 - 2.25}{7.59} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{5.34}{7.59} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 70.4\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b) Final Answer:** $T_{h,new} = 2.25$ s, achieving a **~70.4% reduction** in handling time.

---

### Question B [14 Marks] — KTU ESE December 2023 Pattern (Alternative Choice)

**A cylindrical metal pin is used as a locating pin in a gearbox assembly. The specifications are:**
- Diameter D = 8 mm
- Length L = 45 mm
- Mass = 0.018 kg
- The pin is **rotationally symmetric** (infinite symmetry)
- The pin must be inserted into a blind hole (no bottom access)
- Insertion requires moderate alignment (single chamfer on the pin)
- A circlip must be fitted after insertion to lock the pin

**Solve the following sub-parts:**

**(a) [7 marks]** Determine the total handling time T_h for the pin.

**(b) [7 marks]** If the diameter were reduced to D = 4 mm (mass proportionally reduced), explain how the handling time would change. State which multiplier(s) would be affected and recompute T_h.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Calculation of T_h**

**Step 1: Base time.**
$$T_b = 1.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Symmetry multiplier A.**
The pin is rotationally symmetric — any rotation produces the same part orientation:

$$A = 1.0 \quad (\text{infinite rotational symmetry})$$

**Step 3: Thickness multiplier B.**
The relevant "thickness" for a cylindrical pin is its diameter D = 8 mm (the cross-section that the operator's fingers grip):

$$D = 8 \text{ mm} > 0.8 \text{ mm} \implies B = 1.0$$

**Step 4: Size multiplier C.**
Largest dimension is the length L = 45 mm:

$$25 \text{ mm} \leq 45 \text{ mm} \leq 150 \text{ mm} \implies C = 1.0$$

**Step 5: Access multiplier D.**
The pin goes into a **blind hole** — no top access is needed for the pin itself (it is inserted from the top into a downward-facing hole). However, in the Boothroyd handling code, the "access" refers to whether the part can be placed from the top unobstructed. A blind hole is acceptable from the top; access is fine.

$$D = 1.0 \quad (\text{top access is available})$$

**Step 6: Alignment multiplier E.**
A single chamfer provides self-alignment:

$$E = 1.0 \quad (\text{chamfer eases alignment})$$

**Step 7: Fastener multiplier F.**
A **circlip must be fitted** — this counts as a separate fastener-type operation:

$$F = 1.5$$

**Step 8: Compute total handling time.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_h &= A \times B \times C \times D \times E \times F \times T_b \\
T_h &= 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.5 \times 1.5 \\
T_h &= 1.5 \times 1.5 \\
T_h &= 2.25 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (a) Final Answer:** $T_h = 2.25$ s.

---

**Part (b) — Effect of Reducing Diameter to 4 mm**

**Impact analysis:**
- **Symmetry (A):** Still rotationally symmetric → A = 1.0 (no change).
- **Thickness (B):** The "thickness" being gripped is now D = 4 mm. Since 4 mm > 0.8 mm, B = 1.0 (no change).
- **Size (C):** The largest dimension is still the length L = 45 mm, which is unchanged → C = 1.0 (no change).
- **All other factors (D, E, F):** Unchanged.

**Wait — does reducing diameter affect the size multiplier?**

The size multiplier C is based on the **largest projected dimension**, not the cross-section. Since L = 45 mm is unchanged, C remains 1.0. However, the part becomes more "fiddly" in practice because of the reduced cross-section. In strict Boothroyd modeling, this would not be captured by the standard multiplier; it is implicitly absorbed into the base time T_b or into factor B (since the graspable thickness decreased from 8 mm to 4 mm, but 4 mm is still above 0.8 mm).

**New calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{h,new} &= A \times B \times C \times D \times E \times F \times T_b \\
T_{h,new} &= 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.5 \times 1.5 \\
T_{h,new} &= 2.25 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b) Final Answer:** $T_{h,new} = 2.25$ s. The handling time **remains unchanged** in the strict Boothroyd model because all relevant geometric thresholds are still satisfied. *(In real life, a 4 mm pin would be harder to handle, but the Boothroyd system only flags this when the diameter drops below 0.8 mm, which is a known limitation of the model.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the Base Time T_b = 1.5 s** — Many students multiply all factors and stop, forgetting to multiply by T_b. The result is a dimensionless "penalty index" but not a time. **Always remember to multiply by 1.5 s at the end.**
> 
> 2. **Confusing the thickness threshold (0.8 mm) with the size threshold (25 mm)** — These are completely different dimensions! Thickness is the part's thinnest cross-section; size is the largest projected dimension. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 0 for the relevant sub-part.
> 
> 3. **Treating the size multiplier as a single threshold** — Many students forget the **upper threshold of 300 mm** for the C = 1.5 penalty. Parts between 150 mm and 300 mm are acceptable (C = 1.0); only above 300 mm does the penalty kick in.
> 
> 4. **Using the wrong symmetry rule for "asymmetric" parts** — Asymmetric parts (no symmetry) have A = 1.5, but mirror-symmetric parts have A = 1.0. Do not blindly assign A = 1.5 to every non-circular part.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting the chamfer/alignment factor E** — A chamfered entry that allows self-alignment gives E = 1.0, not 1.5. Always check the problem statement for chamfers.
> 
> 6. **Concluding "no improvement possible"** — When asked for design modifications, students often suggest vague changes like "make it stronger." Specific suggestions earn marks: "Add a 1 mm stiffening rib to increase t from 0.6 to 1.5 mm, reducing B from 1.5 to 1.0."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
> 
> - **Base Time:** $T_b = 1.5$ s (memorize this!)
> - **Thickness threshold:** $t < 0.8$ mm → B = 1.5; else B = 1.0
> - **Size thresholds:** $L < 25$ mm OR $L > 300$ mm → C = 1.5; else C = 1.0
> - **Comfortable size range:** 25 mm to 150 mm (single-hand optimal)
> - **Comfortable thickness range:** 0.8 mm to 6 mm
> - **Formula:** $T_h = A \times B \times C \times D \times E \times F \times T_b$ (six factors, all 1.0 or 1.5)
> - **Multiplicative penalty:** Multiple 1.5 factors compound as powers (1.5² = 2.25, 1.5³ = 3.375, etc.)
> - **Symmetry rule:** Rotational symmetry → A = 1.0; asymmetric → A = 1.5; mirror symmetry → A = 1.0
> - **Fastener rule:** Any separate fastener operation (screw, circlip, rivet) → F = 1.5
> - **Design improvements to suggest:** Flanges, ribs, chamfers, symmetry fixes, recessed grip zones, snap-fits (replace fasteners)
> - **Worst-case handling time:** $T_h = 1.5^6 \times 1.5 = 17.09$ s (avoid at all costs!)
> - **Baseline best case:** $T_h = 1.5$ s (well-designed part)
> - **Realistic good design:** $T_h = 2.25$ s (one penalty only)
> - **Industrial benchmark:** If $T_h > 3$ s, redesign is strongly recommended

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Effect of Part Thickness and Size on Handling Time**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Weight on Handling Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT524 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effect of Weight on Handling Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Design for Assembly (DFA)** — as defined in the **Boothroyd–Dewhurst–Knight (BDK) methodology** adopted in the KTU 2024 scheme — the **Handling Time (tₕ)** is the time required by an operator to acquire, orient, position, and prepare a single component for insertion or fastening during a manual assembly operation.

The **Effect of Weight on Handling Time** describes the empirical relationship between the **mass of a component (W)** and the time needed to manipulate it into its assembly position. This effect is non-linear and is characterized by a **logarithmic growth** in handling time as part weight increases, because heavier components require greater muscular effort, two-handed grasping, and/or mechanical assistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 — DFA):**
> The handling time tₕ is added to the insertion time tᵢ to obtain the **Total Assembly Time tₐ = tₕ + tᵢ**. Therefore, minimizing the weight of a part directly minimizes the assembly cost.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are assembling a mechanical toy at home:

- **Picking up a small plastic washer (5 g):** Your fingers grab it instantly — almost reflexive. Time ≈ 1 second.
- **Picking up a medium steel bracket (200 g):** You still grasp it with one hand, but you steady your wrist a little longer. Time ≈ 1.6 seconds.
- **Picking up a heavy cast-iron housing (3 kg):** You need **both hands**, you look for a firm grip, you might rotate it carefully to align the bolt holes. Time ≈ 2.5 seconds.

👉 Notice the key insight: **Doubling the weight does NOT double the handling time.** The growth is *logarithmic* — exactly like human perception of weight (the Weber–Fechner law of psychophysics).

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental design principle: **"Design the lightest part that meets functional requirements."** Reducing weight from 5 kg to 0.5 kg cuts handling time only modestly (because the curve is flat at low weights), but reduces **operator fatigue, ergonomic injury, and the need for hoists/robots** dramatically.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Weight threshold for "Lightweight" handling:** W ≤ 1 lb (≈ **0.45 kg**)
- **Weight threshold for "Heavy" handling:** W > 5 lb (≈ **2.27 kg**)
- **Empirical constants** in the Boothroyd handling-time equation (see Section 2).
- **Threshold above which mechanical handling is recommended:** W > 11 lb (≈ **5 kg**) — beyond this, manual handling is unsafe per ergonomic standards (NIOSH lifting limit).

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often test whether you can **quote the standard weight thresholds** (1 lb, 5 lb) — memorize them for short-answer questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Weight Influences Handling Time — The Underlying Physics

The operator's hand is a **mechanical manipulator with limited torque capacity**. As part mass W increases, three effects dominate:

1. **Grip Stability Requirement:** Heavier parts tend to slip; the operator compensates by tightening the grip → slower acquisition.
2. **Center-of-Gravity Alignment:** Heavier parts are more sensitive to orientation errors → operator spends more time aligning.
3. **Neuromuscular Effort:** The brain recruits more muscle fibers for heavier loads (Henneman's size principle), increasing reaction latency.

These effects combine to produce a **logarithmic relationship** between tₕ and W, very similar to human perception of stimulus intensity (Weber–Fechner Law):

$$t_{h} \propto \log_{10}(W)$$

## 2.2 The Boothroyd–Dewhurst Handling Time Equation

The canonical empirical formula used in the BDK DFMA charts is:

$$
